[5] "If it pleases the king," she said, "and if I have found favor in his sight, and if the matter seems right to the king, and I am pleasing in his eyes — let an order be written to revoke the dispatches Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite devised, the ones he wrote to wipe out the Jews in every province of the king.

[6] How can I bear to watch the catastrophe fall on my people? How can I bear to watch my kindred destroyed?"

[7] King Ahasuerus said to Queen Esther and to Mordecai the Jew, "Look — I have given Haman's estate to Esther, and they have hanged him on the gallows because he raised his hand against the Jews.

[8] Now you write whatever you want concerning the Jews, in the king's name, and seal it with the king's signet ring — because an edict written in the king's name and sealed with the king's ring cannot be revoked."

[9] So the king's scribes were summoned at that moment — the twenty-third day of the third month, the month of Sivan — and exactly what Mordecai dictated was written to the Jews, and to the satraps, governors, and officials of the provinces from India to Cush — one hundred and twenty-seven provinces — to each province in its own script, to each people in its own language, and to the Jews in their own script and language.

[10] He wrote in the name of King Ahasuerus, sealed it with the king's signet ring, and sent the letters by mounted couriers riding the royal stable's swiftest horses, bred from the king's racing stock.

[11] The king's decree granted the Jews in every city the right to gather and defend their lives — to destroy, kill, and annihilate any armed force of any people or province that attacked them, including women and children, and to plunder their goods —

[12] on a single day, throughout all the provinces of King Ahasuerus: the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar.

[13] A copy of the decree was to be issued as law in every province, published to all peoples, so the Jews would be ready on that day to take vengeance on their enemies.

[14] The couriers, mounted on the royal racing horses, raced out under the king's urgent command. The decree was published in Susa the citadel.

[15] Mordecai walked out from the king's presence in royal robes of blue and white, a great golden crown on his head, and a cloak of fine linen and purple. The city of Susa erupted in celebration.

[16] For the Jews, it was light and gladness, joy and honor.

[17] In every province, in every city, wherever the king's command and decree reached, the Jews had joy and gladness, feasting and a holiday. And many people of the land became Jews — because the terror of the Jews had fallen on them.

[1] The thirteenth day of the twelfth month — the month of Adar — arrived. The day the king's decree was set to take effect. The day the enemies of the Jews had been waiting for, expecting to crush them. The day flipped. The Jews crushed those who hated them.

[2] Across every province of King Xerxes' empire, the Jews gathered in their cities to strike down anyone seeking their destruction. Nobody could stand against them. Terror of them had fallen on every people.

[3] Every official in every province — the satraps, the governors, the administrators, the royal staff — they all backed the Jews. They had seen which way the wind was blowing. Mordecai had become someone to fear.

[4] Mordecai now held real power in the palace. His reputation spread through every province. The man was rising, and everyone knew it.

[5] So the Jews struck their enemies with the sword. Killed them. Destroyed them. Did with their haters whatever they wanted.

[6] In the fortress of Susa alone, they killed five hundred men.

[7] Among the dead: Parshandatha, Dalphon, Aspatha,

[8] Poratha, Adalia, Aridatha,

[9] Parmashta, Arisai, Aridai, and Vaizatha —

[10] the ten sons of Haman son of Hammedatha, the enemy of the Jews. They killed them. But they did not touch the plunder.

[11] That same day, the count of those killed in Susa reached the king.

[12] The king said to Queen Esther, "In the fortress of Susa alone, the Jews have killed five hundred men, including Haman's ten sons. What must they have done in the rest of the provinces? Now — what do you want? It's yours. What else are you asking? Done."

[13] Esther said, "If it pleases the king, let the Jews in Susa be granted one more day to act under today's decree. And let Haman's ten sons be hung on the gallows."

[14] The king ordered it done. The decree was issued in Susa, and they hung Haman's ten sons.

[15] The Jews in Susa gathered again on the fourteenth of Adar and killed three hundred men there. But they did not touch the plunder.

[16] Meanwhile, throughout the provinces, the Jews had gathered to defend themselves and get free of their enemies. They killed seventy-five thousand of those who hated them. But they did not touch the plunder.

[17] This happened on the thirteenth of Adar. On the fourteenth they rested — and made it a day of feasting and celebration.

[18] But the Jews in Susa had gathered on the thirteenth and the fourteenth, and rested on the fifteenth — so that became their day of feasting and celebration.

[19] This is why the Jews in rural towns, the unwalled villages, celebrate the fourteenth of Adar as a day of joy, feasting, and sending gifts of food to one another.

[20] Mordecai wrote it all down. Then he sent letters to every Jew in every province of King Xerxes' empire — near and far —

[21] binding them to observe the fourteenth and fifteenth of Adar every year,

[22] as the days when the Jews got free of their enemies, the month when grief flipped to joy and mourning to celebration. They were to make these days into feasting and gladness, sending food to each other and gifts to the poor.

[23] The Jews took up what they had already begun, and what Mordecai had written to them, as a permanent practice.

[24] Because Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite, enemy of all the Jews, had plotted to destroy them. He had cast the pur — that is, the lot — to crush them and wipe them out.

[25] But when the matter came before the king, he ordered in writing that the wicked scheme Haman had devised against the Jews come back on his own head. He and his sons were hung on the gallows.

[26] That is why these days are called Purim — from the word pur. Because of everything written in this letter, because of everything they had seen, because of everything that had happened to them,

[27] the Jews bound themselves, their descendants, and everyone who joined them to observe these two days without fail, every year, exactly as written, exactly on time.

[28] These days were to be remembered and kept in every generation, by every family, in every province, in every city. The days of Purim would never fade among the Jews. The memory of them would never die out among their descendants.

[29] Queen Esther daughter of Abihail, together with Mordecai the Jew, wrote with full authority to confirm this second letter about Purim.

[30] Letters went out to all the Jews in the hundred and twenty-seven provinces of Xerxes' kingdom — words of peace and truth —

[31] to fix the days of Purim at their appointed times, exactly as Mordecai the Jew and Queen Esther had ordained, exactly as the Jews had taken upon themselves and their descendants, including the matter of the fasts and their cries of grief.

[32] Esther's command sealed the regulations of Purim. And it was written down in the book.

[1] King Ahasuerus laid tribute on the land and on the islands of the sea. [2] Every act of his power and might, and the full account of Mordecai's greatness — how the king elevated him — all of it stands written in the chronicles of the kings of Media and Persia. [3] Mordecai the Jew ranked second only to King Ahasuerus. He was great among the Jews, beloved by the multitude of his kin, working for the good of his people and speaking peace over every last one of them.

[1] These are the names of Israel's sons who came down to Egypt with Jacob, each with his household: [2] Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, [3] Issachar, Zebulun, Benjamin, [4] Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher. [5] Seventy souls in all came from Jacob's own body. Joseph was already in Egypt.

[6] Then Joseph died. His brothers died. That whole generation died. [7] But the Israelites — they bred, they swarmed, they multiplied, they grew strong beyond measure. The land was thick with them.

[8] A new king rose over Egypt, a man who had never heard of Joseph. [9] He said to his people, "Look at them. The Israelites have become too many for us, too strong. [10] We have to outsmart them — otherwise they keep multiplying, and if war breaks out they'll join our enemies, fight against us, and walk right out of this land."

[11] So Egypt set slave drivers over them to crush them under forced labor. They built Pharaoh's supply cities — Pithom and Rameses. [12] But the more Egypt ground them down, the more they multiplied, the more they spread. Egypt began to dread the Israelites. [13] So they drove them harder, [14] poisoning their lives with brutal work — mortar, bricks, every kind of labor in the fields. Every task, ruthless.

[15] Then the king of Egypt spoke to the Hebrew midwives — one named Shiphrah, the other Puah. [16] "When you deliver Hebrew women," he said, "watch the birthstones. If it's a boy, kill him. If it's a girl, let her live."

[17] But the midwives feared God. They ignored the king of Egypt and let the boys live. [18] So the king summoned them. "Why are you doing this? Why are you letting the boys live?"

[19] The midwives told Pharaoh, "Hebrew women aren't like Egyptian women. They're vigorous. They give birth before the midwife even arrives."

[20] And God was good to the midwives. The people kept multiplying, growing stronger and stronger. [21] Because the midwives feared God, he gave them households of their own.

[22] Then Pharaoh issued the order to all his people: "Every boy born to the Hebrews — throw him into the Nile. Let the girls live."

[1] A man from the tribe of Levi went and married a Levite woman. [2] She conceived, gave birth to a son, and when she saw how beautiful he was, she hid him for three months. [3] When she could hide him no longer, she took a basket of papyrus, sealed it with tar and pitch, placed the child inside, and set it among the reeds at the river's edge. [4] His sister stationed herself at a distance to see what would happen to him.

[5] Pharaoh's daughter came down to bathe in the Nile while her attendants walked along the bank. She spotted the basket among the reeds and sent her servant to fetch it. [6] When she opened it and saw the child — a boy, crying — her heart broke. "One of the Hebrew babies," she said.

[7] Then his sister stepped forward and said to Pharaoh's daughter, "Shall I go find a Hebrew woman to nurse the child for you?"

[8] "Go," she said. So the girl went and called the child's own mother. [9] Pharaoh's daughter said to her, "Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will pay you." So the woman took her son and nursed him.

[10] When the boy was old enough, she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. She named him Moses, saying, "Because I drew him out of the water."

[11] Years later, when Moses was grown, he went out to where his own people were and saw the crushing labor they endured. He watched an Egyptian beating a Hebrew — one of his own. [12] He looked this way and that, saw no one, and struck the Egyptian dead, burying him in the sand.

[13] The next day he went out again and found two Hebrews fighting. He said to the one in the wrong, "Why are you hitting your own brother?"

[14] The man shot back, "Who made you ruler and judge over us? Are you going to kill me like you killed the Egyptian?"

Fear stabbed through Moses. *So it's known.* [15] When Pharaoh heard about it, he tried to have Moses killed. But Moses fled from Pharaoh and settled in the land of Midian, where he sat down beside a well.

[16] Now a priest of Midian had seven daughters. They came to draw water and fill the troughs for their father's flock. [17] Some shepherds showed up and drove them off. But Moses stood up, defended them, and watered their flock.

[18] When the daughters returned to their father Reuel, he asked, "How is it you're back so early today?"

[19] "An Egyptian rescued us from the shepherds," they said. "He even drew water for us and watered the flock."

[20] "Where is he?" he asked. "Why did you leave the man out there? Go bring him in — he should eat with us."

[21] Moses agreed to stay with the man, and Reuel gave him his daughter Zipporah in marriage. [22] She bore him a son, and Moses named him Gershom, saying, "I have been a stranger in a foreign land."

[23] Years dragged on. The king of Egypt died. The Israelites groaned under their slavery and cried out, and their cry for help rose up to God from the depths of their bondage. [24] God heard their groaning. He remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, with Jacob. [25] God saw the Israelites. And God knew.

[1] Moses was tending the flock of his father-in-law Jethro, the priest of Midian. He drove the sheep deep into the wilderness and came to Horeb, the mountain of God.

[2] There the angel of the Lord blazed up before him from inside a bush — fire pouring out of it, yet the bush itself did not burn down.

[3] Moses said, "I have to see this. Why isn't the bush consumed?"

[4] The Lord saw him turn aside to look. And God called to him from inside the bush: "Moses! Moses!"

He answered, "I'm here."

[5] "Don't come any closer," God said. "Take off your sandals. The ground you're standing on is holy."

[6] Then he said, "I am the God of your father — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob."

Moses hid his face. He was afraid to look at God.

[7] The Lord said, "I have seen the suffering of my people in Egypt. I have heard them crying out under their slave drivers. I know their pain.

[8] I have come down to pull them out of Egypt's grip and lead them up to a good land, a wide-open land — a land flowing with milk and honey — the country of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.

[9] The cry of Israel has reached me. I have seen how brutally the Egyptians are crushing them.

[10] So go. I'm sending you to Pharaoh. Bring my people, the Israelites, out of Egypt."

[11] Moses said to God, "Who am I — that I should walk up to Pharaoh? That I should bring Israel out of Egypt?"

[12] God said, "I will be with you. And this will be your sign that I sent you: when you have led the people out of Egypt, you will worship God on this very mountain."

[13] Moses pressed further. "Suppose I go to the Israelites and tell them, 'The God of your fathers sent me.' And they ask, 'What is his name?' What do I tell them?"

[14] God said to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM. Tell the Israelites: 'I AM has sent me to you.'"

[15] And God said further to Moses: "Tell the Israelites this: 'The Lord, the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob — has sent me to you.' This is my name forever. This is how I am to be remembered, generation after generation.

[16] Go. Gather the elders of Israel and tell them: 'The Lord, the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — appeared to me. He said: I have been watching. I have seen what is being done to you in Egypt.

[17] And I have promised to bring you up out of your misery in Egypt to the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites — a land flowing with milk and honey.'

[18] They will listen to you. Then you and the elders of Israel will go to the king of Egypt and tell him: 'The Lord, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. Let us go three days into the wilderness to sacrifice to the Lord our God.'

[19] But I know the king of Egypt will not let you go — not unless a mighty hand forces him.

[20] So I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with every wonder I can work in their midst. After that, he will let you go.

[21] And I will make the Egyptians look on my people with favor — so that when you leave, you will not leave empty-handed.

[22] Every woman will ask her neighbor and any woman staying in her house for silver, for gold, for fine clothing. You will put them on your sons and daughters. You will strip Egypt clean."

[1] Moses pushed back: "But what if they don't believe me? What if they won't listen? What if they say, 'The LORD never appeared to you'?"

[2] The LORD said, "What's that in your hand?"

"A staff," Moses answered.

[3] "Throw it on the ground."

He threw it down — and it became a snake. Moses bolted from it.

[4] "Reach out and grab it by the tail," the LORD said. Moses reached out, caught it, and it stiffened back into a staff in his palm.

[5] "Do this," the LORD said, "and they'll believe that the LORD — the God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob — has appeared to you."

[6] Then the LORD said, "Put your hand inside your cloak." Moses slid his hand in, pulled it out — and it was leprous, white as snow.

[7] "Put it back in," the LORD said. He did. When he drew it out again, it was whole, like the rest of his flesh.

[8] "If they won't believe you, if they won't be moved by the first sign, the second one will convince them.

[9] And if they still refuse — both signs — and still won't listen, then take water from the Nile and pour it on dry ground. The water from the Nile will turn to blood on the dust."

[10] Moses said to the LORD, "Please, my Lord — I've never been a man of words. Not yesterday, not the day before, not since you started speaking to your servant. My mouth is heavy. My tongue is slow."

[11] The LORD answered, "Who gave humans a mouth in the first place? Who makes someone mute, or deaf, or sighted, or blind? Isn't it I, the LORD?

[12] Now go. I'll be with your mouth. I'll teach you what to say."

[13] But Moses said, "Please, my Lord — send someone else. Anyone else."

[14] The LORD's anger flared against Moses. "What about your brother Aaron, the Levite? I know he can talk. He's already on his way to meet you, and when he sees you, his heart will leap.

[15] You'll speak to him and put the words in his mouth. I'll be with your mouth and his — and I'll show you both what to do.

[16] He'll speak to the people for you. He'll be your mouth; you'll be like God to him.

[17] And take this staff in your hand. With it, you'll perform the signs."

[18] Moses went back to his father-in-law Jethro. "Let me return to my people in Egypt," he said. "I need to see if any of them are still alive."

"Go in peace," Jethro answered.

[19] In Midian the LORD had told Moses, "Go back to Egypt. Every man who wanted you dead is dead."

[20] So Moses took his wife and his sons, set them on a donkey, and headed back to Egypt — the staff of God in his hand.

[21] The LORD said to Moses, "When you get to Egypt, see that you perform every wonder I've put in your power before Pharaoh. But I'll harden his heart, and he won't let the people go.

[22] Then tell Pharaoh: This is what the LORD says — Israel is my son. My firstborn.

[23] I told you: let my son go, so he can worship me. You refused. So now I will kill your son. Your firstborn."

[24] On the road, at a lodging place, the LORD met Moses — and tried to kill him.

[25] Zipporah grabbed a flint, sliced off her son's foreskin, and pressed it to Moses' feet. "You are a bridegroom of blood to me," she said.

[26] So the LORD let him go. ("Bridegroom of blood," she said — because of the circumcision.)

[27] The LORD said to Aaron, "Go into the wilderness. Meet Moses." Aaron went, found him at the mountain of God, and kissed him.

[28] Moses told Aaron everything — every word the LORD had sent him to say, every sign he'd been ordered to perform.

[29] So Moses and Aaron went and gathered all the elders of Israel.

[30] Aaron repeated every word the LORD had spoken to Moses, and Moses performed the signs before the people's eyes.

[31] And the people believed. When they heard that the LORD had taken notice of Israel — that he had seen their suffering — they bowed their heads and worshiped.

[1] After this, Moses and Aaron walked into Pharaoh's court and delivered the message: "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: Let my people go, so they can hold a festival for me in the wilderness."

[2] Pharaoh shot back, "Who is this LORD, that I should listen to him and release Israel? I don't know any LORD, and I'm not letting Israel go."

[3] They pressed him: "The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Please — let us travel three days into the wilderness and offer sacrifices to the LORD our God, or he'll strike us down with plague or sword."

[4] But the king of Egypt snapped, "Moses, Aaron — why are you pulling the people away from their work? Get back to your labor!"

[5] And Pharaoh added, "Look — there are massive numbers of these people in the land now, and you want to stop them from working?"

[6] That same day, Pharaoh gave the order to the slave-drivers and the Hebrew foremen:

[7] "Stop giving the people straw to make bricks like before. Let them go gather their own straw.

[8] But the brick quota stays the same — every last one. Don't reduce it. They're lazy. That's why they're crying out, 'Let us go sacrifice to our God.'

[9] Pile more work on them. Keep them busy. Then they won't have time for lies."

[10] So the slave-drivers and foremen went out and told the people: "Pharaoh's word: I will not give you straw anymore.

[11] Go find your own straw, wherever you can — but your workload will not be cut by a single brick."

[12] The people scattered across Egypt, scavenging stubble for straw.

[13] The slave-drivers kept driving them: "Hit your daily quota, just like when you had straw."

[14] And the Hebrew foremen — the ones Pharaoh's men had appointed — were beaten and interrogated: "Why haven't you made your brick quota, yesterday or today, like before?"

[15] So the Israelite foremen went to Pharaoh and pleaded: "Why are you treating your servants this way?

[16] No straw is given to us, yet they say, 'Make bricks!' Look — your servants are being beaten, but the fault is with your own people."

[17] He answered, "Lazy. That's what you are. Lazy. That's why you say, 'Let us go sacrifice to the LORD.'

[18] Get back to work. No straw will be given, and you'll still deliver your full quota of bricks."

[19] The Israelite foremen saw the trap they were in when they were told, "You will not reduce your daily brick count by a single brick."

[20] As they came out from Pharaoh's presence, they ran into Moses and Aaron waiting for them —

[21] and they let them have it: "May the LORD see what you've done and judge you. You've made us a stench to Pharaoh and his officials. You've put a sword in their hand to kill us."

[22] Moses went back to the LORD and said, "Lord — why have you brought disaster on this people? Why did you ever send me?

[23] From the moment I walked in to speak in your name, Pharaoh has crushed them harder. And you — you have done nothing to rescue your people."

[1] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Now you'll see what I'm about to do to Pharaoh. A mighty hand will drive him to release them. A mighty hand will drive him to throw them out of his land."

[2] God spoke to Moses. He said: "I am the LORD.

[3] I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, to Jacob — as God Almighty. But by my name, the LORD — I did not let them know me.

[4] I cut a covenant with them: to give them Canaan, the land where they wandered, where they lived as strangers.

[5] And now — I have heard. The groaning of Israel under Egyptian chains has reached me. I have remembered my covenant.

[6] So tell the Israelites this: I am the LORD. I will drag you out from under the burdens of Egypt. I will rip you free from their slavery. I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and acts of crushing judgment.

[7] I will take you to be my people. I will be your God. And you will know — know in your bones — that I am the LORD your God, the one who pulled you out from under the weight of Egypt.

[8] I will bring you into the land I swore — hand raised — to give to Abraham, to Isaac, to Jacob. I will give it to you as your inheritance. I am the LORD."

[9] Moses delivered the message to the Israelites. They didn't listen. Their spirits were crushed. The labor was brutal.

[10] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[11] "Go. Speak to Pharaoh, king of Egypt. Tell him to let the Israelites walk out of his land."

[12] Moses answered the LORD: "Look — the Israelites won't even listen to me. Why would Pharaoh? And my lips — they stumble."

[13] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron. He commissioned them concerning the Israelites and concerning Pharaoh, king of Egypt — to bring Israel out of the land of Egypt.

[14] These are the heads of their fathers' houses:

The sons of Reuben, Israel's firstborn: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, Carmi. These are the clans of Reuben.

[15] The sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar — and Shaul, son of a Canaanite woman. These are the clans of Simeon.

[16] These are the names of Levi's sons by their generations: Gershon, Kohath, Merari. Levi lived 137 years.

[17] The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei — by their clans.

[18] The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel. Kohath lived 133 years.

[19] The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of Levi by their generations.

[20] Amram married Jochebed, his father's sister. She bore him Aaron and Moses. Amram lived 137 years.

[21] The sons of Izhar: Korah, Nepheg, Zichri.

[22] The sons of Uzziel: Mishael, Elzaphan, Sithri.

[23] Aaron married Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon. She bore him Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar.

[24] The sons of Korah: Assir, Elkanah, Abiasaph. These are the clans of the Korahites.

[25] Eleazar, son of Aaron, married one of Putiel's daughters. She bore him Phinehas. These are the heads of the Levite fathers' houses by their clans.

[26] This is the Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said: "Bring the Israelites out of Egypt by their divisions."

[27] These are the ones who confronted Pharaoh, king of Egypt, to bring the Israelites out of Egypt. This Moses. This Aaron.

[28] On the day the LORD spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt —

[29] the LORD said to him: "I am the LORD. Tell Pharaoh, king of Egypt, everything I tell you."

[30] And Moses said before the LORD: "Look — my lips stumble. Why would Pharaoh listen to me?"

[1] The LORD said to Moses: "Look — I'm making you like God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron will be your prophet. [2] You speak everything I command you, and Aaron your brother will deliver it to Pharaoh — that he must release the Israelites from his land. [3] But I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and though I multiply my signs and wonders across Egypt, [4] Pharaoh won't listen. So I will lay my hand on Egypt and bring my armies out — my people, the Israelites — through staggering acts of judgment. [5] And Egypt will know that I am the LORD when I stretch out my hand against them and lead Israel out from among them."

[6] Moses and Aaron did it. Exactly as the LORD commanded, so they did. [7] Moses was eighty. Aaron was eighty-three. That's when they stood before Pharaoh.

[8] The LORD said to Moses and Aaron, [9] "When Pharaoh demands, 'Show me a wonder,' tell Aaron: 'Take your staff. Throw it down in front of Pharaoh.' It will become a serpent."

[10] So Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh and did exactly what the LORD had said. Aaron threw down his staff before Pharaoh and his officials — and it became a serpent. [11] Pharaoh called for his wise men and sorcerers, and Egypt's magicians did the same thing with their secret arts: [12] each one threw down his staff, and each staff became a serpent. But Aaron's staff swallowed theirs whole. [13] Still, Pharaoh's heart turned to stone. He wouldn't listen — just as the LORD had said.

[14] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Pharaoh is immovable. He refuses to let the people go. [15] In the morning, go to him. He'll be heading down to the water. Wait for him on the bank of the Nile, and take in your hand the staff that turned into a serpent. [16] Say this to him: 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, sent me to tell you: Release my people so they can worship me in the wilderness. And you still haven't listened. [17] So here is how you will know that I am the LORD. Watch — I'm striking the water of the Nile with this staff in my hand, and it will turn to blood. [18] The fish in the Nile will die. The river will reek. Egyptians will be unable to drink from it.'"

[19] The LORD said to Moses: "Tell Aaron: Take your staff. Stretch your hand over the waters of Egypt — over their rivers, their canals, their ponds, every reservoir of water — and they will become blood. Blood throughout the land of Egypt, even in vessels of wood and stone."

[20] Moses and Aaron did just as the LORD commanded. Aaron raised the staff and struck the water of the Nile in full view of Pharaoh and his officials — and all the water of the Nile turned to blood. [21] The fish died. The river stank. Egypt couldn't drink from it. Blood was everywhere in the land.

[22] But Egypt's magicians did the same thing with their secret arts. So Pharaoh's heart stayed hard. He wouldn't listen — just as the LORD had said. [23] Pharaoh turned and walked back to his palace. He didn't take any of it to heart. [24] Meanwhile, all of Egypt dug along the Nile for water to drink, because they couldn't drink from the river itself.

[25] Seven full days passed after the LORD struck the Nile.

[1] Then the LORD said to Moses: Go to Pharaoh. Tell him: This is what the LORD says—Let my people go, so they can worship me.

[2] If you refuse, I'm going to strike your entire territory with frogs.

[3] The Nile will swarm with them. They'll climb up out of the water and come into your palace, into your bedroom, onto your bed. Into your officials' houses. Into the homes of your people. Into your ovens. Into your kneading bowls.

[4] They'll be crawling on you, on your people, on every last one of your officials.

[5] The LORD said to Moses: Tell Aaron—stretch out your hand with your staff over the rivers, the canals, the ponds. Bring up the frogs over Egypt.

[6] Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt, and the frogs came up and covered the land.

[7] But the magicians did the same thing with their secret arts. They brought up frogs over Egypt too.

[8] Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron. He said: Pray to the LORD. Get these frogs off me, off my people. I'll let your people go to sacrifice to the LORD.

[9] Moses said to Pharaoh: I'll give you the honor of setting the time. When should I pray for you and your officials and your people—when should the frogs be cleared from you and your houses, left only in the Nile?

[10] Tomorrow, Pharaoh said.

Moses replied: As you say. So you'll know there's no one like the LORD our God.

[11] The frogs will leave you and your houses, your officials and your people. They'll remain only in the Nile.

[12] Moses and Aaron left Pharaoh. Then Moses cried out to the LORD about the frogs he'd brought against Pharaoh.

[13] And the LORD did what Moses asked. The frogs died—in the houses, in the courtyards, in the fields.

[14] They piled them up in heaps. The whole land stank.

[15] But when Pharaoh saw the relief, he hardened his heart. He wouldn't listen. Just as the LORD had said.

[16] The LORD said to Moses: Tell Aaron—stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the ground. It will turn into gnats throughout all of Egypt.

[17] They did it. Aaron stretched out his hand with his staff and struck the dust. Gnats came swarming over people and animals. Every speck of dust in Egypt became a gnat.

[18] The magicians tried—with their secret arts they tried to produce gnats. They couldn't. The gnats were on people and animals alike.

[19] The magicians said to Pharaoh: This is the finger of God.

But Pharaoh's heart stayed hard. He wouldn't listen. Just as the LORD had said.

[20] The LORD said to Moses: Get up early in the morning. Stand in front of Pharaoh as he goes down to the water. Tell him: This is what the LORD says—Let my people go, so they can worship me.

[21] If you don't, I'm sending swarms of flies on you, on your officials, on your people, into your houses. The houses of Egypt will be full of flies. Even the ground they stand on.

[22] But that day I'll set apart the land of Goshen, where my people live. No swarms there. So you'll know that I, the LORD, am here in this land.

[23] I will make a distinction between my people and your people. Tomorrow this sign happens.

[24] And the LORD did it. Dense swarms of flies invaded Pharaoh's palace, his officials' houses, all of Egypt. The land was ruined by them.

[25] Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron. He said: Go. Sacrifice to your God—but here in this land.

[26] Moses said: That wouldn't be right. What we sacrifice to the LORD our God is detestable to the Egyptians. If we sacrifice what they consider sacred right in front of them, they'll stone us.

[27] We have to go three days into the wilderness and sacrifice to the LORD our God as he commands us.

[28] Pharaoh said: I'll let you go. You can sacrifice to the LORD your God in the wilderness. Just don't go very far. Now pray for me.

[29] Moses said: The moment I leave you, I'll pray to the LORD. Tomorrow the flies will leave Pharaoh and his officials and his people. Only—Pharaoh must stop playing games. He must not refuse again to let the people go to sacrifice to the LORD.

[30] Moses left Pharaoh and prayed to the LORD.

[31] And the LORD did what Moses asked. He removed the flies from Pharaoh, from his officials, from his people. Not one was left.

[32] But this time too, Pharaoh hardened his heart. He would not let the people go.

[1] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Go to Pharaoh and tell him — this is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says: Let my people go, so they can worship me.

[2] If you keep refusing — if you keep holding them —

[3] watch what happens. The hand of the LORD is about to fall on your livestock in the fields: your horses, your donkeys, your camels, your cattle, your sheep. A devastating plague.

[4] But the LORD will draw a line between Israel's livestock and Egypt's. Not one animal belonging to the Israelites will die."

[5] The LORD set the time: "Tomorrow. Tomorrow the LORD does this in the land."

[6] And the next day, he did exactly that. Every animal in Egypt's fields died. From Israel's herds — not one.

[7] Pharaoh sent messengers to check. Sure enough — not a single Israelite animal was dead. And still his heart locked shut. He would not let them go.

[8] Then the LORD said to Moses and Aaron, "Take handfuls of soot from a furnace. Have Moses fling it skyward in front of Pharaoh.

[9] It will settle as fine dust across all of Egypt, and on every person and every animal it will erupt into festering boils."

[10] So they took the soot, stood before Pharaoh, and Moses threw it into the air — and boils broke out, oozing and raw, on people and animals alike.

[11] The magicians couldn't even stand in front of Moses now — the boils were on them too, on every Egyptian.

[12] But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart. He wouldn't listen — exactly as the LORD had told Moses.

[13] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Get up early. Stand in front of Pharaoh and tell him — this is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says: Let my people go, so they can worship me.

[14] Because this time I'm sending the full weight of my plagues — straight at you, at your officials, at your people. So you will know there is no one like me in all the earth.

[15] By now I could have stretched out my hand and struck you and your people with a plague that wiped you from the face of the earth.

[16] But this is exactly why I have kept you standing: to show you my power, and to make my name resound across the whole world.

[17] You're still strutting against my people. Still refusing to let them go.

[18] So tomorrow, at this very hour, I will unleash a hailstorm like nothing Egypt has ever seen — not from the day it was founded until now.

[19] Get your livestock under cover. Get everyone out of the fields. Every person, every animal still outside when the hail falls — they die."

[20] Some of Pharaoh's officials took the LORD's word seriously. They rushed their servants and animals indoors.

[21] Others shrugged it off and left everything in the open.

[22] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch your hand toward the sky. Let the hail fall across all Egypt — on people, on animals, on every green thing in the fields."

[23] Moses raised his staff toward the heavens, and the LORD sent thunder and hail, and fire streaked down to the ground. The LORD rained hail on the land of Egypt.

[24] Hail — and fire flashing inside the hail — relentless, terrible, like nothing that nation had seen since it became a nation.

[25] Across all of Egypt the hail struck everything in the open: people, animals, crops. It shredded every tree and flattened every field.

[26] Only in Goshen, where the Israelites lived, was there no hail.

[27] Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron. "This time I've sinned," he said. "The LORD is in the right. I and my people are in the wrong.

[28] Pray to the LORD — we've had enough of this thundering and hail. I'll let you go. You don't have to stay any longer."

[29] Moses said, "The moment I leave the city, I'll lift my hands to the LORD. The thunder will stop, the hail will end — so you will know the earth belongs to the LORD.

[30] But I know you. You and your officials still don't fear the LORD God."

[31] (The flax and the barley were destroyed — the barley was already in head, the flax in bloom.

[32] The wheat and the spelt weren't ruined; they ripen later.)

[33] Moses left Pharaoh and the city, lifted his hands to the LORD, and the thunder and hail stopped. The rain stopped pouring down.

[34] But the moment Pharaoh saw it had stopped — the rain, the hail, the thunder — he sinned again. He hardened his heart, and so did his officials.

[35] Pharaoh's heart was a stone. He would not let the Israelites go — exactly as the LORD had said through Moses.

[1] Then the LORD spoke to Moses: "Go to Pharaoh. I have made his heart heavy, and the hearts of his officials too, so I can plant these signs of mine right in front of him.

[2] And so you can tell your children and your grandchildren how I made a mockery of Egypt, what signs I worked among them — so you'll all know I am the LORD."

[3] So Moses and Aaron walked into Pharaoh's court and said: "This is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says — How long will you refuse to bow before me? Release my people. Let them worship me.

[4] If you keep refusing — tomorrow I bring locusts into your territory.

[5] They will blanket the face of the earth until the ground itself disappears. They will devour whatever the hail left you, strip every tree growing in your fields.

[6] They will fill your palaces, the houses of your officials, every Egyptian home — a sight your fathers and grandfathers never saw, not from the day they first set foot on this land until now." Then Moses turned and walked out.

[7] Pharaoh's officials pressed him: "How long will this man be our trap? Let the people go. Let them worship the LORD their God. Don't you see yet? Egypt is finished."

[8] So Moses and Aaron were brought back before Pharaoh. He said, "Go. Worship the LORD your God. But — exactly who is going?"

[9] Moses answered: "All of us. Young and old. Our sons and our daughters. Our flocks and our herds. We have a festival to keep for the LORD."

[10] Pharaoh sneered: "Sure — may the LORD be with you, the same way I'm letting you leave with your children. Anyone can see what you're really plotting.

[11] No. Just the men. Go worship the LORD — that's what you asked for." And they were driven out of Pharaoh's presence.

[12] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Stretch out your hand over Egypt — bring the locusts. Let them swarm over the land and eat everything the hail left."

[13] So Moses stretched his staff over Egypt, and the LORD drove an east wind across the land — all that day, all that night. By morning the east wind had carried in the locusts.

[14] They poured into Egypt and settled across every inch of it — a swarm beyond anything ever seen, beyond anything that would ever come again.

[15] They blackened the ground until the land itself went dark. They ate everything — every plant, every fruit the hail had spared. Nothing green was left. Not a tree. Not a shoot. Nothing.

[16] Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron in a hurry. "I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you.

[17] Forgive my sin — just this once. Beg the LORD your God to take this death away from me."

[18] Moses left Pharaoh's court and prayed to the LORD.

[19] And the LORD turned the wind — a powerful west wind that lifted the locusts and hurled them into the Red Sea. Not one was left anywhere in Egypt.

[20] But the LORD made Pharaoh's heart hard again. He would not let the Israelites go.

[21] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Stretch out your hand toward the sky. Bring darkness over Egypt — darkness you can feel."

[22] Moses stretched his hand toward the sky, and a thick darkness covered Egypt for three days.

[23] No one could see another person. No one moved from where they were — for three days. But every Israelite had light where they lived.

[24] Pharaoh called for Moses. "Go. Worship the LORD. Your children can go with you. Just leave the flocks and herds behind."

[25] Moses answered: "You're going to hand us sacrifices and offerings to give to the LORD our God yourself?

[26] Our livestock comes with us. Not a hoof stays behind. We need them to worship the LORD our God — and we won't know what to offer until we get there."

[27] But the LORD made Pharaoh's heart hard. He refused to let them go.

[28] Pharaoh barked at Moses: "Get out. And don't ever show your face here again. The day you see my face is the day you die."

[29] Moses answered: "You've said it. I will never see your face again."

[1] The LORD said to Moses: "One more blow. One more, and I bring it down on Pharaoh and on Egypt. After that, he lets you go. When he finally releases you, he won't just send you — he'll drive you out, every last one of you.

[2] Now tell the people — every man, every woman — to ask their Egyptian neighbors for articles of silver and gold."

[3] And the LORD made the Egyptians look on Israel with favor. Moses himself was a towering figure in Egypt — the officials of Pharaoh and the common people alike held him in awe.

[4] Then Moses said: "This is what the LORD declares — At midnight I will move through Egypt.

[5] And every firstborn in the land will die. From Pharaoh's son who sits next in line for the throne, to the son of the slave girl bent over the millstone, to the firstborn of every animal in every field.

[6] A scream will rise across Egypt — a wail like nothing this land has ever heard, and nothing it will hear again.

[7] But in Israel? Not a dog will so much as growl — not at a person, not at an animal. That's how you'll know: the LORD draws a line between Egypt and Israel.

[8] Then these officials of yours will come down to me — they'll bow at my feet and beg: 'Go. Take your people and go.' And after that, I leave." Then Moses walked out of Pharaoh's presence, white-hot with anger.

[9] The LORD had told Moses: "Pharaoh won't listen to you — and that's the point. It clears the way for my wonders to multiply across this land."

[10] Moses and Aaron performed every one of these wonders right in front of Pharaoh. But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he refused to let the Israelites leave his land.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron there in Egypt:

[2] "This month — mark it. From now on it stands at the head of your year, the first of all months.

[3] Tell the whole community of Israel: On the tenth day of this month, every household takes a lamb — one per family, one per house.

[4] If the household is too small to finish a whole lamb, they share with the nearest neighbor, calculating by the number of people and what each one can eat.

[5] The lamb must be flawless — a year-old male, taken from the sheep or the goats.

[6] Keep it until the fourteenth of the month. Then, at twilight, the entire assembly of Israel will slaughter their lambs together.

[7] Take some of the blood. Smear it on the two doorposts and the lintel of every house where the lamb is eaten.

[8] That night, roast the meat over fire. Eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.

[9] Do not eat it raw. Do not boil it in water. Roast it whole over fire — head, legs, organs, all of it.

[10] Leave nothing until morning. Anything left when the sun comes up — burn it.

[11] And this is how you eat it: belt fastened, sandals on your feet, staff in your hand. Eat fast. This is the LORD's Passover.

[12] Tonight I move through Egypt. Every firstborn falls — human and animal alike. And every god of Egypt — I will bring them to judgment. I am the LORD.

[13] The blood will mark the houses where you are. When I see the blood, I pass over you. No plague will touch you when I strike the land of Egypt.

[14] This day will become a memorial. Celebrate it as a feast to the LORD — every generation, forever. This is a permanent decree.

[15] For seven days you eat unleavened bread. On the very first day, clear every trace of leaven from your houses. Anyone who eats anything leavened from day one through day seven — that person is cut off from Israel.

[16] On the first day hold a sacred assembly. On the seventh day, another. No work is to be done on those days — except preparing what each person needs to eat. That alone is permitted.

[17] Guard the Feast of Unleavened Bread, because on this very day I brought your armies out of Egypt. Keep this day across every generation — a permanent decree.

[18] From the evening of the fourteenth day of the first month until the evening of the twenty-first, you eat unleavened bread.

[19] For seven days no leaven is to be found in your houses. Anyone — foreigner or native-born — who eats anything leavened is cut off from the community of Israel.

[20] Eat nothing leavened. Wherever you live, eat unleavened bread."

[21] Moses summoned all the elders of Israel and told them: "Go. Pick out lambs for your families. Slaughter the Passover.

[22] Take a bunch of hyssop, dip it in the blood that's in the basin, and brush it across the lintel and the two doorposts. And no one steps outside his door until morning.

[23] The LORD will pass through to strike Egypt. When He sees the blood on the lintel and the doorposts, He will pass over that door and will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses and strike you.

[24] Keep this practice. It belongs to you and your children — forever.

[25] And when you enter the land the LORD will give you, just as He promised — keep this rite.

[26] When your children ask you, 'What does this ceremony mean to you?' —

[27] tell them: 'It is the Passover sacrifice to the LORD, who passed over the houses of Israel in Egypt when He struck the Egyptians but spared our families.'" And the people bowed low and worshiped.

[28] Then the Israelites went and did it. Exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron — that's what they did.

[29] And at midnight the LORD struck down every firstborn in the land of Egypt — from the firstborn of Pharaoh on his throne to the firstborn of the prisoner in the dungeon, and every firstborn of the livestock.

[30] Pharaoh rose in the night — he and all his officials and all Egypt — and a great cry tore through Egypt. There was not a house without someone dead.

[31] He summoned Moses and Aaron that very night: "Get up. Get out from among my people — you and the Israelites. Go. Worship the LORD as you've been demanding.

[32] Take your flocks. Take your herds. Take everything. Just go. And bless me too."

[33] The Egyptians pressed the people to leave the land — fast. "We're all going to die!" they said.

[34] So the people took their dough before it could rise — kneading bowls bound up in their cloaks, slung over their shoulders.

[35] And the Israelites did what Moses had told them: they asked the Egyptians for articles of silver and gold, and for clothing.

[36] The LORD had given the people such favor in the eyes of the Egyptians that they handed over whatever was asked. And so they stripped Egypt bare.

[37] The Israelites set out from Rameses toward Succoth — six hundred thousand men on foot, not counting women and children.

[38] A mixed multitude went up with them, along with vast herds of livestock — flocks and cattle without number.

[39] They baked the dough they'd carried out of Egypt into unleavened cakes. It hadn't risen — they'd been driven out with no time to wait, no time to prepare provisions for themselves.

[40] The Israelites had lived in Egypt four hundred and thirty years.

[41] And on the very day the four hundred and thirty years ended, all the armies of the LORD walked out of Egypt.

[42] That night the LORD kept watch to bring them out of Egypt. That same night belongs to the LORD — a vigil kept by every Israelite, every generation.

[43] The LORD said to Moses and Aaron: "Here is the rule for the Passover. No foreigner may eat it.

[44] But a slave purchased with money — once you've circumcised him, he may eat it.

[45] A temporary resident or hired worker does not eat it.

[46] It must be eaten inside one house. Carry none of the meat outside. Break none of its bones.

[47] The entire community of Israel keeps this feast.

[48] If a foreigner living among you wants to keep the Passover to the LORD, every male in his household must be circumcised. Then he may approach and keep it — he stands as a native of the land. But no uncircumcised person eats it.

[49] One law — for the native and for the foreigner who lives among you."

[50] All the Israelites did it — just as the LORD commanded Moses and Aaron, exactly so.

[51] And that very day the LORD brought the Israelites out of Egypt, army by army.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Set apart every firstborn for me. The first to break the womb among Israel — every one, human or animal — belongs to me."

[3] Moses said to the people: "Mark this day. The day you walked out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery — the LORD's hand muscled you out of there. No leavened bread on this day.

[4] You're leaving today, in the month of Abib.

[5] When the LORD brings you into the land of the Canaanite, Hittite, Amorite, Hivite, and Jebusite — the land he swore to your ancestors he'd give you, a land flowing with milk and honey — you will keep this ritual in this month.

[6] Seven days you eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day there's a festival for the LORD.

[7] Unleavened bread for seven days. No leaven anywhere in sight. No yeast anywhere within your borders.

[8] On that day, tell your child: 'This is because of what the LORD did for me when I came out of Egypt.'

[9] Wear it like a sign on your hand, like a reminder between your eyes — so the LORD's instruction stays on your tongue. Because the LORD's strong hand brought you out of Egypt.

[10] Keep this practice at its set time, year after year.

[11] When the LORD brings you into the land of the Canaanite — as he swore to you and your ancestors — and gives it to you,

[12] you will hand over to the LORD every firstborn of the womb. Every firstborn male of your livestock — the LORD's.

[13] Every firstborn donkey, redeem with a lamb. If you don't redeem it, break its neck. Every firstborn son among your children — redeem him.

[14] And when your child asks you down the road, 'What does this mean?' — tell them: 'With a strong hand the LORD brought us out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.

[15] When Pharaoh stubbornly refused to let us go, the LORD killed every firstborn in Egypt — from human firstborn to animal firstborn. That's why I sacrifice to the LORD every male that first opens the womb, and redeem every firstborn of my sons.'

[16] It will be like a sign on your hand, like a band between your eyes — because the LORD's strong hand brought us out of Egypt."

[17] When Pharaoh finally let the people go, God did not lead them along the Philistine road, even though it was the short route. God thought: "If they face war, they'll change their minds and run back to Egypt."

[18] So God took them the long way — through the wilderness, toward the Red Sea. Israel marched out of Egypt in formation, ready for battle.

[19] Moses carried Joseph's bones with him. Joseph had made the Israelites swear an oath: "When God comes to you — and he will come — carry my bones with you out of here."

[20] They left Succoth and camped at Etham, on the edge of the wilderness.

[21] The LORD went ahead of them — by day in a pillar of cloud to lead the way, by night in a pillar of fire to give them light. They could travel day or night.

[22] The pillar of cloud by day, the pillar of fire by night — never left its place in front of the people.

[1] Then the LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites to turn back and pitch camp in front of Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and the sea. Camp right by the water, opposite Baal-zephon.

[3] Pharaoh will look at this and say, 'The Israelites are lost. The wilderness has swallowed them.'

[4] I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and he will chase them. Then I will get my glory through Pharaoh and his entire army — and Egypt will know that I am the LORD."

So they did exactly that.

[5] When word reached the king of Egypt that the people had fled, Pharaoh and his officials flipped. "What have we done, letting Israel walk out on us? We've lost our entire labor force!"

[6] So Pharaoh harnessed his chariot and rallied his army.

[7] He took six hundred elite chariots and every other chariot in Egypt — each one with an officer commanding.

[8] The LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, king of Egypt, and he gave chase. The Israelites had marched out bold and unafraid,

[9] but now the Egyptians came after them — every horse, every chariot, every horseman, the whole army — and caught up to them camped by the sea, near Pi-hahiroth, opposite Baal-zephon.

[10] Pharaoh closed in. The Israelites looked up — and there came Egypt, marching down on them. Terror seized them. They cried out to the LORD.

[11] Then they turned on Moses: "Were there no graves in Egypt? Did you have to drag us into the wilderness to die? What have you done to us, hauling us out of Egypt?

[12] Is this not exactly what we told you back there? 'Leave us alone. Let us serve the Egyptians.' Better to slave for Egypt than die in this wasteland!"

[13] Moses answered the people: "Stop being afraid. Stand your ground. Watch the LORD rescue you today. These Egyptians you see right now — you will never see them again, ever.

[14] The LORD will fight for you. All you have to do is be still."

[15] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Why are you crying out to me? Tell the Israelites to move forward.

[16] Lift your staff. Stretch your hand over the sea and split it open. The Israelites will walk through the sea on dry ground.

[17] And I — I am hardening the hearts of the Egyptians so they go in after them. I will get my glory through Pharaoh, his entire army, his chariots, his horsemen.

[18] Egypt will know I am the LORD when I get my glory through Pharaoh, his chariots, his horsemen."

[19] The angel of God, who had been leading the Israelite camp, moved and took position behind them. The pillar of cloud shifted from in front and stood at their rear,

[20] settling between the Egyptian camp and the Israelite camp. To one side it was darkness. To the other it gave light through the night. Neither side could reach the other all night long.

[21] Then Moses stretched his hand over the sea, and the LORD drove the waters back with a fierce east wind that blew all night. He turned the sea into dry ground. The waters split.

[22] The Israelites walked through the middle of the sea on dry ground — a wall of water on their right, a wall of water on their left.

[23] The Egyptians came after them — every one of Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen — straight into the middle of the sea.

[24] Just before dawn, the LORD looked down on the Egyptian army from the pillar of fire and cloud and threw them into chaos.

[25] He jammed their chariot wheels so they could barely move. The Egyptians screamed, "Get out of here! The LORD is fighting for Israel against Egypt!"

[26] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch your hand over the sea. Let the waters come crashing back onto the Egyptians — their chariots, their horsemen."

[27] Moses stretched his hand over the sea. At daybreak the sea snapped back to its place, and the Egyptians ran straight into it. The LORD swept them into the heart of the sea.

[28] The waters returned, covering the chariots and the horsemen — Pharaoh's entire army that had charged into the sea after Israel. Not one survived.

[29] But the Israelites walked through on dry ground in the middle of the sea, the waters a wall to their right and to their left.

[30] That day the LORD rescued Israel from Egypt's grip. Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the shore.

[31] Israel saw the mighty hand the LORD had brought against Egypt — and the people feared the LORD, and they believed in the LORD and in his servant Moses.

[1] Then Moses and the Israelites sang this song to the LORD:

I will sing to the LORD — he has risen up in glory!
Horse and rider — he hurled them into the sea.

[2] The LORD is my strength, my song, my salvation.
This is my God — I will praise him.
My father's God — I will lift him high.

[3] The LORD is a warrior.
The LORD is his name.

[4] Pharaoh's chariots, Pharaoh's army — he flung them into the sea.
The pick of his officers drowned in the Red Sea.

[5] The deep swallowed them.
They sank to the bottom like a stone.

[6] Your right hand, LORD — majestic in power.
Your right hand, LORD — it shatters the enemy.

[7] With towering greatness you broke those who rose against you.
You loosed your fury — it burned them up like straw.

[8] One breath from your nostrils and the waters stacked themselves up,
the currents stood in walls,
the deep froze solid in the heart of the sea.

[9] The enemy boasted: I'll chase. I'll catch. I'll divide the spoil.
My appetite will get its fill of them.
I'll draw my sword. My hand will finish them.

[10] You blew with your breath — the sea covered them.
They sank like lead in the surging waters.

[11] Who is like you among the gods, LORD?
Who is like you — majestic in holiness,
terrible in praises, working wonders?

[12] You stretched out your right hand —
the earth swallowed them whole.

[13] In your steadfast love you led the people you redeemed.
In your strength you guided them to your holy home.

[14] The nations heard. They trembled.
Anguish gripped the inhabitants of Philistia.

[15] The chiefs of Edom panicked.
The leaders of Moab — trembling seized them.
The people of Canaan melted away.

[16] Terror and dread fell on them.
By the greatness of your arm they froze like stone —
until your people passed by, LORD,
until the people you ransomed passed by.

[17] You will bring them in and plant them on your own mountain —
the place, LORD, you made for your dwelling,
the sanctuary, Lord, your hands established.

[18] The LORD will reign — forever and ever.

[19] When Pharaoh's horses and chariots and horsemen plunged into the sea, the LORD brought the waters of the sea crashing back over them. But the Israelites walked through on dry ground in the middle of the sea.

[20] Then Miriam the prophet, Aaron's sister, picked up a tambourine, and all the women followed her out — tambourines in their hands, dancing.

[21] Miriam sang back to them:

Sing to the LORD — he has risen up in glory!
Horse and rider — he hurled them into the sea.

[22] Moses led Israel on from the Red Sea, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur. Three days they walked in that wilderness and found no water.

[23] They reached Marah, but they couldn't drink the water there — it was bitter. (That's why the place is called Marah — "Bitter.")

[24] The people turned on Moses: What are we supposed to drink?

[25] Moses cried out to the LORD, and the LORD showed him a piece of wood. He threw it into the water, and the water turned sweet.

There the LORD gave them a statute and a rule, and there he tested them.

[26] He said: If you'll really listen to the voice of the LORD your God, do what is right in his eyes, pay attention to his commands and keep his statutes — then none of the diseases I unleashed on Egypt will I bring on you. I am the LORD who heals you.

[27] Then they came to Elim — twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees. They camped there beside the water.

[1] They pulled out from Elim, and the whole community of Israel arrived at the wilderness of Sin, the stretch between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after leaving Egypt.

[2] Out there in the wilderness, the whole community came at Moses and Aaron with bitter complaints.

[3] "If only the LORD had killed us in Egypt," they said. "At least there we sat by pots of meat and ate bread until we were full. You dragged us into this wilderness to starve every last one of us to death."

[4] The LORD said to Moses, "I'm about to rain bread down from the sky for you. The people will go out each day and gather what they need for that day. This is how I'll test them — to see if they'll walk in my instruction or not.

[5] On the sixth day, when they prepare what they've brought in, it will turn out to be twice what they gather on the other days."

[6] So Moses and Aaron told all Israel, "By evening you will know it was the LORD who brought you out of Egypt,

[7] and in the morning you will see the glory of the LORD — because he has heard your complaints against him. Who are we, that you complain against us?"

[8] Then Moses said, "The LORD will give you meat to eat in the evening and bread to fill you in the morning, because he has heard the complaints you're hurling at him. Who are we? Your complaints aren't against us — they're against the LORD."

[9] Moses said to Aaron, "Tell the whole community of Israel: Come close to the LORD, because he has heard your complaints."

[10] As Aaron was still speaking to the assembled people of Israel, they turned toward the wilderness — and there it was: the glory of the LORD, blazing in the cloud.

[11] The LORD said to Moses,

[12] "I have heard the complaints of the Israelites. Tell them this: At twilight you will eat meat, and in the morning you will have your fill of bread. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God."

[13] That evening quail came up and blanketed the camp. In the morning, a layer of dew lay all around it.

[14] When the dew burned off, there on the desert floor lay something fine and flaky — fine as frost on the ground.

[15] When the Israelites saw it, they turned to each other and said, "What is that?" — because they had no idea what it was. Moses told them, "It's the bread the LORD has given you to eat.

[16] Here's what the LORD commands: Gather as much as each of you needs to eat — about two quarts per person, based on how many are in your tent."

[17] The Israelites did it. Some gathered more, some less.

[18] But when they measured it out, the one who gathered much had nothing extra, and the one who gathered little had no shortage. Each had gathered exactly what he needed to eat.

[19] Moses told them, "Don't keep any of it until morning."

[20] But some didn't listen to Moses. They kept part of it until morning — and it bred worms and stank. Moses was furious with them.

[21] Every morning they gathered it, each according to his appetite. When the sun grew hot, it melted away.

[22] On the sixth day they gathered twice the bread — four quarts per person — and all the leaders of the community came and reported it to Moses.

[23] He said to them, "This is what the LORD said: Tomorrow is a day of complete rest, a holy sabbath to the LORD. Bake what you want to bake, boil what you want to boil, and whatever is left over, set aside and keep until morning."

[24] So they kept it until morning, as Moses had ordered, and it did not stink — no worms in it.

[25] Moses said, "Eat it today, because today is a sabbath to the LORD. You won't find any out in the field today.

[26] Six days you gather it, but the seventh day — the sabbath — there will be none."

[27] Even so, some of the people went out on the seventh day to gather, and found nothing.

[28] The LORD said to Moses, "How long will you people refuse to keep my commands and my instructions?

[29] Look — the LORD has given you the sabbath. That's why on the sixth day he gives you bread for two. Every one of you, stay where you are on the seventh day. No one is to leave his place."

[30] So the people rested on the seventh day.

[31] Israel called the bread manna. It was white like coriander seed, and it tasted like wafers made with honey.

[32] Moses said, "This is what the LORD commands: Take two quarts of it and keep it for the generations to come, so they can see the bread I fed you in the wilderness when I brought you out of Egypt."

[33] So Moses said to Aaron, "Take a jar, put two quarts of manna in it, and set it before the LORD to be kept for the generations to come."

[34] As the LORD commanded Moses, Aaron set it in front of the covenant tablets to be preserved.

[35] The Israelites ate manna for forty years, until they reached a land where people lived. They ate manna until they came to the edge of Canaan.

[36] (Two quarts — that's a tenth of an ephah.)

[1] The whole community of Israel pulled out of the Sin Wilderness, moving stage by stage as the LORD directed, and pitched camp at Rephidim. But there was no water. None for the people to drink.

[2] So the people came at Moses, swinging: "Give us water. We're thirsty." Moses fired back, "Why are you coming at me? Why are you testing the LORD?"

[3] But thirst gnawed at them, and the people turned on Moses, grumbling: "Why did you drag us out of Egypt? To kill us — us and our children and our livestock — with thirst?"

[4] Moses cried out to the LORD: "What am I supposed to do with these people? Any minute now they'll stone me."

[5] The LORD answered Moses: "Walk out in front of the people. Take some of the elders of Israel with you. Take the staff — the one you struck the Nile with — and go.

[6] I'll be standing there in front of you, on the rock at Horeb. Strike the rock. Water will pour out of it. The people will drink." Moses did exactly that, with the elders of Israel watching.

[7] He named the place Massah and Meribah — Test and Quarrel — because the Israelites had picked a fight with him and put the LORD on trial, asking, "Is the LORD actually with us, or not?"

[8] Then Amalek came. They attacked Israel at Rephidim.

[9] Moses said to Joshua, "Pick our fighters. Go out tomorrow and engage Amalek. I'll be on top of the hill with the staff of God in my hand."

[10] Joshua did as Moses ordered and engaged Amalek, while Moses, Aaron, and Hur climbed the hill.

[11] Here's what happened: as long as Moses held his hand up, Israel was winning. The moment he let it drop, Amalek surged.

[12] Moses' arms grew heavy. So they rolled a stone under him, sat him on it, and Aaron and Hur held his hands up — one on each side — and his hands stayed steady until the sun went down.

[13] And Joshua cut down Amalek and his army with the edge of the sword.

[14] The LORD said to Moses: "Write this in a book as a record, and drill it into Joshua's ears: I will wipe the memory of Amalek clean off the face of the earth."

[15] Moses built an altar there and named it: The LORD Is My Banner.

[16] He said, "A hand has been raised against the throne of the LORD. The LORD is at war with Amalek — generation after generation."

[1] Word reached Jethro, priest of Midian and Moses' father-in-law, about everything God had done for Moses and for Israel his people — how the LORD had marched Israel out of Egypt.

[2] So Jethro took Zipporah, Moses' wife, who had been sent back home,

[3] along with her two sons. One was named Gershom — "I've been a stranger in foreign country" —

[4] and the other Eliezer — "My father's God came through for me; he saved me from Pharaoh's sword."

[5] Jethro came out to Moses in the wilderness, bringing Moses' wife and sons, to the place where Moses was camped at the mountain of God.

[6] He sent word ahead: "I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you — and your wife and her two sons are with me."

[7] Moses went out to meet him, bowed low, kissed him, and they greeted each other warmly before going into the tent.

[8] Then Moses told it all — everything the LORD had done to Pharaoh and Egypt for Israel's sake, every hardship along the road, and every time the LORD had pulled them through.

[9] Jethro was overjoyed at all the good the LORD had done for Israel, rescuing them from Egypt's grip.

[10] "Blessed be the LORD," Jethro said, "who tore you free from Egypt and Pharaoh — who tore this people out from under Egypt's hand.

[11] Now I know it for certain: the LORD towers above every god. In the very thing the Egyptians used to crush them, he crushed the Egyptians."

[12] Then Jethro offered a burnt offering and sacrifices to God, and Aaron came with all the elders of Israel to share a meal with Moses' father-in-law in the presence of God.

[13] The next day Moses sat to judge the people, and they stood around him from morning till evening.

[14] When Jethro saw what Moses was doing for the people, he said, "What is this you're doing? Why are you sitting there alone with everyone standing around you from dawn to dusk?"

[15] Moses answered, "Because the people come to me to inquire of God.

[16] When they have a dispute, they bring it to me. I judge between them and teach them God's statutes and instructions."

[17] His father-in-law said, "This isn't good — what you're doing.

[18] You'll wear yourself out, and these people too. The load is too heavy. You can't carry it alone.

[19] Listen to me — I'll give you counsel, and may God be with you. You stand for the people before God, bringing their cases to him.

[20] Teach them the statutes and instructions. Show them the road to walk and the work to do.

[21] But find capable men from among the people — God-fearing men, trustworthy men who refuse bribes. Set them over the people as leaders of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.

[22] Let them judge the people at all times. The hard cases they bring to you; the small ones they handle themselves. Lighten your load. Let them carry it with you.

[23] If you do this — and God commands it — you'll be able to endure, and all these people will go home in peace."

[24] Moses listened to his father-in-law and did everything he said.

[25] He chose capable men out of all Israel and put them in charge — leaders of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.

[26] They judged the people at all times. The hard cases they brought to Moses; the small ones they decided themselves.

[27] Then Moses sent his father-in-law on his way, and Jethro went home to his own country.

[1] Three months to the day after Israel walked out of Egypt, they arrived at the wilderness of Sinai.

[2] They had broken camp at Rephidim, entered the Sinai wilderness, and pitched there in the open country beneath the mountain. Israel camped, and there before the mountain they stayed.

[3] Moses climbed up to God. And the LORD called down to him from the mountain: "Tell the house of Jacob this. Speak it to the children of Israel.

[4] You saw what I did to Egypt. You saw how I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you here to myself.

[5] Now — if you will truly listen to my voice and hold fast to my covenant, you will be my treasured possession out of all peoples. The whole earth is mine,

[6] but you — you will be my kingdom of priests, a holy nation. These are the words. Speak them to Israel."

[7] Moses came down, called the elders of the people together, and laid out before them everything the LORD had commanded.

[8] And the people answered as one voice: "Everything the LORD has spoken — we will do it." Moses carried their words back up to the LORD.

[9] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Listen — I am coming to you in a dense cloud, so the people will hear me speaking with you and trust you from this day forward." And Moses reported the people's words to the LORD.

[10] The LORD said to Moses, "Go down to the people. Consecrate them today and tomorrow. Have them wash their clothes

[11] and be ready for the third day — because on the third day the LORD is coming down on Mount Sinai before the eyes of all the people.

[12] Mark off a boundary around the mountain. Tell them: 'Do not climb it. Do not even touch its edge. Anyone who touches the mountain dies.

[13] No hand shall touch them — they are to be stoned or shot through. Animal or human, they will not live.' Only when the ram's horn blasts a long note may they come up the mountain."

[14] So Moses came down from the mountain to the people, consecrated them, and they washed their clothes.

[15] He said to them, "Be ready for the third day. Do not go near a woman."

[16] When the third day dawned, thunder cracked the sky and lightning split it open. A heavy cloud settled on the mountain and a shofar blast tore through the camp — louder, louder — until everyone there trembled.

[17] Moses led the people out of the camp to meet God, and they stood at the foot of the mountain.

[18] Mount Sinai was wrapped in smoke from end to end, because the LORD had come down on it in fire. Smoke billowed up like smoke from a furnace, and the whole mountain shook violently.

[19] The shofar grew louder and louder. Moses spoke, and God answered him in thunder.

[20] The LORD came down on Mount Sinai, onto the summit, and called Moses up to the top. Moses went up.

[21] The LORD said, "Go down. Warn the people. They must not break through to look at the LORD, or many of them will fall.

[22] Even the priests who approach the LORD must consecrate themselves, or the LORD will break out against them."

[23] Moses said to the LORD, "The people cannot come up Sinai. You yourself warned us — 'Set the boundary around the mountain. Make it holy.'"

[24] The LORD answered, "Go. Go down. Then come back up — you and Aaron with you. But the priests and the people must not break through to come up to the LORD, or he will break out against them."

[25] So Moses went down to the people and spoke to them.

[1] Then God spoke. All these words:

[2] I am the Lord your God. I am the one who pulled you out of Egypt, out of the house where you were slaves.

[3] No other gods. Not in my presence.

[4] Don't carve idols for yourself. No image of anything — nothing up in the sky, nothing down on the earth, nothing in the waters below.

[5] Don't bow to them. Don't serve them. Because I, the Lord your God, am a jealous God. I track the guilt of fathers down through their children — to the third generation, to the fourth — when they reject me.

[6] But I pour out loyal love to the thousandth generation when they love me and keep my commands.

[7] Don't drag my name through the mud. The Lord will not let anyone walk free who treats his name like it's nothing.

[8] Remember the Sabbath day. Keep it holy.

[9] Six days you work. Six days you do everything you have to do.

[10] But the seventh day belongs to the Lord your God. No work. Not you, not your son, not your daughter, not your servants, not your animals, not the foreigner staying inside your gates.

[11] Because in six days the Lord made the heavens, the earth, the sea, everything in them — and on the seventh day he rested. That's why the Lord blessed the Sabbath day and set it apart as holy.

[12] Honor your father and your mother. So your days will stretch long in the land the Lord your God is giving you.

[13] Don't murder.

[14] Don't commit adultery.

[15] Don't steal.

[16] Don't lie about your neighbor in court.

[17] Don't crave your neighbor's house. Don't crave your neighbor's wife, his servant, his maid, his ox, his donkey — anything that belongs to him.

[18] The whole crowd was watching it — thunder cracking, lightning flashing, the trumpet screaming, the mountain smoking. They saw it, and they shook. They stood far off.

[19] They said to Moses, "You talk to us. We'll listen. But don't let God speak to us, or we'll die."

[20] Moses answered, "Don't be afraid. God has come to test you — to put the fear of him in your faces so you won't sin."

[21] The people kept their distance. Moses walked into the thick darkness where God was.

[22] The Lord said to Moses, "Tell the Israelites this: You saw for yourselves that I spoke to you from heaven.

[23] Don't make gods alongside me. No silver gods. No gold gods. Don't make them.

[24] Build me an altar of earth. Sacrifice your burnt offerings and peace offerings on it — your sheep, your cattle. Wherever I cause my name to be remembered, I will come to you and bless you.

[25] If you build me a stone altar, don't shape the stones with tools. The moment you swing a chisel at them, you've defiled them.

[26] And don't climb steps up to my altar — I won't have your nakedness exposed over it."

[1] These are the rulings you will set before them.

[2] When you buy a Hebrew slave, he serves six years. In the seventh, he walks free — no payment owed.

[3] If he came alone, he leaves alone. If he came with a wife, she walks out with him.

[4] But if his master gave him a wife and she bore him sons or daughters, the woman and her children belong to the master. He leaves alone.

[5] But if the slave declares plainly, "I love my master, my wife, my children — I will not go free,"

[6] then his master brings him before God, stations him at the door or the doorpost, and pierces his ear with an awl. He serves him for life.

[7] When a man sells his daughter as a servant, she does not go free the way the men do.

[8] If she fails to please the master who designated her for himself, he must let her be redeemed. He has no right to sell her to outsiders — he broke faith with her.

[9] If he designated her for his son, he must treat her as a daughter.

[10] If he takes another wife, he must not cut back her food, her clothing, or her marital rights.

[11] If he denies her these three, she walks out free — no payment, no silver.

[12] Whoever strikes a man dead is put to death.

[13] But if it was not premeditated — if God let it happen by his hand — I will appoint a place where he can flee.

[14] But if a man schemes against his neighbor and kills him with cunning, drag him even from my altar. He dies.

[15] Whoever strikes his father or mother is put to death.

[16] Whoever kidnaps a man — whether he has sold him or still holds him — is put to death.

[17] Whoever curses his father or mother is put to death.

[18] If men fight and one strikes the other with a stone or with his fist, and the man does not die but is laid up in bed,

[19] then if he gets back up and walks around outside on his cane, the one who struck him goes free — but he pays for the lost time and sees him fully healed.

[20] If a man strikes his male or female slave with a rod and the slave dies under his hand, he must be punished.

[21] But if the slave survives a day or two, there is no punishment — the slave is his property.
