[8] But Noah — Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD.

[9] This is the story of Noah. Noah was a righteous man, blameless among his generation. Noah walked with God.

[10] He fathered three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

[11] The earth was rotten in God's sight, swollen with violence.

[12] God looked at the earth — and there it was, ruined. Every living thing had corrupted its way.

[13] So God said to Noah: "I have decided to end all flesh. Violence fills the earth because of them. I am going to destroy them — and the earth with them.

[14] Build yourself an ark of cypress wood. Make rooms inside it. Seal it with pitch, inside and out.

[15] These are the dimensions: three hundred cubits long, fifty wide, thirty high.

[16] Build a roof, leaving a cubit's gap at the top. Put the door in the side. Three decks — lower, middle, upper.

[17] Listen — I am bringing a flood of water over the earth, to destroy every living thing under the sky that has the breath of life in it. Everything on the earth will die.

[18] But I will establish my covenant with you. You will enter the ark — you, your sons, your wife, your sons' wives — together.

[19] Of every living creature, bring two of each into the ark to keep them alive with you. Male and female.

[20] Birds of every kind, animals of every kind, every creeping thing of the ground — two of each will come to you, to live.

[21] And take with you every kind of food that is eaten. Store it. It will be food for you and for them."

[22] Noah did it. Everything God commanded him — he did it.

[1] Then the LORD said to Noah, "Get into the ark — you and your whole household. You're the one I've seen as righteous in this generation, the only one.

[2] Take seven pairs of every clean animal, male and mate, and one pair of every unclean animal, male and mate.

[3] And seven pairs of every bird in the sky too, male and female — keep their kinds alive across the face of the earth.

[4] Because in seven days I'm opening the sky. Forty days. Forty nights. Rain that doesn't stop. Every living thing I made — I'm wiping it off the ground."

[5] Noah did it. Everything the LORD told him, exactly.

[6] He was six hundred years old when the flood came — water surging over the earth.

[7] Noah went in. His sons with him, his wife, his sons' wives — into the ark, ahead of the waters.

[8] The clean animals and the unclean, the birds and everything that crawls the ground —

[9] they came to Noah, into the ark, two by two, male and female, just as God had commanded.

[10] Seven days passed. Then the floodwaters hit the earth.

[11] In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, the seventeenth day of the second month — that day — the deep cracked open. Every fountain of the great abyss burst up from below. The floodgates of the sky tore loose from above.

[12] Forty days. Forty nights. Rain.

[13] On that very day Noah walked into the ark — Noah, his sons Shem, Ham, and Japheth, his wife, his three daughters-in-law — all of them together.

[14] And with them every wild animal by its kind, every herd animal by its kind, every creature that crawls the ground by its kind, every bird by its kind — everything with wings.

[15] They came to Noah, into the ark, two by two — every creature with the breath of life in it.

[16] Male and female of every kind, exactly as God had commanded. Then the LORD shut him in.

[17] The flood ran forty days across the earth. The waters rose, lifted the ark, raised it high off the ground.

[18] The waters surged. They piled higher and higher. The ark drifted on the surface.

[19] Higher still. The waters overwhelmed the earth — every high mountain under the whole sky vanished beneath them.

[20] Twenty feet above the peaks — that's how deep. The mountains were gone.

[21] And everything that moved on the earth died — birds, herds, wild animals, the swarming creatures that swarmed the ground, and every human being.

[22] Everything with the breath of life in its nostrils, everything that lived on dry land — dead.

[23] He wiped out every living thing on the face of the earth — human, animal, crawling creature, bird of the sky. All erased. Only Noah was left, and those with him in the ark.

[24] And the waters held the earth under for a hundred and fifty days.

[1] But God remembered Noah — every wild thing, every animal in the ark with him. He sent a wind sweeping across the earth, and the waters began to drop. [2] The deep's springs sealed shut. The sky's floodgates closed. The downpour from heaven stopped. [3] The waters pulled back from the land, steady, slow — and after a hundred fifty days they had dropped low. [4] On the seventeenth day of the seventh month, the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. [5] The waters kept falling until the tenth month. On the first day of that month, the mountaintops broke the surface.

[6] Forty days later, Noah opened the window he had built into the ark [7] and released a raven. It flew out, back and forth, until the waters had dried from the earth. [8] Then he sent out a dove to see if the waters had eased off the ground. [9] But the dove found no place to set her foot. She came back to him in the ark — water still covered everything. He reached out, took her, and brought her in. [10] He waited another seven days and sent the dove out again. [11] She returned to him at evening — and there in her beak: a freshly plucked olive leaf. That was how Noah knew the waters had drained from the earth. [12] He waited seven more days and sent her out once more. This time she did not come back.

[13] In Noah's six hundred and first year, on the first day of the first month, the waters were gone from the earth. Noah pulled back the covering of the ark and looked — and the ground was dry. [14] By the twenty-seventh day of the second month, the earth was completely dry.

[15] Then God spoke to Noah: [16] "Come out of the ark — you, your wife, your sons, your sons' wives. [17] Bring out every living thing with you — birds, animals, every creature that moves along the ground. Let them swarm the earth, be fruitful, multiply across it." [18] So Noah came out, with his sons and his wife and his sons' wives. [19] Every animal, every creeping thing, every bird — everything that moves on the earth — came out of the ark by its kinds.

[20] Then Noah built an altar to the LORD. He took some of every clean animal, every clean bird, and offered burnt offerings on it. [21] The LORD breathed in the pleasing aroma, and said in his heart: *Never again. Never again will I curse the ground because of humanity — even though the bent of the human heart is toward evil from the start. Never again will I strike down every living thing the way I have just done.*

[22] *As long as the earth endures:
seedtime and harvest,
cold and heat,
summer and winter,
day and night —
they will not cease.*

[1] God blessed Noah and his sons. He told them: "Be fruitful. Multiply. Fill the earth.

[2] Every animal on land, every bird in the sky, everything that moves on the ground, every fish in the sea — they will live in dread of you. They are placed in your hand.

[3] Every living thing that moves is now your food. I give it all to you, as freely as I once gave you the green plants.

[4] But one rule: do not eat flesh with its life still in it — that is, with its blood.

[5] And for your own blood — the blood of your lives — I will demand a reckoning. From every animal I will demand it. From every human being I will demand it. If anyone takes a human life, I will require that life back.

[6] Whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed — because humanity was made in the image of God.

[7] As for you: be fruitful, multiply, swarm across the earth, multiply on it."

[8] Then God spoke to Noah and his sons:

[9] "Listen — I am establishing my covenant with you, and with your descendants after you,

[10] and with every living creature that came out of the ark with you — the birds, the livestock, every wild animal — every living thing on earth.

[11] Here is my covenant: never again will all life be wiped out by floodwaters. Never again will a flood destroy the earth.

[12] This," God said, "is the sign of the covenant I am making between me and you and every living creature with you — a covenant for all generations to come, forever:

[13] I have set my bow in the clouds. It will be the sign of the covenant between me and the earth.

[14] When I gather clouds over the earth and the bow appears in them,

[15] I will remember my covenant — the one between me and you and every living creature of every kind. Never again will the waters become a flood to destroy all flesh.

[16] When the bow is in the clouds, I will see it and remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of every kind on the earth."

[17] God said to Noah: "This is the sign of the covenant I have established between me and all flesh on the earth."

[18] The sons of Noah who came out of the ark were Shem, Ham, and Japheth. Ham was the father of Canaan.

[19] These three were Noah's sons, and from them the whole earth was repopulated.

[20] Noah, a man of the soil, was the first to plant a vineyard.

[21] He drank the wine, got drunk, and lay naked inside his tent.

[22] Ham, the father of Canaan, saw his father's nakedness and went outside and told his two brothers.

[23] Shem and Japheth took a cloak, laid it across both their shoulders, walked in backward, and covered their father. Their faces were turned away. They did not see him exposed.

[24] When Noah woke from his wine and learned what his youngest son had done to him,

[25] he said: "Cursed be Canaan. The lowest of slaves he will be to his brothers."

[26] And he said: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem. May Canaan be his slave.

[27] May God enlarge Japheth — let him live in the tents of Shem. And may Canaan be his slave."

[28] After the flood, Noah lived three hundred and fifty years.

[29] All his days came to nine hundred and fifty years. Then he died.

[1] Here is the family tree of Noah's sons — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — and the sons born to them after the flood.

[2] Japheth's line: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, Tiras.

[3] Gomer's sons: Ashkenaz, Riphath, Togarmah.

[4] Javan's sons: Elishah, Tarshish, the Kittim, the Dodanim.

[5] From these the coastland peoples spread out across their territories — each clan with its own tongue, its own land, its own nation.

[6] Ham's line: Cush, Egypt, Put, Canaan.

[7] Cush's sons: Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, Sabteca. Raamah's sons: Sheba and Dedan.

[8] Cush also fathered Nimrod, the first man on earth to wield real power.

[9] He was a hunter without equal, fierce before the LORD himself — which is why people still say, "Like Nimrod, a hunter without equal before the LORD."

[10] His kingdom began with Babel, Erech, Akkad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar.

[11] From there he pushed into Assyria and built Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Calah,

[12] and Resen — the great city — between Nineveh and Calah.

[13] Egypt fathered the Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim, Naphtuhim,

[14] Pathrusim, Casluhim (the Philistines came from there), and Caphtorim.

[15] Canaan fathered Sidon, his firstborn, then Heth,

[16] then the Jebusites, the Amorites, the Girgashites,

[17] the Hivites, the Arkites, the Sinites,

[18] the Arvadites, the Zemarites, the Hamathites. Eventually the Canaanite clans scattered,

[19] and their territory stretched from Sidon down toward Gerar as far as Gaza, then east toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboiim, reaching to Lasha.

[20] These are Ham's descendants — by clan, by language, by land, by nation.

[21] Shem also had sons. He was Japheth's older brother and the father of every descendant of Eber.

[22] Shem's line: Elam, Asshur, Arpachshad, Lud, Aram.

[23] Aram's sons: Uz, Hul, Gether, Mash.

[24] Arpachshad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber.

[25] Eber had two sons. One was named Peleg — "division" — because in his days the earth was split apart. His brother was Joktan.

[26] Joktan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah,

[27] Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah,

[28] Obal, Abimael, Sheba,

[29] Ophir, Havilah, Jobab — all sons of Joktan.

[30] Their land ran from Mesha toward Sephar, into the hill country of the east.

[31] These are Shem's descendants — by clan, by language, by land, by nation.

[32] These were the clans of Noah's sons, traced through their generations, nation by nation. From them the nations of the earth spread out after the flood.

[1] Once, the whole earth shared one tongue, one set of words.

[2] As people drifted east, they came upon a plain in the land of Shinar and settled there.

[3] They said to each other, "Come on — let's mold bricks and fire them hard." Bricks became their stone, tar their mortar.

[4] Then they said, "Come on — let's build ourselves a city, with a tower whose top punches into the sky. Let's make a name for ourselves, so we're not scattered across the face of the earth."

[5] The Lord came down to see the city and the tower these human beings were building.

[6] And the Lord said, "Look at them — one people, one language, and this is only the beginning. Nothing they set their minds to will be beyond their reach.

[7] Come — let us go down and tangle their language, so they can't understand a word the other is saying."

[8] So the Lord scattered them from there across the face of the whole earth, and the city sat unfinished.

[9] That is why it is called Babel — because there the Lord babbled the language of the whole earth, and from there the Lord scattered them across the face of all the earth.

[10] This is the line of Shem. Shem was a hundred years old when he fathered Arpachshad, two years after the flood.

[11] After Arpachshad was born, Shem lived another five hundred years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[12] Arpachshad was thirty-five when he fathered Shelah.

[13] After Shelah, Arpachshad lived another four hundred and three years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[14] Shelah was thirty when he fathered Eber.

[15] After Eber, Shelah lived another four hundred and three years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[16] Eber was thirty-four when he fathered Peleg.

[17] After Peleg, Eber lived another four hundred and thirty years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[18] Peleg was thirty when he fathered Reu.

[19] After Reu, Peleg lived another two hundred and nine years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[20] Reu was thirty-two when he fathered Serug.

[21] After Serug, Reu lived another two hundred and seven years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[22] Serug was thirty when he fathered Nahor.

[23] After Nahor, Serug lived another two hundred years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[24] Nahor was twenty-nine when he fathered Terah.

[25] After Terah, Nahor lived another hundred and nineteen years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[26] Terah was seventy when he fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran.

[27] This is the line of Terah. Terah fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran. Haran fathered Lot.

[28] Haran died in the lifetime of his father Terah, in the land of his birth — Ur of the Chaldeans.

[29] Abram and Nahor both took wives. Abram's wife was Sarai. Nahor's wife was Milcah, daughter of Haran — the father of Milcah and Iscah.

[30] Sarai was barren. She had no child.

[31] Terah gathered his son Abram, his grandson Lot (Haran's boy), and his daughter-in-law Sarai (Abram's wife), and they set out from Ur of the Chaldeans, bound for the land of Canaan. But when they reached Haran, they settled there.

[32] Terah lived two hundred and five years, and he died in Haran.

[1] The LORD said to Abram: Leave. Walk away from your country, from your kin, from your father's house — go to the land I will show you.

[2] I will make you into a great nation. I will bless you. I will make your name weighty, and you yourself will be a blessing.

[3] Those who bless you, I will bless. Whoever curses you, I will curse. And through you, every family on earth will find its blessing.

[4] So Abram went, just as the LORD had told him, and Lot went with him. Abram was seventy-five when he walked out of Haran.

[5] He took Sarai his wife, Lot his nephew, everything they had accumulated, and every person they had gathered in Haran — and they set out for Canaan. They arrived in Canaan.

[6] Abram crossed the land as far as Shechem, to the oak of Moreh. The Canaanites were already there, holding the country.

[7] The LORD appeared to Abram and said: To your offspring I will give this land. So Abram built an altar there to the LORD, who had shown himself to him.

[8] From there he moved on to the hill country east of Bethel and pitched his tent — Bethel to the west, Ai to the east. He built another altar to the LORD and called on his name.

[9] Then Abram pulled up stakes again, journeying south, stage by stage, toward the Negev.

[10] Famine struck the land. It hit hard. So Abram went down to Egypt to wait it out as a foreigner.

[11] As he was about to enter Egypt, he said to Sarai his wife: Listen — I know how beautiful you are.

[12] The moment the Egyptians see you, they'll say, "That's his wife," and they'll kill me and keep you.

[13] So tell them you're my sister. Then it'll go well for me because of you, and I'll stay alive.

[14] Sure enough, when Abram entered Egypt, the Egyptians saw the woman — saw she was stunning.

[15] Pharaoh's officials saw her, raved about her to Pharaoh, and the woman was taken into Pharaoh's palace.

[16] Pharaoh treated Abram well on her account — he ended up with sheep, cattle, donkeys, male and female servants, female donkeys, camels.

[17] But the LORD struck Pharaoh and his household with devastating plagues because of Sarai, Abram's wife.

[18] Pharaoh summoned Abram. What have you done to me? he demanded. Why didn't you tell me she was your wife?

[19] Why did you say, "She's my sister," so that I took her as my own? Here — take her. Go.

[20] Pharaoh gave his men orders, and they escorted Abram out — him, his wife, and everything he owned.

[1] So Abram pulled up from Egypt — he and his wife and everything he owned, with Lot beside him — and headed back into the Negev.

[2] Abram was heavy with wealth: livestock, silver, gold.

[3] He moved by stages out of the Negev, working his way back to Bethel, to the very spot where his tent had stood before, between Bethel and Ai —

[4] the place where he had built the altar at the start. And there Abram called on the name of the LORD.

[5] Lot, traveling with Abram, also had flocks and herds and tents of his own.

[6] But the land couldn't carry them both. Their holdings were too vast — they couldn't stay together.

[7] Friction broke out between Abram's herdsmen and Lot's. (The Canaanites and the Perizzites were living in the land at the time.)

[8] So Abram said to Lot, "Let's not have bad blood between us — between your men and mine. We're family.

[9] The whole land is right here in front of you. Let's split. If you go left, I'll go right. If you go right, I'll go left."

[10] Lot lifted his eyes and surveyed the Jordan Valley — every inch of it well-watered, lush as the garden of the LORD, rich as the land of Egypt — this was before the LORD wiped out Sodom and Gomorrah — all the way to Zoar.

[11] So Lot chose the Jordan Valley for himself and headed east. The two of them parted ways.

[12] Abram settled in Canaan; Lot settled among the cities of the plain and pitched his tents as far as Sodom.

[13] And the men of Sodom were vile — sinners against the LORD, deeply.

[14] After Lot had gone, the LORD said to Abram, "Lift your eyes from where you're standing. Look north, south, east, west.

[15] Every piece of land you can see — I'm giving it to you and to your descendants forever.

[16] I will make your offspring like the dust of the earth. If anyone could count the dust, then your descendants could be counted too.

[17] Get up. Walk the land — its length, its breadth. It's yours. I'm giving it to you."

[18] So Abram moved his tents and came to settle by the oaks of Mamre at Hebron. There he built an altar to the LORD.

[1] In the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim — [2] these four marched out to war against Bera king of Sodom, Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (which is Zoar).

[3] The five kings joined forces in the Valley of Siddim — the Salt Sea now covers it. [4] For twelve years they had bent the knee to Chedorlaomer. In the thirteenth, they rebelled. [5] So in the fourteenth, Chedorlaomer and his allies came crashing down. They crushed the Rephaim at Ashteroth-karnaim, the Zuzim at Ham, the Emim on the plain of Kiriathaim, [6] and the Horites in the hill country of Seir, driving them clear out to El-paran on the edge of the wilderness. [7] Then they wheeled back to En-mishpat (that is, Kadesh), and laid waste to the whole territory of the Amalekites, and the Amorites living at Hazazon-tamar too.

[8] That's when the king of Sodom marched out — with the kings of Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela (Zoar). They drew up battle lines in the Valley of Siddim [9] against Chedorlaomer king of Elam, Tidal king of Goiim, Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar — four kings against five.

[10] But the Valley of Siddim was riddled with tar pits. When the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah broke and ran, men tumbled into them. The survivors fled to the hills. [11] The invaders stripped Sodom and Gomorrah bare — every possession, every scrap of food — and pulled out. [12] And they took Lot, Abram's nephew, with them. He'd been living in Sodom. They took everything he owned.

[13] One man escaped and brought the news to Abram the Hebrew. Abram was camped by the oaks of Mamre the Amorite, who was brother to Eshcol and Aner — all of them bound to Abram in alliance.

[14] When Abram heard his kinsman had been taken, he mustered his trained men — three hundred and eighteen of them, born in his own household — and gave chase as far as Dan. [15] Under cover of night he split his force, fell on the enemy, and routed them. He drove them all the way to Hobah, north of Damascus. [16] He brought back everything — all the plunder, his nephew Lot and his possessions, the women, the people, all of it.

[17] When Abram came back from defeating Chedorlaomer and the kings with him, the king of Sodom rode out to meet him in the Valley of Shaveh (the King's Valley).

[18] And Melchizedek, king of Salem, brought out bread and wine. He was priest of God Most High. [19] He blessed Abram, saying:

"Blessed be Abram by God Most High,
maker of heaven and earth.
[20] And blessed be God Most High,
who delivered your enemies into your hand."

And Abram gave him a tenth of everything.

[21] Then the king of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me the people. Keep the goods for yourself."

[22] But Abram answered, "I have raised my hand to the LORD, God Most High, maker of heaven and earth, and sworn this: [23] not a thread, not a sandal strap, nothing of yours will I take. You will never say, 'I made Abram rich.' [24] Nothing for me — only what my men have already eaten, and the share belonging to Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre, who marched with me. Let them take their portion."

[1] After all this, the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision: "Don't be afraid, Abram. I am your shield. Your reward will be enormous."

[2] Abram answered, "Lord GOD, what could you possibly give me? I'm walking through life childless, and the heir of my house is Eliezer of Damascus."

[3] He pressed in: "You haven't given me offspring. A servant born in my house will inherit everything I have."

[4] Then the word of the LORD came back: "That man will not be your heir. Your own flesh and blood will be."

[5] He took Abram outside and said, "Look up. Count the stars — if you can." Then: "That's your offspring."

[6] Abram trusted the LORD, and the LORD counted it to him as righteousness.

[7] He said to him, "I am the LORD who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land as your inheritance."

[8] Abram asked, "Lord GOD, how will I know I'm going to possess it?"

[9] He answered, "Bring me a heifer three years old, a goat three years old, a ram three years old, a turtledove, and a young pigeon."

[10] Abram brought all of them. He split each animal down the middle and laid the halves opposite each other. The birds he did not divide.

[11] Vultures dropped down on the carcasses, and Abram drove them off.

[12] As the sun was setting, a heavy sleep fell on Abram — and with it, a darkness, thick and terrifying, pressed in on him.

[13] Then the LORD said: "Know this for certain — your descendants will live as strangers in a land that isn't theirs. They'll be enslaved and crushed for four hundred years.

[14] But I will bring judgment on the nation that enslaves them, and afterward they'll walk out loaded with wealth.

[15] As for you — you'll go to your fathers in peace. You'll be buried in a good old age.

[16] In the fourth generation your descendants will come back here, because the wickedness of the Amorites is not yet ripe enough for judgment."

[17] When the sun had gone down and darkness covered everything, suddenly a smoking firepot and a blazing torch passed between the pieces.

[18] That day the LORD cut a covenant with Abram: "To your offspring I give this land — from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates —

[19] the land of the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,

[20] the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaim,

[21] the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites, and the Jebusites."

[1] Sarai, Abram's wife, had given him no children. But she had an Egyptian slave-woman named Hagar.

[2] Sarai said to Abram, "Look — the LORD has shut me out from bearing. Go in to my slave. Maybe I can build a family through her." Abram listened to Sarai.

[3] So after Abram had been in Canaan ten years, Sarai took Hagar her Egyptian slave and handed her to her husband as a wife.

[4] He went in to Hagar, and she conceived. And the moment she knew she was pregnant, her mistress shrank in her eyes.

[5] Sarai said to Abram, "This wrong is on you. I put my slave in your arms, and now that she sees she's pregnant, I'm nothing to her. Let the LORD judge between me and you."

[6] Abram said, "Your slave is in your hands. Do to her whatever seems right to you." So Sarai broke her down, and Hagar ran.

[7] The angel of the LORD found her by a spring of water in the wilderness — the spring on the road to Shur.

[8] He said, "Hagar, slave of Sarai — where have you come from, and where are you going?" She said, "I'm running from Sarai, my mistress."

[9] The angel of the LORD said, "Go back to your mistress. Submit to her hand."

[10] And the angel of the LORD said, "I will multiply your offspring beyond counting — too many to number."

[11] And the angel of the LORD said to her: "You are pregnant. You will bear a son. You will name him Ishmael — *God hears* — because the LORD has heard your suffering.

[12] He will be a wild donkey of a man — his hand against everyone, everyone's hand against him, living defiant in the face of all his kin."

[13] So she named the LORD who spoke to her: *You are the God who sees me.* For she said, "Have I really looked on the One who looks on me — and lived to tell it?"

[14] That is why the well is called Beer-lahai-roi — *the Well of the Living One who sees me.* It lies between Kadesh and Bered.

[15] Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram named the son she bore Ishmael.

[16] Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore him Ishmael.

[1] When Abram was ninety-nine, the LORD appeared to him and said: "I am El Shaddai — God Almighty. Walk before me. Be whole. [2] I will set my covenant between you and me, and I will multiply you beyond counting."

[3] Abram fell on his face. And God spoke with him:

[4] "Here is my covenant with you: you will father a multitude of nations. [5] No more 'Abram.' Your name is Abraham now — because I am making you the father of nations, plural, many. [6] I will make you wildly fruitful. Nations will come out of you. Kings will descend from your line. [7] And I am locking this covenant in — between me and you and every generation that follows you, an everlasting covenant — to be God to you and to your offspring after you. [8] The land you live in as a stranger — all of Canaan — I am giving it to you and to your descendants as a permanent possession. And I will be their God."

[9] Then God said to Abraham: "Your part is this: keep my covenant — you and your descendants, generation after generation. [10] Here is the covenant you will keep, between me and you and your offspring: every male among you will be circumcised. [11] You will cut the flesh of your foreskin, and that cut will be the sign of the covenant between us. [12] Every male at eight days old, in every generation — whether born in your house or bought with silver from a foreigner, anyone not of your bloodline — [13] every one of them, circumcised. The covenant marked in your flesh. An everlasting covenant. [14] Any uncircumcised male — anyone whose foreskin has not been cut — will be cut off from his people. He has broken my covenant."

[15] Then God said to Abraham: "Your wife Sarai — do not call her Sarai anymore. Her name is Sarah. [16] I will bless her. More than that — I will give you a son by her. I will bless her, and she will become nations. Kings of peoples will come from her."

[17] Abraham fell on his face. And he laughed. He said in his heart, "A son? To a hundred-year-old man? And Sarah — ninety — giving birth?" [18] So Abraham said to God: "If only Ishmael could live before you!"

[19] God said: "No — Sarah your wife will bear you a son. You will name him Isaac. And I will establish my covenant with him, an everlasting covenant for his descendants after him. [20] As for Ishmael — I have heard you. I have blessed him. I will make him fruitful. I will multiply him exceedingly. Twelve princes he will father, and I will make him into a great nation. [21] But my covenant — I am establishing it with Isaac, the one Sarah will bear to you this time next year."

[22] When he finished speaking, God went up from Abraham.

[23] That same day, Abraham took his son Ishmael, every man born in his house, every man bought with his silver — every male in his household — and he circumcised the flesh of their foreskins, exactly as God had told him. [24] Abraham was ninety-nine when his foreskin was cut. [25] His son Ishmael was thirteen when his foreskin was cut. [26] On that one day, both of them — Abraham and his son Ishmael — were circumcised. [27] And every man of his house, born there or bought from foreigners, was circumcised with him.

[1] The LORD appeared to Abraham at the oaks of Mamre while he sat at the entrance of his tent in the heat of the day. [2] He looked up and saw three men standing there. The moment he saw them, he ran from the tent door to meet them and bowed himself to the ground.

[3] "My lord," he said, "if I've found favor with you, please don't pass your servant by. [4] Let me bring a little water — wash your feet, rest under the tree. [5] Let me get you something to eat so you can refresh yourselves before going on. After all, you've come this far to your servant."

"Yes," they said, "do as you've said."

[6] Abraham hurried into the tent to Sarah. "Quick — three measures of fine flour. Knead it. Bake bread." [7] Then he ran to the herd, picked out a young calf — tender, choice — and gave it to a servant who hurried to prepare it. [8] He brought curds and milk and the calf he'd prepared and set it before them. He stood under the tree while they ate.

[9] "Where is Sarah your wife?" they asked.

"There — in the tent," he said.

[10] Then one of them said, "I'll come back to you this time next year, and your wife Sarah will have a son."

Sarah was listening at the tent entrance, just behind him. [11] Abraham and Sarah were old — well along in years — and Sarah was past the age of childbearing. [12] So Sarah laughed to herself: *Worn out as I am, and my husband old — am I going to feel pleasure again?*

[13] The LORD said to Abraham, "Why did Sarah laugh? Why did she say, 'Am I really going to bear a child, old as I am?' [14] Is anything too difficult for the LORD? I'll return to you at the appointed time next spring, and Sarah will have a son."

[15] Sarah was afraid. She lied. "I didn't laugh."

"Yes," he said, "you laughed."

[16] The men got up and looked out toward Sodom, and Abraham walked with them to see them off.

[17] The LORD said, "Should I hide from Abraham what I'm about to do? [18] Abraham will become a great and mighty nation, and through him every nation on earth will be blessed. [19] I've chosen him so that he'll direct his children and his household after him to keep the way of the LORD by doing what is right and just — so that the LORD can bring about for Abraham what he promised him."

[20] So the LORD said, "The outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is enormous. Their sin is grievous beyond measure. [21] I'm going down to see whether what they've done matches the cry that has reached me. If not, I'll know."

[22] The men turned and went toward Sodom, but Abraham remained standing before the LORD.

[23] Abraham stepped closer. "Will you really sweep away the righteous with the wicked? [24] What if there are fifty righteous people inside the city? Will you really sweep the place away and not spare it for the sake of the fifty righteous in it? [25] Far be it from you to do such a thing — to kill the righteous with the wicked, treating them the same. Far be it from you. Will the Judge of all the earth not do what is right?"

[26] The LORD said, "If I find fifty righteous in the city of Sodom, I'll spare the whole place for their sake."

[27] Abraham answered, "I've taken it on myself to speak to the Lord — I, who am dust and ashes. [28] What if the fifty righteous fall short by five? Will you destroy the whole city over five?"

"I won't destroy it if I find forty-five."

[29] He spoke to him again. "What if forty are found there?"

"For the sake of forty, I won't do it."

[30] "Please don't be angry, Lord — let me speak. What if thirty are found there?"

"I won't do it if I find thirty."

[31] "I've taken it on myself to speak to the Lord — what if twenty are found there?"

"For the sake of twenty, I won't destroy it."

[32] "Please don't be angry, Lord — one last time. What if ten are found there?"

"For the sake of ten, I won't destroy it."

[33] When the LORD finished speaking with Abraham, he went his way. And Abraham returned home.

[1] The two messengers arrived at Sodom by evening. Lot was sitting at the city gate. The moment he saw them, he stood up to meet them and bowed low, face to the ground.

[2] "Please, my lords," he said, "turn aside to your servant's house. Spend the night. Wash your feet. You can rise early and continue your journey."

"No," they said, "we'll spend the night in the square."

[3] But he pressed them so hard they finally gave in and went with him to his house. He prepared a feast — fresh bread, no leaven — and they ate.

[4] Before they could lie down, every man in Sodom — young and old, the whole city, from every quarter — surrounded the house.

[5] They shouted to Lot: "Where are the men who came to you tonight? Bring them out. We want to know them."

[6] Lot stepped outside to face them, shutting the door behind him.

[7] "Brothers, please," he said, "don't do this evil.

[8] Look — I have two daughters who have never been with a man. Let me bring them out to you. Do whatever you want with them. Only do nothing to these men — they came under the shelter of my roof."

[9] "Out of the way!" they shouted. "This one came here as an outsider, and now he sets himself up as judge? We'll do worse to you than to them." They pressed hard against Lot and moved in to break down the door.

[10] But the men inside reached out, pulled Lot back into the house, and shut the door.

[11] Then they struck the men at the entrance — every one of them, young and old — with blindness, so that they wore themselves out groping for the door.

[12] The men said to Lot, "Who else belongs to you here? Sons-in-law, sons, daughters — anyone in this city who is yours — get them out of this place.

[13] We're about to destroy it. The outcry against its people has risen to the LORD, and he has sent us to wipe it out."

[14] So Lot went out and spoke to his sons-in-law, the men pledged to marry his daughters. "Get up — get out of this place. The LORD is about to destroy the city." But to them he sounded like a man telling a joke.

[15] At dawn the messengers urged Lot on: "Hurry. Take your wife and your two daughters who are here, or you'll be swept away in the city's punishment."

[16] He hesitated. So the men grabbed his hand, and his wife's hand, and the hands of his two daughters — the LORD's mercy on him — and led them out, setting them safely beyond the city.

[17] Once outside, one of them said, "Run for your life. Don't look back. Don't stop anywhere on the plain. Flee to the hills, or you'll be swept away."

[18] Lot said, "No, please, my lords —

[19] your servant has found favor in your eyes; you've shown me great kindness in saving my life. But I can't make it to the hills. The disaster will overtake me, and I'll die.

[20] Look — that town there is close enough to reach, and it's small. Let me flee there. It's only a little place. Let me live."

[21] He said to him, "Granted. I'll spare that town too. I won't overturn it.

[22] Now hurry — flee there. I can do nothing until you arrive." That's why the town is called Zoar — Little.

[23] The sun was rising over the land as Lot reached Zoar.

[24] Then the LORD rained down on Sodom and Gomorrah — sulfur and fire from the LORD, out of the heavens.

[25] He overturned those cities and the whole plain — every inhabitant, everything that grew from the ground.

[26] But Lot's wife looked back. And she became a pillar of salt.

[27] Early that morning Abraham went to the place where he had stood before the LORD.

[28] He looked out toward Sodom and Gomorrah, toward the whole stretch of the plain — and he saw smoke rising from the land like smoke from a furnace.

[29] So when God destroyed the cities of the plain, he remembered Abraham. And he brought Lot out of the catastrophe that swallowed the cities where Lot had lived.

[30] Lot left Zoar and went up into the hills with his two daughters, because he was afraid to stay in Zoar. He lived in a cave with his two daughters.

[31] The older said to the younger, "Our father is old. There's no man left on earth to come to us the normal way.

[32] Let's get our father drunk on wine and sleep with him. We'll preserve a line through our father."

[33] So that night they got their father drunk on wine, and the older went in and slept with her father. He knew nothing — not when she lay down, not when she got up.

[34] The next day the older said to the younger, "Last night I slept with my father. Let's get him drunk again tonight, and you go in and sleep with him. We'll preserve a line through our father."

[35] So again that night they got their father drunk on wine, and the younger got up and slept with him. He knew nothing — not when she lay down, not when she got up.

[36] Both of Lot's daughters became pregnant by their father.

[37] The older gave birth to a son and named him Moab. He is the father of the Moabites of today.

[38] The younger also gave birth to a son and named him Ben-ammi. He is the father of the Ammonites of today.

[1] Abraham pulled up stakes and moved into the Negev, settling between Kadesh and Shur. While staying in Gerar, [2] he said about his wife Sarah, "She's my sister." So Abimelech, king of Gerar, sent for her and took her.

[3] That night God came to Abimelech in a dream. "You're a dead man," he said. "The woman you took — she's married. She belongs to another man."

[4] Abimelech hadn't touched her. "My Lord," he said, "would you really destroy an innocent man? [5] He told me himself, 'She's my sister.' And she said the same — 'He's my brother.' I did this with a clear conscience and clean hands."

[6] God answered him in the dream: "Yes, I know your conscience was clear. That's why I stopped you. I'm the one who kept you from touching her — kept you from sinning against me. [7] Now return the man's wife. He's a prophet. He'll pray for you, and you'll live. But if you don't return her — know this — you're a dead man, you and everyone who belongs to you."

[8] Abimelech was up before dawn. He gathered his officials and told them everything, and fear swept through the room. [9] Then he summoned Abraham. "What have you done to us? What did I ever do to you that you'd bring this kind of guilt down on me and my kingdom? You've done something to me that should never be done." [10] He pressed him: "What were you thinking when you did this?"

[11] Abraham said, "I thought to myself, there's no fear of God in this place. They'll kill me to get my wife. [12] And besides — she really is my sister. My father's daughter, just not my mother's. She became my wife. [13] When God set me wandering from my father's house, I told her, 'Do this kindness for me — wherever we go, say I'm your brother.'"

[14] Then Abimelech brought out sheep and cattle, male and female servants, and gave them to Abraham. He returned Sarah to him. [15] "My land is open to you," Abimelech said. "Settle wherever you like."

[16] To Sarah he said, "I'm giving your brother a thousand pieces of silver. Let it cover what happened — vindication in the eyes of everyone with you. Your name is cleared."

[17] Abraham prayed, and God healed Abimelech, his wife, and his female servants — they could bear children again. [18] Because the LORD had shut every womb in Abimelech's household tight, on account of Sarah, Abraham's wife.

[1] The LORD kept his word to Sarah. He did exactly what he had promised. [2] Sarah conceived. She bore Abraham a son in his old age — right on schedule, the timing God himself had set. [3] Abraham named the boy Isaac — the son Sarah gave him. [4] When Isaac was eight days old, Abraham circumcised him, just as God had commanded. [5] Abraham was a hundred years old the day Isaac was born.

[6] Sarah said, "God has handed me laughter. Everyone who hears about this will laugh with me." [7] And she said, "Who would ever have whispered to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children? Yet here I am — I've borne him a son in his old age."

[8] The boy grew and was weaned, and on the day Isaac was weaned, Abraham threw a great feast. [9] But Sarah saw the son Hagar the Egyptian had borne to Abraham — saw him mocking — [10] and she said to Abraham, "Drive this slave woman out. Her son too. The son of a slave will not share the inheritance with my son. Not with Isaac."

[11] The words cut Abraham deep. This was his son too. [12] But God said to him, "Don't take it so hard — not about the boy, not about the slave woman. Listen to Sarah. Do what she says. Isaac is the one through whom your line will be named. [13] But the slave woman's son — I'll make a nation of him too. He's your seed."

[14] Abraham was up before dawn. He took bread and a skin of water and gave them to Hagar, set them on her shoulder, handed her the boy, and sent her away. She wandered off into the wilderness of Beersheba and lost her way.

[15] When the water was gone, she pushed the boy under one of the bushes [16] and walked off — about a bowshot away — and sat down. "I can't watch him die," she said. And sitting there across from him, she lifted her voice and wept.

[17] God heard the boy crying. The angel of God called down to Hagar out of heaven: "Hagar — what is it? Don't be afraid. God has heard the boy's voice from right where he lies. [18] Get up. Lift him up. Hold him tight. I am going to make a great nation of him."

[19] Then God opened her eyes. She saw a well. She went, filled the skin with water, and gave the boy a drink. [20] God was with the boy as he grew. He lived in the wilderness and became an archer. [21] He settled in the wilderness of Paran, and his mother got him a wife from Egypt.

[22] About that time, Abimelech came to Abraham with Phicol, the commander of his army. "God is with you in everything you do," he said. [23] "Swear to me right here, by God — you won't deal falsely with me, or with my children, or with my descendants. The kindness I've shown you, show me back — and the land where you've lived as a stranger."

[24] "I swear it," Abraham said.

[25] Then Abraham brought up a complaint about a well of water that Abimelech's servants had seized. [26] "I don't know who did this," Abimelech said. "You never told me. I'm hearing about it now for the first time."

[27] So Abraham took sheep and cattle and gave them to Abimelech, and the two of them cut a covenant. [28] Then Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs from the flock. [29] "What are these seven ewe lambs you've set apart?" Abimelech asked. [30] "Take these seven lambs from my hand," Abraham said. "Let them stand as evidence for me — that I dug this well." [31] That's why the place is called Beersheba — the Well of the Oath — because there the two of them swore an oath.

[32] After they sealed the covenant at Beersheba, Abimelech and Phicol, his army commander, headed back to the land of the Philistines. [33] Abraham planted a tamarisk tree at Beersheba, and there he called on the name of the LORD, the Everlasting God. [34] And Abraham stayed in the land of the Philistines a long, long time.

[1] After all this, God tested Abraham. He called out: "Abraham." And Abraham answered, "I'm here."

[2] God said, "Take your son. Your only son. The one you love — Isaac. Go to the land of Moriah. Offer him there as a burnt offering, on a mountain I'll show you."

[3] Abraham got up before dawn. He saddled his donkey, took two of his servants and Isaac his son, split the wood for the offering, and set out for the place God had named.

[4] On the third day, Abraham looked up and saw the place in the distance.

[5] He said to his servants, "Stay here with the donkey. The boy and I will go up there. We'll worship. Then we'll come back to you."

[6] Abraham took the wood for the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son. In his own hand he carried the fire and the knife. The two of them walked on together.

[7] Isaac broke the silence. "Father." Abraham answered, "Yes, my son." Isaac said, "The fire and the wood are here — but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?"

[8] Abraham said, "God himself will see to the lamb, my son." And the two of them walked on together.

[9] They came to the place God had named. Abraham built the altar there. He arranged the wood. He bound Isaac his son and laid him on the altar, on top of the wood.

[10] Abraham reached out his hand and took the knife to kill his son.

[11] Then the angel of the LORD called to him from heaven: "Abraham! Abraham!" He answered, "I'm here."

[12] The angel said, "Don't lay a hand on the boy. Don't do anything to him. Now I know that you fear God — you didn't withhold your son, your only son, from me."

[13] Abraham looked up. There, caught in a thicket by its horns, was a ram. He went and took the ram and offered it as a burnt offering in place of his son.

[14] Abraham named that place *The LORD Will See To It*. And so it's said to this day: "On the mountain of the LORD, it will be provided."

[15] The angel of the LORD called to Abraham from heaven a second time.

[16] "I swear by myself — this is the LORD speaking — because you have done this, because you did not withhold your son, your only son,

[17] I will bless you. I will multiply your descendants like the stars of the sky, like the sand on the seashore. Your offspring will seize the gates of their enemies.

[18] And through your offspring all the nations of the earth will be blessed — because you obeyed my voice."

[19] Abraham returned to his servants. Together they got up and went back to Beersheba. And Abraham settled in Beersheba.

[20] Sometime later, word came to Abraham: "Milcah has borne sons to your brother Nahor —

[21] Uz the firstborn, Buz his brother, Kemuel the father of Aram,

[22] Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel."

[23] Bethuel fathered Rebekah. These eight Milcah bore to Nahor, Abraham's brother.

[24] His concubine, Reumah, also bore children: Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.

[1] Sarah lived a hundred and twenty-seven years — the full measure of her life.

[2] She died at Kiriath-arba, the place now called Hebron, in the land of Canaan. Abraham came to mourn her. He wept.

[3] Then Abraham rose from beside his dead and went to speak with the Hittites.

[4] "I am a stranger among you, a sojourner with no land of my own. Give me a burial site here, so I can bury my dead and not have to look at her any longer."

[5] The Hittites answered him,

[6] "Listen to us, sir. You walk among us as a prince of God. Take the finest of our tombs. Not one of us will refuse you a burial place for your dead."

[7] Abraham stood and bowed low to the people of the land, the Hittites.

[8] He said to them, "If you are truly willing to let me bury my dead and have her out of my sight, then hear me out. Speak to Ephron son of Zohar on my behalf.

[9] Ask him to sell me the cave of Machpelah — the one at the edge of his field. Full price, in your presence. I want it as a burial site I own outright."

[10] Ephron the Hittite was sitting there among them. He answered Abraham in the hearing of every Hittite who came through the city gate:

[11] "No, my lord — listen to me. I give you the field. I give you the cave that is in it. In front of my people, I give it to you. Bury your dead."

[12] Again Abraham bowed before the people of the land.

[13] And he said to Ephron, with everyone listening, "Please — hear me. I will pay for the field. Take the silver from me, and let me bury my dead there."

[14] Ephron answered Abraham,

[15] "My lord, listen — a piece of land worth four hundred shekels of silver, what is that between you and me? Bury your dead."

[16] Abraham heard him. He weighed out for Ephron the silver Ephron had named in front of all the Hittites — four hundred shekels of silver, at the going merchant's rate.

[17] So the field of Ephron at Machpelah, east of Mamre — the field, the cave inside it, and every tree within its borders —

[18] passed to Abraham as his own property, witnessed by every Hittite who came through the city gate.

[19] Then Abraham buried Sarah, his wife, in the cave of the field of Machpelah, east of Mamre — that is, Hebron — in the land of Canaan.

[20] The field and the cave passed from the Hittites to Abraham, a burial site he held in his own name.

[1] Abraham was old now, deep into his years, and the LORD had blessed him in every way. [2] He said to his chief servant, the one who ran the whole estate, "Put your hand under my thigh. [3] I want you to swear by the LORD, the God of heaven and earth, that you will not find a wife for my son from among these Canaanite women I live beside. [4] You will go back — back to my homeland, to my own people — and find a wife for my son Isaac there."

[5] The servant said, "But what if the woman won't come with me? What if she refuses to leave her country? Should I take your son back to the land you came from?"

[6] "No," Abraham said. "Never take my son back there. [7] The LORD, the God of heaven, who pulled me out of my father's house and away from the land of my birth, who spoke to me and swore an oath — 'To your offspring I will give this land' — He will send His messenger ahead of you. You will find a wife for my son there. [8] And if the woman refuses to follow you, you're released from the oath. Just don't take my son back to that place."

[9] So the servant placed his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master and swore to do exactly what he'd said.

[10] He took ten of his master's camels and loaded them with the best of everything Abraham owned, and he set out — heading north to Aram-naharaim, to the city of Nahor. [11] When he arrived, he made the camels kneel outside the city by the well. It was evening — the hour when the women came out to draw water.

[12] Then he prayed: "LORD, God of my master Abraham — let it happen today. Show kindness to my master Abraham. [13] Look — I'm standing here at the spring, and the daughters of the townspeople are coming out to draw water. [14] Let this be the sign: when I ask a young woman, 'Please tip your jar so I can drink,' and she says, 'Drink — and I'll water your camels too' — let her be the one You've chosen for Your servant Isaac. That's how I'll know You've shown kindness to my master."

[15] He hadn't even finished praying when Rebekah came out, her jar on her shoulder. She was the daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, who was the wife of Abraham's brother Nahor. [16] The girl was stunning — a virgin, untouched. She went down to the spring, filled her jar, and came back up.

[17] The servant ran to meet her. "Please — let me drink a little water from your jar."

[18] "Drink, sir," she said, and quickly lowered the jar into her hands and gave him a drink. [19] When she'd finished giving him water, she said, "I'll draw for your camels too — until they've had their fill." [20] She emptied her jar into the trough, ran back to the well, drew again — and again, and again, until every camel had drunk.

[21] The man watched her in silence, studying her, wondering whether the LORD had made his journey succeed.

[22] When the camels finished drinking, he took out a gold nose-ring — half a shekel — and two gold bracelets weighing ten shekels, [23] and said, "Whose daughter are you? Tell me — is there room at your father's house for us to spend the night?"

[24] "I'm the daughter of Bethuel," she said, "the son Milcah bore to Nahor. [25] And yes — we have plenty of straw and feed, and room to stay the night."

[26] The man dropped to his knees and worshiped the LORD. [27] "Blessed be the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who has not abandoned His loyal love and faithfulness to my master. The LORD led me right to the house of my master's kin."

[28] The girl ran home and told her mother's household everything.

[29] Rebekah had a brother named Laban. Laban ran out to the man at the spring. [30] He had seen the nose-ring and the bracelets on his sister's arms, and heard her say, "This is what the man told me." So he came out — and there was the man, still standing with the camels by the spring.

[31] "Come in," Laban said, "blessed of the LORD. Why are you standing out here? I've prepared the house — and a place for the camels."

[32] So the man went in. They unloaded the camels, gave them straw and feed, and brought water for him and his men to wash their feet. [33] Food was set before him, but he said, "I can't eat until I've said what I came to say."

"Speak," Laban said.

[34] "I am Abraham's servant," he began. [35] "The LORD has blessed my master abundantly — he has become a great man. He's given him flocks and herds, silver and gold, male and female servants, camels and donkeys. [36] And Sarah, my master's wife, bore him a son in her old age — and to that son he has given everything he owns.

[37] "My master made me swear an oath: 'Do not get a wife for my son from the Canaanite women whose land I live in. [38] Go to my father's house, to my clan, and find a wife for my son there.'

[39] "I said to my master, 'What if the woman won't come back with me?'

[40] "He said, 'The LORD, before whom I have walked, will send His messenger with you and make your journey succeed. You will find a wife for my son from my clan, from my father's house. [41] Only then will you be released from my oath — when you've gone to my clan. And if they refuse to give her to you, you're free from the oath.'

[42] "So today I came to the spring and prayed: 'LORD, God of my master Abraham — if You would now make the journey I've come on succeed — [43] here I am, standing at the spring. Let it be that the young woman who comes out to draw water, the one I ask, "Please give me a little water from your jar," [44] and she answers, "Drink — and I'll draw for your camels too" — let her be the wife the LORD has chosen for my master's son.'

[45] "Before I'd even finished praying in my heart, Rebekah came out, her jar on her shoulder. She went down to the spring and drew water, and I said to her, 'Please — let me drink.' [46] She lowered her jar at once and said, 'Drink — and I'll water your camels too.' So I drank, and she watered the camels.

[47] "I asked her, 'Whose daughter are you?' She said, 'The daughter of Bethuel, son of Nahor and Milcah.' So I put the ring in her nose and the bracelets on her arms, [48] and I bowed down and worshiped the LORD. I blessed the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who had led me straight to the daughter of my master's kin for his son.

[49] "So now — if you intend to show loyalty and faithfulness to my master, tell me. And if not, tell me that too, so I can turn one way or the other."

[50] Laban and Bethuel answered, "This is from the LORD. We can't say anything to you, good or bad. [51] Here is Rebekah. Take her and go. Let her be the wife of your master's son, just as the LORD has spoken."

[52] When Abraham's servant heard this, he fell to the ground before the LORD. [53] Then he brought out silver and gold jewelry and clothing and gave them to Rebekah, and gave precious gifts to her brother and her mother.

[54] He and the men with him ate and drank and stayed the night. In the morning he said, "Send me back to my master."

[55] But her brother and her mother said, "Let the girl stay with us a few more days — say, ten. Then she can go."

[56] He said, "Don't hold me back. The LORD has made my journey succeed. Send me on so I can return to my master."

[57] They said, "Let's call the girl and ask her." [58] So they called Rebekah and said, "Will you go with this man?"

"I will," she said.

[59] So they sent off Rebekah their sister with her nurse, along with Abraham's servant and his men. [60] And they blessed Rebekah:

"Our sister — may you become
the mother of thousands upon thousands.
May your offspring seize the gates
of those who hate them."

[61] Then Rebekah and her young women got up, mounted the camels, and followed the man. The servant took Rebekah and went.

[62] Isaac had come back from the region of Beer-lahai-roi — he was living in the Negev. [63] One evening he went out into the field to meditate, and he looked up — and there were camels coming.

[64] Rebekah looked up too, and when she saw Isaac, she slipped down from her camel. [65] "Who is that man walking out in the field to meet us?" she asked the servant.

"That is my master," he said.

So she took her veil and covered herself.

[66] The servant told Isaac everything he'd done. [67] And Isaac brought her into the tent of his mother Sarah. He took Rebekah, and she became his wife, and he loved her. And Isaac was comforted, at last, after his mother's death.

[1] Abraham took another wife. Her name was Keturah. [2] She bore him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah. [3] Jokshan fathered Sheba and Dedan. Dedan's descendants were the Asshurim, the Letushim, and the Leummim. [4] Midian's sons were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All of these came from Keturah's line.

[5] Abraham handed everything he owned to Isaac. [6] To the sons of his concubines he gave gifts and sent them east, away from Isaac, while he was still alive — out to the land of the east.

[7] Abraham lived one hundred and seventy-five years. [8] Then he breathed his last and died — old, full of days, satisfied — and was gathered to his people. [9] His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite, just east of Mamre — [10] the field Abraham had bought from the Hittites. There he was buried beside Sarah his wife. [11] After Abraham's death, God blessed Isaac his son. Isaac settled near Beer-lahai-roi.

[12] This is the line of Ishmael, Abraham's son — the one Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's servant, bore to Abraham. [13] These are the names of Ishmael's sons, listed by birth order: Nebaioth the firstborn, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, [14] Mishma, Dumah, Massa, [15] Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah. [16] Twelve princes, each ruling his own tribe, each with his own settlement and stronghold. [17] Ishmael lived one hundred and thirty-seven years before he breathed his last and was gathered to his people. [18] His descendants settled across the territory from Havilah to Shur, near Egypt, stretching toward Asshur. They lived in defiance of all their kin.

[19] This is the story of Isaac, Abraham's son. Abraham fathered Isaac. [20] Isaac was forty when he married Rebekah, daughter of Bethuel the Aramean from Paddan-aram, sister of Laban the Aramean.

[21] Isaac pleaded with the LORD for his wife, because she could not conceive. The LORD answered him, and Rebekah became pregnant. [22] But the children inside her wrestled, and she said, "If this is how it is, why am I even alive?" So she went to inquire of the LORD.

[23] And the LORD told her:

Two nations are in your womb.
Two peoples, already at war within you.
One will be stronger than the other.
The older will serve the younger.

[24] When her time came, twins were there in her womb. [25] The first came out red, his whole body like a hairy cloak. They named him Esau. [26] Then his brother came, gripping Esau's heel. They named him Jacob. Isaac was sixty when they were born.

[27] The boys grew up. Esau became a skilled hunter, a man of the open country. Jacob was a quiet man, at home among the tents. [28] Isaac favored Esau — he had a taste for wild game. Rebekah favored Jacob.

[29] One day Jacob was cooking a stew when Esau came in from the field, exhausted. [30] Esau said, "Let me have a swallow of that red stuff — the red stuff. I'm wiped out." (That's why he was called Edom.)

[31] Jacob said, "Sell me your birthright. Right now."

[32] Esau said, "Look at me — I'm about to die. What good is a birthright to me?"

[33] Jacob said, "Swear it." So Esau swore, and sold his birthright to Jacob.

[34] Then Jacob gave him bread and the lentil stew. Esau ate, drank, got up, and walked off. That's what Esau thought of his birthright.

[1] Famine struck the land — not the old famine from Abraham's day, but a new one. So Isaac packed up and went to Abimelech, king of the Philistines, at Gerar.

[2] The LORD appeared to him and said: "Do not go down to Egypt. Stay in the land I show you.

[3] Live here as a stranger, and I will be with you and bless you. To you and your descendants I will give all these lands, and I will keep the oath I swore to your father Abraham.

[4] I will multiply your descendants like the stars of the sky and hand them all these lands, and through your offspring every nation on earth will find blessing —

[5] because Abraham listened to my voice, kept my charge, my commands, my statutes, my instructions."

[6] So Isaac settled in Gerar.

[7] When the men of the place asked about his wife, he said, "She's my sister." He was afraid to say "wife" — he thought, *They'll kill me to get Rebekah. She's beautiful.*

[8] But Isaac had been there a long time. One day Abimelech, king of the Philistines, looked out a window and saw Isaac with Rebekah — caressing her, the kind of touch a husband gives a wife.

[9] Abimelech summoned him. "She is clearly your wife! Why did you say, 'She's my sister'?" Isaac answered, "Because I thought I'd die over her."

[10] Abimelech said, "What have you done to us? One of our men could easily have slept with her, and you would have brought guilt down on all of us."

[11] Then Abimelech issued a decree to the whole nation: "Anyone who touches this man or his wife will be put to death."

[12] Isaac planted crops in that land, and that very year he reaped a hundredfold. The LORD blessed him.

[13] He grew rich. And richer. And richer still, until he was a very wealthy man.

[14] He had flocks, herds, a large household — and the Philistines envied him.

[15] Every well his father's servants had dug back in Abraham's day, the Philistines stopped up, filling them with dirt.

[16] Finally Abimelech said to Isaac, "Leave us. You've become far too powerful for us."

[17] So Isaac moved on. He camped in the Valley of Gerar and settled there.

[18] He reopened the wells his father Abraham had dug — the ones the Philistines had plugged up after Abraham died — and he gave them back the names his father had given them.

[19] Then Isaac's servants dug in the valley and found a spring of fresh water.

[20] But the herdsmen of Gerar argued with Isaac's herdsmen, claiming, "The water is ours." So he named the well Esek — *Quarrel* — because they fought him over it.

[21] They dug another well. They fought over that one too. He named it Sitnah — *Hostility*.

[22] He moved on and dug another. No fight this time. So he called it Rehoboth — *Wide Open Spaces* — and said, "Now the LORD has made room for us. We will be fruitful in this land."

[23] From there he went up to Beersheba.

[24] That night the LORD appeared to him and said, "I am the God of your father Abraham. Do not be afraid — I am with you. I will bless you and multiply your descendants for the sake of my servant Abraham."

[25] Isaac built an altar there and called on the name of the LORD. He pitched his tent, and his servants dug another well.

[26] Then Abimelech came to him from Gerar, bringing Ahuzzath, his advisor, and Phicol, the commander of his army.

[27] Isaac said, "Why come to me now? You hated me. You drove me away."

[28] They answered, "We've seen plainly that the LORD is with you. So we said — let there be a binding oath between us. Let's make a covenant with you:

[29] you will not harm us, just as we did not lay a hand on you but sent you away in peace. You are now blessed by the LORD."

[30] So Isaac threw them a feast, and they ate and drank.

[31] They rose early in the morning and swore oaths to each other. Then Isaac sent them off, and they left him in peace.

[32] That same day Isaac's servants came and told him about the well they'd been digging. "We've found water," they said.

[33] He named it Shibah. And the city is called Beersheba to this day.

[34] When Esau was forty, he married Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite.

[35] They made life bitter for Isaac and Rebekah.

[1] Isaac had grown old. His eyes had gone dim — he could no longer see. He called for Esau, his older son. "My son," he said. "Here I am," Esau answered.

[2] "Look at me," Isaac said. "I'm old. I don't know the day of my death.

[3] So take your gear — your quiver and your bow — head out into the field and hunt me some game.

[4] Make me the kind of meal I love. Bring it to me. I'll eat. And before I die, my soul will bless you."

[5] Rebekah was listening as Isaac spoke to Esau. When Esau left for the field to hunt game to bring home,

[6] Rebekah turned to her son Jacob: "Listen — I just heard your father telling Esau,

[7] 'Bring me game. Make me a meal. I'll eat it, then bless you in the presence of the LORD before I die.'

[8] So now, my son — listen to me. Do exactly what I tell you.

[9] Go to the flock. Bring me two good young goats. I'll make them into the kind of meal your father loves.

[10] Then you'll take it to your father. He'll eat. And he'll bless you before he dies."

[11] Jacob pushed back. "Mother — Esau is a hairy man. I'm smooth-skinned.

[12] What if my father touches me? I'll look like a con man to him. I'll bring down a curse on myself, not a blessing."

[13] "Let the curse fall on me, my son," she said. "Just listen. Go. Get them."

[14] So he went. He got them. He brought them to his mother, and she made the meal his father loved.

[15] Then Rebekah took Esau's best clothes — the ones she kept in the house — and put them on Jacob, her younger son.

[16] She wrapped the skins of the young goats around his hands and the smooth part of his neck.

[17] Then she put the meal and the bread she had made into the hands of her son Jacob.

[18] He came to his father. "Father," he said. "Here I am," Isaac answered. "Which son are you?"

[19] Jacob said, "I'm Esau, your firstborn. I've done what you asked. Sit up. Eat some of my game. Then bless me."

[20] Isaac said, "How did you find it so fast, my son?" "The LORD your God brought it to me," he said.

[21] Then Isaac said to Jacob, "Come closer. Let me feel you, my son. I need to know if you're really my son Esau."

[22] Jacob came close. Isaac felt him. "The voice is Jacob's voice," he said. "But the hands — the hands are Esau's."

[23] He didn't recognize him — the hands were hairy like his brother Esau's. So he blessed him.

[24] "You really are my son Esau?" he asked. "I am," Jacob said.

[25] "Then bring it here. Let me eat my son's game and bless you with all my soul." Jacob brought it. He ate. He brought him wine. He drank.

[26] Then his father Isaac said, "Come here, my son. Kiss me."

[27] He came close and kissed him. Isaac smelled the scent of his clothes and blessed him: "Ah — the smell of my son is like the smell of a field the LORD has blessed.

[28] May God give you the dew of heaven and the richness of the earth — grain in abundance, wine flowing free.

[29] Let peoples serve you. Let nations bow to you. Be lord over your brothers. Let your mother's sons bow before you. Cursed are those who curse you. Blessed are those who bless you."

[30] Just as Isaac finished blessing Jacob — the moment Jacob stepped out from his father's presence — Esau came in from the hunt.

[31] He too prepared a meal and brought it to his father. "Sit up, Father," he said. "Eat your son's game. Then bless me."

[32] Isaac said, "Who are you?" "I'm your son," he said. "Your firstborn. Esau."

[33] Isaac began to tremble — violently, uncontrollably. "Then who — who was it that hunted game and brought it to me? I ate it all before you came in. I blessed him. And blessed he will remain."

[34] When Esau heard his father's words, he let out a cry — bitter, raw, broken. "Bless me too, Father! Me too!"

[35] But Isaac said, "Your brother came in deception. He took your blessing."

[36] Esau said, "Isn't he rightly named Jacob — the heel-grabber? He's tripped me up twice now. He took my birthright. Now he's taken my blessing." Then he said, "Haven't you saved a blessing for me?"

[37] Isaac answered, "Look — I've made him lord over you. I've given him all his brothers as servants. I've sustained him with grain and wine. What's left for me to do for you, my son?"

[38] Esau said to his father, "Only one blessing, Father? Bless me too — me too, my father!" And Esau lifted his voice and wept.

[39] Then Isaac his father answered him: "Far from the richness of the earth will your home be — far from the dew of heaven above.

[40] You'll live by the sword. You'll serve your brother. But when you grow restless, you'll tear his yoke from your neck."

[41] Esau nursed a hatred for Jacob because of the blessing his father gave him. He said to himself, "The days of mourning my father are coming soon. Then I'll kill my brother Jacob."

[42] Word of Esau's plan reached Rebekah. She sent for Jacob, her younger son. "Listen — your brother Esau is comforting himself with thoughts of killing you.

[43] So do what I say, my son. Run. Get to my brother Laban in Haran.

[44] Stay with him a while — until your brother's fury cools.

[45] Until his anger turns from you and he forgets what you did. Then I'll send for you. Why should I lose both of you in one day?"

[46] Then Rebekah said to Isaac, "I'm sick of life because of these Hittite women. If Jacob takes a wife from the Hittite women — from women of this land — what's the point of my living?"

[1] Isaac called Jacob in, blessed him, and laid down the charge: "Do not marry a Canaanite woman. [2] Get up. Go to Paddan-aram, to the house of Bethuel, your mother's father. Take a wife from there — one of Laban's daughters, your mother's brother. [3] May God Almighty bless you, make you fruitful, multiply you, until you become a whole assembly of peoples. [4] May he hand you the blessing of Abraham — to you and to your descendants with you — so you inherit the land where you now live as a stranger, the land God gave to Abraham."

[5] So Isaac sent Jacob off, and he went to Paddan-aram — to Laban son of Bethuel the Aramean, brother of Rebekah, mother of Jacob and Esau.

[6] Esau saw it all: Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him to Paddan-aram to find a wife there, and in blessing him had ordered him, "Do not marry a Canaanite woman." [7] He saw that Jacob had obeyed his father and mother and gone to Paddan-aram. [8] Esau understood, then, that Canaanite women displeased his father Isaac. [9] So he went to Ishmael and married Mahalath — daughter of Ishmael, Abraham's son, sister of Nebaioth — adding her to the wives he already had.

[10] Jacob left Beersheba and set out for Haran. [11] He came to a certain place and stopped for the night because the sun had set. He took one of the stones lying there, propped it under his head, and lay down to sleep in that place.

[12] And he dreamed. A stairway, planted on the earth, its top piercing the sky — and angels of God climbing up and coming down on it. [13] And there, standing over him, was the Lord, who said: "I am the Lord, the God of Abraham your father, the God of Isaac. The ground you are lying on — I am giving it to you and to your descendants. [14] Your offspring will be like the dust of the earth — spreading west and east, north and south. Through you and your offspring, every family on earth will find blessing. [15] Listen — I am with you. I will guard you wherever you go, and I will bring you back to this land. I will not leave you until I have done everything I have promised you."

[16] Jacob woke from his sleep and said, "The Lord is in this place — and I had no idea." [17] A holy dread came over him. "How terrifying this place is," he said. "This is nothing less than the house of God. This is the gate of heaven."

[18] Early the next morning Jacob took the stone he had used as a pillow, set it up as a pillar, and poured oil on its top. [19] He named the place Bethel — House of God. (The town had been called Luz before.)

[20] Then Jacob made a vow: "If God will be with me, if he will guard me on this road I am walking, if he will give me bread to eat and clothes to wear, [21] and if I return safely to my father's house — then the Lord will be my God. [22] And this stone I have set up as a pillar will be God's house. And of everything you give me, I will give back a tenth to you."

[1] Jacob picked up his pace and made it to the land of the eastern peoples. [2] There it was — a well in the open country, three flocks of sheep bedded down beside it, since this was where the herds drank. A massive stone covered the well's mouth. [3] When all the flocks gathered, the shepherds would roll the stone off the well, water the sheep, then heave the stone back into place over the mouth of the well.

[4] Jacob said to them, "Brothers, where are you from?"

"Haran," they answered.

[5] He said, "Do you know Laban, Nahor's grandson?"

"We know him," they said.

[6] "Is he well?" Jacob asked.

"He's well," they said. "Look — his daughter Rachel is coming right now with the sheep."

[7] Jacob said, "The sun's still high. It's not time to gather the livestock. Water the sheep and get them back to grazing."

[8] "We can't," they said, "not until all the flocks are gathered and the stone is rolled off the well. Then we water them."

[9] He was still talking when Rachel arrived with her father's sheep — she was the shepherd. [10] The moment Jacob saw Rachel, daughter of his mother's brother Laban, with Laban's sheep, he went straight to the well, rolled the stone off the mouth single-handed, and watered his uncle's flock. [11] Then he kissed Rachel and broke into tears. [12] He told her he was her father's kin, Rebekah's son. She ran to tell her father.

[13] When Laban heard the news about his sister's son, he ran out to meet him, threw his arms around him, kissed him, and brought him home. Jacob told Laban everything. [14] And Laban said, "You really are my bone and flesh."

Jacob stayed with him a month. [15] Then Laban said, "Just because you're family doesn't mean you should work for nothing. Tell me what you want for wages."

[16] Now Laban had two daughters. The older was Leah, the younger Rachel. [17] Leah had soft eyes. Rachel was stunning — beautiful in form, beautiful in face. [18] Jacob loved Rachel. So he said, "I'll work seven years for you in exchange for Rachel, your younger daughter."

[19] Laban said, "Better I give her to you than to some other man. Stay here with me."

[20] So Jacob worked seven years for Rachel, and they felt like a few days to him — that's how much he loved her.

[21] Then Jacob said to Laban, "Give me my wife. My time is up. I want to be with her."

[22] Laban gathered everyone in the area and threw a feast. [23] But that evening, he took his daughter Leah and brought her to Jacob, and Jacob slept with her. [24] (Laban gave his servant Zilpah to Leah as her maid.) [25] Morning came — and there was Leah.

Jacob exploded at Laban. "What have you done to me? I worked for you for Rachel! Why did you cheat me?"

[26] Laban said, "It's not done in our country — giving the younger before the firstborn. [27] Finish the bridal week with this one, and we'll give you the other one too, in exchange for another seven years of work."

[28] Jacob agreed. He finished Leah's week, and Laban gave him Rachel as his wife. [29] (Laban gave his servant Bilhah to Rachel as her maid.) [30] Jacob slept with Rachel too, and he loved Rachel more than Leah. And he worked for Laban another seven years.

[31] The LORD saw that Leah was unloved, so he opened her womb. Rachel stayed barren. [32] Leah conceived and bore a son. She named him Reuben — "See, a son!" — because she said, "The LORD has seen my misery. Now my husband will love me."

[33] She conceived again, bore another son, and said, "The LORD heard that I'm unloved, so he gave me this one too." She named him Simeon — "Heard."

[34] She conceived again, bore a third son, and said, "Now, finally, my husband will attach himself to me — I've given him three sons." So he was named Levi — "Attached."

[35] She conceived again, bore a fourth son, and said, "This time I will praise the LORD." She named him Judah — "Praise." Then she stopped bearing.

[1] When Rachel saw that she was giving Jacob no children, she burned with envy toward her sister. "Give me sons," she demanded of Jacob, "or I'll die."

[2] Jacob's anger flared. "Am I God? He's the one who's kept your womb closed."

[3] "Here's my servant Bilhah," she said. "Sleep with her. She'll bear on my knees — through her, I'll build a family."

[4] So she handed him Bilhah as a wife, and Jacob slept with her.

[5] Bilhah conceived and gave Jacob a son.

[6] Rachel said, "God has judged in my favor — he heard me and gave me a son." So she named him Dan.

[7] Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son.

[8] Rachel said, "I've wrestled my sister with the wrestlings of God — and won." She named him Naphtali.

[9] Leah saw she had stopped bearing, so she took her servant Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a wife.

[10] Zilpah bore Jacob a son.
