[10] When they reached the threshing floor of Atad, just beyond the Jordan, they held a funeral — heavy, immense. Joseph mourned his father seven days there. [11] The Canaanites living in the land watched the mourning at Atad and said, "Egypt is grieving hard today." That's why the place is called Abel-mizraim — "Egypt's Mourning" — across the Jordan.

[12] So Jacob's sons did exactly as he had charged them: [13] they carried him into Canaan and buried him in the cave at Machpelah, the field east of Mamre that Abraham had bought from Ephron the Hittite as a burial holding. [14] Then Joseph returned to Egypt — he, his brothers, everyone who had gone with him to bury his father.

[15] With their father gone, Joseph's brothers panicked. "What if Joseph holds the grudge after all? What if he pays us back in full for everything we did to him?" [16] So they sent word to Joseph: "Before he died, your father left instructions. [17] He said this: 'Tell Joseph — please — forgive your brothers' offense, their sin, the evil they did to you.' So now: please forgive the offense of the servants of your father's God."

Joseph wept when the message reached him.

[18] His brothers themselves came and threw themselves down before him. "We are your slaves," they said.

[19] But Joseph answered, "Don't be afraid. Am I in the place of God? [20] You meant evil against me — God meant it for good. He aimed it at this very day, at keeping a great many people alive. [21] So don't be afraid. I'll provide for you and your children." And he comforted them, speaking straight to their hearts.

[22] Joseph stayed in Egypt — he and his father's house. He lived to be a hundred and ten. [23] He saw Ephraim's children to the third generation. The sons of Machir, Manasseh's son, were born on Joseph's knees.

[24] Joseph said to his brothers, "I'm about to die. But God will come for you. He will come, and he will bring you up out of this land to the land he swore to Abraham, to Isaac, to Jacob." [25] And Joseph made the sons of Israel swear an oath: "When God comes for you — carry my bones up from here."

[26] Joseph died, a hundred and ten years old. They embalmed him and laid him in a coffin in Egypt.

[1] The oracle Habakkuk the prophet saw.

[2] How long, Lord? How long do I scream for help while you sit silent? How long do I cry out, *Violence!* and you refuse to save?

[3] Why do you force me to stare at this wreckage? Why do you make me watch this misery? Destruction and brutality are in my face. There is conflict. Strife rises up everywhere.

[4] So the law goes limp. Justice never breaks the surface. The wicked have the righteous surrounded — and what comes out the other side is justice twisted into its own opposite.

[5] *Look around. Look at the nations. Stare until your jaw drops. Because I am about to do something in your lifetime that you would not believe if someone told you straight to your face.*

[6] *Watch — I am raising up the Chaldeans. That bitter, headlong nation. They march across the wide earth to seize houses that are not theirs.*

[7] *They are terror itself. They are dread on the move. Their justice and their dignity come from one source only: themselves.*

[8] *Their horses are faster than leopards, fiercer than wolves at dusk. Their cavalry comes galloping — riders sweeping in from far away, swooping down like a vulture diving for the kill.*

[9] *They come, all of them, hungry for violence. Their faces lean forward like the desert wind. They scoop up captives like sand.*

[10] *They mock kings. Rulers are a joke to them. They laugh at every fortress — build a ramp of dirt and take it.*

[11] *Then they sweep on like the wind and move along — guilty men whose god is their own strength.*

[12] Are you not from everlasting, Lord — my God, my Holy One? We will not die. Lord, you appointed them for judgment. Rock of mine, you set them up to discipline.

[13] Your eyes are too pure to look at evil. You cannot stomach the sight of wrong. So why do you stare at traitors in silence? Why do you say nothing while the wicked swallows the man more righteous than himself?

[14] You have reduced humanity to fish in the sea — to swarming things with no ruler.

[15] He hauls them all up with a hook, drags them off in his net, gathers them in his dragnet — and then he celebrates. He throws a party.

[16] So he sacrifices to his net. He burns incense to his dragnet. Because thanks to them his portion is fat and his food is rich.

[17] Will he just keep emptying his net forever? Slaughtering nations without mercy, without end?

[1] I'll take my post on the watchtower. I'll plant myself on the ramparts and keep watch — to see what he'll say to me, what answer he'll give to my complaint.

[2] Then the LORD answered:

Write the vision down. Carve it into the tablets. Make it legible — so a runner can read it on the fly.

[3] The vision is for an appointed time. It races toward the finish line. It will not lie. If it seems slow, wait for it. It is coming. It will not be late.

[4] Look — the arrogant soul is twisted, out of plumb. But the righteous will live by his faith.

[5] Wealth is treacherous. The proud man never rests. His appetite is wide as the grave, hungry as death itself — never satisfied. He gathers nations to himself, hoards peoples like trophies.

[6] But they will all turn on him with a taunting song, a riddle of mockery:

Disaster to the man who piles up what isn't his — how long can he keep loading himself with stolen pledges?

[7] Won't your creditors suddenly rise? Won't they wake up and shake you down? You'll become their plunder.

[8] Because you stripped nation after nation, what's left of the peoples will strip you — for the blood you spilled, for the violence done to lands and cities and everyone who lived in them.

[9] Disaster to the man who builds his house on dirty profit — who sets his nest sky-high to dodge the reach of harm.

[10] You schemed shame onto your own house. By cutting down so many peoples, you forfeited your own life.

[11] The very stones in the wall will cry out against you. The beams in the woodwork will answer them back.

[12] Disaster to the man who builds a city on bloodshed and founds a town on injustice.

[13] Is this not from the LORD of Armies? — nations exhaust themselves only to feed the fire, peoples wear themselves out for nothing.

[14] Because the earth will fill with the knowledge of the LORD's glory — the way water fills the sea.

[15] Disaster to the man who pours drinks for his neighbor — pouring out your wrath, getting them drunk, just to stare at their nakedness.

[16] You'll get shame instead of glory. Now it's your turn — drink, and stagger, and be exposed. The cup in the LORD's right hand is coming around to you. Disgrace will cover your glory.

[17] The violence you did to Lebanon will bury you. The slaughter of beasts that terrified them will crash down on your head — because of the blood you spilled, the violence done to lands and cities and everyone in them.

[18] What good is a carved idol once its maker has shaped it? A cast image, a teacher of lies? The craftsman trusts his own handiwork — but he's only made mute gods.

[19] Disaster to the man who says to wood, "Wake up!" — to silent stone, "Get on your feet!" Can it teach? Look — it's plated in gold and silver, but there's no breath inside it. None.

[20] But the LORD is in his holy temple. Let all the earth fall silent before him.

[1] A prayer of Habakkuk the prophet. On shigionoth.

[2] LORD, I have heard the report of you, and I am undone.
LORD, your work — revive it in the middle of these years,
in the middle of these years make it known.
In your fury, remember mercy.

[3] God comes striding from Teman,
the Holy One from Mount Paran. *Selah*
His splendor drapes the heavens.
The earth fills with his praise.

[4] His brilliance breaks like sunrise,
rays flashing from his hand —
and there, in that hand, his power lies hidden.

[5] Plague marches before him.
Pestilence trails at his heels.

[6] He stops — and the earth shudders.
He looks — and nations leap apart.
The ancient mountains crack open.
The eternal hills sink down.
His paths are the paths of forever.

[7] I saw the tents of Cushan staggered with terror,
the curtains of Midian trembling in the wind.

[8] LORD, was it the rivers that enraged you?
Was your fury aimed at the streams,
your wrath against the sea —
when you mounted your horses,
your chariots of salvation?

[9] You stripped your bow from its sheath —
arrows commissioned by oath. *Selah*
You split the earth open with rivers.

[10] The mountains saw you and writhed.
A deluge of water swept past.
The deep raised its voice
and lifted its hands to the sky.

[11] Sun and moon froze in their high houses
at the glint of your flying arrows,
at the lightning-flash of your spear.

[12] You strode across the earth in fury.
You trampled the nations in wrath.

[13] You marched out to save your people,
to save your anointed one.
You smashed the roof off the house of the wicked,
laid him bare from foundation to neck. *Selah*

[14] You ran his own spear through his head
when his warriors stormed out to scatter us,
gloating already, as if they were about to devour the poor in some dark corner.

[15] You drove your horses through the sea —
the great waters foaming beneath them.

[16] I heard, and my belly turned to water.
My lips quivered at the sound.
Rot crept into my bones.
My legs gave way beneath me.
Yet I will wait — quietly —
for the day of disaster
to break on the people invading us.

[17] Though the fig tree never blossoms,
though no fruit hangs on the vine,
though the olive crop fails
and the fields produce nothing to eat,
though the sheep vanish from the pen
and not a single cow stands in the stall —

[18] still. I will exult in the LORD.
I will dance in the God who saves me.

[19] The Lord GOD is my strength.
He makes my feet like a deer's,
sure-footed on the heights —

To the choirmaster. With my stringed instruments.

[1] In the second year of Darius the king, on the first day of the sixth month, the word of the LORD broke through the prophet Haggai to Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and to Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest:

[2] This is what the LORD of Armies says: These people keep saying, "The time hasn't come. Not yet. Not yet to rebuild the LORD's house."

[3] Then the word of the LORD came through Haggai the prophet:

[4] So it's time for you to live in your paneled mansions while my house lies in ruins?

[5] Now hear what the LORD of Armies says: Take a hard look at your lives.

[6] You plant much and harvest little. You eat and never feel full. You drink and stay thirsty. You bundle up and can't get warm. The worker earns wages only to pour them into a bag full of holes.

[7] This is what the LORD of Armies says: Take a hard look at your lives.

[8] Climb the hills. Bring back timber. Build the house. I'll take pleasure in it, and I'll be honored — says the LORD.

[9] You expected abundance, and look — barely anything. What little you brought home, I blew away. Why? — declares the LORD of Armies. Because of my house, which lies in ruins, while every one of you runs to tend his own.

[10] That's why the sky above you withholds its dew and the ground withholds its harvest.

[11] I summoned drought against the land, against the hills, against the grain, the new wine, the olive oil, against everything the ground brings forth, against people and livestock, against every labor of your hands.

[12] Then Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, along with all the remnant of the people, listened to the voice of the LORD their God — to the words of Haggai the prophet, because the LORD their God had sent him. And the people stood in awe before the LORD.

[13] Then Haggai, the LORD's messenger, delivered the LORD's message to the people: I am with you — declares the LORD.

[14] And the LORD stirred the spirit of Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and the spirit of Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and the spirit of all the remnant of the people. They came and went to work on the house of the LORD of Armies, their God,

[15] on the twenty-fourth day of the sixth month, in the second year of Darius the king.

[1] On the twenty-first day of the seventh month, the word of the LORD came through Haggai the prophet:

[2] "Speak to Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, to Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and to everyone left of the people. Ask them this:

[3] 'Who's still here who saw this house in its former glory? And how does it look to you now? Like nothing, doesn't it? Like a ghost of what it was.

[4] But be strong now, Zerubbabel — the LORD declares it. Be strong, Joshua son of Jehozadak, high priest. Be strong, all you people of the land — the LORD declares it. Get to work. I am with you — the LORD of Hosts declares it.

[5] This is the covenant I cut with you when you came out of Egypt. My Spirit stands right in your midst. Do not be afraid.'

[6] For this is what the LORD of Hosts says: 'A little while longer, and one more time I will shake the heavens and the earth, the sea and the dry land.

[7] I will shake every nation, and the treasures of all the nations will come pouring in. I will fill this house with glory,' says the LORD of Hosts.

[8] 'The silver is mine. The gold is mine,' declares the LORD of Hosts.

[9] 'The glory of this latter house will eclipse the former,' says the LORD of Hosts. 'And in this place I will give peace,' declares the LORD of Hosts."

[10] On the twenty-fourth day of the ninth month, in the second year of Darius, the word of the LORD came to Haggai the prophet:

[11] "This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Put a legal question to the priests. Ask them:

[12] 'If someone carries consecrated meat in the fold of his garment, and the fold brushes against bread or stew or wine or oil or any food — does that food become holy?'" The priests answered, "No."

[13] Then Haggai said, "And if someone defiled by contact with a corpse touches any of these — does it become unclean?" The priests answered, "Yes. It becomes unclean."

[14] Then Haggai answered: "So it is with this people, this nation in my sight — declares the LORD. So it is with everything their hands produce. Whatever they offer here — it's unclean.

[15] Now think back. Reflect carefully on what life was like before stone was laid on stone in the LORD's temple.

[16] When someone came expecting twenty measures of grain, there were ten. When someone came to the winepress to draw fifty, there were twenty.

[17] I struck your crops with blight and mildew and hail — everything your hands made. And still you would not turn back to me — declares the LORD.

[18] Now mark this day forward — the twenty-fourth of the ninth month — from the day the foundation of the LORD's temple was laid. Mark it carefully.

[19] Is there still seed in the barn? The vine, the fig, the pomegranate, the olive tree — none have borne yet. But starting today, I will bless you."

[20] The word of the LORD came to Haggai a second time on the twenty-fourth of the month:

[21] "Tell Zerubbabel, governor of Judah: I am about to shake the heavens and the earth.

[22] I will overturn the thrones of kingdoms. I will shatter the strength of the nations and their kingdoms. I will overturn chariots and their riders. Horses and horsemen will go down — each one cut down by his brother's sword.

[23] On that day — declares the LORD of Hosts — I will take you, Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, my servant — declares the LORD — and I will make you like the signet ring on my hand. For I have chosen you — declares the LORD of Hosts."

[1] Long ago, in fragments and flashes, God spoke through the prophets to our ancestors—many voices, many ways. [2] But now, at the close of the age, he has spoken to us through a Son: the one he installed as heir of everything, the one through whom he made the universe itself. [3] This Son is the radiance of God's glory, the exact imprint of his being, holding all things together by the sheer force of his word. After he had cleansed us of our sins, he sat down at the right hand of Majesty on high—

[4] —so far above the angels that the name he inherited towers over theirs. [5] After all, to which of the angels did God ever say, *You are my Son; today I have fathered you*? Or again, *I will be his Father, and he will be my Son*?

[6] And when God brings his firstborn into the world, he commands: *Let every angel of God bow before him.*

[7] Of the angels he says: *He makes his messengers winds, his servants flames of fire.*

[8] But to the Son: *Your throne, O God, stands forever and ever; the scepter of your kingdom is a scepter of justice. [9] You loved righteousness and hated lawlessness—so God, your God, anointed you with the oil of joy beyond all your companions.*

[10] And: *In the beginning, Lord, you laid the foundations of the earth, and the heavens are the work of your hands. [11] They will perish, but you remain. They will all wear out like an old coat; [12] you will roll them up like a robe, change them out like clothing. But you—you are the same. Your years will never run out.*

[13] To which of the angels has he ever said, *Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool beneath your feet*?

[14] Are not the angels simply spirits sent out to serve—dispatched on behalf of those about to inherit salvation?

[1] So we have to hold tighter to what we've heard — or we'll drift right past it. [2] If the word delivered through angels stood firm, and every violation and act of defiance received exactly what it had coming, [3] how do we expect to escape if we shrug off a rescue this enormous? It started on the Lord's own lips. It was confirmed to us by those who heard him directly. [4] And God himself backed it — with signs, with wonders, with all kinds of power breaking loose, and with gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed as he willed.

[5] The world to come — the one we're talking about — God did not hand over to angels. [6] Somewhere in Scripture someone makes the point: "What is a human being, that you keep him in mind? What is a son of man, that you bother with him? [7] You made him a little lower than the angels for a moment, then crowned him with glory and honor, [8] and put everything in subjection under his feet."

Everything. That means nothing was left out. Yet here we are — and we don't actually see everything bowing to him yet. [9] But we do see Jesus — the one made lower than the angels for that brief stretch — now crowned with glory and honor because he suffered death. By God's grace, he tasted death for every last one of us.

[10] It fit. The One for whom and through whom all things exist, bringing many sons home to glory — it fit perfectly that he would perfect the pioneer of their rescue through suffering. [11] The one who makes people holy and the ones being made holy all come from the same Father. That's why he isn't embarrassed to call them his brothers. [12] He says, "I'll tell my brothers your name. In the middle of the assembly I'll sing your praise." [13] And again: "I will put my trust in him." And again: "Here I am, with the children God gave me."

[14] Since the children are flesh and blood, he stepped into flesh and blood himself — so that by dying he could dismantle the one who held death's power. That's the devil. [15] And he freed everyone who had been living their whole lives shackled to the fear of dying.

[16] Notice — he doesn't reach down to help angels. He reaches down to help Abraham's offspring. [17] Which is why he had to be made like his brothers in every way — so he could become a merciful, faithful high priest in the things of God, making atonement for the sins of the people. [18] Because he himself suffered when he was tested, he knows how to come to the aid of those being tested right now.

[1] So then, brothers and sisters — you who are set apart, you who share in a calling that comes from heaven — fix your gaze on Jesus. He is the one we confess: the Apostle sent to us, the High Priest who stands for us.

[2] He was faithful to the One who appointed him, just as Moses was faithful in God's household.

[3] But Jesus deserves greater honor than Moses — in the same way the architect of a house outranks the house itself.

[4] Every house has its builder. The one who built everything is God.

[5] Moses served faithfully inside God's house — but as a servant, pointing forward to what was coming.

[6] Christ is faithful as a Son, over the house. And we are that house — provided we hold on, provided we keep our nerve and the hope we boast about.

[7] So listen. The Holy Spirit is speaking: *Today, if you hear his voice —*

[8] *do not harden your hearts the way they did in the rebellion, that day in the wilderness when they put me to the test.*

[9] *Your ancestors tested me there. They tried me. For forty years they watched what I did,*

[10] *and for forty years I was sick of that generation. I said: their hearts wander, they refuse to know my ways.*

[11] *So I swore in my anger: they will never enter my rest.*

[12] Watch yourselves, brothers and sisters. Make sure none of you develops an evil, faithless heart that pulls you away from the living God.

[13] Encourage each other — every single day, as long as it can still be called "today" — so that none of you gets hardened by sin's deceit.

[14] We have come to share in Christ — if we hold our original confidence firm, all the way to the end.

[15] Remember what was said: *Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts the way they did in the rebellion.*

[16] Who were the ones who heard and rebelled? All of them — the whole crowd Moses led out of Egypt.

[17] And who was God sick of for forty years? The ones who sinned — whose bodies fell in the wilderness.

[18] Who did he swear would never enter his rest? The ones who refused to obey.

[19] So we see it clearly: they could not enter. Unbelief shut the door.

[1] So the promise of entering his rest still stands. Be terrified that any one of you might be found to have missed it. [2] We got the same good news they did. But the message they heard did nothing for them, because they never fused it with faith when they heard it. [3] We who believed — we're the ones entering that rest. Just as he said: "So I swore in my anger: they will never enter my rest." And yet — his work has been finished since the foundation of the world. [4] Because somewhere it says of the seventh day, "And God rested on the seventh day from all his works." [5] And here again: "They will never enter my rest." [6] So the rest remains open. Some still get to enter it. The ones who first heard the good news did not — because of disobedience. [7] So God sets another day — "Today" — speaking through David long afterward, in the words already quoted: "Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts."

[8] If Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day still to come. [9] So a Sabbath rest is still waiting for the people of God. [10] Because whoever enters God's rest also rests from his own work — just as God did from his.

[11] So make every effort to enter that rest. Do not fall the way they fell — through the same kind of disobedience.

[12] The word of God is alive. It is active. Sharper than any double-edged blade, it slices clean through to the seam where soul meets spirit, where joint meets marrow. It reads the thoughts and intentions of the heart. [13] Nothing in all creation is hidden from him. Everything lies open, exposed, naked before the eyes of the One we have to answer to.

[14] So — since we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God — let us hold on to what we confess. [15] This high priest is not some distant figure unable to feel what we feel. He was tested in every way we are — and came through without sin. [16] So come. Walk straight up to the throne of grace with confidence. Receive mercy. Find grace, exactly when you need it.

[1] Every high priest pulled from the ranks of humanity is appointed to stand for humans before God — to bring gifts and sacrifices that deal with sin. [2] He can be gentle with those who don't know any better, with those who wander off course, because he himself is wrapped in the same weakness. [3] That weakness is exactly why he has to offer sacrifices for his own sins as well as theirs. [4] And nobody seizes this honor for himself — you're called to it by God, the way Aaron was.

[5] So Christ didn't crown himself High Priest either. The One who said to him, "You are my Son; today I have fathered you" — [6] that same One said elsewhere, "You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek."

[7] In the days when he wore flesh, he offered up prayers and pleas — with loud cries, with tears — to the One who could rescue him from death. And he was heard, because of his reverent surrender. [8] Son though he was, he learned obedience through what he suffered. [9] And once he was brought to completion, he became the source of eternal salvation for everyone who obeys him — [10] designated by God as High Priest in the order of Melchizedek.

[11] There's so much we could say about this, but it's hard to explain because you've gone dull in your hearing. [12] By now you ought to be teachers. Instead, you need someone to start you over from scratch — back to the alphabet of God's words. You've regressed to milk. You can't handle solid food. [13] Anyone living on milk is still an infant — untrained in the language of righteousness. [14] Solid food is for the mature: people whose senses have been exercised by practice to tell the difference between good and evil.

[1] So let's move past the kindergarten lessons about Christ and press on toward what's fully grown. We don't need to keep relaying the foundation—turning away from dead works, putting our trust in God, [2] instruction about washings, the laying on of hands, the dead rising, judgment that lasts forever. [3] And that's exactly what we'll do, if God permits.

[4] Because here's the hard truth: some people have been illuminated. They've tasted the gift from heaven. They've shared in the Holy Spirit. [5] They've savored the goodness of God's word and felt the raw power of the age to come— [6] and then they walked away. For them, there's no road back to fresh repentance. They're nailing the Son of God to the cross all over again, exposing him to public disgrace.

[7] Think of land that soaks up the rain falling steadily on it, then yields a harvest useful to those who farm it. That land receives God's blessing. [8] But land that grows nothing but thorns and thistles? Worthless. One curse away from being burned to ash.

[9] But listen—even though I'm talking like this, I'm convinced of better things about you, beloved. Things that go with salvation. [10] God isn't unjust. He won't forget your work, the love you've shown for his name in serving his people—and serving them still. [11] What I want is for every one of you to show that same eagerness right to the end, so your hope reaches full assurance. [12] Don't go slack. Imitate the ones who, through faith and patient endurance, are inheriting what was promised.

[13] When God made his promise to Abraham, he had no one greater to swear by—so he swore by himself. [14] He said, "I will bless you, absolutely bless you, and multiply you beyond counting." [15] And Abraham, after waiting it out, received what was promised.

[16] People swear by something greater than themselves, and the oath puts an end to every argument, locking the matter down. [17] So God, wanting to make it crystal clear to the heirs of the promise that his purpose would not budge, guaranteed it with an oath. [18] Two unshakable things—his promise and his oath—in which it's impossible for God to lie. And these two things are meant to be massive encouragement for us who have fled for refuge, gripping the hope set before us.

[19] This hope is the anchor of the soul—steady, immovable—reaching past the curtain into the inner sanctuary, [20] where Jesus has gone ahead of us, our forerunner, having become a high priest forever in the order of Melchizedek.

[1] This Melchizedek—king of Salem, priest of God Most High—met Abraham as he returned from slaughtering the kings, and blessed him. [2] Abraham gave him a tenth of everything. Decode his name: first, "king of righteousness." Then his title: "king of Salem"—king of peace. [3] No father on record. No mother. No genealogy. No birth noted, no death noted. He stands there resembling the Son of God—a priest who simply continues.

[4] See how massive this figure is: even Abraham, the patriarch himself, handed over a tenth of the spoils to him. [5] Now, Levi's descendants who become priests are commanded by law to collect a tenth from the people—their own brothers—even though those brothers also trace back to Abraham. [6] But Melchizedek, who doesn't descend from Levi at all, collected a tenth from Abraham and pronounced blessing over the man who carried the promises. [7] And nobody disputes this: the lesser is blessed by the greater. [8] In the Levitical case, mortal men collect the tithes. In Melchizedek's case, the one collecting is testified to be alive. [9] You could even say Levi himself, the tithe-collector, paid tithes through Abraham— [10] because Levi was still in his ancestor's body when Melchizedek met him.

[11] Now follow this. If the Levitical priesthood could have delivered the goods—and the whole legal system rests on it—why would Scripture announce a different priest coming, one in Melchizedek's order rather than Aaron's? [12] Change the priesthood and you've necessarily changed the law. [13] The one Scripture is talking about belonged to a different tribe entirely—a tribe that never served at the altar. [14] Everyone knows our Lord came out of Judah, and Moses said nothing about priests from that tribe. [15] And it gets clearer still: another priest arises in the pattern of Melchizedek, [16] not appointed by some genealogy on a scroll, but by the sheer force of an indestructible life. [17] The verdict is on record: "You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek."

[18] So the old command gets set aside—weak, useless, [19] because the law never brought anyone across the finish line. Something better moves into its place: a hope that actually gets us to God.

[20] And this happened with an oath, unlike the Levites— [21] they became priests without any oath, but he was installed with one: "The Lord has sworn and will not change His mind: you are a priest forever." [22] That oath is why Jesus stands as the guarantee of a better covenant.

[23] The old priests came in a long succession because death kept cutting them down—one after another, no one staying. [24] But he holds his priesthood permanently, because he doesn't die. [25] So he can save—completely, finally, all the way through—anyone who comes to God through him. He is always alive, always interceding for them.

[26] This is exactly the high priest we needed: holy, blameless, untouched by corruption, set apart from sinners, lifted higher than the heavens. [27] He doesn't need to offer daily sacrifices like the other high priests—first for his own sins, then for the people's. He did it once. He offered himself. Done. [28] The law installs flawed men as high priests. The word of the oath, which came after the law, installs the Son—made perfect, forever.

[1] Here's the heart of what we're saying: we have exactly this kind of high priest — one who took his seat at the right hand of the Majesty's throne in the heavens,

[2] serving in the true sanctuary, the real tent God pitched, not human hands.

[3] Every high priest exists to offer gifts and sacrifices, so this one had to have something to offer too.

[4] If he were on earth, he wouldn't even be a priest — the existing system already has people handling the legal offerings.

[5] But they're serving a sketch, a shadow of the real thing in heaven. That's why Moses, when he was about to build the tent, got the explicit warning: *Make sure you build it exactly to the pattern I showed you on the mountain.*

[6] But the ministry Jesus has received is so much greater — as great as the covenant he mediates is better, and it's better because it's built on better promises.

[7] If that first covenant had been flawless, there'd have been no reason to look for a second.

[8] But God finds fault with them. Listen:

*The days are coming, declares the Lord,
when I will cut a new covenant
with the house of Israel
and the house of Judah —*

[9] *not like the covenant I made with their ancestors
the day I took them by the hand
and led them out of Egypt.
They didn't hold up their end,
so I turned away from them, declares the Lord.*

[10] *This is the covenant I will make
with the house of Israel after those days, says the Lord:
I will plant my laws in their minds
and inscribe them on their hearts.
I will be their God,
and they will be my people.*

[11] *No one will have to teach his neighbor
or his brother, saying, "Know the Lord,"
because all of them will know me,
from the smallest to the greatest.*

[12] *I will be merciful toward their wrongs,
and their sins I will remember no more.*

[13] By calling this covenant *new*, he's pronounced the first one obsolete. And anything obsolete and aging is about to disappear.

[1] The first covenant came with its own rules for worship and an earthly sanctuary to host them. [2] A tent was pitched. The outer room held the lampstand, the table, and the consecrated bread — they called it the Holy Place. [3] Behind the second curtain stood another room — the Holy of Holies. [4] Inside: the golden altar of incense and the ark of the covenant, plated in gold from every angle, containing a golden jar of manna, Aaron's staff that had budded, and the stone tablets of the covenant. [5] Above the ark, cherubim of glory cast their shadow over the place of atonement. There's more to say about all this, but not now.

[6] With everything in place, the priests would routinely enter the outer room to carry out their duties. [7] But the inner room — only the high priest, only once a year, and never without blood, offered for his own failures and the people's blind ones. [8] The Spirit was signaling something through this: the way into the true Holy Place hadn't opened yet, not while the first tent was still standing. [9] It's a picture for the present age — a system of gifts and sacrifices that could never reach deep enough to clear the worshiper's conscience. [10] Just food, drink, ceremonial washings — external regulations imposed until the time of setting things right.

[11] Then Christ arrived — high priest of the good that has now come. He passed through a greater, more perfect tent, one not made by human hands, not part of this created order. [12] He did not enter with the blood of goats and calves. He entered once — once — with his own blood, and secured a redemption that will never expire. [13] If the blood of goats and bulls and the ashes of a heifer sprinkled on the defiled can purify the body, [14] how much deeper does the blood of Christ go — who through the eternal Spirit offered himself, unblemished, to God — scrubbing your conscience clean of dead works so you can serve the living God?

[15] This is why he stands as mediator of a new covenant: so that everyone called might receive the promised inheritance — his death covering the debts incurred under the first covenant. [16] Where there's a will, the one who made it has to die. [17] A will only takes effect after death; it carries no weight while the maker still breathes. [18] Even the first covenant needed blood to be ratified. [19] When Moses had announced every command of the law to all the people, he took the blood of calves and goats with water, scarlet wool, and hyssop, and sprinkled the scroll and the people, [20] saying, "This is the blood of the covenant God has commanded for you." [21] He sprinkled the tent and everything used in worship the same way. [22] Almost everything, under the law, is purified by blood — and without blood being poured out, there is no forgiveness.

[23] So the copies of heavenly things had to be purified by these rituals. But the heavenly things themselves require better sacrifices than those. [24] Christ did not enter a sanctuary made by human hands, a model of the real one. He entered heaven itself — to appear now, in person, before God, on your behalf. [25] And he did not go in to offer himself repeatedly, the way the high priest enters the Holy of Holies year after year with blood not his own. [26] If that were the case, he would have had to suffer over and over since the foundation of the world. Instead, once — at the climax of the ages — he appeared to abolish sin by the sacrifice of himself. [27] Just as people die once and then face judgment, [28] Christ was offered once to bear the sins of many. And he will appear a second time — not to deal with sin again, but to bring salvation to those who are waiting for him.

[1] The law was only a shadow of the good things coming — never the actual form of them. That is why it could never, through the same sacrifices repeated year after year, bring the worshipers to completion.

[2] If it could have, wouldn't the offerings have stopped? The worshipers, cleansed once for all, would no longer carry the weight of their sins on their conscience.

[3] But those sacrifices did the opposite — every year they made people remember their sins all over again.

[4] Because the blood of bulls and goats cannot take sins away. It is impossible.

[5] So when Christ entered the world, he said: "You didn't want sacrifices and offerings. Instead, you prepared a body for me.

[6] Burnt offerings and sin offerings — they gave you no pleasure.

[7] So I said: 'Here I am. I have come — it is written about me in the scroll — to do your will, O God.'"

[8] First he says: "You didn't want sacrifices and offerings and burnt offerings and sin offerings; they gave you no pleasure" — though these are exactly what the law required.

[9] Then he says: "Here I am. I have come to do your will." He cancels the first arrangement to establish the second.

[10] And by that will we have been made holy — through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ, once for all.

[11] Every priest stands day after day at his station, performing the same rituals, offering again and again the same sacrifices that can never strip away sin.

[12] But this one — after he offered a single sacrifice for sins for all time — sat down at the right hand of God,

[13] and from then on waits until his enemies are made the footstool under his feet.

[14] Because by one offering he has brought to permanent completion those who are being made holy.

[15] The Holy Spirit testifies to this as well. First he says:

[16] "This is the covenant I will cut with them after those days, says the Lord: I will plant my laws in their hearts, I will inscribe them on their minds."

[17] Then: "Their sins and their lawless acts I will remember no more."

[18] And where these have been forgiven, no further offering for sin is needed.

[19] So then, brothers and sisters — we have boldness to walk straight into the holy place by the blood of Jesus,

[20] by the new and living road he opened for us through the curtain, which is his flesh,

[21] and we have a great priest over the house of God.

[22] So let us approach — with hearts that mean it, in the full assurance of faith, our hearts sprinkled clean from a guilty conscience and our bodies washed in pure water.

[23] Let us hold the line on the hope we confess, without wavering, because the one who promised is faithful.

[24] And let us pay attention to one another — how to spark each other toward love and good works,

[25] not abandoning our gatherings, the way some have made a habit of doing, but encouraging one another — and all the more as you see the Day drawing close.

[26] Because if we keep on sinning deliberately after we have received the knowledge of the truth, there is no sacrifice for sins left —

[27] only the dread of expected judgment and a raging fire ready to consume the enemies of God.

[28] Anyone who threw out the law of Moses died without mercy on the testimony of two or three witnesses.

[29] How much worse a punishment do you think someone deserves who has trampled the Son of God underfoot, who has treated the blood of the covenant — the blood that made him holy — like a common thing, and insulted the Spirit of grace?

[30] We know the one who said: "Vengeance is mine, I will repay" — and again: "The Lord will judge his people."

[31] It is a terrifying thing to fall into the hands of the living God.

[32] Remember those earlier days, right after you were enlightened, when you stood your ground in a brutal battle of suffering —

[33] sometimes publicly exposed to insults and beatings, sometimes standing shoulder to shoulder with those being treated that way.

[34] You suffered alongside the prisoners. You accepted the seizure of your property with joy, because you knew you had a better possession — one that lasts.

[35] So do not throw away your confidence. It carries a massive reward.

[36] What you need is endurance — so that after you have done the will of God, you will receive what was promised.

[37] Because: "In just a little while, the one who is coming will come and will not delay.

[38] My righteous one will live by faith — but if he shrinks back, my soul takes no pleasure in him."

[39] But we are not the shrinking-back kind, the kind that gets destroyed. We are the faith kind — the kind that keeps the soul.

[1] Faith is the ground beneath what we hope for, the evidence of what we cannot see.

[2] By it, the ancients earned their reputation with God.

[3] By faith we understand that the universe was framed by a word from God — that what we see emerged from what nobody could see.

[4] By faith Abel brought God a better offering than Cain, and God himself testified to his gifts. Abel died, but his faith still speaks.

[5] By faith Enoch was lifted out of death — gone, because God took him. Before he vanished, he had this on record: he had pleased God.

[6] And without faith, pleasing God is impossible. Anyone who comes to him has to believe two things — that he exists, and that he rewards those who hunt for him.

[7] By faith Noah, warned about disasters not yet visible, took God seriously and built an ark to save his family. By that act he indicted the world and inherited the righteousness that comes through faith.

[8] By faith Abraham, when God called, walked out toward a country he would one day inherit. He left without knowing where he was going.

[9] By faith he camped in the promised land like a stranger in a foreign country, living in tents — and so did Isaac and Jacob, heirs of the same promise.

[10] He was waiting for the city with foundations, the one whose architect and builder is God.

[11] By faith Sarah herself — past the age, womb long dead — received power to conceive, because she counted the one who promised as trustworthy.

[12] And so from one man, as good as dead, came descendants as numerous as the stars overhead and as countless as the sand on the shore.

[13] All these died still believing. They never got their hands on what was promised — they only saw it from a distance, waved at it, and confessed openly that they were strangers and exiles on the earth.

[14] People who talk like that are making it clear: they are looking for a homeland.

[15] If they had been thinking of the country they left, they could have turned around and gone back.

[16] But they were reaching for something better — a country in heaven. So God is not ashamed to be called their God. He has a city ready for them.

[17] By faith Abraham, when tested, offered up Isaac. The man who had received the promises was about to sacrifice his only son —

[18] the son about whom God had said, "Through Isaac your line will be named."

[19] Abraham had concluded that God could raise the dead — and figuratively, that is exactly what he got: Isaac back from the dead.

[20] By faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau concerning things still to come.

[21] By faith Jacob, dying, blessed each of Joseph's sons and worshipped, leaning on the top of his staff.

[22] By faith Joseph, at the end of his life, spoke of Israel's exodus and gave orders about his bones.

[23] By faith Moses, when he was born, was hidden by his parents for three months — they saw he was a beautiful child and were not afraid of the king's edict.

[24] By faith Moses, grown, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter.

[25] He chose to suffer alongside God's people rather than enjoy the brief pleasures of sin.

[26] He counted disgrace for the Messiah as greater wealth than all the treasures of Egypt — because he was looking ahead to the reward.

[27] By faith he walked out of Egypt, unintimidated by the king's rage. He held steady, as if he could see the invisible one.

[28] By faith he kept the Passover and applied the blood, so the destroyer of the firstborn would not touch Israel.

[29] By faith they crossed the Red Sea as if it were dry ground. The Egyptians tried it and drowned.

[30] By faith the walls of Jericho collapsed after Israel marched around them seven days.

[31] By faith Rahab the prostitute did not die with the unbelievers, because she had welcomed the spies in peace.

[32] What more should I say? I do not have time to tell about Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets —

[33] who by faith conquered kingdoms, dispensed justice, took hold of promises, shut the mouths of lions,

[34] doused infernos, dodged the sword, found strength in weakness, became warriors in battle, sent foreign armies running.

[35] Women received their dead back, raised to life. Others were tortured and refused release, holding out for a better resurrection.

[36] Others endured mockery and whippings — chains, prisons.

[37] They were stoned. Sawed in two. Killed by the sword. They wandered in sheepskins and goatskins, destitute, harassed, brutalized —

[38] the world was not worthy of them. They drifted through deserts and mountains, hiding in caves and holes in the ground.

[39] All of them, though they were vindicated by their faith, did not receive what was promised.

[40] God had something better in mind for us — so that without us, they would not be made complete.

[1] So then — with this vast crowd of witnesses ringed around us like a stadium packed to the rafters — strip off every weight, every sin that wraps itself around your ankles, and run. Run the race marked out for you with everything you have.

[2] Lock your eyes on Jesus. He started this faith in you and he will finish it. For the joy waiting on the other side, he endured the cross — shrugged off its shame — and now sits enthroned at God's right hand.

[3] Think hard about him. He absorbed a tidal wave of hatred from sinners and didn't break. Remember that when your soul starts to buckle.

[4] You haven't yet bled for this. Not really. Not the way he did.

[5] And you've forgotten the word spoken to you as sons: *My son, don't shrug off the Lord's discipline, and don't collapse when he corrects you.*

[6] *Because the Lord disciplines the one he loves. He takes a rod to every son he claims.*

[7] So endure it — endure it as discipline. God is treating you as sons. What son is never disciplined by his father?

[8] If you're skipped over — if everyone else gets the discipline and you don't — then you're not really his. You're illegitimate.

[9] Our human fathers disciplined us and we respected them for it. How much more, then, should we yield to the Father of spirits and truly live?

[10] They disciplined us for a brief stretch as best they could figure. He disciplines us for our good — so we can share in his own holiness.

[11] No discipline feels good in the moment. It hurts. But later — for those trained by it — it yields a harvest: a life set right, at peace.

[12] So strengthen those slack hands. Steady those wobbling knees.

[13] Cut straight paths for your feet, so the limping one isn't thrown out of joint but healed.

[14] Chase peace with everyone. Chase holiness — without it, no one will ever see the Lord.

[15] Watch each other carefully. Don't let anyone drift from the grace of God. Don't let a bitter root push up through the soil and poison the whole community.

[16] Don't let anyone become sexually immoral or godless like Esau, who traded his inheritance for a single bowl of food.

[17] Remember: afterward, when he wanted the blessing back, he was rejected. He couldn't undo what he'd done — though he begged for it with tears.

[18] You haven't come to a mountain you can touch — to blazing fire, swirling darkness, gloom, and storm,

[19] to a trumpet blast and a voice speaking words so terrifying that those who heard begged for it to stop.

[20] They couldn't bear the command: *Even if an animal touches the mountain, it must be stoned.*

[21] The sight was so devastating that even Moses said, "I'm shaking with fear."

[22] No — you've come to Mount Zion. To the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem. To tens of thousands of angels in joyful assembly,

[23] to the gathering of the firstborn whose names are written in heaven. To God, the judge of all. To the spirits of the righteous, finally made whole.

[24] To Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant. To sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel.

[25] So don't refuse the one who is speaking. If they couldn't escape when they rejected the one who warned them on earth, how will we escape if we turn from the one who warns us from heaven?

[26] His voice once shook the earth. Now he has promised: *One more time I will shake — not just the earth, but the heavens too.*

[27] That phrase — *one more time* — means the removal of everything that can be shaken, everything created and temporary, so that what cannot be shaken will remain.

[28] So — since we are receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken — let's hold on to grace, and through grace let's worship God in a way that pleases him: with reverence, with awe.

[29] Because our God is a consuming fire.

[1] Keep loving each other like family. [2] Don't forget hospitality to strangers — some who did this entertained angels without knowing it. [3] Remember the prisoners as if you were chained beside them, and the mistreated as if it were your own body taking the blows.

[4] Marriage deserves honor from everyone, and the marriage bed kept undefiled — God will judge those who treat sex as sport and those who betray their spouses. [5] Strip the love of money out of your life. Be content with what you have, because God himself has said: *I will never leave you. I will never abandon you.* [6] So we can say with confidence: *The Lord is my helper — I will not be afraid. What can a mere human do to me?*

[7] Remember your leaders — the ones who spoke God's word into your life. Look hard at how their lives ended, and imitate their faith. [8] Jesus Christ — the same yesterday, today, and forever.

[9] Don't let yourselves get swept off by every exotic teaching that blows through. The heart is steadied by grace, not by rules about food — rules that never did anything for the people obsessed with them. [10] We have an altar, and those still serving in the old tent have no right to eat from it. [11] The high priest carries the blood of animals into the holy place as a sin offering, but the bodies get burned outside the camp. [12] That's why Jesus also suffered outside the city gate — to make the people holy through his own blood. [13] So let's go to him out there, beyond the camp, willing to wear the same disgrace he wore. [14] This city is not our home. We're searching for the one still to come.

[15] Through him, then, let's keep offering God a sacrifice of praise — the fruit of lips that openly name his name. [16] And don't neglect doing good and sharing what you have. These are the sacrifices that please God.

[17] Trust your leaders. Submit to them. They're keeping watch over your souls and will have to give an account. Let their work be a joy, not a groaning burden — that wouldn't help you either.

[18] Pray for us. We're confident our conscience is clean, and we want to live honorably in every way. [19] I'm asking this all the more urgently so I can be restored to you sooner.

[20] Now may the God of peace — who brought back from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great Shepherd of the sheep, by the blood of an eternal covenant — [21] equip you with everything good for doing his will, working in us what pleases him, through Jesus Christ. To him be the glory forever and ever. Amen.

[22] Bear with my appeal, brothers and sisters — I've kept it short. [23] Know that our brother Timothy has been released. If he comes soon, I'll bring him with me when I see you.

[24] Greet all your leaders and all God's people. Those from Italy send their greetings. [25] Grace be with you all.

[1] The word of the LORD came to Hosea son of Beeri during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah over Judah, and during the reign of Jeroboam son of Joash over Israel.

[2] When the LORD first spoke through Hosea, he gave him this command: "Go. Marry a whore. Have children with her — children of whoredom. Because this land has prostituted itself, abandoning the LORD completely."

[3] So Hosea went and took Gomer, daughter of Diblaim. She conceived and bore him a son.

[4] The LORD said: "Name him Jezreel. Because soon I will call in the blood-debt of Jezreel against the dynasty of Jehu, and I will shut down the kingdom of Israel for good.

[5] On that day I will shatter Israel's bow in the Jezreel valley."

[6] She conceived again and bore a daughter. The LORD said to Hosea: "Name her No-Mercy. Because I am finished pitying the house of Israel — finished forgiving them.

[7] But the house of Judah — them I will pity. I will rescue them. Not by bow or sword or battle, not by horses or cavalry. I, the LORD their God, will rescue them myself."

[8] When Gomer had weaned No-Mercy, she conceived again and bore a son.

[9] And the LORD said: "Name him Not-My-People. Because you are not my people, and I am not your God."

[10] Yet the day is coming when the people of Israel will be like the sand of the sea — beyond measuring, beyond counting. And in the very place where they were told, "You are not my people," they will be called sons of the living God.

[11] The people of Judah and the people of Israel will be gathered together, will choose one leader, will rise up out of the land. Massive will be the day of Jezreel.

[1] Yet the day will come when Israel's people will number like sand on the shore — uncountable, unmeasurable. And in the very place where they were told, "You are not my people," they will be called sons of the living God.

[2] Judah and Israel will gather as one, choose a single head for themselves, and rise up out of the land — for the day of Jezreel will be massive.

[3] Then call your brothers "My People" and your sisters "Loved."

[4] Bring charges against your mother — bring them! Because she is not my wife, and I am not her husband. Tell her: strip the whoring from her face, the adultery from between her breasts.

[5] Or I will strip her naked myself, expose her like the day she was born, turn her into wilderness, parch her into dry land, kill her with thirst.

[6] I will show no mercy to her children — they are bastards of her whoring.

[7] Their mother played the whore. The one who carried them disgraced herself. She said: "I'm going after my lovers — they give me my bread and water, my wool and linen, my oil and wine."

[8] So watch — I'm going to hedge her path with thornbushes. I'll wall her in. She won't find her way.

[9] She'll chase her lovers and never catch them. She'll search and never find them. Then she'll say, "I'm going back to my first husband. Life was better then than now."

[10] She never understood that I was the one giving her the grain, the wine, the oil — the silver I lavished on her, the gold she spent on Baal.

[11] So I'm taking it all back. My grain at harvest. My wine in season. My wool and linen that covered her nakedness — I'm reclaiming them.

[12] I'll lay her shame bare in front of her lovers, and no one will pull her from my hand.

[13] I'll shut down every celebration — her festivals, her new moons, her sabbaths, every appointed feast.

[14] I'll wreck her vines and fig trees — the ones she called her "wages" from her lovers. I'll turn them into a tangled forest, and wild animals will devour them.

[15] I'll make her answer for the days she burned incense to the Baals, decked herself in rings and jewelry, chased her lovers — and forgot me. So says the LORD.

[16] So here's what I'm going to do: I'm going to seduce her. I'll lead her out into the wilderness and speak straight to her heart.

[17] There I'll give her back her vineyards. The Valley of Trouble will become a door of hope. She'll respond to me there the way she did in her youth — the way she did the day she came up out of Egypt.

[18] On that day, says the LORD, you will call me "My Husband." You will no longer call me "My Master."

[19] I will rip the names of the Baals from her mouth — they will never be spoken again.

[20] On that day I will cut a covenant for them with the wild animals, the birds of the sky, the things that crawl on the ground. I'll snap the bow, the sword, the war itself — I'll break them across my knee. And I'll let my people lie down in safety.

[21] I will marry you to myself forever. I will marry you in righteousness, in justice, in fierce love, in tender mercy.

[22] I will marry you in faithfulness — and you will know the LORD.

[23] On that day I will answer, says the LORD. I will answer the sky, and the sky will answer the earth.

[24] The earth will answer the grain, the wine, the oil — and they will all answer Jezreel.

[25] I will plant her for myself in the land. I will love the one called Unloved. I will say to the one called Not-My-People: You are my people. And he will say: You are my God.

[1] Then the LORD told me: "Go again. Love a woman who is loved by another man — an adulteress. Love her the way I love the children of Israel, even as they keep turning to other gods and craving the raisin cakes of pagan feasts."

[2] So I bought her back. Fifteen pieces of silver and a homer and a lethek of barley.

[3] I told her: "You're going to stay with me — a long time. No more running around. No more men. And I will do the same for you."

[4] Because this is Israel's road ahead: a long stretch with no king, no prince, no sacrifice, no sacred pillar, no priestly ephod, no household idols.

[5] And after — the children of Israel will come back. They will seek the LORD their God and David their king. They will come trembling toward the LORD and toward his goodness, when the last days arrive.

[1] Listen up, Israel — the LORD has a case against everyone living in this land. No faithfulness left. No loyalty. No knowledge of God anywhere to be found. [2] Just cursing, lying, murder, theft, adultery — violence breaking loose, one bloodbath after another. [3] So the land itself is in mourning. Everything living in it is wasting away — the wild animals, the birds in the sky, even the fish in the sea are disappearing.

[4] But don't bother pointing fingers. Don't bother with accusations. My quarrel, priest, is with you. [5] You stumble in broad daylight, and the prophet stumbles with you in the dark — and I am going to silence your mother. [6] My people are being destroyed because they don't know me. And because you — yes, you, priest — rejected knowledge, I reject you from serving me. You forgot my instruction, so I will forget your children. [7] The more of them there were, the more they sinned against me. They traded their glory for shame. [8] They feed on the sins of my people. They have an appetite for their guilt. [9] So priest and people will share the same fate — I will deal with both for the road they've taken, pay them back for what they've done.

[10] They'll eat and stay hungry. They'll sleep around and get nowhere — because they've abandoned the LORD to chase [11] sex, wine, and the bottle. It steals the mind right out of them. [12] My people consult a piece of wood. A stick gives them their answers. A spirit of whoring has led them astray — they've prostituted themselves right out from under their God. [13] On the mountaintops they sacrifice, on the hills they burn incense — under oak and poplar and terebinth, anywhere the shade looks nice. So your daughters sleep around and your daughters-in-law commit adultery. [14] But I will not punish your daughters when they sleep around, or your daughters-in-law when they commit adultery — because the men themselves slip off with prostitutes and offer sacrifices with shrine girls. A people with no understanding will be flattened.

[15] Israel, if you want to whore yourself, fine — but don't drag Judah into your guilt. Don't go to Gilgal. Don't climb up to Beth-aven. Don't swear, "As the LORD lives." [16] Israel is stubborn as a wild heifer. So how can the LORD shepherd them like a lamb in open pasture? [17] Ephraim has married himself to idols. Leave him alone. [18] When their drinking is done, they go right to whoring. Their rulers love disgrace more than honor. [19] A wind is going to sweep them away, wings and all — and their sacrifices will become their humiliation.

[1] Listen up, priests. Pay attention, house of Israel. Hear this, royal house — judgment is coming down on you. You've been a trap at Mizpah, a net stretched out on Tabor, [2] a pit dug deep by rebels. I am about to discipline every last one of you.

[3] I know Ephraim. Israel cannot hide from me. Ephraim, you've sold yourself; Israel, you've defiled yourself.

[4] Their deeds won't let them turn back to their God. A spirit of prostitution runs in their veins — they don't know the LORD.

[5] Israel's own arrogance testifies against them in open court. Israel and Ephraim collapse under the weight of their guilt — and Judah stumbles right behind them.

[6] They'll come looking for the LORD with their flocks and herds, but they won't find him. He has slipped away.

[7] They've betrayed him — fathered illegitimate children. Now the new moon festival will devour them and their fields together.

[8] Sound the ram's horn in Gibeah! Blast the trumpet in Ramah! Raise the war cry at Beth-aven — Benjamin, they're coming for you!

[9] Ephraim will be a wasteland on the day of reckoning. I am declaring what is certain to the tribes of Israel.

[10] Judah's leaders are like men who shift boundary stones in the dark. I will pour my fury on them like a flood.

[11] Ephraim is crushed, broken under judgment — because he was determined to chase what was worthless.

[12] So I become like rot in Ephraim's bones, like decay eating through the house of Judah.

[13] When Ephraim saw he was sick and Judah saw his open wound, Ephraim ran to Assyria, sent envoys to the great king. But that king can't heal you. He cannot close your wound.

[14] Because I am the lion to Ephraim, the young lion to Judah. I will tear them apart and walk away. I will carry them off, and no one will rescue them.

[15] Then I will return to my place until they admit their guilt and come looking for my face. In their agony they will search hard for me.

[1] Come on — let's go back to the LORD. He's the one who tore us; he's the one who'll heal us. He struck us down; he'll bind us up.

[2] Two days from now he'll bring us back to life. On the third day he'll raise us up, and we'll live in his presence.

[3] So let's press in to know him — let's chase after the knowledge of the LORD. His arrival is as certain as the dawn. He'll come to us like the rains, like the spring showers soaking the ground.

[4] What do I do with you, Ephraim? What do I do with you, Judah? Your loyalty is morning mist. It's dew that burns off before breakfast.

[5] That's why I hacked at you through the prophets — why I cut you down with the words from my mouth. My judgment breaks over you like sunrise.

[6] I want loyalty, not sacrifice. I want you to know me — that matters more than burnt offerings.

[7] But like Adam, they broke the covenant. Right there, they betrayed me.

[8] Gilead is a city of thugs, its streets stained with blood.

[9] The priests are a gang of muggers. They ambush travelers on the road to Shechem. Murder is their liturgy.

[10] What I've seen in Israel turns my stomach. Ephraim is in bed with idols. Israel has defiled itself.

[11] And you, Judah — there's a harvest waiting for you too, when I finally restore the fortunes of my people.

[1] Every time I move to heal Israel, the rot of Ephraim surfaces, the crimes of Samaria spill into view — they traffic in lies, thieves break in, raiders strip the streets. [2] It never crosses their minds that I keep every wickedness on file. Their own deeds have them surrounded now; they parade across my face.

[3] They entertain the king with their evil, amuse the princes with their lies. [4] All of them — adulterers, every one — burning like an oven the baker doesn't even need to stoke between kneading the dough and watching it rise. [5] On the king's festival day the princes go down sick with wine's fever, and he extends his hand to mockers. [6] Their hearts are an oven and they smolder in it — all night their baker sleeps, by morning the thing is blazing like a furnace. [7] They are all hot as ovens, and they devour their own rulers. Every king of theirs has fallen. Not one of them calls on me.

[8] Ephraim mixes himself with the nations — Ephraim is a half-baked cake, raw on one side, scorched on the other. [9] Foreigners eat his strength and he never notices. Gray hair scattered all through him, and he never notices. [10] Israel's arrogance testifies to his face — and still they don't turn back to the LORD their God, still they don't seek him.

[11] Ephraim has become a dove — silly, brainless — calling to Egypt, flying off to Assyria. [12] As they go, I'll throw my net over them. I'll bring them down like birds out of the sky. I'll discipline them the moment I hear their flocking together. [13] Disaster on them — they've bolted from me. Ruin on them — they've broken faith with me. I would have ransomed them, but they keep telling lies about me. [14] They don't cry out to me from the heart — they wail on their beds. They gash themselves over grain and wine and turn away from me. [15] I trained their arms, made them strong, and they plot evil against me. [16] They turn — but not upward. They're a slack bow that misfires. Their princes will fall by the sword for the venom on their tongues. And Egypt will laugh.

[1] Put the trumpet to your lips. Something is circling the house of the LORD like a vulture — because they shattered my covenant and broke faith with my instruction.

[2] They cry out to me: "My God, we know you — Israel knows you!"

[3] But Israel has thrown away what is good. Now the enemy will hunt him down.

[4] They crowned kings without consulting me. They installed princes I never recognized. They took their silver and their gold and forged idols — engineering their own destruction.

[5] Throw out that calf, Samaria! My anger blazes against it. How long until they come clean?

[6] That thing came from Israel — a craftsman made it. It is not God. Samaria's calf will be smashed into splinters.

[7] They sowed wind. They will reap whirlwind. The stalk has no head; the grain will yield no flour — and if it did, foreigners would devour it.

[8] Israel has been swallowed. They sit among the nations now like a cracked pot nobody wants.

[9] They ran to Assyria like a wild donkey wandering off alone. Ephraim has been buying lovers.

[10] But however many they hire from the nations, I will round them up. Soon they will writhe under the weight of the king of princes.

[11] Ephraim built altar after altar to deal with sin — and the altars themselves became the sin.

[12] I wrote out my instructions for them by the tens of thousands — and they treated them like something foreign, irrelevant.

[13] They love their sacrificial feasts — slaughter the meat, eat their fill — but the LORD takes no pleasure in it. He remembers their guilt now. He will call their sins to account. Back to Egypt they go.

[14] Israel forgot the one who made him and built himself palaces. Judah fortified city after city. So I will send fire on their cities and devour their citadels.

[1] Don't celebrate, Israel. Don't throw a festival like the other nations. You've prostituted yourself away from your God. You've loved the payout — every threshing floor your reward.

[2] But the threshing floor and the winepress won't feed them. The new wine will fail them.

[3] They won't stay in the LORD's land. Ephraim is going back to Egypt. In Assyria they'll eat what's unclean.

[4] No more wine poured out to the LORD. No sacrifices that please him. Their bread will be the bread of mourners — everyone who eats it defiled. Their food stays in their own throats; it never reaches the house of the LORD.

[5] So what will you do on the feast day — the day of the LORD's festival?

[6] Even if they escape the wreckage, Egypt will gather them up, Memphis will bury them. Their silver treasures — nettles will inherit them. Thorns will move into their tents.

[7] The days of reckoning have come. The days of payback are here. Israel, know this. The prophet is a fool, the spiritual man is insane — that's what you say, because your guilt is enormous and your hostility runs deep.

[8] The prophet stands as Ephraim's watchman alongside my God — yet a fowler's snare waits on every road he walks, hostility in the house of his God.

[9] They've sunk deep into corruption, like the days of Gibeah. He'll remember their guilt. He'll deal with their sins.

[10] Finding Israel was like finding grapes in the wilderness. Seeing your ancestors was like spotting the first ripe figs on a new tree. But then they came to Baal-Peor — consecrated themselves to that shame — and became as repulsive as the thing they loved.

[11] Ephraim's glory will fly off like a bird — no birth, no pregnancy, no conception.

[12] Even if they raise their children, I'll bereave them — every last one. Yes, woe to them when I turn away.

[13] Ephraim — I once saw her like Tyre, planted in pleasant ground. But now Ephraim will lead her children out to the slaughterer.

[14] Give them, LORD — what will you give them? Give them wombs that miscarry and breasts gone dry.

[15] All their evil came to a head at Gilgal. There I came to hate them. Because of what they've done, I'm driving them out of my house. I won't love them anymore. Every one of their leaders is a rebel.

[16] Ephraim is struck down — the root withered, no fruit coming. Even if they give birth, I'll kill the darlings of their womb.

[17] My God will reject them, because they wouldn't listen to him. They'll be wanderers among the nations.

[1] Israel was a sprawling vine, fruit hanging heavy from every branch. But the more the fruit, the more the altars. The richer the land, the finer the sacred pillars they built. [2] Their heart was split in two, and now they pay. God himself will smash those altars, demolish those pillars. [3] Soon they'll say, "We have no king — because we never feared the LORD. And even if we did have a king, what could he do for us?" [4] Empty words. Hollow oaths. Covenants that mean nothing. And lawsuits sprout up like poison weeds in a plowed field. [5] The people of Samaria tremble for the calf-idol of Beth-aven. Its worshipers mourn it. Its priests — who once danced over its glory — wail because the glory has been hauled off into exile. [6] The thing itself will be carted to Assyria, tribute for the great king. Ephraim will be humiliated. Israel will choke on the shame of its own counsel. [7] Samaria's king is finished — a splinter of wood floating away on the water. [8] The high places of Aven, Israel's signature sin, will be wiped out. Thorns and thistles will crawl over their altars. And the people will beg the mountains, "Bury us." They'll beg the hills, "Fall on us."

[9] You've been sinning since the days of Gibeah, Israel. You never stopped. Now war is coming for you in Gibeah — coming for the sons of evil. [10] When I'm ready, I will discipline them. Nations will mass against them, chaining them up for their double iniquity. [11] Ephraim was a trained heifer, loved her work threshing grain. But I'm putting a yoke on that pretty neck. I'll harness Ephraim. Judah must plow. Jacob must break up the soil.

[12] Sow righteousness for yourselves. Reap the harvest of covenant love. Break up the hard, fallow ground of your hearts — because it's time to seek the LORD, time for him to come and rain down righteousness on you. [13] But you plowed wickedness. You reaped injustice. You ate the fruit of lies. Because you trusted your own way, your own warriors, your own arsenal — [14] the roar of war will rise against your people. Every fortress of yours will be flattened, the way Shalman smashed Beth-arbel on the day of slaughter — mothers and children dashed to pieces together. [15] That's what's coming to you, Bethel, because of your monstrous evil. At dawn — at the first light of that day — Israel's king will be cut down, finished.

[1] When Israel was a boy, I loved him. Out of Egypt I called my son.

[2] But the more I called, the further they ran — sacrificing to the Baals, burning incense to carved idols.

[3] I was the one who taught Ephraim to walk, taking him up in my arms — but he never knew it was me healing him.

[4] I led him with cords of kindness, with ropes of love. I was the one who lifted the yoke from his neck and bent down to feed him.

[5] So back to Egypt he goes — or Assyria will be his king, because he refused to turn around.

[6] The sword will whirl through his cities, smash the bars of his gates, devour them whole — all because of their own schemes.

[7] My people are bent on turning from me. They call upward, but no one rises to lift them.

[8] How can I give you up, Ephraim? How can I hand you over, Israel? How can I make you another Admah, treat you like Zeboiim? My heart twists inside me. Every flame of compassion in me is blazing.

[9] I will not act on the heat of my anger. I will not destroy Ephraim again. Because I am God, not man — the Holy One among you — and I will not come at you in fury.

[10] They will follow the LORD — he will roar like a lion, and when he roars, his children will come trembling from the west.

[11] They will come trembling like birds out of Egypt, like doves out of Assyria — and I will settle them back in their homes. This is the LORD speaking.

[12] Ephraim has fenced me in with lies, the house of Israel with deceit. But Judah still walks with God, still keeps faith with the Holy One.

[1] Ephraim feeds on wind, chasing the east gale all day long. He piles up lies and ruin, cuts deals with Assyria, ships olive oil to Egypt.

[3] In the womb he grabbed his brother's heel. In his prime he wrestled God.

[5] the LORD, God of armies, the LORD is his name.

[8] Ephraim crows, "I'm loaded. I've made myself a fortune. And in all my labors no one can pin a sin on me, no real guilt."

[9] But I am the LORD your God from the land of Egypt. I'll make you live in tents again, like the days of the festival.

[10] I spoke to the prophets. I gave them vision after vision. Through them I told parables.

[12] Jacob fled to the country of Aram. Israel labored for a wife — for a wife he tended sheep.

[13] By a prophet the LORD brought Israel up from Egypt, and by a prophet he was kept.

[14] Ephraim has provoked him to bitter rage. His Lord will leave his bloodguilt on him and repay him for his contempt.

[1] When Ephraim spoke, the room went silent. He carried weight in Israel. Then he chased Baal — and died.

[3] So they will vanish like morning mist, like dew that burns off at sunrise, like chaff swept off the threshing floor, like smoke curling out a window and gone.

[7] So I will come at them like a lion, crouch beside their road like a leopard.

[8] I will tear into them like a bear robbed of her cubs, rip open their chests, devour them on the spot like a lion — wild beasts shredding them apart.

[9] You have wrecked yourself, Israel — your help was in me, and only me.

[11] I gave you a king in my fury. I took him away in my wrath.

[12] Ephraim's guilt is filed away, his sin sealed up in storage.

[13] Birth pangs hit him — but he is a stupid child. When the moment comes, he refuses to push through the womb.

[14] Should I ransom them from the grave? Redeem them from death? Death — bring out your plagues. Grave — bring out your ruin. Pity is hidden from my eyes.

[15] He may flourish among his brothers — but an east wind is coming. The Lord's wind, rising from the desert, will dry up his springs, drain his wells. It will plunder his treasury, every precious thing.

[16] Samaria will pay — she defied her God. They will fall by the sword. Their infants will be dashed to pieces. Their pregnant women — ripped open.

[1] Samaria will pay for what she's done. She rebelled against her God. Her people will fall by the sword, their infants smashed against the ground, their pregnant women cut open.

[3] Bring words with you and return to the LORD. Say to him: "Take away all our guilt. Receive us with kindness, and we will offer you the praise of our lips instead of bulls.

[4] Assyria cannot save us. We will not ride into battle on warhorses. We will never again call the work of our own hands 'our god.' In you alone the orphan finds mercy."

[6] I will be dew to Israel. He will blossom like a lily. He will sink his roots deep like the cedars of Lebanon.

[1] What Isaiah son of Amoz saw concerning Judah and Jerusalem — visions that came to him during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah.

[2] Listen, sky. Pay attention, earth. The LORD is speaking: I raised children. I brought them up. And they turned on me.

[3] The ox knows who owns it. The donkey knows whose stall feeds it. Israel knows nothing. My people cannot put it together.

[7] Your country is a wasteland. Your cities are ash. Foreigners are eating your fields right in front of you — a wasteland, like the day after a foreign army leveled it.

[8] Daughter Zion is left like a lean-to in a vineyard, a watchman's shack in a cucumber patch, a city under siege and alone.

[9] If the LORD of armies had not left us a handful of survivors, we would be Sodom. We would be Gomorrah. That close.

[10] Hear the word of the LORD, you rulers of Sodom. Listen to the teaching of our God, you people of Gomorrah.

[11] What do I want with your endless sacrifices? — says the LORD. I am stuffed with burnt rams and the fat of fattened calves. The blood of bulls and lambs and goats — I have no appetite for it.

[12] When you come parading into my presence, who asked you to trample my courts?

[13] Stop bringing me your worthless offerings. Your incense disgusts me. New moon, Sabbath, sacred assembly — I cannot stomach evil dressed up as worship.

[14] Your new moons, your appointed feasts — I hate them. They have become a burden on my shoulders. I am exhausted from carrying them.

[15] When you stretch out your hands, I look away. When you pile on the prayers, I am not listening. Your hands are dripping blood.
