[1] Watch for it: a king is coming who will reign in righteousness, and princes who will rule with justice.

[2] Each one will be a shelter from the wind, a hiding place from the storm — streams of water in a desert, the shadow of a massive rock in a land burned dry.

[3] Then the eyes of those who see will not be sealed shut, and the ears of those who hear will actually listen.

[4] The reckless heart will learn discernment, and the stammering tongue will speak fluently, clearly.

[5] No more will the fool be called noble, no more will the swindler be praised as generous.

[6] Because the fool speaks foolishness, and his mind churns out evil — practicing godlessness, peddling lies about the Lord, leaving the hungry empty and cutting off water from the thirsty.

[7] The swindler's methods are wicked. He plots schemes to destroy the poor with lies, even when the needy plead a just case.

[8] But the noble person plans noble things, and stands by what is noble.

[9] Get up, you women lounging in comfort — listen to me. You daughters who feel so secure — hear what I'm saying.

[10] In a little over a year, you complacent ones will tremble. The grape harvest will fail. No fruit will come in.

[11] Shudder, you comfortable ones. Tremble, you who feel so safe. Strip yourselves bare. Tie sackcloth around your waists.

[12] Beat your breasts for the pleasant fields, for the fruitful vines —

[13] for the soil of my people, where thorns and briars will rise up, for every happy house in the joyful city.

[14] The palace is abandoned. The crowded city is empty. Fortress and watchtower become a wasteland forever — wild donkeys' delight, a pasture for flocks.

[15] Until the Spirit is poured out on us from above, and the wilderness becomes orchard, and what we now call orchard looks like a forest.

[16] Then justice will move into the wilderness, and righteousness will live in the orchard.

[17] The work of righteousness will be peace. The result of righteousness — calm and confidence, forever.

[18] My people will live in peaceful homes, in secure dwellings, in untroubled places of rest.

[19] Though the forest comes crashing down in hail, and the city is leveled to the ground —

[20] you will be blessed, you who sow beside every stream, who let your ox and donkey roam free.

[1] Doom to you, destroyer—you who were never destroyed. Doom to you, betrayer—you whom no one ever betrayed. When you finish destroying, you will be destroyed. When you stop betraying, you will be betrayed.

[2] Lord, show us mercy. We have waited for you. Be our strength every morning, our deliverance when the trouble closes in.

[3] At the thunder of your voice, peoples scatter. When you rise up, nations fly apart.

[4] Your plunder is gathered like locusts gathering grain—men swarm over it like a horde of insects on a field.

[5] The Lord is lifted high—he lives in the heights. He fills Zion with justice and right.

[6] He will be the steady ground beneath your days, an abundance of rescue, wisdom, knowledge. Reverence for the Lord—that is the treasure.

[7] Listen—the warriors weep in the streets. The peace envoys break down sobbing.

[8] The highways lie empty. No traveler walks them. Treaties shattered. Witnesses despised. No one counts as human anymore.

[9] The land mourns and withers. Lebanon stands humiliated, rotting. Sharon has become a wasteland. Bashan and Carmel shake their leaves to the ground.

[10] Now I rise, says the Lord. Now I am lifted up. Now I show my full height.

[11] You conceive chaff. You give birth to stubble. Your own breath is a fire that will eat you alive.

[12] Whole peoples will burn to lime—thornbushes hacked down and thrown in the blaze.

[13] You far away—hear what I have done. You close at hand—acknowledge my power.

[14] In Zion the sinners are terrified. Trembling has seized the godless: Who among us can live with a consuming fire? Who among us can survive flames that never go out?

[15] The one who walks straight and speaks true, who refuses the profits of oppression, who shakes the bribe out of his hand, who stops his ears against bloody schemes and shuts his eyes against evil—

[16] that one will live on the heights. Rock fortresses will be his refuge. His bread will be given him. His water never fails.

[17] Your eyes will see the King in his beauty. They will gaze on a land that stretches far.

[18] Your mind will turn back to the old terror and ask: Where is the bureaucrat who counted us? Where is the one who weighed the tribute? Where is the one who tallied the towers?

[19] You will not see that brutal people again—the people whose speech you could not catch, whose stammering tongue made no sense.

[20] Look at Zion, our festival city. Your eyes will see Jerusalem—a quiet home, a tent that will never be moved, whose stakes will never be pulled, whose ropes will never snap.

[21] There the Lord himself will be our magnificent presence—a place of wide rivers and streams, where no enemy galley rows, where no warship passes through.

[22] For the Lord is our judge. The Lord is our lawgiver. The Lord is our King. He is the one who will save us.

[23] Your rigging hangs slack. It cannot hold the mast. It cannot spread the sail. Then plunder beyond counting will be divided up—even the lame will haul off the loot.

[24] No resident will say, I am sick. The people who live there will have their guilt lifted away.

[1] Come close, nations — listen. Peoples, pay attention. Let the earth hear, and everything that fills it; the world, and everything it produces.

[2] The LORD's fury is loose against all nations, his wrath against every army they've raised. He has marked them for destruction, handed them over to the slaughter.

[3] Their dead will be flung out unburied. The stench will rise from their corpses, and the mountains will run with their blood.

[4] The stars will rot in the sky. The heavens will roll up like a scroll. Every star will fall — like a withered leaf off a vine, like a shriveled fig dropping from the tree.

[5] My sword has drunk its fill in the heavens. Now watch — it comes down on Edom, on the people I have already sentenced.

[6] The LORD's sword runs red — dripping with blood, slick with fat — the blood of lambs and goats, the fat from the kidneys of rams. The LORD is holding a sacrifice in Bozrah, a great slaughter in the land of Edom.

[7] Wild oxen will fall with them, young bulls with the mighty bulls. The ground will be drunk on blood, the soil saturated with fat.

[8] This is the LORD's day of reckoning — a year of payback for Zion's cause.

[9] Edom's streams will turn to tar, its soil to sulfur, its land a burning pitch.

[10] Night and day it will not go out. Smoke will rise from it forever. From one generation to the next it will lie in ruins — no one passing through, not ever again.

[11] The desert owl and the screech owl will take it over. The great owl and the raven will nest there. God will stretch over it the measuring line of chaos, the plumb stones of emptiness.

[12] No nobles will be left to call out a kingdom. Every prince — gone.

[13] Thorns will overrun its palaces, nettles and brambles its strongholds. It will be a haunt for jackals, a courtyard for ostriches.

[14] Desert creatures will meet hyenas there. Wild goats will call to each other. There the night-demon will settle and find herself a resting place.

[15] The owl will make her nest there — lay her eggs, hatch them, gather her brood under her shadow. There the hawks will gather, each with its mate.

[16] Search the LORD's scroll and read: not one of these will be missing, none without its mate. His mouth has spoken it — his Spirit will gather them in.

[17] He himself has cast the lot for them. His hand has measured out their portion with the line. They will possess it forever, generation after generation making their home there.

[1] The wilderness will throw a party. The wasteland will burst into bloom — crocus everywhere, riotous color where there was only dust.

[2] It will bloom hard, sing out loud, drunk on joy. The glory of Lebanon will be handed to it, the splendor of Carmel and Sharon. They will see the glory of the LORD — the sheer magnificence of our God.

[3] So put strength back into the slack hands. Steady the knees that have given out.

[4] Tell the ones whose hearts are racing with fear: Hold on. Be strong. Don't be afraid. Look — your God is coming. Vengeance is coming with him. Divine payback. He himself is coming to rescue you.

[5] Then blind eyes will fly open. Deaf ears will be unstopped.

[6] The lame will leap like a deer. The mute tongue will break into singing. Water will erupt in the wilderness, streams cutting through the desert.

[7] The mirage will become a real lake. The parched ground, bubbling springs. Where jackals used to den down in the dust, grass will grow — reeds and rushes.

[8] And there will be a road there. A highway. They'll call it the Way of Holiness. The unclean won't travel it. It belongs to those who walk the way — and even a fool can't get lost on it.

[9] No lions there. No predator sets foot on it. Only the redeemed walk that road.

[10] The ransomed of the LORD will come home — back to Zion with singing, joy crowning their heads like a wreath that won't wilt. Gladness and joy will catch up to them, and sorrow and sighing will turn and run.

[1] In the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah's reign, Sennacherib king of Assyria came up against every fortified city in Judah and took them.

[2] From Lachish, the king of Assyria dispatched his field commander to Jerusalem with a heavy force to confront King Hezekiah. The commander took up position by the aqueduct of the upper pool, on the road to the Launderer's Field.

[3] Out came Eliakim son of Hilkiah, who managed the palace, along with Shebna the scribe and Joah son of Asaph, the recorder.

[4] The field commander said to them: "Tell Hezekiah this — from the Great King, the king of Assyria: What is this confidence you're leaning on?

[5] You think empty words count as strategy and strength in war? Who exactly are you trusting that you'd rebel against me?

[6] Egypt? That cracked reed of a staff? Lean on it and it punctures your hand. That's Pharaoh king of Egypt — to everyone who leans on him.

[7] And if you say to me, 'We trust in the LORD our God' — wasn't that the very one whose high places and altars Hezekiah tore down, telling Judah and Jerusalem, 'You'll worship before this one altar'?

[8] So make a wager with my master the king of Assyria: I'll hand you two thousand horses — if you can even find riders to put on them.

[9] How could you push back even the lowest-ranking officer of my master's servants? And you're trusting Egypt for chariots and horsemen?

[10] Did I come up to wreck this land without the LORD? The LORD himself told me: 'Go up against this land and destroy it.'"

[11] Then Eliakim, Shebna, and Joah said to the field commander: "Please — speak Aramaic to your servants. We understand it. Don't speak Hebrew with us in earshot of the people on the wall."

[12] But the commander shot back: "Did my master send me to speak these words to your master and to you? No — to the men sitting on the wall, who are about to eat their own dung and drink their own urine with you."

[13] Then he stood and shouted in Hebrew at the top of his lungs: "Listen to the words of the Great King, the king of Assyria!

[14] The king says: Don't let Hezekiah deceive you. He cannot save you.

[15] Don't let Hezekiah make you trust in the LORD by saying, 'The LORD will surely rescue us. This city will not be handed to the king of Assyria.'

[16] Don't listen to Hezekiah. The king of Assyria says: Make peace with me. Come out to me. Every one of you will eat from your own vine and your own fig tree and drink water from your own well —

[17] until I come and take you to a land like your own — a land of grain and new wine, a land of bread and vineyards.

[18] Don't let Hezekiah seduce you with 'The LORD will rescue us.' Did any god of any nation rescue his land from the hand of the king of Assyria?

[19] Where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim? Did they rescue Samaria from my hand?

[20] Of all the gods of all these lands — which one rescued his land from my hand, that the LORD should rescue Jerusalem from my hand?"

[21] The people kept silent. Not a word answered him — the king's order had been clear: "Don't answer him."

[22] Then Eliakim son of Hilkiah, the palace manager, Shebna the scribe, and Joah son of Asaph, the recorder, came to Hezekiah with their clothes torn and reported the field commander's words.

[1] When King Hezekiah heard it, he tore his clothes, wrapped himself in sackcloth, and went into the house of the LORD.

[2] He sent Eliakim, who ran the palace, Shebna the scribe, and the senior priests — all draped in sackcloth — to the prophet Isaiah, son of Amoz.

[3] They told him: "Hezekiah says this — today is a day of agony, of rebuke, of disgrace. Children have come to the moment of birth, and there is no strength to push them out.

[4] Maybe the LORD your God heard what the Rabshakeh said — sent by the king of Assyria, his master, to mock the living God — and maybe he will answer the words your God heard. So lift up a prayer for the remnant that still survives."

[5] When Hezekiah's officials came to Isaiah,

[6] Isaiah told them: "Tell your master this — the LORD says: Don't be afraid of what you heard, those words the lackeys of the king of Assyria used to blaspheme me.

[7] Watch — I am about to put a spirit in him. He will hear a rumor, return to his own land, and there I will cut him down with the sword."

[8] The Rabshakeh went back and found the king of Assyria attacking Libnah — he had heard the king had left Lachish.

[9] Then word reached Sennacherib about Tirhakah, king of Cush: "He has marched out to fight you." When he heard it, he sent messengers to Hezekiah again with this message:

[10] "Say this to Hezekiah, king of Judah — don't let the God you trust deceive you when he says Jerusalem won't fall into the hand of the king of Assyria.

[11] You've heard what the kings of Assyria have done to every country — wiped them out. And you think you'll be spared?

[12] Did the gods of the nations my fathers destroyed save them? Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, the people of Eden in Telassar — gone.

[13] Where is the king of Hamath, the king of Arpad, the king of the city of Sepharvaim, of Hena, of Ivvah?"

[14] Hezekiah took the letter from the messengers' hands and read it. Then he went up to the house of the LORD and spread it out before the LORD.

[15] And Hezekiah prayed to the LORD:

[16] "LORD of Hosts, God of Israel, enthroned above the cherubim — you alone are God over every kingdom on earth. You made the heavens and the earth.

[17] Bend your ear, LORD, and listen. Open your eyes, LORD, and see. Hear every word Sennacherib sent to taunt the living God.

[18] It's true, LORD — the kings of Assyria have laid waste to all those nations and their lands,

[19] and thrown their gods into the fire. But those weren't gods — just wood and stone, the work of human hands. So of course they were destroyed.

[20] Now, LORD our God, rescue us from his hand, so every kingdom on earth will know that you alone are the LORD."

[21] Then Isaiah, son of Amoz, sent word to Hezekiah: "The LORD, the God of Israel, says: Because you have prayed to me about Sennacherib, king of Assyria —

[22] this is the word the LORD has spoken against him:

She despises you, she laughs at you —
the virgin daughter of Zion.
She shakes her head behind your back —
the daughter of Jerusalem.

[23] Who have you mocked? Who have you reviled?
Against whom have you raised your voice
and lifted your eyes in arrogance?
Against the Holy One of Israel.

[24] Through your servants you taunted the Lord and said:
'With my swarm of chariots
I climbed the mountain heights,
the far recesses of Lebanon.
I cut down its tallest cedars,
its choicest cypresses.
I reached its remotest peak,
its densest forest.

[25] I dug wells and drank foreign water.
I dried up every river of Egypt
with the soles of my feet.'

[26] Haven't you heard?
Long ago I planned it.
In days of old I shaped it.
Now I have brought it to pass —
that you should crush fortified cities into heaps of rubble.

[27] Their people, drained of strength,
were shattered and ashamed.
They were grass in the field, tender green shoots,
weeds on the roof,
scorched before they could grow.

[28] But I know where you sit,
when you come and when you go,
and how you rage against me.

[29] Because of your raging,
because your arrogance has reached my ears,
I will put my hook in your nose,
my bit in your mouth,
and turn you back the way you came.

[30] And this will be the sign for you, Hezekiah: This year you will eat what grows on its own. Next year, what springs up from that. But in the third year — sow and reap, plant vineyards, eat their fruit.

[31] The surviving remnant of the house of Judah will sink roots downward and bear fruit upward.

[32] Out of Jerusalem will come a remnant, out of Mount Zion survivors. The zeal of the LORD of Hosts will do this.

[33] So this is what the LORD says about the king of Assyria:

He will not enter this city.
He will not shoot an arrow here.
He will not face it with a shield
or pile up a siege ramp against it.

[34] By the way he came, he will return.
He will not enter this city —
declares the LORD.

[35] I will defend this city and save it,
for my own sake,
and for the sake of David my servant."

[36] Then the angel of the LORD went out and struck down a hundred and eighty-five thousand in the Assyrian camp. When the rest woke at dawn — they were corpses, every one of them.

[37] So Sennacherib, king of Assyria, broke camp and went home. He stayed in Nineveh.

[38] And while he was worshiping in the temple of his god Nisroch, his own sons — Adrammelech and Sharezer — cut him down with the sword and fled to the land of Ararat. His son Esarhaddon took the throne.

[1] In those days Hezekiah fell deathly ill. The prophet Isaiah son of Amoz came to him and said, "This is what the LORD says: Put your house in order. You are going to die. You will not recover."

[2] Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to the LORD.

[3] "LORD," he said, "remember — please remember — how I walked before you faithfully, with a whole heart, doing what was good in your sight." And Hezekiah broke down and wept, hard.

[4] Then the word of the LORD came to Isaiah:

[5] "Go back. Tell Hezekiah: This is what the LORD, the God of your father David, says: I have heard your prayer. I have seen your tears. Listen — I am adding fifteen years to your life.

[6] And I will pull you and this city out of the Assyrian king's grip. I will defend this city.

[7] Here is the sign from the LORD that he will do what he has promised:

[8] Look at the shadow on the stairway of Ahaz, where the sun has been descending. I am going to drag it backward — ten steps." And the sun retreated ten steps on the stairway it had already come down.

[9] What Hezekiah king of Judah wrote after he had been sick and recovered:

[10] I said: In the prime of my life
I have to walk through the gates of death,
robbed of the years I had left.

[11] I said: I will never see the LORD again —
the LORD in the land of the living.
I will never look on another human face,
never see those who dwell in the world.

[12] My life has been pulled up and carried off
like a shepherd's tent.
I have rolled up my days like a weaver
who cuts the cloth from the loom.
From dawn to dusk you are finishing me.

[13] I wait through the night, composing myself —
but like a lion he is breaking every bone.
From dawn to dusk you are finishing me.

[14] I cry like a swift, like a swallow.
I moan like a dove.
My eyes grow tired looking up.
LORD, I am crushed — stand surety for me!

[15] But what can I say?
He has spoken to me, and he himself has done it.
I will walk slowly all my years
because of this bitterness in my soul.

[16] LORD, this is how people live —
this is how my spirit finds its life:
you restored me. You let me live.

[17] Look — the bitterness was for my good.
You loved me back from the pit of annihilation.
You hurled all my sins
behind your back.

[18] The grave cannot praise you.
Death cannot sing.
Those going down into the pit
have no more hope in your faithfulness.

[19] The living, the living — they praise you,
the way I do today.
Fathers tell their children
about your faithfulness.

[20] The LORD will save me —
and we will play music all the days of our lives
in the house of the LORD.

[21] Isaiah had said, "Get a cake of pressed figs. Spread it on the boil. He will recover."

[22] And Hezekiah had asked, "What is the sign that I will go up to the house of the LORD?"

[1] Around that time, Merodach-baladan son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent letters and a gift to Hezekiah — word had reached him that Hezekiah had been sick and had recovered.

[2] Hezekiah welcomed the envoys warmly and gave them the tour: the treasury, the silver, the gold, the spices, the fine oil, the entire armory, everything in his storehouses. There was nothing in his palace or anywhere in his realm that Hezekiah did not show them.

[3] Then Isaiah the prophet came to King Hezekiah and asked, "What did those men say? Where did they come from?"

"They came from a distant country," Hezekiah answered. "From Babylon."

[4] "What did they see in your palace?" Isaiah pressed.

"Everything," Hezekiah said. "There is nothing in my storehouses I did not show them."

[5] Then Isaiah said to Hezekiah, "Hear the word of the LORD of Hosts:

[6] The days are coming when everything in your palace — everything your ancestors stockpiled to this day — will be hauled off to Babylon. Nothing will be left. The LORD has spoken.

[7] And some of your own sons, your own flesh and blood, will be taken too. They will serve as eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon."

[8] Hezekiah replied to Isaiah, "The word of the LORD you have spoken is good." For he was thinking: at least there will be peace and security in my lifetime.

[1] Comfort. Comfort my people — this is what your God is saying.

[2] Speak straight to Jerusalem's heart. Tell her the hard service is finished, the debt is settled, that she has taken from the LORD's hand double for every wrong.

[3] A voice is calling out: Clear a road for the LORD through the wilderness. Cut a straight highway across the wasteland for our God.

[4] Every valley — fill it in. Every mountain and hill — bring it down. The crooked places, make them straight. The rough country, smooth it out.

[5] Then the glory of the LORD will be unveiled, and every human being alive will see it together. The mouth of the LORD has spoken.

[6] A voice says: Call out. And I said: What should I call? — All flesh is grass, and every kindness of it like the wildflower in the field.

[7] The grass withers, the flower fades, when the breath of the LORD blows across it. Yes — the people are grass.

[8] The grass withers. The flower fades. But the word of our God stands forever.

[9] Climb a high mountain, you who carry good news to Zion. Lift your voice with everything in you, you who carry good news to Jerusalem. Lift it — don't be afraid. Tell the cities of Judah: Here is your God.

[10] Look — the Lord GOD is coming in strength, and his arm rules for him. His reward travels with him; what he has won walks ahead of him.

[11] He will tend his flock the way a shepherd does — gathering lambs into the crook of his arm, carrying them against his chest, leading the nursing ewes with care.

[12] Who has measured the oceans in the cup of his hand? Who marked off the sky with the span of his fingers? Who held the dust of the earth in a bucket, weighed the mountains on a scale, the hills in a balance?

[13] Who has charted the Spirit of the LORD, or stood at his elbow giving counsel?

[14] Whom did he consult? Who taught him? Who showed him the path of justice, schooled him in knowledge, walked him through the way of understanding?

[15] Look — the nations are a drop falling from a bucket. They count as dust on the scales. He lifts whole islands like grains of sand.

[16] All Lebanon could not fuel his fire. All its beasts could not be sacrifice enough.

[17] Every nation, set before him, is nothing. Less than nothing. Empty.

[18] So who will you compare to God? What image will you set beside him?

[19] An idol? A craftsman pours it. A goldsmith plates it. He fits it with silver chains.

[20] Too poor for that? Then someone picks out wood that won't rot, finds a skilled carver, and sets up an idol that will not move.

[21] Do you not know this? Have you not heard? Was it not told to you from the beginning? Have you not understood since the foundations of the earth were laid?

[22] He sits enthroned above the circle of the earth, and the people on it look like grasshoppers. He stretches out the heavens like fabric, spreads them like a tent to live under.

[23] He reduces rulers to nothing. He makes the judges of the earth meaningless.

[24] Barely planted, barely sown, barely rooted in the ground — he blows on them, and they wither. The whirlwind carries them off like straw.

[25] So to whom will you compare me? Who is my equal? — says the Holy One.

[26] Lift your eyes. Look up. Who created all these? He brings out their armies by number; he calls each one by name. Because his power is overwhelming, his strength absolute, not one of them is missing.

[27] So why are you saying, Jacob — why claim, Israel — My road is hidden from the LORD; my God has lost track of my cause?

[28] Have you not known? Have you not heard? The LORD is the everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth. He does not grow tired. He does not grow weary. His understanding has no bottom.

[29] He gives strength to the exhausted. To those with nothing left, he multiplies power.

[30] Even the young grow tired and stumble. Even the strong fall flat.

[31] But those who wait on the LORD will find their strength renewed. They will rise on wings like eagles. They will run and not collapse. They will walk and not give out.

[1] Quiet, you coastlands. Listen. Let the nations gather their strength, step forward, and speak. Let us meet in court together.

[2] Who stirred up the one from the east, summoned righteousness to his heel? Who hands nations over to him, makes kings bow before him, turns them to dust under his sword, to windblown straw under his bow?

[3] He chases them down, passes through untouched, his feet barely brushing the road.

[4] Who pulled this off? Who orchestrated it? The one who has been calling out the generations from the start. I am the LORD — first, last, and all of it.

[5] The coastlands see and tremble. The ends of the earth shake. They come crawling forward together.

[6] Each one props up his neighbor, tells his brother, "Hold steady."

[7] The craftsman steadies the goldsmith. The hammer-wielder cheers on the one at the anvil. "Good weld," they say. They nail the idol down so it won't tip over.

[8] But you, Israel — my servant. Jacob — the one I chose. Offspring of Abraham, who loved me.

[9] I grabbed you from the ends of the earth, called you in from its farthest corners. I told you, "You are mine. I have not thrown you away."

[10] Don't be afraid — I am with you. Don't look around in panic — I am your God. I will brace you. I will help you. I will hold you up with the right hand of my righteousness.

[11] Everyone burning with rage against you — they'll be humiliated, disgraced. The ones who pick fights with you — they'll come to nothing, vanish.

[12] You'll search for your enemies and not find them. The ones at war with you will be as if they never existed.

[13] Because I am the LORD your God, gripping your right hand, telling you: Don't be afraid. I am the one helping you.

[14] Don't be afraid, you worm Jacob, you handful of Israel — I am helping you. This is the LORD speaking. Your redeemer is the Holy One of Israel.

[15] Look — I am turning you into a threshing sledge, sharp, new, bristling with teeth. You will thresh mountains and pulverize them. You will reduce hills to chaff.

[16] You will winnow them. The wind will carry them off. A storm will scatter them. And you — you will exult in the LORD, glory in the Holy One of Israel.

[17] The poor, the destitute, searching for water and finding none, tongues cracked with thirst — I, the LORD, will answer them. I, the God of Israel, will not abandon them.

[18] I will crack open rivers on the bare heights, springs in the valleys. I will turn the wilderness into a lake, dry ground into bubbling streams.

[19] In the desert I will plant cedar, acacia, myrtle, olive. In the wasteland I will set cypress, pine, and box tree all together —

[20] so that everyone will see and know, consider and understand: the hand of the LORD did this. The Holy One of Israel created it.

[21] Bring your case forward, the LORD says. State your arguments, the King of Jacob says.

[22] Bring them out and let them tell us what's coming. Tell us what already happened — what was the meaning of it? — and we'll think it over and see how it landed. Or announce what's still to come.

[23] Tell us what the future holds, and then we'll know you are gods. Do something — anything — good or bad — so we can stand here amazed and afraid together.

[24] Look at you — you are nothing. Your work is less than nothing. Anyone who picks you is disgusting.

[25] I roused one from the north, and he comes. From the rising of the sun he calls on my name. He tramples rulers like mortar, like a potter kneading clay.

[26] Who announced this from the beginning so we'd know? Who said it ahead of time so we could say, "He was right"? Nobody announced it. Nobody said a word. Nobody heard you say anything at all.

[27] I was the first to tell Zion, "Look — here they are." I am the one who sent Jerusalem the messenger of good news.

[28] I look around — no one. Among them, not one counselor. I ask them a question, and they have nothing to say.

[29] All of them — fraud. Their works — empty air. Their cast images — wind and chaos.

[1] Look — my servant, the one I hold up, my chosen, the one I delight in. I've put my Spirit on him. He'll bring justice to the nations.

[2] He won't shout. He won't raise his voice. He won't make a scene in the streets.

[3] A bruised reed — he won't snap it. A wick barely glowing — he won't snuff it out. Faithfully, he'll bring justice into the open.

[4] He won't burn out. He won't be crushed. Not until he's set justice in the earth itself — and the distant coastlands wait for his instruction.

[5] This is what God says — the LORD, the one who created the skies and stretched them out, who hammered out the earth and everything that grows from it, who breathes life into its people and spirit into everyone who walks on it:

[6] I, the LORD, called you in righteousness. I'll grip your hand. I'll guard you. I'm making you a covenant for the people, a light for the nations —

[7] to open eyes that cannot see, to bring prisoners out of the dungeon, to free those sitting in the dark.

[8] I am the LORD. That is my name. I don't hand my glory to anyone else. I don't share my praise with carved idols.

[9] The first things — they've happened. Now I'm telling you new things, before they even break the surface. Listen.

[10] Sing a new song to the LORD — sing his praise from the ends of the earth. You who go down to the sea and everything in it, you coastlands and everyone who lives on them.

[11] Let the desert and its towns lift their voice. Let the villages of Kedar shout. Let the people of Sela sing for joy — let them cry out from the mountaintops.

[12] Let them give glory to the LORD. Let his praise ring across the islands.

[13] The LORD strides out like a warrior. Like a soldier he stirs up his fury. He raises the war cry. He roars. He overpowers his enemies.

[14] For a long time I held my tongue. I stayed silent, holding it all in. But now — now I cry out like a woman in labor. I gasp. I pant.

[15] I'll lay waste to mountains and hills. I'll dry up every green thing on them. I'll turn rivers into islands. I'll drain the pools.

[16] I'll lead the blind down a road they don't know. I'll guide them on paths they've never walked. I'll turn the darkness in front of them into light. I'll smooth out the rough places. These are the things I'll do — and I will not abandon them.

[17] But those who trust in carved images, who say to molten figures, "You're our gods" — they'll be driven back, drowned in shame.

[18] You deaf ones — listen! You blind ones — look up and see!

[19] Who's blind like my servant? Who's deaf like the messenger I sent? Who's blind like the one I've made my own — blind like the servant of the LORD?

[20] You've seen so much, but you take in nothing. Your ears are wide open, but you hear nothing.

[21] The LORD wanted, for the sake of his own righteousness, to make his instruction great and glorious.

[22] But look at this people — robbed, plundered, all of them trapped in pits, hidden away in prisons. They're prey, and nobody rescues them. They're loot, and nobody says, "Give them back."

[23] Who among you will hear this? Who will listen close for what comes next?

[24] Who handed Jacob over to be plundered? Who turned Israel over to the robbers? Wasn't it the LORD — the one we sinned against, the one whose ways we refused to walk in, whose instruction we wouldn't obey?

[25] So he poured out his burning anger on us. He poured out the violence of war. It set us on fire all around — and we still didn't understand. It burned us — and we still didn't take it to heart.

[1] But now — listen to what the LORD says, the One who made you, Jacob, the One who shaped you, Israel: Don't be afraid. I've bought you back. I've called you by name. You are mine.

[2] When you wade through deep water, I'll be there. When you cross rivers, they won't sweep you under. When you walk through fire, you won't be burned — the flames won't even singe you.

[3] Because I am the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel, your rescuer. I handed over Egypt as your ransom, traded Cush and Seba for your life.

[4] That's how precious you are to me. That's how much I love you — enough to give whole nations in exchange for you, whole peoples for your breath.

[5] Don't be afraid. I'm with you. I'll bring your children from the east, gather them from the west.

[6] I'll tell the north: Hand them over. And the south: Don't hold them back. Bring my sons from the far edges, my daughters from the ends of the earth —

[7] every soul who carries my name, whom I created for my glory, whom I shaped and made.

[8] Bring out the people who have eyes but cannot see, ears but cannot hear.

[9] Let every nation gather. Let all the peoples assemble. Who among their gods predicted this? Who announced what was coming? Let them produce their witnesses to prove it — let people hear and say, "Yes, it's true."

[10] You are my witnesses, says the LORD — you and my servant, the one I chose. So you would know me. So you would trust me. So you would finally understand: I am the One. No god was formed before me. None will come after.

[11] I — I alone — am the LORD. There is no rescuer apart from me.

[12] I declared it. I delivered. I announced it — not some foreign god among you. You are my witnesses, says the LORD, that I am God.

[13] From the first day onward, I am the One. No one can snatch anything from my hand. When I act, who can reverse it?

[14] This is what the LORD says — your redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: For your sake I'm sending judgment to Babylon. I'll bring them all down as refugees, even the Chaldeans, whose pride rode in their ships.

[15] I am the LORD, your Holy One, the One who created Israel, your King.

[16] This is what the LORD says — the One who carved a road through the sea, a path through raging water,

[17] who marched out chariots and horses, army and warrior together, and they fell — they never rose again, extinguished, snuffed like a wick:

[18] Stop dwelling on what came before. Stop replaying ancient history.

[19] Look — I'm doing something new. It's already breaking through. Can't you see it? I'm cutting a road through the wilderness, opening rivers in the wasteland.

[20] Wild animals will honor me — jackals and ostriches — because I'm pouring out water in the desert, rivers in the dry country, to give my chosen people something to drink.

[21] These are the people I shaped for myself, so they could sing my praise.

[22] But you didn't call on me, Jacob. You grew tired of me, Israel.

[23] You didn't bring me your sheep for burnt offerings. You didn't honor me with your sacrifices. I never crushed you with demands for grain offerings, never wore you out asking for incense.

[24] You didn't spend silver to buy me sweet cane. You didn't drench me with the fat of your sacrifices. Instead, you crushed me with your sins. You wore me out with your guilt.

[25] And yet — I am the One, I myself, who wipes your rebellions away for my own sake. I will not remember your sins.

[26] Help me remember, then. Let's go to court together. Make your case. Show me you're in the right.

[27] Your first father sinned. Your spokesmen rebelled against me.

[28] So I disgraced the officials of the sanctuary. I handed Jacob over to destruction, gave Israel up to scorn.

[1] But now listen, Jacob, my servant — Israel, the one I chose.

[2] This is what the LORD says — the One who made you, who shaped you in the womb, who will help you: Don't be afraid, Jacob, my servant. Jeshurun, the one I chose.

[3] I will pour water on the thirsty ground and streams across the parched earth. I will pour my Spirit on your children, my blessing on your descendants.

[4] They will spring up like grass in a well-watered meadow, like willows along flowing streams.

[5] One will say, "I belong to the LORD." Another will take the name of Jacob. Another will write on his hand, "Property of the LORD," and take Israel as his title of honor.

[6] This is what the LORD says — Israel's King, Israel's Redeemer, the LORD of Armies: I am the first and I am the last. There is no God but me.

[7] Who is like me? Let him speak up. Let him lay it out and make his case — everything from the moment I founded this ancient people. Let him announce what's coming, what hasn't happened yet.

[8] Don't tremble. Don't be afraid. Haven't I been telling you all along, declaring it from way back? You are my witnesses. Is there any God besides me? No — there is no other Rock. I know of none.

[9] Every idol-maker is empty, and the precious things they treasure are worthless. Their own witnesses see nothing, know nothing — and they'll be humiliated for it.

[10] Who would shape a god, cast a metal image, that gains him nothing?

[11] Look — every craftsman behind it will be shamed. They're only human. Let them all gather and stand together — they'll cower in terror and be disgraced together.

[12] The blacksmith takes his tool, works the coals, hammers the metal into shape with his strong arm. He gets hungry and his strength fails. He doesn't drink water and he grows faint.

[13] The carpenter stretches a measuring line, marks the wood with chalk, shapes it with chisels, scribes it with calipers. He carves it into the form of a man — a beautiful human figure, fit to live in a shrine.

[14] He cuts down cedars — or maybe selects a cypress or an oak — letting it grow strong among the trees of the forest. He plants a pine, and the rain makes it grow.

[15] Then a man uses some of it for fuel. He warms himself with part of it, bakes bread over it. From the same wood he makes a god and worships it — fashions an idol and bows down to it.

[16] Half he burns in the fire. Over that half he roasts meat, eats his fill, warms himself and says, "Ah, I'm warm. I can feel the fire."

[17] And from what's left — he makes a god, his idol. He kneels before it, worships it, prays to it: "Save me — you're my god."

[18] They don't get it. They don't understand. Their eyes are sealed shut, so they cannot see. Their minds are closed, so they cannot grasp it.

[19] Not one of them stops to think. None of them has the knowledge or the sense to say, "Half of this wood I burned in the fire. I baked bread on its coals. I roasted meat and ate. And now I'm going to make the leftover scrap into something detestable? Bow down to a block of wood?"

[20] He's feeding on ashes. A deluded heart has led him off course. He can't save himself, can't even ask, "Isn't this thing in my hand a lie?"

[21] Remember this, Jacob — Israel, you are my servant. I shaped you. You are my servant. Israel, I will not forget you.

[22] I have wiped away your rebellion like a cloud burned off by the sun, your sins like morning mist. Come back to me — I have redeemed you.

[23] Sing, you heavens — the LORD has done it! Shout, you depths of the earth! Burst into song, you mountains — you forests and every tree in them! For the LORD has redeemed Jacob. In Israel he shows his glory.

[24] This is what the LORD says — your Redeemer, who shaped you in the womb: I am the LORD, the maker of everything. I stretched out the heavens by myself. I spread out the earth — who was there to help me?

[25] I undo the signs of the fortune-tellers and make fools of the diviners. I overturn the wisdom of the wise and turn their knowledge into nonsense.

[26] I confirm the word of my servant and bring my messengers' plans to pass. I say of Jerusalem, "She will be inhabited again," and of Judah's towns, "They will be rebuilt — I'll raise up their ruins."

[27] I say to the deep, "Dry up — I'll drain your rivers."

[28] I say of Cyrus, "He is my shepherd. He will carry out everything I want." I say of Jerusalem, "She will be rebuilt." Of the temple: "Its foundations will be laid."

[1] This is what the LORD says to his anointed, to Cyrus — whose right hand I have grasped to flatten nations before him, to strip kings of their armor, to throw open doors ahead of him so no gate stands shut:

[2] I will march out in front of you. I will level every ridge. I will shatter the bronze gates and snap the iron bars.

[3] I will hand you the treasures hoarded in darkness, the riches stashed in secret places — so you will know it is I, the LORD, the God of Israel, who calls you by name.

[4] For the sake of Jacob my servant, for Israel my chosen, I summoned you by name. I gave you a title of honor — and you did not even know me.

[5] I am the LORD. There is no other. There is no God besides me. I armed you, though you did not know me —

[6] so that from the rising of the sun to the place it sets, everyone would know there is no one but me. I am the LORD. There is no other.

[7] I form light and I create darkness. I make peace and I create disaster. I, the LORD, do all of this.

[8] Rain down, you heavens, from above. Let the skies pour righteousness. Let the earth crack open and let salvation sprout, let righteousness spring up alongside it. I, the LORD, have made it grow.

[9] Trouble waits for the one who picks a fight with his Maker — a clay shard squabbling with the shards in the dirt. Does the clay say to the potter, "What are you making?" Does what you've shaped say, "He has no hands"?

[10] Trouble waits for the one who says to his father, "What are you fathering?" — or to his mother, "What are you laboring to bring forth?"

[11] This is what the LORD says — the Holy One of Israel, the one who shaped him: Are you really going to interrogate me about my children? Are you going to give me orders about the work of my hands?

[12] I made the earth. I created the human beings on it. My own hands stretched out the heavens, and I commanded every star in their ranks.

[13] I have raised him up in righteousness. I will straighten every road in front of him. He will rebuild my city and set my exiles free — not for a price, not for a bribe. The LORD of Hosts has spoken.

[14] This is what the LORD says: Egypt's wealth, Cush's profit, the tall Sabeans — they will come over to you. They will be yours. They will walk behind you in chains. They will bow down before you and plead: "God is with you, with you alone. There is no other God."

[15] Truly, you are a God who hides himself — God of Israel, Savior.

[16] The idol-makers will all be humiliated, disgraced together, marched off into shame, every last one of them.

[17] But Israel — Israel will be saved by the LORD with a salvation that has no end. You will never be humiliated. You will never be disgraced. Not now. Not ever.

[18] This is what the LORD says — the one who created the heavens, who is God; who shaped the earth and made it, who fixed it in place; who did not create it to be a wasteland but formed it to be inhabited: I am the LORD. There is no other.

[19] I did not speak in secret, from some dark corner of the earth. I did not tell Jacob's descendants, "Seek me in chaos." I am the LORD. I speak what is right. I declare what is true.

[20] Gather. Come. Draw near, you survivors of the nations. They know nothing — those who haul around their wooden idols, who pray to a god that cannot save.

[21] Speak up. Make your case. Take counsel together. Who announced this long ago? Who declared it back then? Was it not I, the LORD? There is no God besides me — a just God and a Savior. There is no one but me.

[22] Turn to me and be saved, every last corner of the earth — for I am God, and there is no other.

[23] By myself I have sworn it. From my mouth has gone out a word of righteousness that will not return: every knee will bend before me, every tongue will swear allegiance.

[24] They will say of me, "Only in the LORD are righteousness and strength." Everyone who raged against him will come to him, and they will be ashamed.

[25] In the LORD all of Israel's offspring will be vindicated, and in him they will boast.

[1] Bel buckles. Nebo bows. Their idols ride away on the backs of beasts and cattle — the gods you carried in parade now a dead weight, a burden hauled off by exhausted animals.

[2] Down they go, bowing together, unable to save the cargo. They themselves head into captivity.

[3] Listen to me, house of Jacob — every last remnant of Israel. I have carried you since the womb, lifted you since before you drew breath.

[4] Even when you are old, I am the one. When your hair turns white, I am still carrying you. I made you. I will bear you. I will carry and I will rescue.

[5] Who will you compare me to? Who is my equal? Who looks anything like me?

[6] Look at them — pouring gold from the purse, weighing silver on the scale, hiring a craftsman to hammer it into a god. Then they fall down. They worship it.

[7] They hoist it on their shoulders, lug it around, set it in its spot — and there it stands. It does not move. Cry out to it and it answers nothing. It cannot save anyone from anything.

[8] Remember this. Stand firm. Take it to heart, you rebels.

[9] Remember what came before — the ancient things. I am God. There is no other. I am God and nothing matches me.

[10] I announce the ending from the beginning. From long ago I declare what has not yet happened. I say: my plan stands. Everything I want, I do.

[11] I am calling a bird of prey from the east — the man of my purpose from a far country. I said it. I will bring it. I shaped it. I will do it.

[12] Listen to me, you stubborn hearts, you who think rescue is far off —

[13] I am bringing my rescue close. It is not far. My salvation will not be delayed. I am placing salvation in Zion, my splendor on Israel.

[1] Down. Get down in the dust, virgin daughter Babylon. Sit on the bare ground — there's no throne for you anymore, daughter of the Chaldeans. They'll never call you soft and delicate again.

[2] Pick up the millstone. Grind the flour. Pull off your veil, hike up your skirts, bare your legs, wade through the rivers.

[3] Your nakedness will be exposed, your shame laid open. I'm taking vengeance, and no one is going to stop me.

[4] Our Redeemer — the LORD of Armies is his name — the Holy One of Israel.

[5] Sit there in silence. Crawl into the dark, daughter of the Chaldeans. They'll never again call you Queen of Kingdoms.

[6] I was furious with my people. I dishonored my own inheritance. I handed them over to you — and you showed them no mercy. Even on the old, you laid your iron yoke without lifting it.

[7] You told yourself, "Forever — I'll reign forever." You never took it to heart, never saw what was coming, never thought about how this ends.

[8] So listen now, you lover of luxury, lounging in your false security, telling yourself, "I am, and there is no one else. I'll never be a widow. I'll never lose my children."

[9] Both are coming for you. In a single day. In a single instant — widowhood and the loss of your children. They will hit you in full force, despite all your spells, despite the staggering power of your enchantments.

[10] You trusted in your wickedness. You said, "Nobody sees me." Your wisdom and your knowledge — they twisted you, and you told yourself, "I am, and there is no one else."

[11] But disaster is coming, and you won't know how to charm it away. Calamity is going to crash down on you, and no ransom will buy you out. Devastation will hit suddenly — you won't see it coming.

[12] Go on, then — stand your ground with your spells, with the legion of enchantments you've labored over since you were young. Maybe they'll work. Maybe you'll scare something off.

[13] You've worn yourself out on advice. Let them stand up, then — your sky-watchers, your stargazers, the ones who carve up the heavens month by month predicting what's coming. Let them save you.

[14] Look at them — straw. Fire takes them. They can't even save themselves from the flame. This isn't a cozy hearth to sit by. This isn't a fire to warm your hands.

[15] That's what they are to you — the ones you've slaved for, the ones you've trafficked with since your youth. Each one stumbles off in his own direction. No one is left to save you.

[1] Listen to this, house of Jacob — you who go by the name Israel, who came out of Judah's womb, who swear by the name of the LORD and invoke the God of Israel, but not in truth, not with integrity.

[2] You call yourselves citizens of the holy city, you lean on the God of Israel — the LORD of armies is his name.

[3] The earlier events — I announced them long ago. They came out of my mouth, I made them known. Then suddenly I acted, and they happened.

[4] I knew how stubborn you were — neck of iron, forehead of bronze.

[5] So I told you in advance. Before any of it happened, I made sure you heard it — so you couldn't say, "My idol did this. My carved god, my cast-metal image — they're the ones who ordered it."

[6] You've heard it all. Now look at it — every word. Will you not admit it? From this moment forward, I'm telling you new things — secrets you knew nothing about.

[7] They're being created right now, not long ago. Until today you'd heard nothing of them — so you can't say, "Oh, I already knew that."

[8] You never heard it. You never knew it. Your ear was never open. I know exactly how treacherous you are — "rebel" has been your name since you were born.

[9] For the sake of my own name I hold back my anger. For the sake of my reputation I restrain it — I will not cut you off.

[10] Look — I refined you, but not as silver. I tested you in the furnace of suffering.

[11] For my own sake, for my own sake, I act. Why would I let my name be dragged through the mud? My glory I will not hand to another.

[12] Listen to me, Jacob — Israel, the one I called. I am he. I am the first. I am the last.

[13] My hand laid the foundations of the earth. My right hand stretched out the heavens. I call to them, and they stand up together.

[14] Gather, all of you, and listen. Which of your idols predicted any of this? The LORD loves him — he will accomplish what the LORD wants against Babylon, his arm against the Chaldeans.

[15] I — I spoke. I called him. I brought him. He will succeed in what he came to do.

[16] Come close. Listen to this. From the beginning I have not spoken in secret. From the moment it began, I was there. And now the Lord GOD has sent me — me and his Spirit.

[17] This is what the LORD says — your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: I am the LORD your God, the one who teaches you what works, who guides you down the road you should walk.

[18] If only you had listened to my commands — your peace would have rolled like a river, your righteousness like waves on the sea.

[19] Your descendants would have been like sand, your offspring like its uncountable grains. Their name would never have been erased, never wiped from before me.

[20] Get out of Babylon! Run from the Chaldeans! Shout it with joy, broadcast it, send it to the ends of the earth — say: The LORD has redeemed his servant Jacob!

[21] They didn't go thirsty when he led them through the wastelands. He made water gush from the rock for them — he split the rock, and water poured out.

[22] There is no peace, says the LORD, for the wicked.

[1] Listen to me, you islands. Pay attention, peoples far away. The LORD called me before I was born. While I was still in my mother's womb, he spoke my name.

[2] He made my mouth a blade — sharp, lethal — and hid me in the shadow of his hand. He shaped me into a polished arrow and tucked me away in his quiver.

[3] He said to me: "You are my servant, Israel — through you I will display my splendor."

[4] But I said: "I've worn myself out for nothing. Everything I've spent, spent on emptiness, on wind." Yet my case rests with the LORD, and my reward is with my God.

[5] Now the LORD speaks — the one who shaped me in the womb to be his servant, to bring Jacob back to him, to gather Israel home. I carry weight in the LORD's eyes; my God has become my strength.

[6] He says: "It's too small a thing for you to be my servant only to restore the tribes of Jacob and bring back the survivors of Israel. I'm making you a light to the nations — so my salvation reaches the ends of the earth."

[7] This is what the LORD says — Redeemer of Israel, his Holy One — to the one despised, to the one nations loathe, to the servant of tyrants: "Kings will see you and stand up. Princes will see you and bow down. Because of the LORD, who is faithful — the Holy One of Israel, who has chosen you."

[8] This is what the LORD says: "At the right moment I answered you. On the day of rescue I helped you. I will guard you and make you a covenant for the people — to restore the land, to hand out the inheritances that were lost,

[9] to tell prisoners, 'Come out,' and those locked in darkness, 'Show yourselves.' They'll graze along the roads; even the bare hills will become their pasture.

[10] They won't go hungry or thirsty. The desert heat and the burning sun won't strike them down. The one who has compassion on them will lead them, guiding them to springs of water.

[11] I will turn all my mountains into roads, and my highways will rise up.

[12] Look — they're coming from far away, some from the north, some from the west, some from the land of Sinim."

[13] Sing, heavens! Celebrate, earth! Mountains, break into song! Because the LORD has comforted his people. He's tender with the broken ones.

[14] But Zion said: "The LORD has abandoned me. The Lord has forgotten I exist."

[15] Can a mother forget the baby at her breast? Can she feel nothing for the child she carried? Even if she could — I will never forget you.

[16] Look — I have engraved you on the palms of my hands. Your walls are always before me.

[17] Your children are racing back. The ones who tore you down, who left you in ruins — they're walking away.

[18] Lift your eyes. Look around. They're all gathering, coming to you. As surely as I live, declares the LORD — you will wear them like jewels, fasten them on like a bride.

[19] Your wastelands, your ruins, your devastated country — soon you'll be too cramped for everyone living there, and the ones who devoured you will be far away.

[20] The children born during your grief will whisper in your ear: "This place is too tight. Make room — I need space to live."

[21] And you'll think: "Who gave birth to all these for me? I was bereaved, barren — exiled, cast off. Who raised them? I was left alone. Where did they come from?"

[22] This is what the Lord GOD says: "Watch — I will lift my hand to the nations, raise my signal to the peoples. They will bring your sons in their arms, carry your daughters on their shoulders.

[23] Kings will be your foster fathers. Their queens will nurse you. They'll bow before you, faces to the ground, and lick the dust off your feet. Then you'll know I am the LORD — and those who wait for me are never put to shame."

[24] Can plunder be torn from a warrior? Can captives be rescued from a tyrant?

[25] Yes — this is what the LORD says: "Even the warrior's captives will be taken. Even the tyrant's plunder will be rescued. I will fight whoever fights you. I will save your children.

[26] I'll make your oppressors eat their own flesh. They'll be drunk on their own blood as if it were wine. Then everyone alive will know that I, the LORD, am your Savior — your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob."

[1] This is what the LORD says: Where is your mother's divorce papers — the certificate I supposedly wrote when I sent her away? Which of my creditors did I sell you to? Look at the record. You were sold because of your own corruption. Your mother was sent off because of your own rebellion.

[2] Why was no one here when I came? Why did no one answer when I called? Is my arm too short to rescue? Do I lack the power to save? Watch — one rebuke from me and the sea goes dry, rivers turn to wasteland, their fish rot for lack of water and die of thirst.

[3] I dress the heavens in mourning black and drape them in sackcloth.

[4] The Sovereign LORD has given me the tongue of someone trained — words that sustain the exhausted. Morning after morning he wakes me. He wakes my ear to listen like a student.

[5] The Sovereign LORD opened my ears, and I did not resist. I did not turn away.

[6] I gave my back to the ones who beat me, my cheeks to the ones who ripped out my beard. I did not hide my face from their mockery and spit.

[7] But the Sovereign LORD helps me — so I am not destroyed by their shame. I have set my face like flint. I know I will not be put to shame.

[8] The one who vindicates me is close. Who dares accuse me? Let us face each other in court. Who is my adversary? Step forward.

[9] Look — the Sovereign LORD himself stands with me. Who can condemn me? Every one of them will wear out like a garment; moths will eat them.

[10] Anyone here who fears the LORD, who listens to the voice of his servant — anyone walking through darkness with no light to see by — trust the name of the LORD. Lean into your God.

[11] But all of you who light your own fires, who walk by the torches you made — go on, walk by your own flame, by the sparks you struck. Here is what you get from my hand: you will lie down in agony.

[1] Listen to me, you who chase after righteousness, you who hunt for the LORD. Look at the rock you were cut from, the quarry you were dug out of.

[2] Look at Abraham, your father. Look at Sarah, who bore you. He was one man when I called him — and I blessed him, and I multiplied him.

[3] So the LORD will comfort Zion. He will comfort every ruin of her. He will turn her wilderness into Eden, her desert into the garden of the LORD. Joy and gladness will be found there — thanksgiving, and the sound of singing.

[4] Pay attention to me, my people. Listen, my nation. Instruction will go out from me, and my justice — I will set it up as a light for the peoples.

[5] My righteousness is on its way. My salvation has gone out. My arms will judge the nations. The coastlands wait for me. They put their hope in my arm.

[6] Lift your eyes to the sky. Look at the earth beneath you. The heavens will dissolve like smoke. The earth will wear out like a garment. Its inhabitants will die like gnats. But my salvation will stand forever. My righteousness will not break.

[7] Listen to me, you who know what righteousness is — you, the people with my instruction written on your heart. Do not fear the contempt of mortals. Do not crumble at their insults.

[8] The moth will eat them like wool. The worm will devour them like cloth. But my righteousness stands forever. My salvation runs through every generation.

[9] Wake up! Wake up! Put on your strength, arm of the LORD! Wake up like the old days, like the generations long gone. Were you not the arm that hacked Rahab to pieces, that ran the dragon through?

[10] Were you not the arm that dried up the sea, the waters of the great deep — that carved a road through the seabed for the redeemed to cross?

[11] The ransomed of the LORD will come home. They will enter Zion singing. Joy will crown their heads — joy that never ends. Gladness and joy will overtake them. Sorrow and sighing will run for their lives.

[12] I am the one who comforts you. I am. So who are you, that you should be afraid of a mortal who dies — of a human being who is grass?

[13] You have forgotten the LORD who made you, who stretched out the heavens, who laid the foundations of the earth. And every day, all day, you tremble at the fury of the oppressor as he sets himself to destroy. But where is the fury of the oppressor now?

[14] The prisoner bent low will soon be released. He will not die in the pit. His bread will not run out.

[15] I am the LORD your God — the one who churns the sea so its waves roar. The LORD of Armies is my name.

[16] I have put my words in your mouth. I have hidden you in the shadow of my hand — I, who stretched out the heavens and laid the foundations of the earth, who say to Zion: You are my people.

[17] Wake up! Wake up! Get on your feet, Jerusalem — you who have drunk from the LORD's hand the cup of his fury, who have drained the goblet of staggering down to the dregs.

[18] Of all the sons she bore, not one to guide her. Of all the sons she raised, not one to take her by the hand.

[19] Two disasters have struck you — who will grieve for you? Devastation and destruction. Famine and sword. Who will comfort you?

[20] Your sons lie unconscious at every street corner, like antelope tangled in a net — full to the brim with the fury of the LORD, with the rebuke of your God.

[21] So listen to this, you afflicted one — drunk, but not from wine.

[22] This is what your Lord says — the LORD your God, who fights for his people: Look. I am taking the cup of staggering out of your hand. The goblet of my fury — you will never drink from it again.

[23] I am putting it into the hand of those who tormented you — those who said to you, Bow down so we can walk over you. And you made your back the ground. You made yourself the street they walked on.

[1] Wake up, wake up, Zion — put on your strength. Slip into your beautiful clothes, Jerusalem, holy city. The uncircumcised and the unclean will never set foot in you again.

[2] Shake off the dust. Rise. Take your throne, Jerusalem. Loosen the chains around your neck, captive daughter of Zion.

[3] This is what the Lord says: You were sold for nothing, and you'll be bought back without money.

[4] This is what the Sovereign Lord says: Long ago my people went down to Egypt to live there as foreigners. Then Assyria crushed them for no reason at all.

[5] And now — what do I have here? My people hauled off for nothing. Their rulers howl in triumph. My name is dragged through the dirt all day long.

[6] So my people will know my name. On that day they'll understand — it is I who speaks. Here I am.

[7] How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of the messenger running with good news — peace announced, salvation declared, telling Zion: Your God reigns.

[8] Listen — your watchmen lift their voices, shouting together for joy. With their own eyes they see it: the Lord coming home to Zion.

[9] Burst into song together, ruins of Jerusalem — the Lord has comforted his people, he has redeemed Jerusalem.

[10] The Lord has bared his holy arm in front of every nation, and every corner of the earth will see the salvation of our God.

[11] Leave. Leave. Get out of there. Touch nothing unclean. Come out from among them. Purify yourselves, you who carry the vessels of the Lord.

[12] But you won't leave in a panic, you won't go as fugitives — because the Lord walks ahead of you, and the God of Israel guards your back.

[13] Look — my servant will succeed. He will rise, be lifted up, exalted beyond measure.

[14] Just as crowds were horrified at the sight of him — his face so disfigured it barely looked human, his form scarcely belonging to a son of man —

[15] so he will startle many nations. Kings will clamp their mouths shut in his presence. What they were never told, they will see. What they never heard, they will finally understand.

[1] Who believed what we heard? Who saw the arm of the LORD revealed in this?

[2] He grew up in front of him like a tender shoot, like a root pushing out of parched ground. Nothing about him turned heads. No royal bearing. No magnetism to pull us in.

[3] Despised. Rejected. A man acquainted with pain, on a first-name basis with suffering. The kind of face people look away from. We counted him as nothing.

[4] But it was *our* pain he carried. *Our* suffering he hauled on his own back. And we — we looked at him and decided God must be punishing him, that he had it coming.

[5] We were wrong. He was pierced for what *we* did. Crushed for what *we* twisted. The discipline that bought our peace fell on him. His wounds — those are what healed us.

[6] All of us wandered off like sheep, each one charting his own crooked path. And the LORD piled it all — every last sin of every last one of us — on him.

[7] He was tortured, and he took it. He never opened his mouth. Like a lamb walked to slaughter, like a sheep silent before the shears — he said nothing.

[8] They dragged him through a rigged trial and hauled him off, and who in his generation even noticed? He was cut out of the land of the living, struck down for the rebellion of my people.

[9] They gave him a grave with criminals — and a rich man's tomb when he died — though he had done no violence and never spoke a lie.

[10] And yet — it was the LORD's will to crush him, to break him with grief. When his life is made a guilt offering, he will see his offspring, his days will stretch on, and the LORD's purpose will succeed in his hands.

[11] After the anguish of his soul, he will see the light and be satisfied. By what he knows, my righteous servant will set many right — because he is the one who shouldered their guilt.

[12] So I will give him a portion among the great, and he will divide the spoils with the strong — because he poured out his life, all the way to death. He let himself be counted with criminals. He carried the sin of multitudes and stood in the gap for rebels.

[1] Sing, barren woman — you who never bore a child. Break out in song, shout for joy — you who never went into labor. Because the abandoned woman will have more children than the married one, says the LORD.

[2] Stretch your tent wider. Pull the curtains out. Don't hold back. Lengthen your ropes. Drive your stakes deep.

[3] You're about to spill out in every direction. Your children will take over nations and resettle the ruined cities.

[4] Don't be afraid — you won't be humiliated. Don't flinch — you won't be shamed. You'll forget the disgrace of your youth. The shame of your widowhood will fade from memory.

[5] Because your Maker is your husband — the LORD of armies is his name. The Holy One of Israel is your redeemer — the God of all the earth, that's what they'll call him.

[6] The LORD called you back like a wife abandoned and broken — a wife married young, then cast off, says your God.

[7] "For a brief moment I left you. With overflowing compassion I'll bring you home.

[8] In a flash of anger I hid my face from you for an instant. But with everlasting love I will have mercy on you," says the LORD, your redeemer.

[9] "This is like the days of Noah to me. I swore then that the waters of Noah would never again drown the earth. So now I swear I will not be angry with you, will not rebuke you again.

[10] The mountains may walk away. The hills may collapse. But my faithful love will never leave you. My covenant of peace will never break," says the LORD, who has compassion on you.

[11] Battered city, storm-tossed, never comforted — look: I'm setting your stones in turquoise. I'm laying your foundations with sapphires.

[12] I'll make your battlements of rubies, your gates of sparkling jewels, every wall of yours from precious stones.

[13] All your children will be taught by the LORD, and great will be their peace.

[14] You will be established in righteousness. Tyranny will be far from you — you'll have nothing to fear. Terror will be far from you — it will never come near.

[15] If anyone moves against you, it won't be from me. Whoever attacks you will fall on their own face.

[16] Look — I created the blacksmith who fans the coals into flame and forges weapons for their trade. And I created the destroyer who lays waste.

[17] No weapon shaped against you will succeed. Every tongue that rises to accuse you in court — you will silence. This is the inheritance of the LORD's servants, and their vindication comes from me. This is the declaration of the LORD.

[1] Hey — everyone who's thirsty, come to the water. You without a coin in your pocket, come. Buy and eat. Come, buy wine and milk — no money, no price. Free.

[2] Why do you spend your wages on what isn't bread? Why pour out your labor for what never satisfies? Listen to me — really listen — and you'll eat what's good. Your soul will feast on richness.

[3] Lean in. Come close. Hear me, and you'll live. I'm cutting a covenant with you that lasts forever — the unbreakable mercies promised to David.

[4] I made him a witness to the nations, a leader, a commander of peoples —

[5] and now you will summon nations you've never heard of, and nations that never knew you will come running to you, because of the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel — because he has made you radiant.

[6] Seek the LORD while he can still be found. Call on him while he's close.

[7] Let the wicked walk away from their road. Let the schemer abandon his schemes. Let him turn back to the LORD — who will pour mercy on him — to our God, whose forgiveness runs deep and wide.

[8] My thoughts are not your thoughts. My ways are not your ways — this is the LORD speaking.

[9] As high as the sky stretches above the ground, that's how far my ways tower over yours, my thoughts above your thoughts.

[10] Rain comes down. Snow falls. They don't go back up until they've soaked the earth, made it bloom, made it sprout, given seed to the sower and bread to the hungry.

[11] That's how my word works. It leaves my mouth and it doesn't come back empty. It accomplishes what I sent it to do. It hits the target.

[12] You're going to walk out of here in joy. You're going to be led out in peace. The mountains and hills will burst into song right in front of you. Every tree in the field will clap its hands.

[13] Where the thornbush grew — a cypress. Where the briar choked the ground — a myrtle. And this will be a monument to the LORD, a sign that never gets erased, never fades.

[1] This is what the LORD says: Hold to justice. Do what is right. My rescue is on its way; my deliverance is about to break.

[2] Blessed is anyone who lives this out — anyone who grips it and won't let go: who keeps the Sabbath unprofaned, who keeps their hand clean of evil.

[3] And let no foreigner who has bound himself to the LORD say, "The LORD will surely cut me off from his people." And let no eunuch say, "I'm nothing but a dried-up tree."

[4] Because this is what the LORD says to the eunuchs who keep my Sabbaths, who choose what pleases me, who hold fast to my covenant:

[5] Inside my house, inside my walls, I will give them a monument and a name better than sons and daughters. I will give them a name that lasts forever — a name death cannot erase.

[6] And the foreigners who attach themselves to the LORD — to serve him, to love his name, to be his — every one who keeps the Sabbath unprofaned and holds fast to my covenant —

[7] these I will bring to my holy mountain and fill with joy in my house of prayer. Their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be welcome on my altar — because my house will be called a house of prayer for all peoples.

[8] This is the declaration of the Sovereign LORD, who gathers Israel's scattered: I have more to gather still, beyond those already gathered.

[9] Come and feast, every beast of the field — come and devour, every beast of the forest!

[10] Israel's watchmen are blind, every one of them. They see nothing. They're mute dogs — can't even bark. Dreaming, sprawled out, in love with sleep.

[11] Greedy dogs, never satisfied. Shepherds with no understanding — all of them have turned their own way, each one chasing his own profit to the last drop.

[12] "Come on," they say, "let me grab some wine. Let's drink ourselves stupid. And tomorrow — tomorrow will be the same, only better."

[1] The righteous die and no one takes it to heart. The faithful are swept away and no one understands — that the righteous are pulled from the path of evil, [2] taken into peace. They rest on their beds, those who walked the straight road.

[3] But you — come here, you children of the witch, you offspring of the adulterer and the whore. [4] Who are you mocking? At whom do you stretch your mouth wide and stick out your tongue? Aren't you the rebellion's children, the seed of the lie — [5] burning with lust under every leafy tree, slaughtering your own children in the riverbeds, beneath the cracks in the rocks?

[6] Your portion is the smooth stones of the wadi — yes, those are your inheritance. To them you've poured out your offerings, to them you've raised your grain. Should I be soothed by any of this? [7] On a high and towering mountain you spread your bed, and up there you climbed to make your sacrifice. [8] Behind the door, behind the doorpost, you set your shameless memorial. You stripped naked and climbed in, far from me, and made your bed wide. You bought their company. You loved their bodies. You stared at their nakedness. [9] You decked yourself out for the king with oil, layered on the perfumes. You sent your envoys to the ends of the earth — you descended even to Sheol itself.

[10] You wore yourself out on the long road, but you never said, "It's hopeless." You kept finding fresh strength in your hand, so you never collapsed.

[11] Whom did you dread, whom did you fear, that you should lie — that you forgot me, that you never gave me a thought? Is it because I've held my peace, held it for ages, that you no longer fear me? [12] Let me expose your "righteousness" and all your "deeds" — they won't help you. [13] When you cry out, let your collection of idols rescue you. The wind will carry them all off. A breath will sweep them away.

But the one who takes refuge in me will inherit the land and possess my holy mountain.

[14] And the voice says: Build up, build up, clear the road, lift every obstacle out of my people's way.

[15] For this is what the High and Lifted One says — the One who lives in eternity, whose name is Holy: I dwell in the high and holy place, and also with the crushed and lowly of spirit, to revive the spirit of the lowly, to revive the heart of the crushed.

[16] I will not accuse forever. I will not stay angry without end. The breath would fail before me — the souls I myself made. [17] His greedy sin enraged me. I struck him, hid my face, burned with anger — and he just kept walking the way his own heart chose.

[18] I have seen his ways. And I will heal him. I will lead him. I will fill him and those who mourn for him with comfort, [19] creating praise on the lips. Peace, peace — to the far and to the near, says the LORD — and I will heal him.

[20] But the wicked are like a churning sea that cannot rest, whose waters dredge up mud and filth. [21] There is no peace, says my God, for the wicked.

[1] Shout it out — don't hold back. Lift your voice like a war trumpet. Confront my people with what they've done. Tell the house of Jacob the truth about their rebellion.

[2] Day after day they come looking for me. They act like they want to know my ways — like a nation that actually does what's right, that hasn't walked away from its God's standards. They ask me for verdicts that favor them. They love the feeling of God drawing near.

[3] "Why have we fasted," they ask, "and you don't notice? Why have we humbled ourselves and you don't even register it?" Here's why: on the very day you fast, you're chasing your own agenda and squeezing every worker under you.

[4] Your fasting ends in arguments and brawls, throwing punches with vicious fists. The way you're fasting today will never get your voice heard on high.

[5] Is this the kind of fast I'm looking for? A day for a person to perform self-denial? Bowing your head like a wilted reed? Sprawling in sackcloth and ashes? You call that a fast? You call that a day the LORD welcomes?

[6] This is the fast I want: tear off the chains of injustice. Snap the yoke-straps. Set the crushed go free. Smash every yoke.

[7] Break your bread for the hungry. Take the homeless poor into your house. When you see someone naked, clothe them. Don't hide from your own flesh and blood.

[8] Then your light will burst through like dawn breaking. Your healing will come fast. Your righteousness will lead the way; the glory of the LORD will guard your back.

[9] Then you'll call, and the LORD will answer. You'll cry for help, and he'll say, *Here I am.* If you get the yoke out of your midst — the pointing finger, the poisonous talk —

[10] if you pour yourself out for the hungry and fill the gut of the afflicted, then your light will rise in the darkness, and your midnight will turn to noon.

[11] The LORD will guide you without fail. He'll satisfy you in scorched places and put iron in your bones. You'll be a watered garden, a spring whose waters never lie.

[12] Your people will rebuild the ancient ruins. You'll raise foundations laid generations ago. They'll call you Repairer of Broken Walls, Restorer of Streets Where People Live.

[13] If you keep your foot off the Sabbath — stop running your own business on my holy day — if you call the Sabbath a delight, the LORD's holy day honorable, and honor it by not doing your usual thing, not chasing your own interests, not talking shop —

[14] then you'll find your joy in the LORD. I'll lift you up over the high places of the earth. I'll feed you the inheritance of Jacob your father. The mouth of the LORD has spoken it.

[1] Look — the LORD's arm isn't too short to save. His ear isn't too dull to hear. [2] It's your crimes that have driven a wedge between you and your God. Your sins have made him turn his face away. He won't listen.

[3] Your hands are stained with blood. Your fingers drip with guilt. Your lips spew lies. Your tongue mutters poison. [4] Nobody files an honest lawsuit. Nobody pleads a case in good faith. They rely on empty arguments and speak fraud. They conceive trouble. They give birth to ruin.
