[5] They hatch snake eggs and weave spider webs. Eat one of those eggs and you die. Crack one open and a viper slithers out. [6] Their webs make no clothing — you can't cover yourself in what they spin. Their work is violence. Their hands are full of it.

[7] Their feet sprint toward evil. They race to spill innocent blood. Their thoughts churn out destruction. Wreckage and ruin are their roadmap. [8] They don't know the road to peace. There's no justice in the ruts they've worn. They've twisted every path they walk — anyone following them never finds peace.

[9] So justice stays far from us. Righteousness never catches up. We hoped for light — only darkness. We waited for dawn — and walked in shadow. [10] We grope along walls like the blind, feeling our way like men with no eyes. We stumble at noon as if it were dusk. Among the strong, we're already dead. [11] We growl like bears. We moan like doves. We strain for justice — there is none. For rescue — and it stays out of reach.

[12] Our defiance against you piles high. Our sins testify against us in open court. Our betrayals follow us everywhere. We know what we've done. [13] Treason against the LORD. Denial. Turning our backs on our God. Plotting oppression. Stirring up rebellion. Conceiving lies in our hearts and pushing them out through our mouths.

[14] So justice gets shoved aside. Righteousness stands at a distance. Truth has collapsed in the public square. Honesty can't even find a way in. [15] Truth has gone missing. Anyone who refuses evil becomes prey.

The LORD saw it. It disgusted him — that justice had vanished. [16] He saw that no one would step up. He was stunned that no one would intervene. So his own arm brought the rescue. His own righteousness held him steady.

[17] He strapped on righteousness like body armor. He set salvation on his head like a helmet. He wrapped himself in vengeance and pulled zeal around him like a cloak. [18] He will repay according to what they've done — fury to his enemies, payback to his foes. To the distant coastlands, he will deliver their due.

[19] From the west they will fear the name of the LORD. From the rising sun they will tremble at his glory. He comes like a river in flood, driven by the breath of the LORD.

[20] A Redeemer is coming to Zion — to those in Jacob who turn from rebellion. That's the LORD's word.

[21] "As for me, this is my covenant with them," says the LORD. "My Spirit, which rests on you, and my words, which I've placed in your mouth — they will not leave your mouth, or your children's mouths, or your grandchildren's mouths. Not now. Not ever." That's what the LORD says.

[1] Get up. Shine. Your light has come, and the glory of the LORD is rising over you like the sun. [2] Look — darkness blankets the earth, thick night wraps the nations, but over you the LORD breaks like dawn, and his glory becomes visible on you. [3] Nations will travel toward your light. Kings will come to the brightness of your sunrise.

[4] Lift your eyes. Look around. They are gathering — all of them — coming home to you. Your sons arrive from far away, your daughters carried on the hip. [5] You will see it and your face will glow, your heart will pound and swell, because the wealth of the sea will pour into your lap, the riches of nations will arrive at your door. [6] A flood of camels will cover your land — young camels from Midian and Ephah, caravans from Sheba, every one of them loaded with gold and frankincense, every one of them announcing the praise of the LORD. [7] Every flock of Kedar will be gathered to you, the rams of Nebaioth will be at your service, climbing my altar as welcome offerings — and I will make my glorious house even more glorious.

[8] Who are these, drifting in like a cloud, like doves homing to their roosts? [9] The coastlands are waiting for me. The ships of Tarshish lead the way, bringing your children from far off, their silver and gold with them, all for the name of the LORD your God — the Holy One of Israel — because he has made you beautiful.

[10] Foreigners will rebuild your walls. Their kings will serve you. I struck you in my anger, yes — but in my favor I have shown you mercy. [11] Your gates will stand open day and night and never close, so that the wealth of nations can be brought in, with their kings led in procession. [12] Any nation or kingdom that refuses to serve you will perish — those nations will be ruined, completely.

[13] The glory of Lebanon will come to you — cypress, pine, and box tree together — to beautify the place of my sanctuary, and I will make the place where my feet rest magnificent. [14] The sons of those who crushed you will come bowing at your feet. Everyone who scorned you will fall down at your soles and call you the City of the LORD, Zion of the Holy One of Israel.

[15] Once you were abandoned and hated, with no one passing through. I will make you the pride of the ages, the joy of every generation. [16] You will drink the milk of nations, nurse at the breasts of kings — and you will know that I, the LORD, am your Savior, your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob.

[17] Instead of bronze I will bring you gold. Instead of iron, silver. Instead of wood, bronze. Instead of stones, iron. I will appoint peace as your overseer and righteousness as your taskmaster. [18] No more reports of violence in your land, no more wreckage and ruin inside your borders. You will name your walls Salvation and your gates Praise.

[19] You will not need the sun for daylight anymore, nor the moon to light your nights. The LORD himself will be your everlasting light. Your God will be your glory. [20] Your sun will never set again. Your moon will never wane. The LORD will be your everlasting light, and your days of mourning will be finished.

[21] Your people — all of them — will be righteous. They will inherit the land forever, the shoot I planted with my own hands, the work that puts my glory on display. [22] The smallest will become a thousand. The least, a mighty nation. I am the LORD. When the time comes, I will make it happen — fast.

[1] The Spirit of the Sovereign LORD is on me — because the LORD has anointed me. He sent me to carry good news to the wrecked and the poor, to bind up the ones whose hearts are in pieces, to announce liberation to captives and the prison doors flung open for those locked in the dark.

[2] To declare the year the LORD says yes — and the day our God settles accounts. To comfort everyone who mourns.

[3] To give the grieving of Zion something better than ash: a crown. Oil of joy instead of funeral weeping. A cloak of praise instead of a spirit collapsed in despair. And they will be called oaks of righteousness — planted by the LORD himself, to show off his glory.

[4] They will rebuild what has been in ruins for ages. They will raise up what fell generations ago. The shattered cities, abandoned for centuries — they will restore them all.

[5] Foreigners will tend your flocks. Outsiders will work your fields and your vineyards.

[6] But you — you will be called priests of the LORD. Ministers of our God, they will name you. You will feast on the wealth of nations. Their splendor will be yours to inherit.

[7] Instead of your shame, a double portion. Instead of disgrace, you will shout over your inheritance. In your own land you will own twice what you lost — joy that never ends will be yours.

[8] Because I — the LORD — love justice. I hate robbery and wrong. I will pay them in full, faithfully. I will cut an everlasting covenant with them.

[9] Their descendants will be known among the nations. Their children among the peoples. Everyone who sees them will recognize it: these are the people the LORD has blessed.

[10] I will sing my heart out over the LORD. My whole soul will dance in my God. Because he has dressed me in the clothes of salvation, wrapped me in a robe of righteousness — like a bridegroom crowned like a priest, like a bride decked out in her jewels.

[11] As the earth pushes up its green shoots, as a garden makes its seeds break open and grow — that's how the Sovereign LORD will make righteousness break open, and praise rise up, in front of every nation watching.

[1] For Zion's sake I will not shut my mouth. For Jerusalem's sake I will not rest — not until her vindication breaks like dawn, not until her rescue blazes like a torch in the dark.

[2] The nations will see your righteousness. Every king will see your glory. And you'll be called by a new name — a name spoken by the mouth of the LORD himself.

[3] You will be a crown of beauty in the hand of the LORD, a royal diadem in the palm of your God.

[4] No one will call you Abandoned anymore. No one will call your land Desolate. You'll be named My Delight Is in Her, and your land will be called Married — because the LORD delights in you, and your land has a husband now.

[5] As a young man marries a young woman, so your sons will marry you. As a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so your God will rejoice over you.

[6] On your walls, Jerusalem, I have stationed watchmen. Day and night, they will never fall silent. You who call on the LORD — take no rest,

[7] and give him no rest, until he establishes Jerusalem and makes her the praise of all the earth.

[8] The LORD has sworn it — by his right hand, by his mighty arm: Never again will I hand your grain to your enemies for food. Never again will foreigners drink the wine you sweated to make.

[9] The ones who harvest it will eat it and praise the LORD. The ones who gather the grapes will drink it in the courts of my sanctuary.

[10] Move! Move through the gates! Clear the road for the people. Build it up, build it up — pull out every stone. Lift a banner for the nations to see.

[11] Listen — the LORD has sent word to the ends of the earth: Tell Daughter Zion, Look — your Savior is coming. His reward is with him. His recompense walks ahead of him.

[12] And they will be called The Holy People, The Redeemed of the LORD. And you — you will be called Sought After, A City No Longer Forsaken.

[1] Who is this coming from Edom, robes stained crimson from Bozrah? Who is this, splendid in his garments, striding forward in the fullness of his strength?

"It is I — speaking justice, mighty to save."

[2] Why is your clothing red? Why do your garments look like someone who has been treading grapes?

[3] "I trampled the winepress alone. From the nations, no one was with me. I trampled them in my fury, ground them down in my wrath. Their lifeblood splashed my robes; I stained every garment I wore.

[4] The day of vengeance was burning in my heart, and the year of redemption had come.

[5] I looked — no one to help. I was stunned — no one to hold me up. So my own arm brought the victory. My own fury sustained me.

[6] I trampled the peoples in my rage, broke them apart in my wrath, poured their lifeblood out on the ground."

[7] I will tell of the loyal love of the LORD — his praises, everything he has done for us, the flood of goodness he poured out on the house of Israel from his deep compassion, his unfailing mercy.

[8] He said, "Surely they are my people, children who will not betray me." And he became their Savior.

[9] In every moment of their distress, he was distressed. The angel of his presence rescued them. In his love and pity he redeemed them. He lifted them, carried them, all the days of long ago.

[10] But they rebelled. They grieved his Holy Spirit. So he became their enemy — he himself fought against them.

[11] Then his people remembered the old days — the days of Moses. Where is the one who brought them up out of the sea with the shepherds of his flock? Where is the one who set his Holy Spirit among them,

[12] who sent his glorious arm to march at Moses' right hand, who split the waters before them to make himself an everlasting name,

[13] who led them through the depths? Like a horse galloping across open country, they did not stumble.

[14] Like cattle going down to a quiet valley, the Spirit of the LORD gave them rest. This is how you led your people — and made for yourself a name of splendor.

[15] Look down from heaven. See — from your high and holy and beautiful throne. Where is your zeal? Where is your power? The stirring of your heart, your compassion toward me — held back. Why?

[16] You are our Father. Abraham doesn't know us; Israel doesn't claim us. You, LORD, you are our Father, our Redeemer from ancient days — that is your name.

[17] LORD, why do you let us wander from your ways? Why do you harden our hearts so we no longer fear you? Come back — for the sake of your servants, the tribes that belong to you.

[18] For a little while your holy people possessed the land. Then our enemies trampled your sanctuary into the dust.

[19] We have become like people you never ruled, like those who were never called by your name.

[1] If only you would tear the heavens open and come down — mountains buckling at the sight of you, [2] the way fire devours brushwood, the way fire makes water boil — come, make your name known to your enemies, make the nations tremble at your face. [3] When you did the unthinkable, things we never saw coming, you came down, and the mountains melted before you.

[4] From the beginning of time no ear has heard, no eye has seen any god but you — a God who acts on behalf of those who wait for him. [5] You meet the one who delights in doing right, the one who remembers your ways. But look — you were angry, and we kept sinning. We have been at this so long. Can we still be saved?

[6] We have all become like something unclean, and every righteous deed of ours is a filthy rag. We wither like leaves, and our sins, like wind, sweep us away. [7] Nobody calls on your name. Nobody rouses themselves to grab hold of you. You hid your face from us. You handed us over to our own corruption.

[8] And yet — Lord, you are our Father. We are the clay, you are the potter. We are all the work of your hand. [9] Don't stay furious, Lord. Don't hold our sins against us forever. Look at us — please — we are your people, all of us.

[10] Your holy cities have become wilderness. Zion is wilderness. Jerusalem — a wasteland. [11] Our holy and beautiful house, where our ancestors praised you — burned to the ground. Everything we treasured lies in ruins.

[12] After all this, Lord — will you hold yourself back? Will you stay silent and crush us beyond bearing?

[1] I made myself available to people who never asked for me. I let myself be found by people who never went looking. To a nation that never called my name, I said: Here I am. Here I am.

[2] All day long I stretched out my hands to a stubborn people — people walking a road that leads nowhere good, chasing their own ideas,

[3] a people who insult me to my face, nonstop — sacrificing in their backyard gardens, burning incense on bricks like it means something,

[4] crouching in graveyards, holding all-night vigils in secret places, eating pig flesh, broth of unclean things simmering in their pots,

[5] then telling everyone else, "Stay back — don't come near me, I'm holier than you." These people are smoke in my nostrils, a fire that burns all day.

[6] Look — it's written down in front of me. I won't stay quiet. I will pay it back. I will pay it back in full, right into their laps —

[7] your sins and your ancestors' sins together, says the Lord. They burned incense on the mountains, mocked me on the hills — so I will measure out exactly what their conduct earned, straight into their laps.

[8] This is what the Lord says: When you find good juice still in a cluster, someone says, "Don't destroy it — there's a blessing in there." That's how I'll handle my servants. I won't destroy everything.

[9] I will bring offspring out of Jacob, an heir to my mountains out of Judah. My chosen ones will inherit the land. My servants will live there.

[10] Sharon will be pastureland for flocks again. The Valley of Achor will be a resting place for cattle — for my people who searched for me.

[11] But as for you — you who abandoned the Lord, who forgot my holy mountain, who set tables for the god of Luck and filled cups for the god of Fate —

[12] I will assign you to the sword. All of you will kneel for the slaughter. Because I called and you didn't answer. I spoke and you didn't listen. You did what I hate. You chose what I refuse.

[13] So here is what the Lord God says: Look — my servants will eat, and you will go hungry. My servants will drink, and you will go thirsty. My servants will celebrate, and you will be humiliated.

[14] My servants will sing from the joy in their hearts, and you will scream from heartbreak, howl from a shattered spirit.

[15] Your name will become a curse my chosen ones spit when they swear. The Lord God will kill you. But his servants — he will give them a new name.

[16] Whoever blesses himself on earth will bless himself by the God of truth. Whoever takes an oath on earth will swear by the God of truth. Because the old griefs are forgotten. They are hidden from my eyes.

[17] Look — I am creating new heavens and a new earth. The former things will not be remembered. They won't even cross the mind.

[18] So rejoice. Be wildly glad forever in what I am creating. Look — I am creating Jerusalem as joy itself, her people as delight.

[19] I will rejoice over Jerusalem. I will take delight in my people. The sound of weeping will not be heard in her anymore, the sound of crying — gone.

[20] No more infants who only live a few days. No more elders who don't get to finish their years. A person who dies at a hundred will be considered young. Anyone who falls short of a hundred will be considered cursed.

[21] They will build houses and actually live in them. They will plant vineyards and actually eat the fruit.

[22] They won't build for someone else to move in. They won't plant for someone else to eat. My people will live as long as trees live. My chosen ones will wear out what their hands have made.

[23] They won't work for nothing. They won't bring children into the world for catastrophe. They are the offspring of the Lord's blessing — them and their descendants together.

[24] Before they call, I'll answer. While they're still talking, I'll hear.

[25] Wolf and lamb will graze side by side. The lion will eat straw like an ox. And the serpent? Dust will be its food. Nothing will harm. Nothing will destroy on my entire holy mountain — says the Lord.

[1] This is what the LORD says: The sky is my throne. The earth is the stool beneath my feet. So what house could you possibly build for me? What place could ever contain my rest?

[2] My own hand made all of this. Every last thing exists because I willed it. But here is who catches my eye — the one bowed low, the one whose spirit is shattered, the one who trembles at my word.

[3] Slaughter a bull? You might as well murder a man. Sacrifice a lamb? You might as well break a dog's neck. Bring a grain offering? It reeks like pig's blood. Burn incense in tribute? You're praising an idol. You chose your own roads. Your souls feast on what disgusts me.

[4] So I will choose too. I'll choose the harsh treatment for you. I'll bring down on you the very thing that terrifies you. Because I called and nobody answered. I spoke and nobody listened. You did what I hate. You picked what I refuse.

[5] Listen — you who tremble at his word: Your own brothers despise you. They cast you out for the sake of my name. They sneer, "Let the LORD reveal his glory, then, so we can watch you celebrate." But they are the ones who will be shamed.

[6] A sound — listen — uproar from the city, a voice thundering from the temple. It is the voice of the LORD repaying his enemies what they have earned.

[7] Before she even felt the first pang, she gave birth. Before the contractions hit, she delivered a son.

[8] Who has ever heard of such a thing? Who has ever seen it? Can a country be born in a single day? Can a nation come forth in one moment? But the instant Zion's labor began, she brought her children into the world.

[9] Would I bring a child to the brink of birth and then refuse to deliver? says the LORD. Would I open the womb and slam it shut? says your God.

[10] Celebrate with Jerusalem! Rejoice for her, all of you who love her. Let your joy run wild — all of you who once mourned over her.

[11] So you can nurse and be filled at her comforting breast. So you can drink deep and delight in her glorious abundance.

[12] Because this is what the LORD says: I am rerouting peace toward her like a river, the glory of the nations like a flooding stream. You will nurse there. You will be carried on her hip. You will be bounced on her knees.

[13] The way a mother comforts her child — that is how I will comfort you. In Jerusalem you will find your comfort.

[14] You will see this and your heart will leap. Your bones will bloom like fresh grass. The LORD's hand will be known by his servants — and his fury, by his enemies.

[15] Look — the LORD comes wrapped in fire, his chariots a hurricane, to release his anger in full heat and his rebuke in flames of fire.

[16] With fire the LORD will execute judgment. With his sword he will judge all flesh. The slain of the LORD will be many.

[17] Those who consecrate themselves and purify themselves to slip into the gardens, following the one in the middle, eating pig's flesh and vermin and rats — they will all meet their end together. This is the LORD's word.

[18] I know what they do. I know what they think. The hour is coming to gather every nation and every language. They will come, and they will see my glory.

[19] I will set a sign among them. I will send the survivors to the nations — to Tarshish, to Put and Lud (the famous archers), to Tubal and Javan, to the distant coastlands that have never heard a whisper of my name or seen a flicker of my glory. They will proclaim my glory to the nations.

[20] And they will bring all your brothers and sisters back from every nation as an offering to the LORD — on horses, in chariots, in wagons, on mules, on camels — to my holy mountain, Jerusalem, says the LORD. They'll bring them the way the Israelites bring grain offerings in clean vessels to the house of the LORD.

[21] And from among them — yes, from these foreigners — I will take some to be priests and Levites, says the LORD.

[22] Because just as the new heavens and the new earth I am making will endure before me — this is the LORD's word — so your name and your descendants will endure.

[23] And it will happen — every new moon, every Sabbath, all humanity will come to bow before me, says the LORD.

[24] Then they will walk out and look at the corpses of those who rebelled against me. Their worm will not die. Their fire will not go out. They will be a horror to all flesh.

[1] James — slave of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ — to the twelve tribes scattered across the nations: greetings.

[2] Brothers and sisters, when life ambushes you with trials of every kind, count it pure joy. [3] You know what's happening: the testing of your faith is forging endurance in you. [4] Let that endurance finish its work, so you emerge mature, complete, lacking nothing.

[5] If any of you is short on wisdom, ask God — he hands it out freely, without making you feel small for asking — and it will be given. [6] But ask in faith, with no inner double-mindedness. The doubter is a wave at sea, whipped and tossed by the wind. [7] That kind of person should not expect to receive anything from the Lord — [8] divided in mind, unstable in every step.

[9] Let the believer with nothing boast in being lifted high, [10] and the rich one boast in being brought low — because wealth withers like a wildflower. [11] The sun rises, the heat scorches, the bloom falls, the beauty is gone. So it is with the rich, fading mid-stride through their busy lives.

[12] Blessed is the one who endures testing. Having stood the trial, that person will receive the crown of life — promised to all who love him.

[13] When tempted, no one should say, "God is tempting me." God cannot be tempted by evil, and he tempts no one. [14] Every person is tempted when their own appetite drags them off and baits the hook. [15] Then desire conceives and gives birth to sin; and sin, fully grown, breeds death.

[16] Don't be deceived, dear brothers and sisters. [17] Every good gift, every perfect gift, comes down from above — from the Father of lights, in whom there is no flicker, no shifting shadow. [18] He chose to birth us by the word of truth, making us the firstfruits of all he has made.

[19] Hold this close, my dear friends: be quick to listen, slow to speak, slow to anger — [20] because human rage never produces the righteousness of God. [21] So strip off every moral filth and the runoff of evil, and welcome with humility the implanted word that has the power to save your souls.

[22] Become doers of the word, not just hearers who con themselves. [23] Anyone who hears the word but doesn't act on it is like someone who studies their face in a mirror — [24] glances at themselves, walks away, and immediately forgets what they look like. [25] But the one who leans in and stays with the perfect law, the law that sets people free — not a forgetful listener but an active doer — that person will be blessed in everything they do.

[26] If anyone thinks they're religious but can't bridle their tongue, they're fooling themselves. Their religion is worthless. [27] Pure religion, untainted before God the Father, looks like this: caring for orphans and widows in their distress, and refusing to let the world stain you.

[1] My brothers and sisters, if you actually believe in our glorious Lord Jesus the Messiah, stop playing favorites.

[2] Picture this: a man walks into your gathering wearing gold rings and designer clothes. Right behind him, a poor man shuffles in wearing rags.

[3] You fawn over the well-dressed one — "Here, take this seat of honor" — while you tell the poor man, "Stand over there," or "Sit on the floor by my feet."

[4] Do you see what you've done? You've split people into categories and made yourselves judges driven by twisted motives.

[5] Listen to me, brothers and sisters I love: hasn't God deliberately chosen the poor of this world to be rich in faith and to inherit the kingdom he promised to those who love him?

[6] But you — you humiliate the poor. Meanwhile, isn't it the rich who crush you, who drag you into court,

[7] who sneer at the beautiful name you bear?

[8] Now, if you actually live out the royal law that Scripture spells out — *Love your neighbor as yourself* — you're doing it right.

[9] But the moment you show favoritism, you're sinning. The law itself convicts you as a lawbreaker.

[10] Because whoever keeps the whole law but trips on a single point is guilty of breaking all of it.

[11] The same God who said "Do not commit adultery" also said "Do not murder." Skip the adultery but commit murder, and you're still a lawbreaker.

[12] So speak and act like people who will be judged by the law of freedom.

[13] Because judgment falls without mercy on anyone who refused to show mercy. Mercy laughs in judgment's face.

[14] What good is it, brothers and sisters, if someone claims to have faith but has nothing to show for it? Can that kind of faith save anyone?

[15] Imagine a brother or sister with no clothes on their back and no food on the table.

[16] And one of you says, "Hey, God bless — stay warm, eat well!" — but you give them nothing. What good is that?

[17] That's faith for you: alone, with no action behind it. Dead. Stone-cold dead.

[18] Someone will object: "You have faith, I have works." Fine — show me your faith without the works. I'll show you my faith *by* my works.

[19] You believe God is one? Good for you. The demons believe that too — and they shudder.

[20] Do you want proof, you empty-headed person, that faith without works is useless?

[21] Wasn't our father Abraham declared righteous by what he did when he laid his son Isaac on the altar?

[22] You see — his faith was working alongside his actions, and his faith was completed by what he did.

[23] That's when the Scripture came true: *Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness* — and he was called God's friend.

[24] So you see: a person is justified by what they do, not by faith alone.

[25] Same with Rahab the prostitute. Wasn't she justified by what she did — taking in the spies, sending them out a different way?

[26] A body without breath is a corpse. Faith without works is the same thing.

[1] Not many of you should rush to become teachers, my brothers. Understand this: those of us who teach will face a stricter reckoning.

[2] We all stumble — constantly, in countless ways. But the one who never trips over their words? That's a complete human being, capable of bridling the whole body.

[3] Slip a bit into a horse's mouth and you steer the entire animal wherever you want.

[4] Or take ships: massive vessels driven by violent winds, yet a tiny rudder turns them wherever the pilot decides.

[5] The tongue works the same way — a small thing that boasts enormous power. Think how a tiny spark sets a whole forest ablaze.

[6] The tongue is fire. A whole world of corruption planted right inside us. It stains the entire body, torches the course of our existence, and the flame itself is lit straight from hell.

[7] Humans have tamed — and continue to tame — every species: beasts, birds, reptiles, sea creatures.

[8] But the tongue? No human can tame it. It's a restless evil, brimming with lethal poison.

[9] With it we bless the Lord and Father, and with the same mouth we curse people made in God's image.

[10] Out of the same opening pours blessing and cursing. My brothers, this should not be happening.

[11] Does a spring gush fresh water and brackish water from the same source?

[12] Can a fig tree produce olives, my brothers? Can a grapevine bear figs? Neither can a salt spring give fresh water.

[13] Who among you is wise and understanding? Prove it. Show it by the way you live — actions marked by the gentleness that wisdom breeds.

[14] But if you're harboring bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your heart, don't strut around claiming wisdom. You're lying against the truth.

[15] That kind of "wisdom" doesn't come down from above. It's earthbound, animal, demonic.

[16] Wherever jealousy and selfish ambition take root, you get chaos and every kind of rotten thing.

[17] But the wisdom that comes from above? First of all, it's pure. Then peaceable, considerate, willing to yield, overflowing with mercy and good fruit, undivided, free of pretense.

[18] And those who plant peace will harvest a crop of righteousness from seeds sown in peace.

[1] Where do the fights come from? Where do the feuds among you come from? Right here — from the appetites that wage war inside you. [2] You crave, and you have nothing. You kill and burn with envy, and you still can't get what you want. You brawl, you battle — and you have nothing, because you never ask. [3] And when you do ask, you don't receive, because you're asking with rotten motives — wanting only to spend it on your pleasures.

[4] Adulterers. Don't you understand that flirting with the world means declaring war on God? Anyone who decides to be the world's friend has made themselves God's enemy. [5] Or do you think Scripture speaks for nothing when it says the Spirit God planted in us yearns for us with a fierce, jealous love? [6] And he gives more grace still. That's why it says: God blocks the arrogant, but pours grace on the humble.

[7] So surrender to God. Stand your ground against the devil, and he will run. [8] Come close to God, and he will come close to you. Clean your hands, sinners. Purify your hearts, you double-souled. [9] Be miserable. Mourn. Weep. Let your laughter turn to grief and your celebration to sorrow. [10] Bow low before the Lord, and he will lift you high.

[11] Stop slandering each other, brothers and sisters. Whoever tears down a brother or judges a brother tears down the law and judges the law. And if you're judging the law, you're not keeping it — you've installed yourself as its judge. [12] There is only one Lawgiver and Judge — the one who can save and destroy. Who do you think you are, judging your neighbor?

[13] Now listen, you who say, "Today or tomorrow we're heading to this city or that one, spending a year there, running our business, turning a profit" — [14] you don't even know what tomorrow holds. What is your life? A puff of mist, here for a moment, then gone. [15] What you should be saying is: "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that." [16] Instead, you swagger around boasting about your plans. All such boasting is evil. [17] So then: anyone who knows the good they ought to do, and doesn't do it — that's sin.

[1] Now a word for you, the rich: weep. Wail over the misery rushing toward you. [2] Your wealth has rotted. Your fine clothes are moth-eaten rags. [3] Your gold and silver are corroded, and that corrosion will testify against you — it will eat your flesh like fire. You stockpiled treasure in the final hour. [4] Listen — the wages you cheated out of the workers who mowed your fields are crying out. The cries of the harvesters have reached the ears of the Lord of Heaven's Armies. [5] You lived in luxury here on earth, you indulged every appetite — you fattened your own hearts for the day of slaughter. [6] You condemned the righteous one, you murdered him, and he offered no resistance.

[7] So be patient, brothers and sisters, until the Lord's return. Think of the farmer waiting for the precious harvest from the soil — patient through the early rains and the late rains. [8] Be patient like that. Hold your ground. The Lord's coming is close. [9] Stop grumbling against each other, brothers and sisters, or you'll be the ones judged. The Judge is standing right at the door.

[10] When you need a model of endurance under suffering, look at the prophets who spoke in the name of the Lord. [11] We call them blessed because they held on. You've heard about Job's perseverance, and you've seen how the Lord brought it to its end — because the Lord is overflowing with compassion and mercy.

[12] Above everything, brothers and sisters, do not swear oaths — not by heaven, not by earth, not by anything. Let your yes be yes and your no be no. Otherwise you fall under judgment.

[13] Is anyone among you suffering? Pray. Is anyone cheerful? Sing. [14] Is anyone among you sick? Call the elders of the church. Let them pray over the person and anoint them with oil in the name of the Lord. [15] The prayer offered in faith will save the one who is sick, and the Lord will raise them up. And if they have sinned, they will be forgiven. [16] So confess your sins to one another, and pray for one another, so that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person is powerful — it gets things done. [17] Elijah was a human being just like us. He prayed, fervently, that it would not rain — and for three and a half years not a drop fell. [18] Then he prayed again, and the sky opened, and the earth produced its harvest.

[19] Brothers and sisters, if any of you wanders from the truth and someone brings them back, [20] understand this: whoever turns a sinner from the wrong road saves that soul from death and covers a multitude of sins.

[1] These are the words of Jeremiah son of Hilkiah, one of the priests at Anathoth in the territory of Benjamin.

[2] The word of the LORD came to him in the thirteenth year of King Josiah of Judah, son of Amon,

[3] and kept coming through the days of King Jehoiakim son of Josiah, all the way to the end of the eleventh year of King Zedekiah son of Josiah — the year Jerusalem was dragged off into exile in the fifth month.

[4] The word of the LORD came to me:

[5] "Before I shaped you in the womb, I knew you. Before you drew your first breath, I set you apart. I appointed you a prophet to the nations."

[6] I said, "No — Lord GOD, look at me. I don't know how to speak. I'm just a boy."

[7] But the LORD said: "Don't tell me you're just a boy. You will go wherever I send you. You will say whatever I command you.

[8] Don't be afraid of their faces. I am with you. I will pull you out of every fire." This is the LORD's declaration.

[9] Then the LORD reached out his hand and touched my mouth. He said, "There. I have placed my words in your mouth.

[10] Look — today I am setting you over nations and over kingdoms: to uproot and to tear down, to destroy and to overthrow, to build and to plant."

[11] The word of the LORD came to me: "Jeremiah, what do you see?" I said, "I see the branch of an almond tree."

[12] The LORD said, "You've seen it right. I am watching over my word to make sure it happens."

[13] The word of the LORD came again: "What do you see?" I said, "I see a boiling pot, tipping toward us from the north."

[14] And the LORD said: "From the north — that's where disaster will boil over onto everyone living in this land.

[15] I am calling up every clan of every kingdom in the north" — the LORD's declaration. "They will come. Each one will set his throne at the gates of Jerusalem, against her walls on every side, against every town in Judah.

[16] I will pronounce my verdict on them for all their evil — for abandoning me, for burning incense to other gods, for bowing down to things their own hands made.

[17] So pull yourself together. Stand up. Tell them everything I command you. Don't crumble in front of them, or I will crumble you in front of them.

[18] Look — today I am making you a fortified city, an iron pillar, a wall of bronze against the whole land: against the kings of Judah, its officials, its priests, the people of the land.

[19] They will fight you. They will not win. I am with you" — the LORD's declaration — "and I will pull you out."

[1] The word of the LORD hit me:

[2] "Go. Shout this in Jerusalem's streets:

This is what the LORD says—

I remember you. The devotion of your youth. The love of your wedding days. How you followed me into the wilderness, into a land nobody plants.

[3] Israel was set apart for the LORD then—the firstfruits of his harvest. Anyone who tried to consume her was found guilty. Disaster swallowed them. I declare it."

[4] Listen to the LORD, house of Jacob, every clan of Israel.

[5] This is what the LORD says:

What did your fathers find wrong with me that they wandered so far away? They chased after nothing—and became nothing themselves.

[6] They never asked, "Where is the LORD who hauled us up out of Egypt, who led us through the wasteland—through canyons of sand and pits of darkness, through a land no one crosses, where no one lives?"

[7] I brought you into a country of orchards and vineyards, to feast on its fruit and its richness. And you walked in and defiled it. You took my inheritance and made it disgusting.

[8] The priests never asked, "Where is the LORD?" The legal scholars didn't know me. The shepherds turned on me. The prophets prophesied by Baal and chased gods that cannot save.

[9] So I'm bringing charges against you, says the LORD. Charges against your children's children too.

[10] Cross the sea to Cyprus and look. Send word to Kedar and study it carefully. See if anything like this has ever happened—

[11] Has a nation ever traded its gods? (And those weren't even gods.) But my people have traded their Glory for what is worthless.

[12] Heavens, recoil at this. Shudder. Be utterly appalled, declares the LORD.

[13] My people have committed two crimes: They abandoned me—the spring of living water—and dug their own cisterns. Cracked cisterns. Cisterns that hold nothing.

[14] Is Israel a slave? Was he born into servitude? Then why has he been plundered?

[15] Young lions roar over him. They growl and snarl. They've reduced his land to wasteland. His cities are burned out, empty.

[16] Even the men of Memphis and Tahpanhes have shaved your skull bare.

[17] You brought this on yourself—abandoning the LORD your God while he was leading you down the road.

[18] So what are you doing now, running to Egypt to drink from the Nile? What are you doing in Assyria, drinking from the Euphrates?

[19] Your own wickedness will discipline you. Your faithlessness will indict you. Understand this and feel it: it is a bitter, evil thing to abandon the LORD your God, to have no fear of me, says the Lord, the LORD of Armies.

[20] Long ago you snapped your yoke, ripped off your harness, and said, "I will not serve!" Then on every high hill, under every leafy tree, you sprawled out like a prostitute.

[21] I planted you as a choice vine, pure stock, the best seed. How did you mutate into a wild, rotten vine I don't even recognize?

[22] Scrub yourself with lye, use all the soap you want—the stain of your guilt is still there in front of me, declares the Lord GOD.

[23] How can you say, "I'm not defiled. I haven't run after the Baals"? Look at your tracks in the valley. Face what you've done. You're a young camel in heat, running every direction—

[24] a wild donkey at home in the desert, sniffing the wind in her lust. Who can rein her in? No male has to wear himself out chasing her—when she's ready, they'll find her.

[25] Save your feet from going barefoot. Save your throat from going dry. But you said, "It's pointless. I love these foreigners. I'm going after them."

[26] Like a thief humiliated when he's caught—that's how the house of Israel will be humiliated. They, their kings, their officials, their priests, their prophets,

[27] saying to a block of wood, "You're my father," and to a stone, "You gave birth to me." They've turned their backs on me, not their faces. But when trouble comes, they cry, "Get up! Save us!"

[28] Where are these gods you made for yourself? Let them get up—if they can save you when trouble comes! You've got as many gods, Judah, as you've got towns.

[29] Why argue with me? You've all rebelled against me, declares the LORD.

[30] I disciplined your children for nothing. They wouldn't take correction. Your own sword devoured your prophets like a ravenous lion.

[31] You, this generation—pay attention to the word of the LORD:

Have I been a desert to Israel? A land of suffocating darkness? Then why do my people say, "We're free to roam—we're done coming to you"?

[32] Does a young woman forget her jewelry? A bride her wedding sash? But my people have forgotten me—days beyond counting.

[33] How skilled you've become at chasing love. Even the worst women could learn from you.

[34] And on your clothes—the blood of the innocent poor. You didn't catch them breaking in. It's just there, on everything.

[35] And still you say, "I'm innocent. His anger has surely turned away from me." Watch me bring you to trial for saying, "I have not sinned."

[36] How casually you switch your loyalties. Egypt will shame you the way Assyria already did.

[37] You'll leave that place too, hands on your head in grief—because the LORD has rejected the ones you trusted. They will not help you.

[1] Here's how it works: if a man divorces his wife and she leaves him and marries another, does he ever take her back? Wouldn't that defile the land beyond all reckoning? But you — you've played the whore with a hundred lovers, and now you want to come home to me? This is the LORD speaking.

[2] Look up at the barren heights. Is there a single spot where you haven't been mounted? You sat by the roadsides waiting for them like a bandit in the desert, and you poisoned the land with your endless harlotry and evil.

[3] So I held back the rains. The spring showers never came. But you had a prostitute's forehead — incapable of shame, immune to embarrassment.

[4] And still you call out to me, "My father! You've been my friend since I was young —

[5] You won't stay angry forever, will you? You won't hold the grudge for good?" That's how you talk. And then you go right back to doing every evil thing you can manage.

[6] During the reign of King Josiah, the LORD said to me: Did you see what faithless Israel did? She climbed every high hill and crawled under every leafy tree to play the whore.

[7] I thought, "After all this, surely she'll come back to me." But she didn't. And her treacherous sister Judah watched it all.

[8] Judah saw that I divorced faithless Israel and handed her a certificate of divorce because of her endless adulteries. But Judah — that treacherous sister — wasn't afraid. She went out and prostituted herself too.

[9] Israel's whoring was so casual she defiled the land itself, committing adultery with stones and wooden idols.

[10] And after all of this, her treacherous sister Judah didn't come back to me with her whole heart — only with a performance. This is the LORD speaking.

[11] Then the LORD said to me: Faithless Israel looks righteous compared to treacherous Judah.

[12] Go north and shout this message: Come home, faithless Israel — this is the LORD speaking. I won't look at you with anger anymore. I am committed in love. I won't hold the grudge forever.

[13] Just admit your guilt — that you rebelled against the LORD your God, that you scattered your favors among strangers under every leafy tree, that you wouldn't listen to my voice. This is the LORD speaking.

[14] Come back, faithless children — this is the LORD speaking — because I am still your husband. I'll take you, one from a city and two from a clan, and bring you to Zion.

[15] I'll give you shepherds after my own heart, who'll feed you with knowledge and wisdom.

[16] In those days, when your numbers swell across the land — this is the LORD speaking — no one will talk anymore about the Ark of the Covenant of the LORD. It won't cross anyone's mind. They won't remember it, won't miss it, won't build another one.

[17] In that day they'll call Jerusalem itself the throne of the LORD, and every nation will gather there to honor the name of the LORD, and they'll stop chasing the stubborn impulses of their own dark hearts.

[18] In that day, the house of Judah will walk together with the house of Israel. Together they'll come home from the land of the north to the land I gave your ancestors as their inheritance.

[19] And I said to myself: How I wanted to treat you like sons, to give you a beautiful land, the most stunning inheritance among the nations. I thought you'd call me "Father" and never turn from following me.

[20] But like a wife unfaithful to her husband, you've been unfaithful to me, house of Israel. This is the LORD speaking.

[21] Listen — a sound rising from the barren heights: the weeping and pleading of Israel's children. They twisted their path. They forgot the LORD their God.

[22] Come back, faithless children. I'll heal your faithlessness. "Here we are. We're coming to you. You are the LORD our God.

[23] The hills were a lie. The mountain orgies were noise and nothing. Only in the LORD our God is the salvation of Israel.

[24] From the time we were young, that shameful thing has devoured everything our ancestors worked for — their flocks and herds, their sons and daughters.

[25] Let us lie down in our shame. Let our disgrace cover us like a blanket. We've sinned against the LORD our God — we and our fathers, from the time we were young to this very day. We never listened to the voice of the LORD our God."

[1] "If you're coming back, Israel," the LORD says, "come back to me. Get your filth out of my sight and stop wandering. [2] If you can swear 'as surely as the LORD lives' and mean it — in truth, in justice, in righteousness — then the nations will bless themselves through him and find their boasting in him."

[3] Here's what the LORD says to the men of Judah and Jerusalem: "Break up your hard ground. Stop planting among thorns. [4] Circumcise yourselves to the LORD — cut away the foreskin of your hearts, men of Judah, people of Jerusalem — or my fury will break out like fire and burn with no one to put it out, because of the evil you've done."

[5] Sound the alarm in Judah. Shout it through Jerusalem. Say it: blow the trumpet across the land. Cry out at full voice: "Gather up! Get to the fortified cities!" [6] Raise the signal toward Zion. Run for shelter — do not stop. I am bringing disaster from the north. Catastrophic ruin.

[7] A lion has come up from his thicket. A destroyer of nations is on the move — he has left his lair to turn your land into a wasteland. Your cities will lie in rubble, empty. [8] So put on sackcloth. Wail. Howl. Because the burning anger of the LORD has not turned away from us.

[9] "On that day," says the LORD, "the king's nerve will fail. The princes too. The priests will stand stunned. The prophets — speechless."

[10] Then I said, "Lord GOD, you have completely deceived this people and Jerusalem — telling them 'You'll have peace' while the sword is at their throat."

[11] At that time it will be said to this people and to Jerusalem: "A scorching wind off the bare heights of the wilderness blows toward my people — not to winnow, not to cleanse. [12] A wind too strong for that comes at my command. Now I will pronounce my judgments against them."

[13] Look — he rises like the clouds. His chariots like a whirlwind. His horses faster than eagles. We are finished. We are destroyed.

[14] Wash the evil from your heart, Jerusalem, so you might be saved. How long will you let wicked thoughts lodge inside you?

[15] A voice is calling from Dan. Disaster reported from the hills of Ephraim. [16] Warn the nations. Sound it out in Jerusalem: besiegers are coming from a distant land. They raise their war cry against the cities of Judah. [17] They've surrounded her like men guarding a field — because she rebelled against me, declares the LORD.

[18] Your own ways, your own deeds — these have done this to you. This is your evil come home. It is bitter. It strikes the heart.

[19] My guts — my guts! I writhe in pain. The walls of my heart! My heart is pounding. I cannot keep silent — because I have heard the trumpet, the alarm of war. [20] Disaster on disaster — the whole land is shattered. In an instant my tents are gone. My curtains in a heartbeat. [21] How long must I see the banner, hear the trumpet?

[22] "Because my people are fools. They do not know me. They are senseless children with no understanding. They are experts at evil — but doing good? They have no idea."

[23] I looked at the earth — and it was formless, empty. At the heavens — and their light was gone. [24] I looked at the mountains — and they were quaking, every hill trembling. [25] I looked — and there were no people. Every bird had fled the sky. [26] I looked — and the fertile land was wilderness. All its cities lay in ruins before the LORD, before the heat of his anger.

[27] This is what the LORD says: "The whole land will be devastated — but I will not destroy it completely. [28] For this the earth will mourn and the heavens above turn black. Because I have spoken. I have decided. I will not relent. I will not turn back."

[29] At the sound of horsemen and archers every city flees. They run into the thickets, scramble up the rocks. Every city — abandoned. No one lives there anymore.

[30] And you — devastated one — what are you doing? Dressing in scarlet? Putting on gold jewelry? Smearing your eyes with paint? You beautify yourself for nothing. Your lovers despise you. They want your life. [31] I hear a cry like a woman in labor, anguish like one giving birth her first time — the cry of Daughter Zion gasping for air, stretching out her hands: "I'm fainting. My life is being handed to killers."

[1] Comb the streets of Jerusalem. Look around. Search the public squares. Find me one person — just one — who deals honestly, who actually seeks truth. Find that one, and I'll pardon this city.

[2] They swear by the living God, sure — but every oath is a lie.

[3] Lord, your eyes hunt for truth. You struck them, and they felt nothing. You broke them, and they refused correction. They set their faces harder than stone and refused to come back.

[4] So I told myself: these are just the poor, the unschooled. They don't know any better. They've never learned the way of the Lord, the requirements of their God.

[5] I'll go to the leaders, the powerful ones. They'll know. They understand God's way, the demands of their God. But they too had snapped the yoke clean off, ripped the harness loose.

[6] So a lion from the forest will tear them apart. A wolf from the wasteland will ravage them. A leopard crouches outside their cities — anyone who steps out gets shredded. Because their rebellions have piled high, their backslidings without count.

[7] How am I supposed to forgive this? Your children abandoned me to swear by gods that aren't gods. I filled them full — and they committed adultery, lining up at the brothel.

[8] Well-fed stallions in heat, every man whinnying after his neighbor's wife.

[9] Should I not call this to account? declares the Lord. On a nation like this, should my soul not take vengeance?

[10] Climb her vineyard walls. Tear them down — but don't finish the job entirely. Strip her branches off, because they don't belong to the Lord.

[11] The house of Israel and the house of Judah have betrayed me utterly, declares the Lord.

[12] They've lied about him. They said, "He's nothing. Nothing bad will come to us. We won't see sword or famine.

[13] The prophets are just wind. The word isn't in them. Let what they threaten fall on their own heads."

[14] So this is what the Lord, the God of armies, says: Because you've spoken like this, watch — I'm putting my words in your mouth as fire, and this people as wood. The fire will eat them alive.

[15] I'm bringing a nation against you from far away, house of Israel, declares the Lord. An ancient nation. An enduring nation. A nation whose language you don't know, whose speech you can't understand.

[16] Their quivers gape like open graves. Every one of them — warriors.

[17] They'll devour your harvest and your bread. They'll devour your sons and daughters. They'll devour your flocks and herds, your vines and fig trees. They'll smash your fortified cities — the ones you trust — with the sword.

[18] But even then, declares the Lord, I won't finish you off completely.

[19] And when they ask, "Why has the Lord our God done all this to us?" — tell them this: Just as you abandoned me to serve foreign gods in your own land, so you'll serve foreigners in a land that isn't yours.

[20] Announce this in the house of Jacob. Sound it through Judah:

[21] Listen to this, you foolish people without sense — eyes that don't see, ears that don't hear.

[22] Don't you fear me? declares the Lord. Don't you tremble in my presence? I'm the one who set sand as a boundary for the sea — a permanent decree it cannot cross. The waves rage but cannot prevail. They roar but cannot pass over.

[23] But this people — they have a stubborn, rebellious heart. They've turned aside and walked away.

[24] They don't say in their hearts, "Let's fear the Lord our God, who gives rain in its season — autumn rains and spring rains — who keeps for us the appointed weeks of harvest."

[25] Your wrongs have knocked these blessings off course. Your sins have robbed you of good.

[26] Among my people are wicked men. They crouch low like fowlers lying in wait, setting traps — but the prey they catch is human.

[27] Like cages packed with birds, their houses are packed with deceit. That's how they've grown big and rich.

[28] Fat. Glossy. Their evil knows no limit. They won't defend the orphan's case so it can prosper. They won't grant justice to the poor.

[29] Should I not call this to account? declares the Lord. On a nation like this, should my soul not take vengeance?

[30] Something appalling, something horrifying, has happened in this land:

[31] The prophets prophesy lies. The priests rule on their own authority. And my people love it that way. But what will you do when the end comes?

[1] Run, children of Benjamin — get out of Jerusalem! Sound the trumpet in Tekoa, raise the signal over Beth-hakkerem, because catastrophe is leaning down from the north, a shattering on its way.

[2] That lovely, pampered daughter, Zion — I'm cutting her down.

[3] Shepherds are coming with their flocks. They'll pitch their tents around her, each one grazing his own patch of her.

[4] "Consecrate war against her! Up — let's strike at noon!" "Too late — the day is fading, the evening shadows stretch long."

[5] "Then up — let's strike by night! Burn her fortresses to the ground!"

[6] This is what the LORD of Armies says: Cut down her trees. Throw up a siege ramp against Jerusalem. This is the city marked for reckoning — nothing inside her but oppression.

[7] As a well keeps its water fresh, she keeps her wickedness fresh. Violence and ruin echo from her streets; sickness and wounds are always in my sight.

[8] Take the warning, Jerusalem — or I'll turn from you in disgust and make you a wasteland, an unlivable ruin.

[9] This is what the LORD of Armies says: They'll strip what's left of Israel like a vineyard picked clean. Run your hand back over the branches like a grape-gatherer.

[10] Who can I even speak to? Who will listen if I warn them? Their ears are sealed shut — they can't hear. The word of the LORD has become a joke to them. They want nothing to do with it.

[11] But I'm full of the LORD's fury — I can't hold it in any longer. Pour it out, then — on the children in the streets, on the young men gathered together. Husband and wife will be swept up together, the old and the ancient alike.

[12] Their houses will pass to strangers, their fields and their wives along with them — when I stretch out my hand against everyone living in this land. The LORD has spoken.

[13] From the smallest to the greatest, every one of them is on the take. Prophet and priest alike — all of them frauds.

[14] They patch up my people's wound like it's nothing. "Peace, peace," they say. There is no peace.

[15] Are they ashamed of the disgusting things they've done? No — they don't even know how to blush anymore. So they'll fall with everyone else. When I come to settle accounts, they'll go down. The LORD has spoken.

[16] This is what the LORD says: Stand at the crossroads and look. Ask for the ancient paths — where the good road lies — and walk it. You'll find rest for your souls. But you said, "We're not walking it."

[17] I posted watchmen over you and said, "Listen for the trumpet!" But you said, "We're not listening."

[18] So hear this, you nations. Take note, you witnesses — see what's coming to them.

[19] Listen, earth: I am bringing disaster on this people, the fruit of their own scheming — because they refused to hear my words, refused my instruction, threw it away.

[20] What do I care about frankincense imported from Sheba, or sweet cane from a distant country? Your offerings don't please me. Your sacrifices give me no joy.

[21] So this is what the LORD says: I'm laying stumbling blocks in front of this people. Fathers and sons together will trip over them. Neighbor and friend — they'll all go down.

[22] This is what the LORD says: Look — an army is marching from the land of the north. A great nation is being roused from the ends of the earth.

[23] They grip the bow and the spear. They are merciless — no pity in them. Their voices roar like the sea. They ride on horses, drawn up like men for war, against you, daughter of Zion.

[24] We've heard the report. Our hands hang limp. Panic seizes us — pain like a woman in labor.

[25] Don't go out to the fields. Don't walk the roads — the enemy has a sword, terror on every side.

[26] My dear people — put on sackcloth. Roll yourself in ashes. Mourn as for an only son. Wail bitterly. Because the destroyer is suddenly upon us.

[27] I've made you a tester of metals among my people — an assayer. You will watch, you will weigh their ways.

[28] All of them are stubborn rebels, going around as slanderers — bronze and iron, every one of them corrupt.

[29] The bellows roar. The lead is consumed by fire. The refiner refines for nothing — the wicked are not separated out.

[30] Call them rejected silver — because the LORD has rejected them.

[1] The word came to Jeremiah from the LORD:

[2] Stand at the gate of the LORD's house and preach this. Tell every Judean who walks through these gates to worship — listen to the word of the LORD.

[3] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Fix your ways. Fix what you're doing. Then I'll let you stay in this place.

[4] Stop trusting the lie they keep repeating: "The temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD." It means nothing.

[5] Here's what it takes. Truly fix your ways. Truly fix what you're doing. Deal justly with each other.

[6] Stop crushing the immigrant, the orphan, the widow. Stop spilling innocent blood in this place. Stop chasing other gods — it's destroying you.

[7] Do that, and I'll let you stay in this place, in this land I gave your ancestors, forever and ever.

[8] But look at you — trusting words that can't deliver.

[9] You steal. You murder. You commit adultery. You swear false oaths. You burn incense to Baal. You chase gods you've never known —

[10] and then you waltz into this house that bears my name, stand in my presence, and say, "We're safe!" — safe to keep doing every disgusting thing on the list?

[11] Has this house — the one that bears my name — become a hideout for thieves in your eyes? I've seen it. I've seen all of it. This is the LORD speaking.

[12] Go to Shiloh. Go to the place where I first made my name dwell. Look at what I did to it because of the wickedness of my people Israel.

[13] You've done all the same things, says the LORD. I spoke to you over and over — you didn't listen. I called you — you didn't answer.

[14] So I'm going to do to this house — the one that bears my name, the one you're trusting in, the place I gave to you and your ancestors — exactly what I did to Shiloh.

[15] I'm going to hurl you out of my sight, the way I hurled out all your brothers, the entire bloodline of Ephraim.

[16] As for you, Jeremiah — don't pray for these people. Don't lift up a cry or a prayer for them. Don't intercede with me. I'm not listening.

[17] Don't you see what they're doing in the cities of Judah, in the streets of Jerusalem?

[18] The children gather wood. The fathers light the fire. The women knead dough — making cakes for the Queen of Heaven. They pour out drink offerings to other gods just to provoke me.

[19] Is it really me they're provoking? says the LORD. Or is it themselves — to their own ruin?

[20] So this is what the Lord GOD says: My anger, my fury, is about to pour out on this place — on people, on animals, on the trees in the field, on the fruit of the ground. It will burn, and nothing will put it out.

[21] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Go ahead — pile your burnt offerings onto your sacrifices and eat the meat yourselves.

[22] Because on the day I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, I didn't speak to them or command them about burnt offerings and sacrifices.

[23] This is what I commanded them: Listen to my voice, and I will be your God and you will be my people. Walk the road I lay out for you, and it will go well with you.

[24] But they didn't listen. They didn't bend their ear. They walked their own counsel, the stubbornness of their evil hearts. They went backward, not forward.

[25] From the day your ancestors left Egypt until today, I have sent you my servants the prophets — day after day, again and again.

[26] They wouldn't listen. They wouldn't bend their ear. They stiffened their necks. They were worse than their ancestors.

[27] So when you say all this to them, they won't listen. When you call out to them, they won't answer.

[28] Then say this: This is the nation that won't listen to the voice of the LORD their God, that won't take correction. Truth has perished — it's cut off from their lips.

[29] Cut off your hair and throw it away. Lift a lament on the bare heights. The LORD has rejected and forsaken the generation he's furious with.

[30] The people of Judah have done evil in my sight, says the LORD. They've set their abominations inside the house that bears my name — defiling it.

[31] They've built the high places of Topheth in the Valley of Ben-Hinnom — to burn their own sons and daughters in fire. I never commanded it. It never entered my mind.

[32] So here it comes, says the LORD — days when no one will call it Topheth or the Valley of Ben-Hinnom anymore. They'll call it the Valley of Slaughter. They'll bury in Topheth because there's no room left anywhere else.

[33] The corpses of this people will be food for the birds of the sky and the beasts of the earth, with no one to scare them off.

[34] I will silence the voice of celebration and the voice of joy, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride, in the cities of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem. The land will be a wasteland.

[1] When that day comes—this is the LORD's word—they will drag the bones of Judah's kings out of their tombs, the bones of her princes, her priests, her prophets, every last citizen of Jerusalem.

[2] They will spread them out beneath the sun and moon and the whole starry host—the very gods they loved and served, chased after, consulted, bowed before. No one will gather them. No one will bury them. They will lie on the open ground like manure.

[3] And the survivors—what's left of this evil tribe in every place I scatter them—will choose death over life. So says the LORD of Heaven's Armies.

[4] Tell them this is what the LORD says: When people fall, don't they get back up? When they wander off, don't they turn around?

[5] Then why has this people—Jerusalem—wandered off forever? Why do they cling to deception, refuse to come home?

[6] I have listened carefully. They don't speak truth. Not one of them grieves his evil and asks, "What have I done?" Each one charges back into his own track like a warhorse racing into battle.

[7] Even the stork in the sky knows her seasons; the dove, the swift, the crane keep their migrations. But my people? They have no clue what the LORD requires.

[8] How can you say, "We are wise, we have the law of the LORD"? Look—the lying pen of the scribes has turned it into a lie.

[9] The wise will be humiliated, broken, captured. They threw away the LORD's word—what wisdom is left in them?

[10] So I will hand their wives to other men, their fields to new owners. From the smallest to the greatest, every one of them is on the take. Prophet and priest alike—all of them peddle fraud.

[11] They patch up my people's wound like it's a scratch, chanting, "Peace, peace"—when there is no peace.

[12] Are they ashamed of the disgusting things they did? No. They don't even know how to blush. So they will fall with the fallen, collapse when I bring the reckoning—says the LORD.

[13] I will gather them up and be done with them—the LORD's word. No grapes on the vine. No figs on the tree. Even the leaves are wilting. What I gave them is slipping through their fingers.

[14] Why are we just sitting here? Pull together—run for the fortified cities and die there. The LORD our God has sentenced us. He has given us poisoned water to drink, because we sinned against him.

[15] We hoped for peace—nothing good came. We hoped for healing—and instead, terror.

[16] From Dan you can already hear it—the snorting of his horses. The whole land shakes at the neighing of his stallions. They are coming to devour the land and everything in it, the city and everyone who lives there.

[17] Watch—I am loosing snakes among you, vipers no charmer can soothe. They will sink their fangs in. The LORD's word.

[18] My grief has no cure. My heart is sick inside me.

[19] Listen—the cry of my people from a distant land: "Is the LORD not in Zion? Is her King not there?" Why did they provoke me with their carved gods, their foreign nothings?

[20] The harvest is over. The summer is gone. And we are not saved.

[21] My people are shattered—and I am shattered. I walk in black. Horror has seized me.

[22] Is there no balm in Gilead? No physician there? Then why hasn't the wound of my people closed?

[1] If only my head were a spring and my eyes a fountain of tears — I would weep day and night for the slain of my people.

[2] If only I had a traveler's shelter out in the wilderness — I would walk away from my people and never look back. Every one of them cheats. The whole crowd is a gang of traitors.

[3] They bend their tongues like bows and shoot lies. Truth has no foothold in this land. They move from one evil to the next, and me — they do not know, declares the Lord.

[4] Watch your back, even with your friend. Trust no brother. Every brother is a swindler, every friend a peddler of slander.

[5] They deceive each other and never speak truth. They have trained their tongues to lie. They wear themselves out doing wrong.

[6] You live in the middle of deception, and the deception is why they refuse to know me, declares the Lord.

[7] So this is what the Lord of Hosts says: Watch — I am about to refine them, test them in the fire. What else can I do with a people like this?

[8] Their tongues are arrows that kill. Their mouths speak peace to a neighbor's face while setting an ambush in the heart.

[9] Should I not call them to account for this? declares the Lord. Should I not take vengeance on a nation like this?

[10] I will lift up weeping and wailing for the mountains, a funeral song for the wilderness pastures — because they are scorched, no one passes through, the lowing of cattle is gone. Birds and beasts have fled.

[11] I will turn Jerusalem into rubble, a haunt for jackals. The towns of Judah I will make a wasteland — empty of every living soul.

[12] Who is wise enough to understand this? Who has heard from the mouth of the Lord and can explain it? Why does the land lie ruined, scorched like a desert no one crosses?

[13] The Lord says: Because they abandoned my teaching — the one I set before them. They did not listen to my voice. They did not walk in it.

[14] They followed the stubborn churn of their own hearts and chased after the Baals their fathers taught them to chase.

[15] So this is what the Lord of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: I am about to feed this people wormwood and make them drink poisoned water.

[16] I will scatter them among nations neither they nor their ancestors have known, and I will send the sword after them until I have wiped them out.

[17] This is what the Lord of Hosts says: Pay attention. Call for the women who keen at funerals — send for the most skilled mourners. Bring them here.

[18] Let them hurry. Let them raise the lament for us, until our eyes run with tears and our eyelids stream with water.

[19] A voice of wailing is heard from Zion: We are ruined! We are exposed! We are leaving the land — they have torn down our homes.

[20] Listen, women — hear the word of the Lord. Open your ears to what comes from his mouth. Teach your daughters to mourn. Teach each other this dirge.

[21] Death has climbed in through our windows. It has entered our fortresses — cutting down children in the streets, young men in the public squares.

[22] Speak this — declares the Lord — the corpses of men will fall like dung across the open field, like cut grain behind the reaper, with no one to gather them in.

[23] This is what the Lord says: Let no wise man boast of his wisdom. Let no strong man boast of his strength. Let no rich man boast of his riches.

[24] If anyone is going to boast, let him boast of this: that he understands me and knows me — that I am the Lord, the one who acts with loyal love, with justice, with righteousness on the earth. These are what I delight in, declares the Lord.

[25] The days are coming, declares the Lord, when I will deal with everyone who is circumcised only in the flesh —

[26] Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and all the desert dwellers who shave their temples. Every one of these nations is uncircumcised — and the whole house of Israel is uncircumcised in the heart.

[1] Listen, house of Israel — hear what the LORD is telling you.

[2] This is what the LORD says: Don't pick up the habits of the nations. Don't lose your nerve over signs in the sky just because the nations are terrified of them.

[3] The religion of these peoples is empty. Someone chops a tree out of the forest — a craftsman shapes it with his chisel —

[4] decks it out in silver and gold, hammers it down with nails so it won't tip over.

[5] These gods are scarecrows in a cucumber patch. They can't speak. They have to be carried because they can't walk. Don't be afraid of them — they can't hurt you, and they can't help you either.

[6] No one is like you, LORD. You are vast. Your name is power itself.

[7] Who wouldn't tremble before you, King of the nations? You deserve the awe. Search every wise man in every kingdom on earth — none of them comes close to you.

[8] They are all fools, every last one — instructed by a block of wood.

[9] Hammered silver shipped in from Tarshish, gold imported from Uphaz, the work of the craftsman and the goldsmith's hands, dressed in blue and purple — every bit of it the product of skilled labor.

[10] But the LORD is the real God. He is the living God, the everlasting King. When he is angry, the earth shudders. The nations can't endure his fury.

[11] (Tell them this: The gods who didn't make the heavens and the earth — they're going to vanish from the earth and from under these heavens.)

[12] He made the earth by his power. He set the world in place by his wisdom. He stretched out the heavens by his understanding.

[13] When his voice thunders, the waters in the sky roar. He sends clouds rising from the ends of the earth. He hurls lightning with the rain and releases the wind from his storehouses.

[14] Every human being stands there stupid, ignorant. Every goldsmith is humiliated by his own idol — because the figure he cast is a lie. There's no breath in it.

[15] These things are worthless, a joke. When their day of reckoning comes, they will be gone.

[16] But the One who is Jacob's portion is nothing like them. He fashioned everything that exists, and Israel is the tribe he calls his own. The LORD of Hosts — that is his name.

[17] Gather up your belongings off the ground, you who are penned in under siege.

[18] Because this is what the LORD says: Watch — this time I'm flinging the inhabitants of this land out like stones from a sling. I will press them until they feel it.

[19] My wound — it's catastrophic. My injury won't heal. And I said to myself: Fine. This is my suffering. I'll bear it.

[20] My tent is in ruins. Every cord, snapped. My children have walked out on me — they're gone. No one left to set up my tent again, no one to hang the curtains.

[21] The shepherds have gone stupid. They didn't seek the LORD. That's why they failed, and why their whole flock is scattered.

[22] Listen — a rumor. A roar coming. A massive convulsion out of the northern country, coming to turn the cities of Judah into rubble, a den for jackals.

[23] LORD, I know — a person's life is not their own. No one walking out the door has the power to direct his own steps.

[24] Discipline me, LORD — but with justice, not in your anger, or you'll grind me to nothing.

[25] Pour out your fury on the nations that refuse to know you, on the peoples who never call on your name — because they have devoured Jacob. Devoured him and finished him off and laid waste to the place where he lived.

[1] The word came to Jeremiah from the LORD:

[2] Listen to the terms of this covenant. Speak them to the people of Judah and everyone in Jerusalem.

[3] Tell them: This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — Cursed is the one who refuses to obey the terms of this covenant,

[4] the covenant I bound on your ancestors the day I dragged them out of Egypt, out of that iron furnace. I said: Obey me. Do everything I command. Then you will be my people, and I will be your God.

[5] That way I could keep the oath I swore to your ancestors — to give them a land flowing with milk and honey, the land you stand on today.

I answered, "Amen, LORD."

[6] Then the LORD said: Proclaim every word of this in the streets of Judah and in Jerusalem. Say: Listen to the terms of this covenant. Do them.

[7] From the day I brought your ancestors out of Egypt until now, I have warned them — again and again, without stopping — Obey me.

[8] They did not listen. They did not bend an ear. Every one of them followed the stubborn pull of his own wicked heart. So I unleashed on them every threat in this covenant — the covenant I commanded them to keep, and they refused.

[9] The LORD said to me: There is a conspiracy among the people of Judah and the citizens of Jerusalem.

[10] They have circled back to the sins of their forefathers — the ones who refused to listen to my words. They have chased after other gods to serve them. Israel and Judah both — they have shattered the covenant I made with their fathers.

[11] Therefore, this is what the LORD says: I am bringing down on them a disaster they cannot escape. They will cry out to me, and I will not listen.

[12] Then the towns of Judah and the people of Jerusalem will run to the gods they have been burning incense to, begging for help — and those gods will not save them. Not when the disaster comes.

[13] Judah — you have as many gods as you have towns. And the altars you have built to that shameful thing, to Baal — there are as many of those as there are streets in Jerusalem.

[14] So you, Jeremiah — do not pray for these people. Do not lift up a cry or a plea for them. When their trouble comes and they call out to me, I will not hear.

[15] What is my beloved doing in my house, scheming her schemes with the crowd? Can sacred meat ward off your doom? When evil hits, will you still be celebrating?

[16] The LORD once called you a thriving olive tree, lush with beautiful fruit. But now — with a roar like a hurricane he will set it ablaze, and its branches will burn to nothing.

[17] The LORD of Hosts, the one who planted you, has spoken disaster against you — because of the evil committed by Israel and Judah, provoking me by burning incense to Baal.

[18] The LORD let me in on it. He showed me. He opened my eyes to what they were doing.

[19] I had been like a docile lamb led to slaughter. I had no idea they were plotting against me: "Let us destroy the tree along with its fruit. Let us cut him out of the land of the living — let his name be forgotten."

[20] But LORD of Hosts, you are the righteous judge. You test the heart and the gut. Let me see your vengeance fall on them — because I have laid my case before you.

[21] Therefore, this is what the LORD says about the men of Anathoth, who want me dead — who say, "Stop prophesying in the name of the LORD or we will kill you with our own hands."

[22] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: I am going to deal with them. Their young men will die by the sword. Their sons and daughters will starve to death.

[23] Not one will be left. I am bringing catastrophe on the men of Anathoth — the year of their reckoning.

[1] You're always right, LORD, when I bring my case. Still — I need to talk to you about justice. Why does the road of the wicked run smooth? Why do all the treacherous live without a care?

[2] You planted them. They took root. They grow, they bear fruit. Your name sits close on their lips — and nowhere near their hearts.

[3] But you know me, LORD. You see me. You've tested what's inside me, and it leans toward you. So drag them out like sheep to slaughter. Set them apart for the day of butchering.
