[4] How long will the land mourn? How long will the grass in every field wither? Animals and birds are vanishing because the people who live here are evil — and they say, "He can't see what we're up to."

[5] If running with men has worn you out, how will you keep pace with horses? If you stumble in open country where it's safe, what will you do in the tangled thickets of the Jordan?

[6] Even your own brothers — your father's house — even they've betrayed you. Even they've raised the hue and cry behind your back. Don't trust them when they speak kindly to you.

[7] I'm walking out of my own house. I'm abandoning my inheritance. The one my soul loved — I'm handing her over to her enemies.

[8] My inheritance turned on me like a lion in the forest, roaring against me. So I've come to hate her.

[9] My inheritance has become a speckled bird of prey, and the other birds of prey are circling. Go on — gather every wild beast. Bring them in to feast.

[10] Shepherd after shepherd has trampled my vineyard, stomped my field into dust, turned the land I delighted in into a desert wasteland.

[11] They made it a wasteland. It mourns to me, empty. The whole country lies devastated — and not one person takes it to heart.

[12] Across every bare hill in the wilderness the destroyers have come. The sword of the LORD is devouring from one end of the land to the other. Nothing alive finds peace.

[13] They planted wheat and harvested thorns. They wore themselves out for nothing. Be ashamed of your harvest — it's the burning anger of the LORD that brought it in.

[14] This is what the LORD says about all my wicked neighbors — the ones who put their hands on the inheritance I gave my people Israel: I'm about to uproot them from their own land, and I'll uproot the house of Judah right out from among them.

[15] But after I've uprooted them, I'll turn back and have compassion on them. I'll bring each one home to his own inheritance, his own land.

[16] And if they truly learn the ways of my people — if they swear by my name, "As the LORD lives," the way they once taught my people to swear by Baal — then they'll be planted right in the middle of my people.

[17] But any nation that refuses to listen — I'll uproot it and destroy it. Declaration of the LORD.

[1] This is what the LORD told me: Go buy yourself a linen loincloth. Strap it around your waist. But do not let it touch water.

[2] So I bought the loincloth, just as the LORD said, and put it on.

[3] Then the LORD's word came to me again:

[4] Take that loincloth you bought — the one wrapped around you — and head for the Euphrates. When you get there, hide it in a crevice in the rocks.

[5] So I went and buried it by the Euphrates, exactly as the LORD commanded.

[6] A long stretch of days passed. Then the LORD said: Get up. Go back to the Euphrates. Retrieve the loincloth I told you to hide there.

[7] So I went back. I dug it out of the spot where I had buried it. And there it was — ruined. Rotted through. Worthless.

[8] Then the LORD's word came to me:

[9] This is what the LORD says — this is exactly how I will rot the arrogance of Judah and the towering pride of Jerusalem.

[10] These people are vile. They refuse to listen to me. They follow the stubborn pull of their own hearts. They chase after other gods to serve them and bow to them. They will become like this loincloth — good for nothing.

[11] Because just as a loincloth clings tight to a man's waist, I bound the whole house of Israel and the whole house of Judah to myself — to be my people, my name, my praise, my glory. And they would not listen.

[12] So tell them this — and tell them exactly: This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — Every wine jar gets filled with wine. And when they sneer back, You think we don't know that? Of course every jar gets filled with wine —

[13] then tell them: Here is what the LORD says. I am about to fill every person in this land with drunkenness — the kings who sit on David's throne, the priests, the prophets, every last citizen of Jerusalem — drunk out of their minds.

[14] Then I will smash them against each other — fathers and sons together, says the LORD. No pity. No mercy. No restraint. I will not hold back from destroying them.

[15] Listen. Pay attention. Drop the arrogance — the LORD has spoken.

[16] Give glory to the LORD your God before he brings the darkness, before your feet stumble on the twilight mountains. You will look for light and he will turn it to deep shadow, will fold it into pitch black.

[17] But if you will not listen, I will weep in secret over your pride. My eyes will run with tears, will overflow, because the LORD's flock has been led away into captivity.

[18] Say to the king and the queen mother: Step down from your thrones. The crown of your glory has fallen from your heads.

[19] The cities of the Negev are sealed shut — no one to open them. All Judah is dragged into exile — every last one of them, hauled away.

[20] Lift your eyes. Look at them coming down from the north. Where is the flock you were given — those magnificent sheep?

[21] What will you say when he sets over you the very ones you cultivated as your closest allies? Will the pain not seize you like a woman in labor?

[22] And when you ask yourself, Why has this happened to me? — it is because of the size of your guilt. That is why your skirts are torn off, why your body is violated.

[23] Can a Cushite change his skin? A leopard its spots? Then neither can you do good — you who have been schooled in evil.

[24] So I will scatter them like chaff blown away on the desert wind.

[25] This is your portion. This is the measure I have weighed out for you, says the LORD — because you forgot me and trusted in lies.

[26] So I myself will rip your skirts up over your face. Your shame will be on full display.

[27] Your adulteries. Your lust-drenched neighing. The obscenity of your prostitution on the hills and in the fields. I have seen every revolting thing you have done. Damn you, Jerusalem. How much longer until you are clean?

[1] The word of the LORD came to Jeremiah concerning the drought:

[2] Judah is in mourning. Her gates collapse. Her people slump to the ground in black grief, and a wail rises from Jerusalem.

[3] The wealthy send their servants for water. They reach the cisterns — empty. They come back with empty jars, heads down, faces covered, hollowed out by shame.

[4] The ground itself is cracked open because no rain has fallen on the land. The farmers are gutted. They cover their heads.

[5] Even the doe in the field abandons her newborn fawn — there is no grass.

[6] Wild donkeys stand on the bare hills, panting like jackals, their eyes burning out in their sockets because there is nothing green left to find.

[7] LORD — our sins testify against us, but act for the sake of your name. We have turned from you again and again. We have sinned against you.

[8] You are Israel's only hope, the one who saves us when everything falls apart. So why do you act like a stranger in your own land — like a traveler who pitches his tent for one night and moves on?

[9] Why do you look stunned, like a warrior frozen, unable to save? You are right here among us, LORD. We carry your name. Do not abandon us.

[10] Here is what the LORD says about this people: They love to wander. They cannot keep their feet still. So the LORD will not accept them. Now he remembers their guilt. Now he calls their sins to account.

[11] Then the LORD said to me: Do not pray for the good of this people.

[12] When they fast, I will not listen to their cry. When they offer burnt offerings and grain offerings, I will not accept them. I will finish them with sword, with famine, with plague.

[13] So I said: LORD God — look, the prophets keep telling them, "You will not see the sword. You will not go hungry. The LORD will give you lasting peace right here in this place."

[14] And the LORD said to me: The prophets are prophesying lies in my name. I never sent them. I never commissioned them. I never spoke to them. What they peddle is hallucination, hollow divination, fraud spun from their own minds.

[15] So this is what the LORD says about the prophets who speak in my name when I never sent them — the ones promising no sword and no famine will touch this land: by sword and by famine those very prophets will die.

[16] And the people they preach to will be thrown out into the streets of Jerusalem — starved, run through — with no one left to bury them. Not them. Not their wives. Not their sons. Not their daughters. I will pour their own evil back over their heads.

[17] Tell them this: Let my eyes run with tears night and day. Let them not stop. Because the virgin daughter of my people has been shattered — a catastrophic wound, a blow no one walks away from.

[18] Walk out into the field — bodies cut down by the sword. Walk into the city — the wasting sickness of famine. Even the prophet and the priest stagger through a land they no longer recognize.

[19] Have you completely rejected Judah? Do you despise Zion deep in your soul? Why have you struck us so hard there is no healing left? We waited for peace — nothing good came. We waited for a time of healing — only terror.

[20] LORD, we know our wickedness. We know the guilt of our ancestors. We have sinned against you.

[21] For the sake of your name, do not despise us. Do not disgrace your glorious throne. Remember. Do not break your covenant with us.

[22] Can any of the worthless idols of the nations bring rain? Can the skies on their own send showers? No — only you, LORD our God. So we wait on you. You are the one who made all of this.

[1] Then the LORD said to me: "Even if Moses and Samuel themselves stood here pleading for these people, I would not turn toward them. Get them out of My sight. Send them away.

[2] And when they ask you, 'Where are we supposed to go?'—tell them this is what the LORD says: Those marked for plague, to the plague. Those marked for the sword, to the sword. Those marked for famine, to the famine. Those marked for exile, to exile.

[3] I am assigning four destroyers against them, declares the LORD: the sword to butcher, the dogs to drag away the carcasses, the birds of the sky and the beasts of the earth to devour and destroy what remains.

[4] I will make them a horror to every kingdom on earth—because of what Manasseh son of Hezekiah, king of Judah, did in Jerusalem.

[5] Who will pity you, Jerusalem? Who will even pause to grieve? Who will turn aside just to ask how you are?

[6] You walked out on Me, declares the LORD. You kept walking. So I am reaching out My hand against you—I am done relenting. I am worn out from holding back.

[7] I have winnowed them with a pitchfork at the gates of the land. I have made them childless. I have destroyed My own people—because they would not turn from their ways.

[8] Their widows outnumber the sand of the sea. Against the mothers of young men I have brought a destroyer at noon. I have dropped anguish and terror on the city without warning.

[9] The mother of seven collapses, gasping out her life. Her sun has set while it was still day—she is shamed, humiliated. And the survivors? I will hand them to the sword in front of their enemies, declares the LORD.

[10] Woe is me, Mother, that you ever gave birth to me—a man the whole land fights with, a man the whole land argues against. I have lent to no one, borrowed from no one, and still everyone curses me.

[11] The LORD said: I swear I will release you for good. I swear I will make your enemies come begging in the day of disaster, in the day of distress.

[12] Can anyone snap iron—iron from the north—or bronze?

[13] Your wealth and your treasures I will hand over as plunder, no payment required—because of every sin throughout your borders.

[14] I will make you serve your enemies in a land you do not know. My anger has kindled a fire, and it will burn against you.

[15] You know, LORD. Remember me. Come for me. Avenge me on my persecutors. Do not, in Your patience with them, take me away. See what I endure for Your name's sake.

[16] When Your words came, I devoured them. Your words became my joy, the delight of my heart—because I bear Your name, LORD, God of armies.

[17] I never sat in the circle of the partying crowd, never joined the laughter. I sat alone, because Your hand was on me—You had filled me with fury.

[18] Why is my pain unending, my wound incurable, refusing to heal? Will You be to me like a stream that runs dry—water you cannot trust?

[19] So the LORD answered: If you come back, I will bring you back. You will stand before Me again. If you separate the precious from the worthless, you will be My mouthpiece. Let them turn to you—but do not you turn to them.

[20] I will make you a wall to this people—a fortified wall of bronze. They will fight you, but they will not overcome you, because I am with you to rescue you, to save you, declares the LORD.

[21] I will pull you free from the grip of the wicked. I will redeem you from the fist of the ruthless.

[1] Then the word of the LORD came to me:

[2] Do not take a wife. Do not father sons or daughters in this place.

[3] Because this is what the LORD says about the children born here, and about the mothers who carry them, and the fathers who beget them in this land:

[4] They will die of diseases that gut them. No one will mourn them. No one will bury them. They will lie on the ground like dung. Sword and famine will finish them, and their corpses will feed the birds of the sky and the beasts of the earth.

[5] This is what the LORD says: Do not enter a house of mourning. Do not go to grieve. Do not offer comfort. Because I have pulled my peace away from this people — my loyal love, my mercy — gone.

[6] Great and small will die in this land. No burial. No mourning. No cutting the skin. No shaving the head for them.

[7] No one will break bread with the bereaved to console them for the dead. No cup of comfort will be raised for a lost father or mother.

[8] And do not enter a house of feasting either. Do not sit down to eat and drink with them.

[9] Because this is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Watch. Right before your eyes, in your own lifetime, I am silencing this place — the voice of joy and the voice of gladness, the voice of the groom and the voice of the bride. Silence.

[10] When you deliver all this to the people, they will ask you: Why? Why has the LORD pronounced this disaster against us? What did we do wrong? What sin did we commit against the LORD our God?

[11] Then tell them: Because your ancestors abandoned me — declares the LORD — and chased after other gods, served them, bowed to them. They walked away from me. They did not keep my instruction.

[12] And you — you have done worse than your ancestors. Look at you, each one trailing the stubborn cravings of his own evil heart, refusing to listen to me.

[13] So I am hurling you out of this land into a country neither you nor your ancestors have known. There you can serve other gods day and night to your heart's content — because I will show you no favor.

[14] Yet the days are coming — declares the LORD — when people will no longer swear, "As the LORD lives, who brought Israel up out of Egypt,"

[15] but instead, "As the LORD lives, who brought Israel up out of the north country and out of every land where he had banished them." I will bring them back to their own soil — the soil I gave their ancestors.

[16] Right now, though — I am sending for fishermen, declares the LORD, and they will haul them in. Then I will send for hunters, and they will track them down on every mountain, every hill, every crevice in the rocks.

[17] My eyes are on every road they walk. They cannot hide from me. Their guilt is not concealed from my sight.

[18] First I will repay them double for their guilt, their sin — because they polluted my land with the carcasses of their vile idols, and stuffed my inheritance full of their abominations.

[19] LORD — my strength, my stronghold, my refuge in the day of distress — the nations will come to you from the ends of the earth and say: Our ancestors inherited nothing but lies, hollow gods that do nothing.

[20] Can a person make gods for himself? Those are not gods.

[21] So now — watch — I am going to teach them. This time, I will make them know my hand and my power. They will know that my name is the LORD.

[1] Judah's sin is engraved with an iron stylus, etched with a diamond point — carved deep into the tablet of their hearts, chiseled onto the horns of their altars. [2] Even their children remember the altars and the Asherah poles standing beside every leafy tree, on every high hill, [3] on the mountains of the open country. Your wealth, every treasure you've hoarded — I am handing it all over as plunder, along with your high places, payment for sin sown through your borders. [4] You will lose your grip on the inheritance I gave you. I will make you slaves to your enemies in a land you've never seen — because you have set my anger on fire, and it will burn forever.

[5] This is what the LORD says: Cursed is the one who leans on mere humans, who makes flesh his strength, whose heart turns away from the LORD. [6] He will be like a shriveled bush in the wasteland, blind to any good thing coming — planted in the salt-burnt places of the desert, in a land where no one lives.

[7] Blessed is the one who trusts the LORD, whose confidence rests entirely in him. [8] He is like a tree planted by water, sending its roots toward the stream. It does not flinch when the heat comes; its leaves stay green. It does not panic in a year of drought, and it never stops bearing fruit.

[9] The heart — more crooked than anything, sick beyond cure. Who can possibly understand it? [10] I, the LORD, search the heart. I test the gut. I give each person exactly what their life has earned, exactly what their actions deserve.

[11] Like a partridge sitting on eggs she didn't lay — that's the person who builds a fortune by injustice. Halfway through his life it abandons him, and in the end he is exposed as a fool.

[12] A throne of glory, lifted high from the beginning — that is the place of our sanctuary. [13] LORD, hope of Israel — all who abandon you will be shamed. Those who turn from you will be written in the dust, because they have forsaken the LORD, the fountain of living water.

[14] Heal me, LORD, and I will be healed. Save me, and I will be saved. You are the one I praise. [15] Listen to them taunting me: "Where is this word from the LORD? Let's see it come true." [16] But I haven't run from being your shepherd; I have not longed for the day of disaster — you know this. Every word that left my lips was spoken in your presence. [17] Don't become a terror to me. You are my refuge in the day of catastrophe. [18] Let my persecutors be the ones shamed — not me. Let them be shattered — not me. Bring the day of disaster down on them. Crush them — double.

[19] This is what the LORD said to me: Go and stand at the People's Gate, where the kings of Judah enter and leave, and then at every gate of Jerusalem. [20] Say to them: Hear the word of the LORD, kings of Judah, all of Judah, every resident of Jerusalem who walks through these gates. [21] This is what the LORD says: Watch yourselves. Do not haul any load on the Sabbath day. Do not bring it through the gates of Jerusalem. [22] Do not carry a load out of your houses on the Sabbath. Do no work at all. Keep the Sabbath holy, as I commanded your ancestors. [23] But they didn't listen, didn't lean in — they stiffened their necks against hearing, refused to learn anything.

[24] But if you actually listen to me, says the LORD — if you stop hauling loads through these city gates on the Sabbath, if you keep the day holy and do no work on it — [25] then through these gates will come kings and princes who sit on David's throne, riding in chariots and on horses, accompanied by their officials, by the people of Judah and the residents of Jerusalem. And this city will be inhabited forever. [26] People will stream in from the towns of Judah, from the villages around Jerusalem, from the territory of Benjamin, from the foothills, from the hill country, from the Negev — bringing burnt offerings, sacrifices, grain offerings, frankincense, thank offerings to the house of the LORD.

[27] But if you will not listen to me — if you refuse to keep the Sabbath holy, if you keep hauling loads through the gates of Jerusalem on the Sabbath — then I will set fire to those gates. The flames will consume the citadels of Jerusalem, and nothing will put them out.

[1] The word came to Jeremiah from the LORD:

[2] "Get up. Go down to the potter's house. I'll speak to you there."

[3] So I went down to the potter's house, and there he was, working at the wheel.

[4] The vessel he was shaping collapsed in his hands — clay gone wrong. So he started over. Reworked it into something else. Whatever seemed right to him.

[5] Then the word of the LORD came to me:

[6] "House of Israel — can't I do with you what this potter does with his clay? Look at you: clay in my hand. That's all. Just clay in my hand.

[7] One moment I declare that a nation, a kingdom, will be uprooted, torn down, destroyed —

[8] but if that nation turns from its evil, I relent. I do not bring the disaster I had planned.

[9] And one moment I declare that a nation, a kingdom, will be built up and planted —

[10] but if it does evil in my sight and refuses to listen to my voice, I reconsider the good I intended to do for it.

[11] So now — say this to the men of Judah and the people of Jerusalem: 'This is what the LORD says: I am shaping disaster against you. Drawing up a plan. So turn — every one of you — from your evil road. Straighten out your ways and what you're doing.'"

[12] But they'll say: "No use. We're going to follow our own plans. Every one of us will do whatever his stubborn, evil heart wants."

[13] So this is what the LORD says:

Ask among the nations —
who has ever heard of such a thing?
Virgin Israel has done something
unspeakable.

[14] Does the snow of Lebanon ever leave
the rocky slopes?
Do the cold streams from distant mountains
ever run dry?

[15] But my people — my people have forgotten me.
They burn incense to nothing.
They've stumbled off the ancient roads,
wandered onto side paths,
trails that go nowhere.

[16] They've turned their land into a horror,
a thing people whistle at in disgust forever.
Anyone passing through will be shocked,
will shake his head in disbelief.

[17] Like a hot east wind I'll scatter them
before the enemy.
My back, not my face, is what they'll see
on the day of their ruin.

[18] Then they said: "Let's plot against Jeremiah. The priests will still have their instruction, the wise their counsel, the prophets their word. None of that depends on him. Come on — let's attack him with our tongues. Ignore everything he says."

[19] Pay attention to me, LORD!
Listen to what my accusers are saying!

[20] Is evil really the way to repay good?
They've dug a pit for me.
Remember — I stood before you
and spoke up on their behalf,
trying to turn your anger away from them.

[21] So now — hand their children over to famine.
Let the sword do its work on them.
Make their wives childless widows.
Strike down their men with plague,
their young men cut down in battle.

[22] Let screaming break out from their houses
when you bring raiders against them without warning,
because they dug a pit to trap me,
hid snares for my feet.

[23] But you, LORD — you know
every plan they've made to kill me.
Don't cover their guilt.
Don't wipe their sin from your sight.
Let them be thrown down right there before you.
Deal with them in the heat of your anger.

[1] This is what the LORD said: Go buy a clay jar from the potter. Take with you some of the elders of the people and the senior priests. [2] Walk out to the Valley of Ben-Hinnom, to the entrance of the Potsherd Gate, and there proclaim the words I give you.

[3] Say this: Hear the word of the LORD, kings of Judah, people of Jerusalem. This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, declares — I am about to bring catastrophe on this place, and every ear that hears of it will ring.

[4] Because they abandoned me. They turned this place into something foreign. They burned incense here to gods they never knew — they, their ancestors, the kings of Judah — and they soaked this ground with the blood of innocents. [5] They built shrines to Baal and threw their own children into the fire as offerings — something I never commanded, never spoke of, something that never crossed my mind.

[6] So watch — the days are coming, declares the LORD, when this place will no longer be called Topheth or the Valley of Ben-Hinnom. It will be called the Valley of Slaughter.

[7] Right here, in this place, I will unravel every plan Judah and Jerusalem ever made. I will drop them by the sword in front of their enemies, by the hand of those who hunt their lives. I will give their corpses to the vultures and the wild dogs. [8] I will turn this city into a horror, a thing people whistle at as they pass — anyone who walks by will gasp and shake his head at the wounds of it. [9] I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and their daughters. They will devour one another in the siege — when their enemies, the ones hunting their lives, press in and crush them.

[10] Then smash the jar — right there, in front of the men who walked out with you. [11] And say to them: This is what the LORD of Armies declares — I will shatter this people and this city the way a potter's jar is shattered, beyond all repair. They will bury their dead in Topheth until there is no more room to bury.

[12] That is what I will do to this place and to those who live in it, says the LORD. I will make this city itself a Topheth. [13] Every house in Jerusalem, every palace of Judah's kings — every place where they climbed to the rooftops to burn incense to the whole army of heaven and pour out drink offerings to other gods — every one of them will be defiled, every one like Topheth.

[14] Then Jeremiah came back from Topheth, where the LORD had sent him to prophesy, and he stood in the courtyard of the LORD's house and said to all the people: [15] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, declares — listen — I am bringing on this city and on every town around it every disaster I spoke against it, because they stiffened their necks and refused to hear my words.

[1] Pashhur son of Immer, the priest who served as chief officer in the LORD's house, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things.

[2] So Pashhur had the prophet Jeremiah beaten and locked him in the stocks at the Upper Benjamin Gate of the LORD's house.

[3] The next morning, when Pashhur released him from the stocks, Jeremiah told him: "The LORD is no longer calling you Pashhur. Your new name is Terror-on-Every-Side.

[4] Because this is what the LORD says: I am turning you into a thing of terror — to yourself and to everyone you love. You will watch them cut down by enemy swords, and your eyes will not look away. I am handing all of Judah over to the king of Babylon — he will drag them off into exile, he will strike them down.

[5] Every treasure of this city, every hoard of wealth, every precious thing, every royal stockpile of Judah's kings — I am giving it all to their enemies. They will loot it. They will seize it. They will haul it to Babylon.

[6] And you, Pashhur — you and everyone in your house — you will go into exile. You will reach Babylon. You will die there. You will be buried there. You and every friend you ever lied to with your false prophecies."

[7] You seduced me, LORD, and I let myself be seduced. You overpowered me — you won. Now I am the joke of the town, the punchline they pass around all day.

[8] Every time I open my mouth, I'm screaming — violence! ruin! Your word has made me a laughingstock, insulted from sunrise to sunset.

[9] So I told myself: I'm done. I won't mention him. I won't speak his name again. But then it's there — fire shut up in my bones, a blaze in my chest. I wear myself out trying to hold it in. I can't.

[10] I hear them whispering — Terror everywhere! Turn him in! Let's turn him in! Even my so-called friends are watching for me to slip — Maybe he'll trip up, then we'll have him, then we'll get our revenge.

[11] But the LORD is with me like a soldier nobody messes with. So my hunters will stumble. They will not win. Their failure will be permanent — a humiliation no one ever forgets.

[12] LORD of armies — you who test the righteous, who see straight through to the heart and gut — let me watch you settle the score with them. I have laid my case at your feet.

[13] Sing to the LORD! Praise the LORD! He pulls the life of the needy out of the hands of those who would crush it.

[14] Curse the day I was born. The day my mother delivered me — let it not be blessed.

[15] Curse the man who brought my father the news, who made him glad with the words: A son! You have a son!

[16] Let that man be like the cities the LORD overthrew without mercy. Let him hear screams in the morning and war cries at noon —

[17] because he didn't kill me in the womb, didn't make my mother's belly my grave, her womb forever swollen with me inside.

[18] Why? Why did I come out of the womb at all — just to see trouble, just to drown in grief, just to spend my days drenched in shame?

[1] The word came to Jeremiah from the LORD when King Zedekiah sent Pashhur son of Malkijah and Zephaniah the priest, son of Maaseiah, with this message:

[2] "Consult the LORD for us. Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is laying siege to us. Maybe the LORD will do something extraordinary, the kind of thing he's done before, and force Babylon to pull back."

[3] Jeremiah answered them: "Tell Zedekiah this —

[4] The LORD, the God of Israel, says: I am turning your own weapons against you. The swords you're holding to fight Babylon and the Chaldeans surrounding your walls — I'm going to pile them up inside this city.

[5] And I myself will fight against you, with an outstretched hand and a powerful arm, with fury and rage and overwhelming wrath.

[6] I will strike down everyone living in this city — people and animals alike. A devastating plague will sweep through.

[7] After that, says the LORD, I will hand Zedekiah king of Judah and his officials and whatever's left of this city — whoever survives the plague, the sword, the starvation — into the hands of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, into the hands of their enemies, into the hands of those hunting their lives. He will cut them down without mercy. No pity. No compassion.

[8] And to this people you will say: The LORD says — listen carefully. I am placing before you the road to life and the road to death.

[9] Whoever stays in this city will die — by the sword, by famine, by plague. But whoever walks out and surrenders to the Chaldeans laying siege to you will live. Their life will be the only spoil they carry out.

[10] I have set my face against this city for catastrophe, not for good, says the LORD. It will be handed over to the king of Babylon, and he will torch it.

[11] And to the royal house of Judah, hear the word of the LORD —

[12] House of David, the LORD says: Render justice every morning. Rescue the one being robbed from the hand of the oppressor. Or my fury will break out like fire and burn with no one to put it out — all because of what you've done.

[13] I am against you, you who sit enthroned above the valley, rock of the plain, says the LORD. You who say, 'Who could ever come down on us? Who could break into our stronghold?'

[14] I will deal with you according to the fruit of your deeds, says the LORD. I will kindle a fire in your forest, and it will devour everything around you."

[1] This is what the LORD said: Go down to the palace of the king of Judah. Stand there and deliver this message.

[2] Say: Listen to the word of the LORD, king of Judah, you who sit on David's throne — you, your officials, your people who pass through these gates.

[3] This is what the LORD demands: Do justice. Rescue the robbed from the hand of the oppressor. Don't exploit the immigrant, the orphan, the widow. Don't shed innocent blood in this place.

[4] If you actually do this, kings will keep coming through these palace gates — kings on David's throne, riding in chariots and on horses, the king himself with his officials and his people.

[5] But if you ignore these words, I swear by myself, says the LORD, this palace will become rubble.

[6] This is what the LORD says about the royal house of Judah: You are Gilead to me, the peak of Lebanon — but I swear I'll make you a wasteland, a city no one lives in.

[7] I'm consecrating destroyers against you, each with his weapon. They'll cut down your finest cedars and throw them into the fire.

[8] Nations will pass by this city and ask each other: Why did the LORD do this to such a great city?

[9] And the answer will come: Because they abandoned the covenant of the LORD their God. They bowed to other gods. They served them.

[10] Don't weep for the dead. Don't mourn him. Weep instead for the one who is leaving — he'll never come back, never see the land of his birth again.

[11] This is what the LORD says about Shallum son of Josiah, king of Judah, who took the throne after his father and then was taken from this place: He will not return.

[12] He'll die in the place they exiled him to. He'll never see this land again.

[13] Disaster on the man who builds his house with injustice, his upper rooms with theft — who works his neighbor for nothing and refuses to pay him.

[14] Who says: I'll build myself a grand palace with spacious upper rooms. Who cuts out windows, panels it in cedar, paints it red.

[15] Does cedar make you a king? Your father ate and drank too — but he did justice and righteousness, and it went well with him.

[16] He defended the poor and the needy — and it went well. That's what it means to know me, says the LORD.

[17] But your eyes, your heart — they're set on nothing but your own gain, on shedding innocent blood, on oppression and extortion.

[18] So this is what the LORD says about Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah: No one will mourn him — no Alas, my brother! No Alas, my sister! No Alas, lord! No Alas, his majesty!

[19] He'll be buried like a donkey — dragged out and dumped beyond the gates of Jerusalem.

[20] Climb Lebanon and scream. Lift your voice in Bashan. Wail from Abarim — every one of your lovers is broken.

[21] I spoke to you when you were comfortable — you said: I'm not listening. This has been your pattern since you were young. You've never obeyed my voice.

[22] The wind will herd away your shepherds. Your lovers will march into exile. Then, finally, you'll be ashamed — humiliated by all your wickedness.

[23] You who nest in Lebanon, perched in cedar — how you'll groan when the labor pains hit, the agony of a woman in childbirth.

[24] As I live, says the LORD, even if you, Coniah son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, were the signet ring on my right hand — I'd rip you off.

[25] I'm handing you over to those who want you dead, the ones you're terrified of — Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and the Chaldeans.

[26] I'm hurling you and the mother who bore you into a land neither of you was born in. That's where you'll die.

[27] The land you long to return to — you'll never set foot in it again.

[28] Is this man Coniah some shattered, despised pot, some piece of junk nobody wants? Why are he and his children thrown out, flung into a land they don't know?

[29] Land, land, land — hear the word of the LORD!

[30] This is what the LORD says: Record this man as childless — a man who failed in his lifetime. None of his offspring will succeed. None will sit on David's throne or rule again in Judah.

[1] Disaster on the shepherds who scatter and shred the sheep of my pasture — this is the LORD's word.

[2] So here is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says to the shepherds tending my people: You scattered my flock. You drove them off. You never once checked on them. Now I will check on you — for the evil of what you have done. This is the LORD's word.

[3] And I myself will gather what is left of my flock from every country where I drove them, and I will bring them home to their own pasture. They will be fruitful. They will multiply.

[4] I will raise up shepherds over them who will actually shepherd them, and they will never again be afraid, never again be terrified, never again go missing. This is the LORD's word.

[5] Look — the days are coming, says the LORD, when I will raise up for David a Branch, a righteous one. A king who will reign with wisdom and execute justice and righteousness across the land.

[6] In his days Judah will be saved and Israel will live in safety. And this is the name they will call him: *The LORD Is Our Righteousness.*

[7] So the days are coming, declares the LORD, when people will no longer swear, "As surely as the LORD lives who brought Israel up out of Egypt" —

[8] but instead, "As surely as the LORD lives who brought the offspring of Israel up out of the north, out of all the countries where he had driven them." And they will live on their own soil again.

[9] About the prophets:

My heart is shattered inside me.
Every bone in me trembles.
I am like a drunk man,
like a man overcome by wine —
because of the LORD,
because of his holy words.

[10] The land is crawling with adulterers. Under the curse the land lies parched, the desert pastures dried to dust. Their course is evil. Their power is misused.

[11] Even prophet and priest are godless — I have found their wickedness right inside my own house, declares the LORD.

[12] So their path will turn slick beneath them — they will be shoved into the dark and fall. I am bringing disaster down on them in the year of their reckoning. This is the LORD's word.

[13] Among the prophets of Samaria I saw something disgusting: they prophesied by Baal and led my people Israel astray.

[14] But among the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen something horrifying: they commit adultery. They walk in lies. They prop up the hands of evildoers so that no one turns back from his wickedness. To me they have all become like Sodom — and the people there like Gomorrah.

[15] So this is what the LORD of Hosts says about the prophets:

I will feed them bitter herbs
and make them drink poisoned water,
because from the prophets of Jerusalem
godlessness has spread out
into every corner of the land.

[16] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Do not listen to the words of the prophets who prophesy to you. They are filling you with empty hope. The vision they speak comes from their own minds — not from the mouth of the LORD.

[17] They keep telling those who despise the LORD's word, "You'll have peace." And to everyone walking in the stubbornness of his own heart, they say, "No harm will come to you."

[18] But who has stood in the council of the LORD to see and hear his word? Who has paid attention to his word and listened?

[19] Look — the storm of the LORD! Fury has broken loose, a whirling storm bursting over the heads of the wicked.

[20] The LORD's anger will not turn back until he has done it — until he has carried out the purposes of his heart. In days to come you will understand it clearly.

[21] I did not send these prophets — but they ran. I did not speak to them — but they prophesied.

[22] If they had stood in my council, they would have proclaimed my words to my people and turned them back from their evil road, from the evil of what they were doing.

[23] Am I only a God up close, says the LORD — and not a God far off?

[24] Can anyone hide in some secret place where I cannot see him? declares the LORD. Do I not fill heaven and earth? This is the LORD's word.

[25] I have heard what the prophets are saying — the ones who prophesy lies in my name. "I had a dream! I had a dream!"

[26] How long will this go on — in the hearts of prophets who prophesy lies, prophets of their own self-deception?

[27] They think they can make my people forget my name with the dreams they keep telling each other — the same way their fathers forgot my name for Baal.

[28] Let the prophet who had a dream tell his dream. But let the one who has my word speak my word faithfully. What does straw have to do with grain? declares the LORD.

[29] Is not my word like fire — declares the LORD — like a hammer that shatters rock?

[30] So I am against the prophets, declares the LORD — the ones who steal my words from one another.

[31] I am against the prophets, declares the LORD — the ones who wag their tongues and announce, "The LORD declares."

[32] I am against those who prophesy lying dreams, declares the LORD — who tell them and lead my people astray with their lies and their reckless boasting. I never sent them. I never commissioned them. They are no help at all to this people. This is the LORD's word.

[33] When this people — or a prophet or a priest — asks you, "What is the burden of the LORD?" — you tell them: *You* are the burden. And I am throwing you off, declares the LORD.

[34] As for the prophet or priest or anyone of the people who says, "The burden of the LORD" — I will punish that man and his household.

[35] Here is what each of you should say to his neighbor and his brother: "What did the LORD answer? What has the LORD spoken?"

[36] But never again mention "the burden of the LORD" — because the burden turns out to be each man's own word. You are twisting the words of the living God, the LORD of Hosts, our God.

[37] This is what you say to a prophet: "What did the LORD answer you? What has the LORD spoken?"

[38] But if you say, "The burden of the LORD," then this is what the LORD says: Because you keep using that phrase — "the burden of the LORD" — after I sent you word: *do not say it* —

[39] then look: I will lift you up, completely. I will hurl you out of my presence, you and the city I gave to you and your fathers.

[40] And I will fasten on you an everlasting disgrace, a humiliation that will never be forgotten.

[1] After Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon hauled Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim — king of Judah — into exile from Jerusalem, along with the officials of Judah, the craftsmen, and the metalworkers, dragging them off to Babylon, the LORD showed me something. Two baskets of figs, set out in front of the temple of the LORD.

[2] One basket held figs so good they looked like the season's first ripe pick. The other basket held figs so rotten you couldn't put them in your mouth.

[3] The LORD said to me, "Jeremiah, what do you see?" I said, "Figs. The good ones, very good. The bad ones, very bad — too foul to eat."

[4] Then the word of the LORD came to me:

[5] "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: Like these good figs, that is how I will regard the exiles of Judah — the ones I sent out from this place into the land of the Babylonians. For their good.

[6] I will fix my eyes on them with favor. I will bring them home to this land. I will build them up and not tear them down. I will plant them and not pull them up.

[7] I will give them a heart that knows me — knows that I am the LORD. They will be my people, and I will be their God, because they will turn back to me with everything in them.

[8] But like the rotten figs — too foul to eat — that is how I will treat Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, what's left of Jerusalem still in this land, and the ones who took refuge in Egypt. So says the LORD.

[9] I will make them a horror, a disaster, to every kingdom on earth. A byword. A punchline. A curse and a taunt wherever I scatter them.

[10] I will send sword, famine, and plague chasing them down until they are wiped clean off the soil I gave their ancestors — and gave to them."

[1] This is the word that came to Jeremiah about all the people of Judah, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah — the same year Nebuchadnezzar took the throne in Babylon.

[2] Jeremiah the prophet delivered it to everyone in Judah and everyone living in Jerusalem:

[3] For twenty-three years — from the thirteenth year of Josiah son of Amon, king of Judah, until this very day — the word of the LORD has been coming to me, and I have spoken it to you again and again. You did not listen.

[4] And the LORD kept sending you his servants the prophets — sending them over and over. You did not listen. You did not even bother to turn your head.

[5] Their message was simple: "Turn back, every one of you, from your evil road, from the corruption of what you're doing. Then you can stay in the land the LORD gave to you and your ancestors — yours forever.

[6] Do not chase after other gods to serve them and bow down to them. Do not provoke me with the idols your own hands have made — and I will not bring disaster on you."

[7] But you didn't listen, says the LORD. You provoked me anyway, with the work of your hands — to your own ruin.

[8] So here is what the LORD of Heaven's Armies says: Because you refused to listen to my words,

[9] I am sending for every clan from the north — this is the LORD's declaration — and for my servant Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon. I am bringing them down on this land and everyone in it, and on all the nations around. I will set them apart for destruction. I will turn them into a horror, a hiss, a wasteland forever.

[10] I will silence the sound of joy and gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the bride, the grinding of the millstone, the light of the lamp.

[11] This whole land will become rubble and ruin, and these nations will serve the king of Babylon for seventy years.

[12] But when those seventy years are up, I will turn on the king of Babylon and that nation — this is the LORD's declaration — for their guilt. I will turn the land of the Chaldeans into a desolation forever.

[13] I will bring down on that land every word I have spoken against it — everything written in this scroll, everything Jeremiah prophesied against all the nations.

[14] Many nations and great kings will press them into service in turn. I will pay them back for what their hands have done.

[15] This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, said to me: Take this cup of wrath from my hand and make every nation I send you to drink from it.

[16] They will drink and stagger and lose their minds — because of the sword I am sending among them.

[17] So I took the cup from the LORD's hand and made every nation he sent me to drink:

[18] Jerusalem and the towns of Judah, her kings and her officials — to turn them into a ruin, a horror, a hiss, a curse, which is exactly what they are today.

[19] Pharaoh king of Egypt, his servants, his officials, his whole people,

[20] and the mongrel crowd among them; all the kings of the land of Uz; all the kings of Philistia — Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron, and what's left of Ashdod;

[21] Edom, Moab, the Ammonites;

[22] all the kings of Tyre and Sidon, the kings of the coastlands across the sea;

[23] Dedan, Tema, Buz, and everyone with shaved temples;

[24] all the kings of Arabia and all the kings of the desert nomads;

[25] all the kings of Zimri, Elam, and Media;

[26] all the kings of the north, near and far, one after another — every kingdom on the face of the earth. And after all of them, the king of Sheshach will drink.

[27] Tell them: This is what the LORD of Heaven's Armies, the God of Israel, says — drink. Get drunk. Vomit. Fall down and do not get up, because of the sword I am sending among you.

[28] And if they refuse to take the cup from your hand and drink it, tell them: The LORD of Heaven's Armies says — you will drink it. There is no way out.

[29] Look — I am starting with the city that bears my name. Do you really think you will get off clean? You will not get off clean. I am calling down a sword on every inhabitant of the earth. This is the declaration of the LORD of Heaven's Armies.

[30] So you — prophesy all these words against them. Tell them:

The LORD roars from the heights.
From his holy dwelling he thunders.
He roars like a lion over his pasture.
He shouts like men trampling grapes —
the cry rolls out against everyone on earth.

[31] The uproar reaches the ends of the earth,
for the LORD has a case against the nations.
He is bringing every living thing to trial.
The guilty — he gives to the sword.
This is the LORD's declaration.

[32] This is what the LORD of Heaven's Armies says:

Disaster is unfolding —
nation to nation —
a massive storm churning up
from the farthest corners of the earth.

[33] On that day, the LORD's slain will stretch from one end of the earth to the other. No one will mourn them. No one will gather them up or bury them. They will lie like dung on the ground.

[34] Wail, you shepherds. Cry out.
Roll in the dust, you lords of the flock.
Your slaughter has come, your scattering —
you will fall like a precious vessel
shattered on the stones.

[35] No refuge for the shepherds.
No escape for the lords of the flock.

[36] Listen — the shepherds are screaming.
The lords of the flock are howling.
The LORD is ravaging their pasture.

[37] The peaceful meadows lie silent
under the heat of the LORD's burning anger.

[38] He has left his den like a young lion.
Their land is a wasteland
under the cruelty of the oppressor,
under the burn of his anger.

[1] Early in the reign of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah, this word came from the LORD:

[2] "Stand in the courtyard of my house. Speak to everyone who comes from the towns of Judah to worship there. Give them every word I command you. Don't trim a syllable.

[3] Maybe they'll listen. Maybe each one will turn from his ruinous road, and I'll relent — call off the disaster I'm preparing for them because of the evil they've done.

[4] Tell them: This is what the LORD says — If you won't listen to me, won't walk in the law I've set before you,

[5] won't heed the words of my servants the prophets — the ones I've kept sending, again and again, though you never listened —

[6] then I will make this house like Shiloh. This city I will turn into a curse word among all the nations on earth."

[7] The priests, the prophets, the whole crowd — they heard Jeremiah speaking these words in the house of the LORD.

[8] And the moment he finished saying everything the LORD had ordered him to say, the priests and the prophets and the whole crowd seized him. "You die for this," they said.

[9] "How dare you prophesy in the name of the LORD that this house will go the way of Shiloh — that this city will be emptied, abandoned?" The whole crowd closed in on Jeremiah inside the house of the LORD.

[10] When the officials of Judah heard what was happening, they came up from the palace to the house of the LORD and took their seats at the entrance of the New Gate.

[11] The priests and the prophets addressed the officials and the assembled crowd: "This man deserves a death sentence. He prophesied against this city — you heard him with your own ears."

[12] Then Jeremiah spoke to all the officials and all the people: "The LORD sent me. He sent me to prophesy against this house and this city every word you have heard.

[13] So now — straighten out your ways, your actions. Listen to the voice of the LORD your God. And the LORD will relent — he will call off the disaster he has spoken against you.

[14] As for me, I'm in your hands. Do with me whatever seems right and good to you.

[15] But know this for certain: if you kill me, you'll be bringing innocent blood down on yourselves, on this city, on everyone who lives here. Because the truth is — the LORD sent me to you to speak every one of these words in your hearing."

[16] Then the officials and the whole crowd said to the priests and the prophets: "This man does not deserve death. He has spoken to us in the name of the LORD our God."

[17] Some of the elders of the land stood up and addressed the entire assembly:

[18] "Micah of Moresheth prophesied in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah. He told all the people of Judah: 'This is what the LORD of Hosts says — Zion will be plowed like a field, Jerusalem will become a heap of rubble, and the temple mount will revert to wild forest.'

[19] Did Hezekiah king of Judah and all Judah put him to death for that? No — Hezekiah feared the LORD. He sought the LORD's favor, and the LORD relented and called off the disaster he had spoken against them. And we — we're about to bring down catastrophic evil on our own heads."

[20] There was another man prophesying in the name of the LORD — Uriah son of Shemaiah, from Kiriath-jearim. He prophesied against this city and this land in words very much like Jeremiah's.

[21] King Jehoiakim and all his warriors and officials heard him, and the king tried to have him killed. When Uriah found out, he was terrified and fled to Egypt.

[22] But King Jehoiakim sent men after him — Elnathan son of Achbor, with a contingent — into Egypt.

[23] They dragged Uriah out of Egypt and brought him back to King Jehoiakim, who put him to the sword and threw his body into a common grave.

[24] But Ahikam son of Shaphan stood by Jeremiah, and he was not handed over to the crowd to be killed.

[1] At the start of Zedekiah son of Josiah's reign over Judah, this word came to Jeremiah from the LORD.

[2] The LORD told me: Make yourself a yoke — straps and crossbars — and strap it onto your own neck.

[3] Then send word to the kings of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon. Use the envoys who've come to Jerusalem to meet with King Zedekiah of Judah.

[4] Give them this message for their masters — and tell them the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says to say this:

[5] I am the one who made the earth — every human, every animal — by my outstretched arm and overwhelming power. I hand it to whoever I choose.

[6] And right now, I have handed every one of these lands to my servant Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon. Even the wild animals are his to command.

[7] Every nation will serve him, and his son, and his grandson — until the hour of his own kingdom comes, when the great powers and their kings will harness him instead.

[8] Any nation, any kingdom, that refuses to bend its neck under the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar — I will visit that nation with sword and famine and plague, says the LORD, until I have finished them off by his hand.

[9] So don't listen to your prophets, your diviners, your dreamers, your fortune-tellers, your sorcerers — the ones telling you, You won't have to serve the king of Babylon.

[10] They're lying to you. The result will be exile — you ripped from your own soil, driven out, finished.

[11] But the nation that bows its neck under the yoke of Babylon's king and serves him — that nation I will leave on its own land, says the LORD. They will work it. They will live on it.

[12] I delivered the same message to Zedekiah king of Judah: Bend your necks under the yoke of the king of Babylon. Serve him. Serve his people. Live.

[13] Why should you die — you and your people — by sword and famine and plague, the way the LORD has warned every nation that refuses to serve the king of Babylon?

[14] Stop listening to the prophets who keep telling you, You won't have to serve the king of Babylon. They're lying.

[15] I never sent them, says the LORD. They prophesy in my name — falsely — and the outcome will be this: I will drive you out, and you will die, you and the prophets who keep prophesying to you.

[16] To the priests and to all these people I said: This is what the LORD says. Don't listen to your prophets telling you the vessels of the LORD's house are about to be brought back from Babylon, any moment now. They're lying to you.

[17] Don't listen to them. Serve the king of Babylon and live. Why turn this city into a graveyard?

[18] If they really are prophets, if the word of the LORD really is with them, then let them intercede with the LORD of Armies — let them beg him that the vessels still in the LORD's house, in the king of Judah's palace, in Jerusalem itself, not also be carted off to Babylon.

[19] Because this is what the LORD of Armies says about the pillars, the bronze sea, the stands, and the rest of the vessels still in this city —

[20] the ones Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon did not take when he deported Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, from Jerusalem to Babylon along with all the nobility of Judah and Jerusalem —

[21] yes, this is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says about the vessels left in the LORD's house, the king's palace, and Jerusalem:

[22] They will be hauled off to Babylon. They will stay there until the day I attend to them, says the LORD. Then I will bring them up. I will return them to this place.

[1] That same year, early in the reign of Zedekiah king of Judah—the fifth month of the fourth year—Hananiah son of Azzur, a prophet from Gibeon, confronted me in the house of the LORD, right in front of the priests and everyone gathered there. He said:

[2] "This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: I have shattered the yoke of the king of Babylon.

[3] Within two years I will bring back to this place every sacred vessel of the LORD's house that Nebuchadnezzar carried off to Babylon.

[4] And Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, along with every exile from Judah hauled into Babylon—I am bringing them home too. I will break the yoke of the king of Babylon."

[5] Then Jeremiah the prophet answered Hananiah the prophet, with the priests and all the people listening in the house of the LORD.

[6] Jeremiah said: "Amen. May the LORD do it. May the LORD make your words come true—may he bring back the vessels of his house and every exile from Babylon to this place.

[7] But hear this word I'm about to speak in your ears and in the ears of all these people:

[8] The prophets who came before me and before you, from ancient times—they prophesied war, disaster, and plague against many lands and great kingdoms.

[9] The prophet who prophesies peace—only when his word comes to pass will it be known that the LORD truly sent him."

[10] Then Hananiah the prophet took the yoke off the neck of Jeremiah the prophet and smashed it.

[11] And Hananiah declared in front of everyone: "This is what the LORD says: Just like this, within two years I will break the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon from the neck of every nation." And Jeremiah the prophet walked away.

[12] After Hananiah broke the yoke off Jeremiah's neck, the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[13] "Go and tell Hananiah: This is what the LORD says—you have broken a yoke of wood, but in its place you will forge a yoke of iron.

[14] Because this is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: I have fastened an iron yoke on the neck of all these nations. They will serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and serve him they will. I have handed him even the wild beasts of the field."

[15] Then Jeremiah the prophet said to Hananiah the prophet: "Listen to me, Hananiah. The LORD did not send you. You have made this people trust a lie.

[16] So this is what the LORD says: I am about to send you off the face of the earth. This year you die—because you have preached rebellion against the LORD."

[17] And in the seventh month of that very same year, Hananiah the prophet died.

[1] This is the letter the prophet Jeremiah sent from Jerusalem to the surviving elders of the exile, to the priests and prophets and all the people Nebuchadnezzar had dragged from Jerusalem to Babylon.

[2] It went out after King Jeconiah and the queen mother, the court officials, the leaders of Judah and Jerusalem, the craftsmen and the metalworkers had all been hauled away.

[3] He sent it by the hand of Elasah son of Shaphan and Gemariah son of Hilkiah — men Zedekiah king of Judah had dispatched to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon. The letter read:

[4] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says to every exile I sent from Jerusalem to Babylon:

[5] Build houses. Live in them. Plant gardens. Eat what they produce.

[6] Marry. Have sons and daughters. Find wives for your sons, give your daughters to husbands, let them have children of their own. Multiply there. Do not dwindle.

[7] And seek the welfare of the city where I have exiled you. Pray for it. Because if it flourishes, you flourish.

[8] Hear me — the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel: Do not let the prophets among you fool you. Do not let your fortune-tellers deceive you. Do not pay attention to the dreams they keep dreaming up —

[9] because what they prophesy in my name is a lie. I never sent them. This is the LORD's word.

[10] Here is what the LORD actually says: When Babylon's seventy years are finished, I will come for you. I will keep my promise. I will bring you home.

[11] I know exactly what I am doing with you — this is the LORD speaking. Plans for your flourishing, not your ruin. Plans to give you a future. Plans to give you hope.

[12] You will call to me. You will come. You will pray, and I will listen.

[13] You will search for me, and you will find me — when you search with everything in you.

[14] I will let myself be found, says the LORD. I will reverse your captivity. I will gather you from every nation, every place I scattered you, and I will bring you back to the land I exiled you from. This is the LORD's word.

[15] Because you keep saying, "The LORD has raised up prophets for us right here in Babylon" —

[16] hear what the LORD says about the king sitting on David's throne and all the people still left in this city, your own kin who were not deported with you:

[17] This is what the LORD of Armies says — I am sending sword, famine, and plague against them. I will make them like rotten figs, too spoiled to eat.

[18] I will hunt them down with sword and famine and plague. I will make them a horror to every kingdom on earth — a curse, an object of shock, a hiss of scorn, a byword among all the nations I scatter them to,

[19] because they refused to listen to my words, says the LORD — words I sent again and again through my servants the prophets. You refused to listen. This is the LORD's word.

[20] So listen — all of you exiles I sent from Jerusalem to Babylon — to the word of the LORD.

[21] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says about Ahab son of Kolaiah and Zedekiah son of Maaseiah, who are prophesying lies to you in my name: I am handing them over to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and he will execute them before your eyes.

[22] And from them every exile in Babylon will take up a curse, saying: "May the LORD make you like Zedekiah and Ahab — whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire."

[23] Because they committed an outrage in Israel. They slept with their neighbors' wives. They spoke lies in my name — words I never commanded. I know. I am the witness. This is the LORD's word.

[24] And to Shemaiah the Nehelamite, say this:

[25] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: You sent letters in your own name to all the people in Jerusalem, to Zephaniah son of Maaseiah the priest, and to all the priests, saying —

[26] "The LORD has made you priest in place of Jehoiada, to be the chief officer of the house of the LORD. Anyone who acts like a madman and plays the prophet — your job is to clamp him in stocks and an iron collar.

[27] So why have you not rebuked Jeremiah of Anathoth, who is playing the prophet to you?

[28] He even sent word to us in Babylon saying, 'This exile is going to be long. Build houses. Settle in. Plant gardens. Eat what they yield.'"

[29] Zephaniah the priest read the letter aloud in the hearing of Jeremiah the prophet.

[30] Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[31] Send word to all the exiles. Tell them: This is what the LORD says about Shemaiah the Nehelamite — Because Shemaiah has prophesied to you when I never sent him, and made you trust a lie,

[32] this is what the LORD says: I am about to deal with Shemaiah the Nehelamite and his descendants. He will have no one left among this people to see the good I am going to do for my people — declares the LORD — because he preached rebellion against the LORD.

[1] The word came to Jeremiah from the LORD:

[2] This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — write down every word I have spoken to you in a book.

[3] The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will reverse the captivity of my people Israel and Judah. I will bring them back to the land I gave their ancestors, and they will possess it again.

[4] Here is what the LORD said concerning Israel and Judah:

[5] This is what the LORD says: I hear screaming — terror, no peace.

[6] Ask around. See if a man ever gave birth. Then why am I watching every grown man clutching his stomach like a woman in labor? Why has every face gone pale as death?

[7] That day — there has never been one like it. A day of catastrophe for Jacob. But he will be pulled out of it.

[8] On that day, declares the LORD of Armies, I will snap the yoke off their necks and rip the chains off their wrists. Foreigners will never enslave them again.

[9] Instead they will serve the LORD their God and David their king — the one I will raise up for them.

[10] So you — Jacob, my servant — do not be afraid, declares the LORD. Israel, do not panic. I am coming to save you from that distant land, your descendants from the country of their exile. Jacob will come home to peace and quiet, and no one will make him flinch.

[11] I am with you, declares the LORD, here to save you. I will wipe out every nation where I scattered you — but I will not wipe you out. I will discipline you, yes — fairly — but I will not let you off entirely unpunished.

[12] This is what the LORD says: Your wound is incurable. Your injury beyond healing.

[13] No one will plead your case. No medicine for your sore. No recovery for you.

[14] Every lover has forgotten you. None of them is looking for you. I struck you the way an enemy strikes — savage discipline — because your guilt is massive, your sins more than I can count.

[15] Why are you crying about your wound? The pain is unstoppable. I did this to you — because your guilt is massive, your sins beyond counting.

[16] But everyone who devoured you will be devoured. Every one of your enemies — all of them — will march into exile. Those who plundered you will be plundered. Those who looted you I will hand over to looters.

[17] I will bring your health back. I will heal your wounds, declares the LORD — because they called you Outcast, the Zion nobody wants.

[18] This is what the LORD says: I am about to reverse the fortunes of Jacob's tents and have compassion on his dwellings. The city will be rebuilt on its mound of ruins. The palace will stand where palaces stand.

[19] Out of them will rise thanksgiving — the sound of people celebrating. I will multiply them; they will not dwindle. I will give them weight; they will not be brushed aside.

[20] Their children will be as they once were. Their community will stand firm in my presence. And I will deal with everyone who oppresses them.

[21] Their leader will be one of their own. Their ruler will come from among them. I will bring him close, and he will approach me — because who would dare risk his life to draw near me? declares the LORD.

[22] You will be my people. I will be your God.

[23] Look — the storm of the LORD has broken loose, fury unleashed, a whirlwind raging — it will not stop until it crashes down on the heads of the wicked.

[24] The LORD's burning anger will not turn back until he has carried out everything in his heart, finished it completely. In the days ahead, you will understand this.

[1] When that moment comes, says the LORD, I will be God to every clan of Israel, and they will be my people.

[2] This is what the LORD says: The people who survived the sword found grace out in the wilderness. Israel, on the way to her rest—

[3] from a distance, the LORD appeared to me: I have loved you with a love that never ends. That is why I have drawn you in with unbroken kindness.

[4] I will rebuild you, and you will stand again, Virgin Israel. You will pick up your tambourines again and dance out among the celebrating.

[5] Again you will plant vineyards on the hills of Samaria—planters will plant, and they themselves will enjoy the harvest.

[6] A day is coming when watchmen on the heights of Ephraim will shout: Get up! Let us go up to Zion, to the LORD our God!

[7] This is what the LORD says: Sing for Jacob with joy! Shout over the chief of nations! Let your praise ring out: LORD, save your people, the remnant of Israel!

[8] Watch—I am bringing them home from the north country, gathering them from the ends of the earth. The blind and the lame, pregnant women and women in labor—a vast crowd, returning.

[9] They will come weeping, and I will lead them as they pray. I will walk them beside streams of water along a level path where they cannot stumble. Because I am Israel's father, and Ephraim is my firstborn son.

[10] Listen to the word of the LORD, you nations! Announce it to the distant shores: The one who scattered Israel will gather her back and guard her like a shepherd guards his flock.

[11] The LORD has ransomed Jacob, rescued him from a hand stronger than his own.

[12] They will come and sing on the heights of Zion. They will stream toward the LORD's good gifts—the grain, the wine, the olive oil, the young of the flocks and herds. Their lives will be like a well-watered garden, and they will never languish again.

[13] Then young women will dance with joy, young men and old together. I will turn their mourning into celebration. I will comfort them. I will trade their sorrow for gladness.

[14] I will saturate the priests with abundance, and my people will have their fill of my goodness. The LORD has spoken.

[15] This is what the LORD says: A voice is heard in Ramah—weeping, bitter grieving. Rachel is weeping for her children and refuses to be comforted, because they are gone.

[16] But this is what the LORD says: Stop your crying. Wipe your eyes. There is a reward for what you have endured, says the LORD—your children will come home from the land of the enemy.

[17] There is a future for you, says the LORD. Your children will return to their own country.

[18] I have heard Ephraim grieving over himself: You disciplined me, and I took the blow like an untrained calf. Bring me back—let me come back, because you are the LORD my God.

[19] After I turned away, I felt the regret cut deep. After I finally understood, I beat my chest in shame. I was humiliated, disgraced, because I carried the dishonor of my youth.

[20] Is Ephraim not my dear son, my own beloved child? Every time I speak against him, I still remember him. My heart aches for him. I will have mercy on him without fail. The LORD has spoken.

[21] Set up road markers. Plant signposts. Pay attention to the highway, the road you traveled. Come home, Virgin Israel—come back to these cities of yours.

[22] How long will you keep wandering, faithless daughter? The LORD is doing something new on the earth: a woman will surround a man.

[23] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: When I restore their fortunes, they will say this again in the land of Judah and in its cities: May the LORD bless you, righteous home, holy mountain.

[24] Judah and all her cities will live there together—farmers and those who move with the flocks.

[25] I will refresh the weary and fill up everyone who is fading.

[26] At this point I woke up and looked around. My sleep had been sweet.

[27] Days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will sow the house of Israel and the house of Judah with the seed of both people and animals.

[28] Just as I watched over them to uproot, tear down, demolish, destroy, and bring disaster—so I will watch over them to build and to plant. The LORD has spoken.

[29] In those days, no one will say anymore: The fathers ate sour grapes, and the children's teeth got the ache.

[30] Each person will die for his own sin. Whoever eats the sour grapes—his own teeth will ache.

[31] Look—days are coming, says the LORD, when I will cut a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.

[32] Not like the covenant I made with their ancestors on the day I took them by the hand to lead them out of Egypt. That covenant they broke, even though I was their husband, says the LORD.

[33] This is the covenant I will make with the house of Israel after those days, says the LORD: I will plant my law inside them and write it on their hearts. I will be their God, and they will be my people.

[34] No one will need to teach his neighbor or his brother, saying, Know the LORD—because all of them will know me, from the smallest to the greatest. I will forgive their guilt and never call their sin to mind again.

[35] This is what the LORD says—the one who set the sun to shine by day, who ordered the moon and stars for the night, who stirs the sea so its waves crash; the LORD of Armies is his name:

[36] Only if these fixed laws collapse from before me, says the LORD, will Israel's offspring ever cease to be a nation before me.

[37] This is what the LORD says: Only if someone can measure the heavens above and chart the foundations of the earth below—only then would I reject all the offspring of Israel for everything they have done. The LORD has spoken.

[38] Days are coming, says the LORD, when the city will be rebuilt for the LORD—from the Tower of Hananel to the Corner Gate.

[39] The measuring line will stretch straight out to the hill of Gareb and curve around to Goah.

[40] The whole valley of corpses and ashes, and all the fields out to the Kidron and the corner of the Horse Gate to the east—it will all be holy to the LORD. It will never be uprooted or torn down again.

[1] This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD in the tenth year of Zedekiah king of Judah — which was the eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar.

[2] By then the army of Babylon had Jerusalem in a stranglehold, and Jeremiah the prophet was locked up in the courtyard of the guard inside the palace of the king of Judah.

[3] Zedekiah had thrown him there. The king had demanded: "Why do you keep prophesying like this? You say, 'Thus says the LORD: I am handing this city over to the king of Babylon. He will take it.

[4] Zedekiah king of Judah will not escape the Chaldeans — he will be delivered straight into the hands of the king of Babylon. He will speak with him face to face. He will see him eye to eye.

[5] Zedekiah will be hauled off to Babylon, and there he will stay until I deal with him, declares the LORD. Fight the Chaldeans all you want — you will not win.'"

[6] Jeremiah said: The word of the LORD came to me:

[7] Listen — Hanamel, the son of your uncle Shallum, is coming to you. He's going to say, "Buy my field in Anathoth. You're next in line. The right of redemption is yours."

[8] And just as the LORD had said, my cousin Hanamel showed up in the courtyard of the guard and said to me, "Buy my field in Anathoth, in the territory of Benjamin. The inheritance is yours. The redemption is yours. Buy it." Then I knew — this was the LORD.

[9] So I bought the field from my cousin Hanamel in Anathoth. I weighed out the silver — seventeen shekels.

[10] I signed the deed, sealed it, brought in witnesses, and weighed the silver on the scales.

[11] Then I took the deed — the sealed copy with the terms and conditions, and the open copy —

[12] and I handed it to Baruch son of Neriah, son of Mahseiah. I did it in front of Hanamel my cousin, in front of the witnesses who had signed the deed, and in front of all the Judeans sitting there in the courtyard of the guard.

[13] With them all watching, I gave Baruch these instructions:

[14] "This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: Take these deeds — the sealed one and the open one — and put them in a clay jar. They need to last a long time.

[15] Because this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: Houses and fields and vineyards will be bought and sold in this land again."

[16] After I handed the deed to Baruch son of Neriah, I prayed to the LORD:

[17] "Sovereign LORD. You made the heavens and the earth by your outstretched arm and your overwhelming power. Nothing is too hard for you.

[18] You pour out love on thousands, but you also lay the consequences of fathers' sins onto the laps of their children after them. Great God. Mighty God. The LORD of Hosts is your name.

[19] Vast in purpose. Mighty in action. Your eyes are open to every road every human walks — you reward each one according to their ways, according to the fruit of what they do.

[20] You worked signs and wonders in Egypt — and you've kept working them, in Israel and among the nations, right up to this day. You've made a name for yourself that still stands.

[21] You brought your people Israel out of Egypt with signs and wonders, with a strong hand and an outstretched arm and terror that shook the earth.

[22] You gave them this land — the land you swore to their ancestors you would give them — a land flowing with milk and honey.

[23] They came in. They took it. But they would not listen to your voice. They would not walk by your instruction. Nothing you commanded — they did none of it. So you brought all this catastrophe down on them.

[24] Look at the siege ramps. They're piled right up to the walls. The city is about to fall to the Chaldeans who are fighting against it — falling to the sword, to famine, to plague. What you said would happen is happening. You see it. You're watching it now.

[25] And yet you, Sovereign LORD, said to me: 'Buy the field. Pay in silver. Get witnesses' — even as the city is being handed over to the Chaldeans."

[26] Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[27] "I am the LORD, the God of all flesh. Is anything too hard for me?

[28] So here is what the LORD says: I am handing this city over to the Chaldeans, to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon. He will take it.

[29] The Chaldeans who are attacking this city will come in. They will set it on fire. They will burn it down — along with all the houses where the people stoked my fury by burning incense to Baal on their roofs and pouring out drink offerings to other gods.

[30] The people of Israel and Judah have done nothing but evil in my sight from the time they were young. The people of Israel have done nothing but provoke me with the work of their hands. This is the LORD's word.

[31] This city has been nothing but a fire in my face from the day they built it until now. It needs to come down.

[32] All the evil the people of Israel and Judah have done — all of it — has been calculated to provoke me: them, their kings, their officials, their priests, their prophets, the men of Judah, the residents of Jerusalem.

[33] They turned their backs to me, not their faces. I taught them — taught them again and again — but they would not listen. They would not learn.

[34] They set up their disgusting idols inside the house that bears my name and defiled it.

[35] They built shrines to Baal in the Valley of Ben-Hinnom and used them to pass their sons and daughters through the fire to Molech — something I never commanded, something that never even crossed my mind: that they would commit this obscenity and pull Judah into sin.

[36] And yet — this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says about this city you keep saying will fall to the king of Babylon by sword and famine and plague:

[37] I am going to gather them. From every country where I drove them in my burning rage, I will bring them back to this place. I will let them live here in safety.

[38] They will be my people. I will be their God.
