[39] I will give them one heart and one way — so they will revere me always — for their good and the good of their children after them.

[40] I will cut an everlasting covenant with them. I will never stop doing good to them. I will plant the fear of me deep in their hearts, so they will never turn away from me again.

[41] I will delight in doing them good. With my whole heart and my whole soul, I will plant them in this land — and mean it.

[42] This is what the LORD says: Just as I brought all this catastrophe down on this people, I will bring on them every good thing I have promised.

[43] Fields will be bought in this land — this land you call a wasteland, abandoned, given over to the Chaldeans.

[44] People will buy fields with silver. They will sign deeds, seal them, call in witnesses — in the territory of Benjamin, in the villages around Jerusalem, in the cities of Judah, in the hill country, in the lowlands, in the Negev. Because I will restore them. This is the LORD's word."

[1] The word of the LORD came to Jeremiah a second time while he was still locked up in the courtyard of the guard:

[2] This is what the LORD says — the One who made the earth, the One who shaped it and set it firm, the One whose name is the LORD:

[3] Call to me. I will answer. I will tell you staggering things, hidden things, things you could never figure out on your own.

[4] Here is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says about the houses of this city and the palaces of Judah's kings — the ones torn down to throw up ramparts against the siege ramps and the sword:

[5] They went out to fight the Babylonians, and now those houses will be stuffed with corpses — men I struck down in my fury, in my rage. I have turned my face away from this city because of everything they have done wrong.

[6] But watch — I am going to bring healing. I will mend what is broken. I will pour out on them a flood of peace and truth they never imagined.

[7] I will bring Judah and Israel back from exile, and I will rebuild them — they will stand exactly as they did at the beginning.

[8] I will scrub them clean of every sin they committed against me. I will forgive every act of rebellion.

[9] And this city will become my crown — my joy, my praise, my glory — in front of every nation on earth. When they hear what I am doing for these people, they will shake. They will tremble at the goodness, the sheer prosperity, I pour out on this place.

[10] This is what the LORD says: You call this place a wasteland — no people, no animals. The streets of Judah, the squares of Jerusalem, lie deserted, empty, silent. But listen — there will be heard again

[11] the sound of joy and the sound of celebration, the voice of the groom and the voice of the bride, the voices of those bringing thanksgiving into the house of the LORD, singing:

*Give thanks to the LORD of Hosts,
for the LORD is good,
and his love never ends.*

I will restore the fortunes of this land — they will be as they were at the beginning. The LORD has said it.

[12] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: In this place — this wasteland, empty of people and animals, every town a graveyard — there will once again be pastures where shepherds rest their flocks.

[13] In the towns of the hills, the towns of the lowlands, the towns of the Negev, in Benjamin's territory and the villages around Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, flocks will once again pass under the hand that counts them. The LORD has said it.

[14] The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will make good on the promise I made to the house of Israel and the house of Judah.

[15] In those days, at that time, I will cause a righteous Branch to sprout from David's line. He will do what is just and right in the land.

[16] In those days Judah will be rescued and Jerusalem will live in safety. And this is the name she will be called by: *The LORD Our Righteousness.*

[17] Here is what the LORD says: David will never lack a man to sit on the throne of the house of Israel.

[18] And the Levitical priests will never lack a man standing before me, bringing burnt offerings, burning grain offerings, presenting sacrifices — day after day, without end.

[19] The word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[20] This is what the LORD says: If you could shatter my covenant with the day and my covenant with the night — so that day and night no longer arrive at their appointed times —

[21] then, and only then, could my covenant with my servant David be broken, so that he no longer has a son to reign on his throne, and my covenant with the Levitical priests who serve me.

[22] As innumerable as the stars of the sky, as uncountable as the sand on the shore — that is how vast I will make the descendants of my servant David and the Levites who serve me.

[23] The word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[24] Have you noticed what these people are saying? *The LORD picked two families and then threw them away.* They despise my people. They no longer count them as a nation at all.

[25] This is what the LORD says: If I have not established my covenant with day and night, if I have not fixed the laws that govern heaven and earth —

[26] then I will reject the descendants of Jacob and David my servant, and I will not choose any of his line to rule over the descendants of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. But I will restore their fortunes. I will have compassion on them.

[1] This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD while Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and his entire army, along with every kingdom of the earth under his control and all the peoples with him, were laying siege to Jerusalem and every town around her:

[2] This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — Go. Tell Zedekiah king of Judah, here is what the LORD declares: I am handing this city over to the king of Babylon. He will burn it to the ground.

[3] You will not slip away. You will be seized, dragged before him, and stand face to face with the king of Babylon. He will speak to you mouth to mouth, and Babylon is where you are going.

[4] But hear the word of the LORD, Zedekiah king of Judah. This is what the LORD says about you: You will not die by the sword.

[5] You will die in peace. They will burn spices for you the way they did for the kings who reigned before you, your ancestors. They will mourn you crying, "Oh, master!" I myself am making this promise — declares the LORD.

[6] So Jeremiah the prophet delivered every word of this to Zedekiah king of Judah, in Jerusalem,

[7] while the army of the king of Babylon was hammering Jerusalem and the last two cities of Judah still standing — Lachish and Azekah. They were the only fortified cities left.

[8] The word came to Jeremiah from the LORD after King Zedekiah had made a covenant with all the people in Jerusalem to proclaim freedom to the slaves —

[9] that every man should release his Hebrew slaves, both male and female, so that no one would keep a fellow Jew, a brother, in bondage.

[10] All the officials and all the people who had entered the covenant agreed to set their slaves free and not hold them any longer. They agreed. They let them go.

[11] But then they turned around. They took back the men and women they had freed and forced them back into slavery.

[12] So the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[13] This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — I cut a covenant with your ancestors on the day I brought them out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery. I told them,

[14] Every seventh year, each of you must release the Hebrew brother who sold himself to you. He serves you six years, then you let him go free. But your ancestors did not listen. They did not bend their ears to me.

[15] Recently you turned. You did what was right in my sight. Each of you proclaimed freedom for your neighbor. You made a covenant before me, in the house that bears my name.

[16] Then you turned again. You profaned my name. Each of you took back the slaves you had set free — the ones you had released to go wherever they wanted — and forced them back into slavery.

[17] So this is what the LORD says: You did not listen to me. You did not proclaim freedom — each man to his brother, each man to his neighbor. Fine. I now proclaim freedom for you — declares the LORD — freedom to fall by sword, plague, and famine. I will make you a horror to every kingdom on earth.

[18] The men who broke my covenant, who did not honor the terms of the covenant they cut before me — I will treat them like the calf they cut in two and walked between.

[19] The officials of Judah, the officials of Jerusalem, the court officers, the priests, all the people of the land who walked between the pieces of that calf —

[20] I will hand them over to their enemies, to those who want them dead. Their corpses will become carrion for the birds of the sky and the beasts of the earth.

[21] And Zedekiah king of Judah and his officials — I will hand them to their enemies, to those who want them dead, to the army of the king of Babylon that has just pulled back from you.

[22] Watch. I am giving the order — declares the LORD — and I am bringing them back to this city. They will fight against it. They will capture it. They will burn it down. And the towns of Judah — I will make them a wasteland with no one living in them.

[1] This is what came to Jeremiah from the LORD back in the days of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah:

[2] "Go to the Rechabite clan. Talk with them. Bring them into the LORD's house, into one of the side rooms, and set wine in front of them."

[3] So I went and got Jaazaniah son of Jeremiah son of Habazziniah — along with his brothers, all his sons, the whole Rechabite household.

[4] I brought them into the LORD's house, into the chamber belonging to the sons of Hanan son of Igdaliah, the man of God — a room right next to the officials' chamber, just above the chamber of Maaseiah son of Shallum, the doorkeeper.

[5] I set out pitchers brimming with wine and cups, and I said to them, "Drink."

[6] They said: "We do not drink wine. Jonadab son of Rechab, our ancestor, commanded us: 'You shall never drink wine — you or your sons — ever.

[7] Don't build houses. Don't sow seed. Don't plant vineyards or own them. Live in tents all your days, so you'll live long on the land where you sojourn.'

[8] We have obeyed the voice of Jonadab our ancestor in everything he commanded us — never tasting wine, not us, not our wives, not our sons or daughters,

[9] not building houses to live in, owning no vineyard, no field, no seed.

[10] We've lived in tents. We've listened. We've done everything Jonadab our ancestor commanded.

[11] When Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon swept into the land, we said, 'Come, let's get inside Jerusalem — away from the Chaldean and Aramean armies.' That's why we're staying in Jerusalem."

[12] Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[13] "This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Go and tell the men of Judah and the people of Jerusalem — Will you not learn the lesson? Will you not listen to my words? — declares the LORD.

[14] The command of Jonadab son of Rechab, telling his descendants not to drink wine, has been kept. To this day they don't drink — they obey their ancestor's command. But I — I have spoken to you again and again, and you have not listened to me.

[15] I sent you my servants the prophets, sending them tirelessly, saying: Turn — every one of you — from your evil way. Mend your deeds. Don't chase other gods to serve them. Then you can live on the land I gave to you and your ancestors. But you didn't bend your ear. You didn't listen to me.

[16] The descendants of Jonadab son of Rechab have honored their ancestor's command, the one he laid on them — but this people has not listened to me.

[17] Therefore, this is what the LORD, God of Armies, the God of Israel, says: I am bringing down on Judah and on every resident of Jerusalem every disaster I pronounced against them. Because I spoke and they would not hear. I called and they would not answer."

[18] To the Rechabite household Jeremiah said: "This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Because you have obeyed the command of Jonadab your ancestor — kept all his instructions, done everything he ordered you —

[19] therefore this is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Jonadab son of Rechab will never lack a man to stand before me. Not ever."

[1] In the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah, the word came to Jeremiah from the LORD:

[2] "Get a scroll. Write on it every word I've spoken to you about Israel, about Judah, about all the nations — from the day I first spoke to you in Josiah's reign until right now.

[3] Maybe — just maybe — when the house of Judah hears all the disaster I'm planning to bring down on them, each one of them will turn from his rotten road, and I'll forgive their guilt, their sin."

[4] So Jeremiah called Baruch son of Neriah, and Baruch wrote down on the scroll every word the LORD had spoken to Jeremiah — Jeremiah dictating, Baruch's pen moving.

[5] Then Jeremiah gave Baruch his orders: "I'm under restriction. I can't go to the house of the LORD myself.

[6] So you go. On a fast day, read aloud from the scroll — the words of the LORD that you wrote at my dictation — read them in the LORD's house, in the hearing of all the people. Read them so every man of Judah who's come in from his town can hear.

[7] Maybe their plea will rise before the LORD, and each will turn from his evil road — because the fury and the wrath the LORD has spoken against this people is enormous."

[8] Baruch son of Neriah did exactly what Jeremiah the prophet told him. He read the LORD's words from the scroll in the house of the LORD.

[9] It was the fifth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah, in the ninth month. A fast before the LORD had been proclaimed for all the people in Jerusalem and everyone streaming in from the towns of Judah.

[10] That's when Baruch read Jeremiah's words from the scroll — in the house of the LORD, in the chamber of Gemariah son of Shaphan the scribe, in the upper court, at the entrance of the New Gate of the LORD's house — read it where all the people could hear.

[11] Micaiah son of Gemariah, son of Shaphan, heard every word of the LORD from the scroll.

[12] He went down to the king's house, to the scribe's chamber. And there they all were, sitting in session: Elishama the scribe, Delaiah son of Shemaiah, Elnathan son of Achbor, Gemariah son of Shaphan, Zedekiah son of Hananiah — all the officials.

[13] Micaiah reported to them every word he'd heard when Baruch read the scroll out loud to the people.

[14] So the officials sent Jehudi son of Nethaniah, son of Shelemiah, son of Cushi, to Baruch: "That scroll you read aloud to the people — bring it here." Baruch son of Neriah took the scroll in hand and came to them.

[15] "Sit down," they said. "Read it to us." So Baruch read it in their hearing.

[16] When they'd heard it all — every word — they turned and looked at each other in dread. "We have to report all of this to the king."

[17] Then they pressed Baruch: "Tell us — how did you write all these words? Was it from his mouth?"

[18] Baruch answered, "Yes. He spoke every one of these words to me, and I wrote them on the scroll with ink."

[19] The officials said to Baruch, "Go hide. You and Jeremiah both. Don't let anyone know where you are."

[20] Then they went in to the king — in the court — having stored the scroll in the chamber of Elishama the scribe. They reported every word in the king's hearing.

[21] The king sent Jehudi to fetch the scroll. Jehudi brought it from Elishama the scribe's chamber and read it aloud to the king and to all the officials standing around the king.

[22] The king was sitting in the winter house — it was the ninth month — with a fire burning in the brazier in front of him.

[23] As Jehudi read three or four columns, the king would slice them off with a scribe's knife and toss them into the fire on the brazier — until the entire scroll was consumed by the flames.

[24] And no one shuddered. Not the king, not any of his servants who heard every word — no one tore his clothes.

[25] Even though Elnathan, Delaiah, and Gemariah had begged the king not to burn the scroll, he wouldn't listen.

[26] Instead, the king ordered Jerahmeel the king's son, Seraiah son of Azriel, and Shelemiah son of Abdeel to seize Baruch the scribe and Jeremiah the prophet. But the LORD had hidden them.

[27] After the king burned the scroll — the words Baruch had written at Jeremiah's dictation — the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[28] "Get another scroll. Write on it all the original words — the ones that were on the first scroll that Jehoiakim king of Judah burned.

[29] And to Jehoiakim king of Judah say this: The LORD says — You burned that scroll. You said, 'Why did you write on it that the king of Babylon will come and ruin this land and wipe out every human and animal in it?'

[30] So hear the LORD's verdict on Jehoiakim king of Judah: He will have no one to sit on David's throne. His corpse will be flung out — exposed to the heat of day and the frost of night.

[31] I will punish him, his offspring, and his servants for their guilt. I will bring down on them and on the people of Jerusalem and the men of Judah every disaster I pronounced against them — because they would not listen."

[32] So Jeremiah took another scroll and gave it to Baruch son of Neriah, the scribe. Baruch wrote on it, at Jeremiah's dictation, every word of the scroll Jehoiakim king of Judah had burned in the fire. And many more words like them were added.

[1] Zedekiah son of Josiah took the throne in place of Coniah son of Jehoiakim—Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had installed him as king over Judah.

[2] But neither he nor his court nor the people of the land listened to a single word the LORD spoke through Jeremiah the prophet.

[3] Still, King Zedekiah sent Jehucal son of Shelemiah and Zephaniah the priest, son of Maaseiah, to Jeremiah with this message: "Pray for us. Pray to the LORD our God."

[4] At this point Jeremiah was still moving freely among the people—he had not yet been thrown into prison.

[5] Meanwhile, Pharaoh's army had marched out of Egypt, and when the Babylonians besieging Jerusalem caught word of it, they pulled back.

[6] Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah the prophet:

[7] This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: Tell the king of Judah—the one who sent you here looking for answers—this: Pharaoh's army that marched out to help you? It's turning around. Heading home to Egypt.

[8] And the Babylonians are coming back. They will fight against this city, take it, and burn it to the ground.

[9] This is what the LORD says: Do not fool yourselves. Do not say, "The Babylonians are gone for good." They are not gone.

[10] Even if you struck down the entire Babylonian army attacking you, and only wounded men were left—lying in their tents, half-dead—they would still rise up and burn this city to ashes.

[11] When the Babylonian army withdrew from Jerusalem because of Pharaoh's advance,

[12] Jeremiah set out from Jerusalem to head into the territory of Benjamin to claim his share of family property among his people.

[13] But when he reached the Benjamin Gate, the captain of the guard on duty—a man named Irijah son of Shelemiah, son of Hananiah—seized him. "You're defecting to the Babylonians!" he shouted.

[14] "That's a lie," Jeremiah said. "I'm not defecting to anyone." Irijah refused to listen. He arrested Jeremiah and hauled him before the officials.

[15] The officials were furious. They beat him and locked him up in the house of Jonathan the secretary, which they had converted into a prison.

[16] So Jeremiah ended up in a dungeon cell, in the vaulted chambers below. He stayed there a long time.

[17] Then King Zedekiah sent for him. The king received him in secret at the palace and asked, quietly, "Is there any word from the LORD?" "There is," Jeremiah answered. And then: "You will be handed over to the king of Babylon."

[18] Then Jeremiah said to King Zedekiah, "What crime have I committed—against you, your officials, or this people—that you've thrown me in prison?

[19] Where are your prophets now—the ones who kept telling you, 'The king of Babylon will never attack you or this land'?

[20] Listen to me, my lord the king. Please—hear my plea. Don't send me back to the house of Jonathan the secretary. I will die there."

[21] So King Zedekiah gave orders that Jeremiah be held in the courtyard of the guard instead, and that he be given a loaf of bread from the bakers' street every day, as long as there was still bread in the city. So Jeremiah stayed in the courtyard of the guard.

[1] Shephatiah son of Mattan, Gedaliah son of Pashhur, Jucal son of Shelemiah, and Pashhur son of Malchijah heard what Jeremiah was telling the people:

[2] "This is what the LORD says: Anyone who stays in this city dies—by sword, by famine, by plague. Anyone who surrenders to the Babylonians lives. They escape with their life as plunder, and they live."

[3] And: "This is what the LORD says: This city will absolutely be handed over to the army of the king of Babylon. He will take it."

[4] The officials went to the king. "This man has to die. He's gutting the morale of every soldier left in this city, and every civilian too, by saying things like this. He isn't seeking the welfare of these people. He's seeking their ruin."

[5] King Zedekiah said, "He's in your hands. The king can't stop you."

[6] So they took Jeremiah and threw him into the cistern of Malchijah, the king's son, in the courtyard of the guard. They lowered him in with ropes. There was no water in the cistern—only mud—and Jeremiah sank into the mud.

[7] Now Ebed-melech the Cushite, a court official in the palace, heard that they had put Jeremiah in the cistern. The king was sitting at the Benjamin Gate at the time.

[8] Ebed-melech left the palace and went to the king.

[9] "My lord the king, these men have done evil in everything they've done to the prophet Jeremiah. They've thrown him into a cistern. He'll starve to death down there—there's no bread left in the city anyway."

[10] The king ordered Ebed-melech the Cushite: "Take thirty men with you from here. Get the prophet Jeremiah out of that cistern before he dies."

[11] So Ebed-melech took the men, went into the palace to a room under the treasury, gathered some old rags and worn-out clothes, and lowered them down to Jeremiah on ropes.

[12] "Put these old rags and worn-out clothes under your armpits, under the ropes," he called down. Jeremiah did.

[13] They pulled him up out of the cistern with the ropes. And Jeremiah remained in the courtyard of the guard.

[14] King Zedekiah sent for the prophet Jeremiah and had him brought to the third entrance of the LORD's house. "I'm going to ask you something," the king said. "Don't hide anything from me."

[15] Jeremiah answered, "If I tell you the truth, you'll kill me. And if I give you advice, you won't listen."

[16] But King Zedekiah swore to Jeremiah in secret: "As surely as the LORD lives, who gave us the breath in our lungs—I will not kill you. I will not hand you over to the men who want you dead."

[17] So Jeremiah said to Zedekiah, "This is what the LORD God of Armies, the God of Israel, says: If you surrender to the officers of the king of Babylon, you live. This city won't burn. You and your household will survive.

[18] But if you don't surrender, this city goes to the Babylonians. They'll torch it. And you—you won't escape their grip."

[19] King Zedekiah said to Jeremiah, "I'm afraid of the Judeans who've already defected to the Babylonians. The Babylonians might hand me over to them, and they'd torture me."

[20] "They won't hand you over," Jeremiah said. "Just listen to the LORD in what I'm telling you. It will go well for you. You'll live.

[21] But if you refuse to surrender, here's what the LORD has shown me—

[22] every woman left in the palace of the king of Judah will be marched out to the officers of the king of Babylon. And they'll be saying:

*Your trusted friends misled you and beat you.
Now that your feet are sunk in the mud,
they've turned their backs and walked away.*

[23] They'll lead out all your wives and your children to the Babylonians. You yourself won't escape their hand. You'll be seized by the king of Babylon, and this city will be burned to the ground."

[24] Then Zedekiah said to Jeremiah, "Don't let anyone know about this conversation, or you'll die.

[25] If the officials hear I spoke with you and they come and demand, 'Tell us what you said to the king, and what the king said to you—don't hide anything or we'll kill you'—

[26] tell them this: 'I was begging the king not to send me back to Jonathan's house to die there.'"

[27] Sure enough, all the officials came and questioned Jeremiah. He told them exactly what the king had instructed. So they dropped it—no one had overheard the actual conversation.

[28] And Jeremiah remained in the courtyard of the guard until the day Jerusalem fell.

[1] In the ninth year of Zedekiah king of Judah, in the tenth month, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon marched on Jerusalem with his entire army and locked the city in a siege.

[2] In Zedekiah's eleventh year, fourth month, ninth day — the wall cracked open.

[3] Every officer of the king of Babylon poured through and took their seats at the Middle Gate: Nergal-sharezer, Samgar-nebo, Sarsekim the chief eunuch, Nergal-sharezer the chief magus — the whole roster of Babylon's brass.

[4] When Zedekiah king of Judah saw them — he and every fighting man — they ran. They slipped out of the city by night through the king's garden, past the gate between the two walls, and bolted toward the Arabah.

[5] But the Chaldean army gave chase. They caught Zedekiah on the plains of Jericho, dragged him to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah in the land of Hamath, and there the king pronounced sentence.

[6] At Riblah, the king of Babylon slaughtered Zedekiah's sons before his eyes. Every nobleman of Judah — slaughtered too.

[7] Then he gouged out Zedekiah's eyes and bound him in bronze chains to haul him off to Babylon.

[8] The Chaldeans torched the king's palace and the houses of the people and tore down the walls of Jerusalem.

[9] Nebuzaradan, captain of the guard, deported the survivors — those still in the city, the defectors who had crossed over to him, the whole remnant — and carried them off to Babylon.

[10] But the poor — those who owned nothing — Nebuzaradan left behind in the land of Judah and handed them vineyards and fields that day.

[11] Now Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had given Nebuzaradan, captain of the guard, a direct order regarding Jeremiah:

[12] "Take him. Watch over him. Do him no harm. Whatever he asks of you, do it."

[13] So Nebuzaradan the captain, Nebushazban the chief eunuch, Nergal-sharezer the chief magus, and every senior officer of the king of Babylon

[14] sent for Jeremiah. They pulled him from the courtyard of the guard and delivered him to Gedaliah son of Ahikam son of Shaphan, to bring him home. And he lived among his own people.

[15] While Jeremiah was still locked in the courtyard of the guard, the word of the LORD had come to him:

[16] "Go and tell Ebed-melech the Cushite: This is what the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel, says — I am about to bring my words against this city to pass. Disaster, not good. It will all happen right in front of you on that day.

[17] But you — I will rescue you on that day, declares the LORD. You will not be handed over to the men you fear.

[18] I will save you. The sword will not touch you. Your life will be your prize — because you trusted me. That is the declaration of the LORD."

[1] This is what came to Jeremiah from the LORD after Nebuzaradan, captain of the imperial guard, set him free at Ramah — found him there in chains, lumped in with the exiles of Jerusalem and Judah being marched off to Babylon.

[2] The captain pulled Jeremiah aside and said: "The LORD your God called down this disaster on this place.

[3] And now he's done what he said he'd do. All of this happened because your people sinned against the LORD and refused to listen to him. That's why this has come down on you.

[4] But look — today I'm taking the chains off your wrists. If you want to come with me to Babylon, come, and I'll look out for you. If you don't, stay. The whole land is open. Go wherever you think best."

[5] Before Jeremiah could answer, Nebuzaradan added: "Go back to Gedaliah son of Ahikam, grandson of Shaphan — the king of Babylon has put him over the towns of Judah. Live with him among the people. Or go wherever you want." Then the captain gave him provisions and a gift, and let him go.

[6] So Jeremiah went to Gedaliah son of Ahikam at Mizpah and settled in with him among the people left in the land.

[7] When the army commanders still out in the open country heard that the king of Babylon had appointed Gedaliah son of Ahikam over the land — putting him in charge of the men, women, and children, the poorest of the poor who hadn't been deported —

[8] they came to Gedaliah at Mizpah: Ishmael son of Nethaniah, Johanan and Jonathan sons of Kareah, Seraiah son of Tanhumeth, the sons of Ephai the Netophathite, Jezaniah son of the Maakathite — they came with their men.

[9] Gedaliah son of Ahikam, grandson of Shaphan, swore an oath to them and their men: "Don't be afraid to serve the Babylonians. Settle in the land. Serve the king of Babylon, and it'll go well for you.

[10] As for me, I'll stay at Mizpah and represent you before the Babylonians when they come. You — gather wine and summer fruit and oil. Store them in your jars. Live in the towns you've taken."

[11] And when all the Judeans in Moab, Ammon, Edom, and every other country heard that the king of Babylon had left a remnant in Judah and put Gedaliah son of Ahikam over them,

[12] they all came streaming back — from every place they'd been scattered — back to the land of Judah, to Gedaliah at Mizpah. They harvested wine and summer fruit in abundance.

[13] Then Johanan son of Kareah and all the army commanders still in the field came to Gedaliah at Mizpah

[14] and said to him: "Do you have any idea? Baalis king of Ammon has sent Ishmael son of Nethaniah to kill you." But Gedaliah son of Ahikam wouldn't believe them.

[15] Then Johanan son of Kareah pulled Gedaliah aside privately at Mizpah and said: "Let me go take Ishmael out. No one will know. Why should he kill you and scatter the Judeans who've gathered around you? Why let the remnant of Judah perish?"

[16] But Gedaliah son of Ahikam told Johanan son of Kareah: "Don't do it. What you're saying about Ishmael — it's a lie."

[1] In the seventh month, Ishmael son of Nethaniah, son of Elishama — royal blood, one of the king's officers — came to Gedaliah son of Ahikam at Mizpah with ten men. They sat down to a meal together.

[2] Then Ishmael and his ten men rose from the table, drew their swords, and cut down Gedaliah son of Ahikam, son of Shaphan — the man the king of Babylon had appointed over the land. They killed him there.

[3] Ishmael also killed every Judean with Gedaliah at Mizpah, and the Babylonian soldiers stationed there — the warriors.

[4] The day after the murder, before anyone knew,

[5] eighty men arrived from Shechem, Shiloh, and Samaria — beards shaved, clothes torn, bodies gashed — carrying grain offerings and incense to bring to the house of the Lord.

[6] Ishmael went out from Mizpah to meet them, weeping as he walked. When he reached them, he said, "Come — come to Gedaliah son of Ahikam."

[7] But the moment they were inside the town, Ishmael and his men slaughtered them and threw their bodies into a cistern.

[8] Ten of them, though, said to Ishmael, "Don't kill us — we have stores hidden in the field: wheat, barley, oil, honey." So he held back and didn't kill them with the others.

[9] The cistern where Ishmael dumped every corpse of the men he struck down — bodies thrown in with Gedaliah's — was the one King Asa had dug long ago as a defense against Baasha king of Israel. Ishmael son of Nethaniah filled it with the slain.

[10] Then Ishmael took captive everyone left in Mizpah — the king's daughters and all the people Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard had entrusted to Gedaliah son of Ahikam. Ishmael son of Nethaniah took them and set out to cross over to the Ammonites.

[11] When Johanan son of Kareah and all the army officers with him heard about every atrocity Ishmael son of Nethaniah had committed,

[12] they gathered all their men and went out to fight Ishmael son of Nethaniah. They caught up with him at the great pool in Gibeon.

[13] When all the people Ishmael was holding saw Johanan son of Kareah and the army officers with him, they were overjoyed.

[14] Every captive Ishmael had taken from Mizpah turned around and ran back to Johanan son of Kareah.

[15] But Ishmael son of Nethaniah broke away from Johanan with eight men and escaped to the Ammonites.

[16] Then Johanan son of Kareah and the army officers with him gathered up the rest of the people Ishmael son of Nethaniah had taken from Mizpah after killing Gedaliah son of Ahikam — soldiers, women, children, and court officials — bringing them back from Gibeon.

[17] They moved on and stopped at Geruth Kimham near Bethlehem, intending to push on to Egypt —

[18] away from the Babylonians. They were terrified of them, because Ishmael son of Nethaniah had killed Gedaliah son of Ahikam, the man the king of Babylon had set over the land.

[1] Then all the commanders of the forces, along with Johanan son of Kareah and Jezaniah son of Hoshaiah — every last person from the least to the greatest — stepped forward [2] and said to Jeremiah the prophet: "Please. Let our plea land with you. Pray to the LORD your God for us — for this whole battered remnant. We were many once; look at us now, a handful, as your own eyes can see. [3] Ask the LORD your God to show us where to walk and what to do."

[4] Jeremiah the prophet answered them: "I hear you. I will pray to the LORD your God exactly as you have asked. And whatever the LORD answers, I will tell you — I will hold nothing back."

[5] They said to Jeremiah: "Let the LORD stand as a true and faithful witness against us if we fail to do everything the LORD your God sends you to tell us. [6] Good or bad — we will obey the voice of the LORD our God, the one we are sending you to. Because when we obey the LORD our God, things will go well with us."

[7] Ten days passed. Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah. [8] He summoned Johanan son of Kareah and all the commanders with him, and all the people — least to greatest — [9] and said to them: "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — the one you sent me to lay your plea before:

[10] If you will settle down and stay in this land, I will build you up and not tear you down. I will plant you and not uproot you. The disaster I brought on you — I grieve it. [11] Do not be afraid of the king of Babylon, the one whose shadow has you trembling. Do not fear him, declares the LORD, because I am with you. I will rescue you. I will pull you out of his grip. [12] I will give you mercy — and he will show you mercy and send you back to your own soil.

[13] But if you say, 'No — we will not stay in this land,' refusing to obey the voice of the LORD your God, [14] saying, 'No, we are going to Egypt, where we will see no war, hear no trumpet, and not starve for bread — there we will live' — [15] then listen, you remnant of Judah, to the word of the LORD. This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: If you are dead set on going to Egypt, if you go there to settle, [16] then the sword you are running from will catch you there in Egypt. The famine you are afraid of will chase you down to Egypt. And there you will die. [17] Every man who turns his face toward Egypt to settle there will die — by sword, by famine, by plague. Not one of them will survive or escape the disaster I am about to bring.

[18] Because this is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Just as my anger and fury poured out on the people of Jerusalem, so my fury will pour out on you when you set foot in Egypt. You will become a curse, a horror, a name people spit out, a thing of shame. You will never see this place again.

[19] The LORD has spoken to you, remnant of Judah: Do not go to Egypt. Know it clearly — I have warned you this day. [20] You played yourselves when you sent me to the LORD your God, saying, 'Pray to the LORD our God for us; tell us exactly what the LORD says, and we will do it.' [21] Now I have told you this day — and you are not obeying the voice of the LORD your God in any of it, not in anything he sent me to say. [22] So know this, and know it for certain: by sword, by famine, by plague you will die — in the very place you are so desperate to go and settle."

[1] Jeremiah finished. Every word the LORD their God had sent him to speak — all of it, to the very last syllable — was now out in the open before the people.

[2] Then Azariah son of Hoshaiah and Johanan son of Kareah and every arrogant man there turned on Jeremiah: "You're lying. The LORD our God didn't send you to tell us, 'Don't go down to Egypt to settle there.'

[3] This is Baruch son of Neriah pulling your strings — turning you against us so we'll be handed over to the Chaldeans, killed or hauled off to Babylon."

[4] So Johanan son of Kareah, all the army officers, and all the people refused to listen to the LORD's command to stay in Judah.

[5] Johanan and the officers gathered up everyone left of Judah — every refugee who had drifted back from the nations they'd been scattered to, to live in Judah again:

[6] the men, the women, the children, the king's daughters, every soul Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard had left with Gedaliah son of Ahikam, son of Shaphan — along with the prophet Jeremiah and Baruch son of Neriah.

[7] And they went to Egypt. They would not listen to the LORD. They marched all the way to Tahpanhes.

[8] There at Tahpanhes, the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:

[9] "Take some large stones in your hands. In front of everyone watching, bury them in the mortar of the brick pavement at the entrance to Pharaoh's palace in Tahpanhes.

[10] Then tell them: This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says — Watch. I am sending for my servant Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and I will set his throne right here, over these stones I have buried. He will pitch his royal canopy above them.

[11] He will come and strike Egypt — delivering to death those marked for death, to captivity those marked for captivity, to the sword those marked for the sword.

[12] He will set fire to the temples of Egypt's gods — burn them, haul the idols off. He will pick Egypt clean the way a shepherd picks lice from his cloak. And he will walk out untouched.

[13] He will smash the sacred pillars at Heliopolis, that temple of the sun in Egypt, and torch the temples of the Egyptian gods to the ground."

[1] The word came to Jeremiah for all the Judeans living in Egypt — those settled at Migdol, Tahpanhes, Memphis, and throughout the land of Pathros:

[2] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: You saw the catastrophe I brought on Jerusalem and every town of Judah. Look at them now — smoking ruins, abandoned, no one living there —

[3] because of the evil they committed. They provoked me by burning incense and bowing to gods they never knew — gods you never knew, gods your ancestors never knew.

[4] I sent you my servants the prophets — sent them again and again — pleading: Stop. Do not do this disgusting thing I hate.

[5] But they would not listen. They would not turn. They kept burning incense to other gods, kept refusing to abandon their evil.

[6] So my fury poured out. It blazed through the towns of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem until those places became the wreckage and waste they are today.

[7] So now, this is what the LORD God of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Why are you doing this to yourselves? Why cut yourselves off — man, woman, child, infant — leaving no remnant of Judah?

[8] You provoke me with the work of your hands, burning incense to other gods here in Egypt — where you came to live. You will destroy yourselves. You will become a curse and a mockery among every nation on earth.

[9] Have you forgotten the evil of your ancestors, the evil of the kings of Judah, the evil of their wives, your own evil, your wives' evil — everything done in the land of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem?

[10] To this day they have not humbled themselves. They have no fear of me. They have not walked in my instruction or the statutes I placed before you and your ancestors.

[11] So this is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: I am setting my face against you for disaster — to wipe out all of Judah.

[12] The remnant of Judah that insisted on going to Egypt to live there — I will take them. They will fall in Egypt. They will die there — by sword, by famine — from the smallest to the greatest. They will become an object of horror, a curse, a hiss of contempt.

[13] I will punish those living in Egypt the same way I punished Jerusalem — with sword, famine, and plague.

[14] None of the Judean remnant who came to Egypt will escape or survive to return to Judah, where their hearts long to go back and live. None will return — except a few fugitives.

[15] Then all the men who knew their wives had been burning incense to other gods, along with the women standing there — a huge crowd — and all the Judeans living in Pathros, Egypt, answered Jeremiah:

[16] As for the word you have spoken to us in the name of the LORD — we are not listening to you.

[17] We will do exactly what we said we would do. We will burn incense to the Queen of Heaven. We will pour out drink offerings to her — just like we did, like our ancestors did, like our kings and officials did in the towns of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem. Back then we had plenty of bread. We did well. We saw no trouble.

[18] But the moment we stopped burning incense to the Queen of Heaven and pouring out drink offerings to her — we lost everything. We have been finished off by sword and famine.

[19] And the women added: When we burned incense to the Queen of Heaven and poured out drink offerings to her — do you think we did it without our husbands? We baked cakes shaped like her image. We poured the drink offerings. Our husbands were right there with us.

[20] Then Jeremiah turned to all the people — the men, the women, the whole crowd answering him back — and said:

[21] The incense you burned in the towns of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem — you, your ancestors, your kings, your officials, the people of the land — do you think the LORD forgot? Do you think it never crossed his mind?

[22] He could not bear it any longer. The evil of your practices, the disgusting things you did — he could not endure them. That is why your land became wreckage, desolation, a curse, empty of people — exactly what it is today.

[23] Because you burned incense, because you sinned against the LORD, because you would not listen to his voice, would not walk in his instruction, his statutes, his testimonies — that is why this disaster has fallen on you. Exactly as it has today.

[24] Then Jeremiah said to all the people, especially the women: Listen to the word of the LORD, every Judean here in Egypt.

[25] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: You and your wives have done with your hands what your mouths declared — saying, "We will absolutely keep our vows to burn incense to the Queen of Heaven and pour out drink offerings to her." Fine. Confirm your vows. Carry them out. Do exactly what you promised.

[26] But listen to the word of the LORD, all Judeans living in Egypt: I swear by my great name, says the LORD — never again will my name be spoken by any Judean mouth anywhere in Egypt, saying, "As the Lord GOD lives."

[27] I am watching them — for harm, not for good. Every Judean in Egypt will be finished off by sword and famine until they are gone.

[28] Only a handful will escape the sword and return from Egypt to the land of Judah. And the remnant of Judah who came to Egypt to live — they will find out whose word stands: mine or theirs.

[29] And here is the sign — declares the LORD — that I am about to punish you in this place. Then you will know my words against you will stand — for harm:

[30] This is what the LORD says: I am about to hand Pharaoh Hophra, king of Egypt, over to his enemies — to those who want him dead — just as I handed Zedekiah king of Judah over to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, his enemy, the one who wanted him dead.

[1] This is the word Jeremiah the prophet delivered to Baruch son of Neriah, when Baruch wrote these words on a scroll, dictated by Jeremiah, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah:

[2] The LORD, the God of Israel, has this to say to you, Baruch:

[3] You said, "I'm finished. The LORD has piled grief on top of my pain. I'm worn out from groaning. I cannot find rest."

[4] Now tell him this — the LORD says: Look around. What I built, I am tearing down. What I planted, I am ripping up. The whole land.

[5] And you — you go chasing greatness for yourself? Stop. I am bringing disaster on every living thing — this is the LORD's word. But you, I will give you your life as your prize. Wherever you go, you walk away breathing.

[1] What came to Jeremiah the prophet — the word of the LORD against the nations.

[2] Concerning Egypt. Concerning the army of Pharaoh Neco, king of Egypt — the force shattered at Carchemish on the Euphrates by Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah:

[3] Strap on the shield. Lock the buckler. March to the front.

[4] Harness the horses. Mount up, riders. Take your positions — helmets on, spears polished, armor strapped tight.

[5] But what do I see? Terror. They're falling back. Their warriors — crushed. Running. Not even looking over their shoulders. Horror on every side. This is the LORD speaking.

[6] The swift can't escape. The strong can't get away. Up north, by the Euphrates — they stumble and fall.

[7] Who is this rising like the Nile, waters churning like rivers in flood?

[8] Egypt rises like the Nile, waters surging like rivers. He said: I'll rise. I'll cover the earth. I'll wipe out cities and everyone in them.

[9] Charge, horses! Drive hard, chariots! Let the warriors march out — Cush and Put with their shields, Lud bending their bows.

[10] But this day belongs to the Lord, the LORD of armies — a day of reckoning, vengeance on his enemies. The sword will eat until it's full, drunk on their blood. For the Lord, the LORD of armies, has a sacrifice to make in the northern country, by the Euphrates River.

[11] Go up to Gilead. Get balm, virgin daughter Egypt. You've tried every remedy — nothing works. There is no healing for you.

[12] The nations heard your humiliation. The earth is full of your wailing. Warrior collides with warrior — both go down together.

[13] The word the LORD spoke to Jeremiah the prophet about Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon coming to strike the land of Egypt:

[14] Announce it in Egypt. Broadcast it in Migdol. Proclaim it in Memphis and Tahpanhes: Take your stand. Get ready. The sword is devouring everything around you.

[15] Why has your mighty bull been thrown down? He couldn't stand — the LORD shoved him over.

[16] He made them stumble in heaps, soldier collapsing onto soldier. They said: Get up. Let's go home — back to our people, back to the land of our birth, away from this murderous sword.

[17] There, they renamed Pharaoh king of Egypt: Big Noise Who Missed His Moment.

[18] As I live — this is the King speaking, whose name is the LORD of armies — he is coming. Like Tabor among the mountains, like Carmel rising from the sea, he is coming.

[19] Pack your bags for exile, you who live in Egypt, daughter — for Memphis will become wasteland, burned out, no one left.

[20] Egypt is a beautiful heifer — but a horsefly is coming from the north. Coming.

[21] Even her hired soldiers in her midst — fattened calves, every one — they too turn and run together. They don't hold the line. The day of their disaster has come down on them, their hour of reckoning.

[22] Egypt hisses away like a snake slithering off as the enemy advances in force — they come at her with axes like men chopping wood.

[23] They will hack down her forest — this is the LORD speaking — impenetrable though it seems. They outnumber the locusts. Beyond counting.

[24] Daughter Egypt is disgraced — handed over to the people of the north.

[25] The LORD of armies, the God of Israel, has said: I am bringing judgment on Amon of Thebes, on Pharaoh, on Egypt, on her gods and her kings — on Pharaoh and everyone who trusts in him.

[26] I'll hand them over to those who want them dead — to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and his officers. But afterward — Egypt will be inhabited again, as in days long past. This is the LORD speaking.

[27] But you — don't be afraid, my servant Jacob. Don't fall apart, Israel. Look: I am rescuing you from the far country, your children from the land of their captivity. Jacob will come home. He will rest. He will be at ease. No one will make him afraid again.

[28] Don't be afraid, my servant Jacob — this is the LORD speaking — because I am with you. I will make a complete end of every nation where I scattered you. But I will not make a complete end of you. I will discipline you with justice. I will not let you go unpunished — but I will not destroy you.

[1] What came to Jeremiah the prophet from the LORD about the Philistines — before Pharaoh struck Gaza:

[2] This is what the LORD says:

Look north. Waters are rising.
They surge into a flood,
they crash over the land and everything in it,
the cities and everyone who lives there.
Men will scream.
Every soul in the land will howl

[3] at the thunder of stallions' hooves,
the rumble of chariots,
the roar of wheels.
Fathers won't turn back for their sons —
their hands will hang useless at their sides.

[4] Because the day has come
to destroy every last Philistine,
to cut off Tyre and Sidon
down to the final ally still standing.
The LORD is wiping out the Philistines,
the survivors from the coasts of Caphtor.

[5] Gaza shaves her head in grief.
Ashkelon is silenced.
You remnant of their valley —
how long will you slash yourselves?

[6] "Sword of the LORD —
how long until you rest?
Sheathe yourself. Stop. Be still."

[7] But how can it rest
when the LORD has given it orders?
Against Ashkelon, against the coast —
that's where he's pointed it.

[1] About Moab. This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Disaster on Nebo — it is wrecked. Kiriathaim is humiliated, captured. The fortress is shamed, shattered.

[2] Moab's glory is gone. In Heshbon they plotted her ruin: "Come, let's cut her off from being a nation." You too, Madmen — you'll be silenced; the sword is hunting you.

[3] Listen — a scream from Horonaim: devastation, massive ruin.

[4] Moab is broken. Her little ones cry out in anguish.

[5] Up the road to Luhith they climb, weeping as they go. Down the slope to Horonaim, the cries of the broken echo.

[6] Run! Save your lives! Become like a desert shrub in the wilderness.

[7] Because you trusted in your works and your treasures, you'll be taken too. Chemosh will march into exile — his priests and officials with him.

[8] The destroyer will hit every city — not one escapes. The valley will be ruined, the plateau wiped out. The LORD has spoken.

[9] Give Moab wings — she has to fly. Her cities will become wastelands, no one left to live in them.

[10] Cursed is anyone who does the LORD's work halfheartedly. Cursed is anyone who holds back his sword from blood.

[11] Moab has been comfortable since youth, settled like wine on its sediment — never poured from jar to jar, never sent into exile. So her flavor stayed, her aroma unchanged.

[12] But the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I'll send pourers who will pour her out, empty her jars, smash her containers.

[13] Then Moab will be ashamed of Chemosh the way Israel was ashamed of Bethel — that trust they leaned on.

[14] How can you say, "We are warriors, men ready for battle"?

[15] Moab is destroyed, her cities stormed, her finest young men marched down to slaughter — declares the King, whose name is the LORD of Armies.

[16] Moab's disaster is close. Her ruin is racing toward her.

[17] Mourn for her, all you neighbors, everyone who knows her name. Say it: "How the mighty scepter is broken — the staff of beauty, snapped."

[18] Come down from your glory, sit in the dust, you who live in Daughter Dibon. Moab's destroyer is coming up against you — your fortresses are rubble.

[19] Stand by the road and watch, you who live in Aroer. Ask the man fleeing, the woman escaping: "What happened?"

[20] Moab is shattered, disgraced. Wail and scream! Tell it along the Arnon: Moab is ruined.

[21] Judgment has fallen on the plateau — on Holon, Jahzah, Mephaath,

[22] Dibon, Nebo, Beth-diblathaim,

[23] Kiriathaim, Beth-gamul, Beth-meon,

[24] Kerioth, Bozrah — every city in Moab, far and near.

[25] Moab's horn is hacked off. Her arm is broken. Declared by the LORD.

[26] Get her drunk — she defied the LORD. Let Moab roll in her own vomit, become a joke to everyone.

[27] Wasn't Israel a joke to you? Was she caught with thieves, that every time you spoke of her you sneered and shook your head?

[28] Abandon the cities, you who live in Moab. Camp on the cliffs. Be like the dove that nests at the mouth of a chasm.

[29] We've heard about Moab's pride — and pride it is. Towering, swollen, arrogant, the heart lifted up.

[30] I know her insolence, declares the LORD. Her boasts are empty. Her deeds will collapse.

[31] So I wail for Moab. I cry out for all of her. I moan for the men of Kir-heres.

[32] More than I wept for Jazer I weep for you, vine of Sibmah. Your branches reached past the sea, stretched to Jazer. But the destroyer has crashed in on your summer fruit, on your harvest.

[33] Joy and gladness — stripped from the orchards, from the land of Moab. I've stopped the wine flowing from the presses. No one shouts the harvest cheer anymore. The shouts are there — but they're not joy.

[34] From Heshbon to Elealeh — to Jahaz — they raise their cries. From Zoar to Horonaim, to Eglath-shelishiyah, even the waters of Nimrim run dry.

[35] I'll finish off in Moab, declares the LORD, anyone who climbs the high places to burn incense to his gods.

[36] So my heart moans for Moab like a flute. It moans like a flute for the men of Kir-heres. The wealth they piled up — gone.

[37] Every head is shaved bald, every beard cut short. Every hand is slashed, every waist wrapped in sackcloth.

[38] On every roof in Moab, on every street — only mourning. I shattered Moab like a jar nobody wants. Declared by the LORD.

[39] How she's broken! Listen to the wailing. How Moab turns her back in shame! Moab has become a joke, a horror to all who surround her.

[40] This is what the LORD says: Look — he swoops like an eagle, wings spread over Moab.

[41] The cities are seized, the strongholds taken. On that day, Moab's warriors will feel like a woman in labor.

[42] Moab will be wiped out as a people — because she defied the LORD.

[43] Terror, the pit, the trap — they're coming for you, you who live in Moab. Declared by the LORD.

[44] Whoever runs from the terror falls into the pit. Whoever climbs out of the pit gets caught in the trap. Yes — I'm bringing all this on Moab, the year of her reckoning. Declared by the LORD.

[45] In the shadow of Heshbon the refugees stop, exhausted. But fire has burst from Heshbon, flame from inside Sihon — and it devours Moab's forehead, the skulls of the proud and loud.

[46] You're finished, Moab. The people of Chemosh are done. Your sons hauled into exile, your daughters into captivity.

[47] But in the days to come, I will restore Moab's fortunes. Declared by the LORD. Here ends the judgment on Moab.

[1] About the Ammonites. This is what the LORD says: Does Israel have no sons? No heir? Then why has Milcom seized Gad? Why are his people squatting in Israel's cities?

[2] So the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will sound the war-cry against Rabbah of the Ammonites. It will become a heap of ruins, its villages torched. Then Israel will dispossess those who dispossessed her, says the LORD.

[3] Wail, Heshbon — Ai is destroyed! Scream, daughters of Rabbah! Wrap yourselves in sackcloth, mourn, run frantic among the sheep pens — because Milcom is going into exile, his priests and officials with him.

[4] Why brag about your valleys, your fertile, flowing valleys, you faithless daughter? You trusted in your treasures, sneered, "Who would dare come at me?"

[5] Watch — I am about to bring terror on you from every side, declares the Lord, the LORD of Hosts. You will be driven out, every one of you, headlong — and no one will gather the runaways.

[6] But afterward, I will restore the fortunes of the Ammonites, declares the LORD.

[7] About Edom. This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Is there no more wisdom in Teman? Has counsel vanished from the prudent? Has their wisdom gone rancid?

[8] Run! Turn back! Burrow deep, you who live in Dedan — because I am bringing disaster down on Esau, the hour of his reckoning.

[9] If grape-pickers came to you, would they not leave a few clusters? If thieves came in the night, would they not stop when they had enough?

[10] But I — I have stripped Esau bare. I have uncovered his hiding places. He cannot conceal himself. His offspring, his kin, his neighbors — all destroyed. He himself — gone.

[11] Leave your orphans; I will keep them alive. Let your widows trust in me.

[12] This is what the LORD says: Look — if even those who did not deserve to drink the cup must drink it, do you really think you will escape unpunished? You will not escape. You will drink.

[13] I have sworn by myself, declares the LORD, that Bozrah will become a horror, a disgrace, a wasteland, a curse-word — and all her towns, ruins forever.

[14] I have heard a dispatch from the LORD: a herald has been sent among the nations — Gather! March against her! Rise up for battle!

[15] Look — I am making you small among the nations, despised among men.

[16] The terror you inspired deceived you. The arrogance of your heart deceived you — you who roost in the clefts of the rock, who perch on the heights. Even if you nest as high as the eagle, I will pull you down from there, declares the LORD.

[17] Edom will become an object of horror. Everyone passing by will gasp, will hiss at her wounds.

[18] Like the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah and their neighbors, says the LORD — no one will live there, no human being will settle there.

[19] Watch — like a lion springing from the thickets of the Jordan into rich pastureland, in an instant I will chase Edom off her own ground. And whom will I appoint over her? Who is like me? Who can summon me to court? What shepherd can stand before me?

[20] So hear what the LORD has plotted against Edom, what he is planning against the people of Teman: even the smallest of the flock will be dragged off; even their pasture will recoil in horror at them.

[21] At the sound of their collapse, the earth quakes. Their scream — the noise of it carries to the Red Sea.

[22] Watch — an eagle climbs and swoops, spreading its wings over Bozrah. On that day, the hearts of Edom's warriors will be like the heart of a woman in labor.

[23] About Damascus. Hamath is shaken. Arpad too — they have heard bad news, and they melt. Anxiety churns like the restless sea; it cannot be calmed.

[24] Damascus has gone limp. She turns to flee, panic seizing her, anguish and pangs gripping her like a woman in labor.

[25] How could the famous city be abandoned — the town I once delighted in?

[26] So her young men will fall in her streets; all her soldiers will be silenced on that day, declares the LORD of Hosts.

[27] I will set fire to the walls of Damascus; it will consume the fortresses of Ben-Hadad.

[28] About Kedar and the kingdoms of Hazor, which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon struck down. This is what the LORD says: Get up! March on Kedar! Plunder the people of the east!

[29] Their tents, their flocks will be taken — their tent curtains and all their gear, their camels carried off. People will shout at them: Terror on every side!

[30] Run! Flee far away! Burrow deep, you who live in Hazor, declares the LORD — Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon has plotted against you. He has devised a plan.

[31] Get up! March against a nation at ease, living secure, declares the LORD — a nation with no gates, no bars, dwelling alone.

[32] Their camels will be loot; their vast herds, spoil. I will scatter to every wind those who shave their temples, and from every direction I will bring disaster on them, declares the LORD.

[33] Hazor will be a jackals' lair, a wasteland forever. No one will live there; no human being will settle there.

[34] The word of the LORD that came to Jeremiah the prophet about Elam, at the beginning of the reign of Zedekiah king of Judah:

[35] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Watch — I am breaking the bow of Elam, the very heart of their strength.

[36] I will bring against Elam the four winds from the four corners of the sky. I will scatter them to every wind — there will not be a nation on earth without Elamite refugees.

[37] I will shatter Elam before their enemies, before those who hunt their lives. I will bring catastrophe down on them, the fury of my anger, declares the LORD. I will send the sword after them until I have finished them.

[38] I will set my throne in Elam and destroy her king and her officials, declares the LORD.

[39] But in the days to come, I will restore the fortunes of Elam, declares the LORD.

[1] This is the word the LORD spoke against Babylon, against the land of the Chaldeans, through Jeremiah the prophet:

[2] Announce it among the nations. Raise the signal. Spread the word — hold nothing back. Say it: Babylon is taken. Bel is humiliated. Marduk is shattered. Her idols stand disgraced; her filthy gods lie broken.

[3] A nation is coming up against her from the north. It will turn her land into wasteland — nothing left living there. Everything that breathes, human and animal, will run.

[4] In those days, at that time — this is the LORD speaking — the people of Israel will come, and the people of Judah with them. They will come weeping as they walk, and they will come looking for the LORD their God.

[5] They will ask the road to Zion and set their faces toward it. They will come and bind themselves to the LORD in a covenant that will not be forgotten, will not be broken — ever.

[6] My people have been lost sheep. Their shepherds led them astray, took them wandering across the mountains. They drifted from hill to hill and forgot where they belonged.

[7] Everyone who found them devoured them. Their enemies said, "We're not guilty — these people sinned against the LORD, their true pasture, the hope of their ancestors."

[8] Get out of Babylon. Flee the land of the Chaldeans. Be like the lead goats out front of the flock.

[9] Watch — I am stirring up a coalition of mighty nations from the land of the north. They will line up against Babylon, and from there she will be captured. Their arrows are like a master warrior's — none of them come back empty.

[10] Chaldea will be plundered. Everyone who plunders her will leave full — this is the LORD speaking.

[11] You celebrated, didn't you. You danced in the spoil of my inheritance. You frolicked like a calf in the grain, you neighed like stallions —

[12] but your mother is going to be humiliated. The one who gave birth to you will be shamed. Look — she will be the least of the nations, a wilderness, a parched land, a desert.

[13] Because of the LORD's wrath, no one will live there. The whole place will become desolation. Everyone passing by Babylon will gasp and whistle at her wounds.

[14] Take your positions around Babylon, every archer of you. Shoot. Spare no arrow — she sinned against the LORD.

[15] Raise the war cry against her on every side. She has surrendered. Her towers have collapsed, her walls torn down. This is the LORD's vengeance. Take your vengeance on her. Do to her what she did to others.

[16] Cut off the sower from Babylon, and the one who swings the sickle at harvest. Faced with the sword of the oppressor, every man will turn back to his own people, every man will run for his own land.

[17] Israel is a scattered sheep, hunted by lions. First the king of Assyria devoured her; now last, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon has gnawed her bones.

[18] So this is what the LORD of Heaven's Armies, the God of Israel, says: I am about to punish the king of Babylon and his land the way I punished the king of Assyria.

[19] I will bring Israel back to her own pasture. She will graze on Carmel and Bashan. On the hills of Ephraim and Gilead, she will eat her fill.

[20] In those days, at that time — this is the LORD speaking — they will search for Israel's guilt and find none. They will search for Judah's sins, and there will be nothing to find. Because I will forgive the remnant I leave alive.

[21] Attack the land of Merathaim. Attack it. And the people of Pekod. Hunt them down, kill them, finish them off — this is the LORD's command. Do everything I told you.

[22] The roar of battle is in the land. Massive destruction.

[23] Look how the hammer of the whole earth has been broken and shattered. Look at Babylon — what a horror she has become among the nations.

[24] I set a trap for you, Babylon, and you were caught before you knew it. You were found out and seized — because you took on the LORD.

[25] The LORD has opened his armory and brought out the weapons of his wrath. The Lord GOD of Heaven's Armies has work to do in the land of the Chaldeans.

[26] Come at her from every direction. Throw open her granaries. Pile her up like heaps of grain and destroy her completely. Leave nothing.

[27] Slaughter every bull of hers. Send them down to the butcher. Pity them — their day has come, the hour of their reckoning.

[28] Listen — refugees, survivors fleeing Babylon, are coming to Zion to announce the vengeance of the LORD our God, the vengeance for his temple.

[29] Summon the archers against Babylon — every man who bends a bow. Pitch your tents around her. Let no one escape. Pay her back for what she's done. Everything she did, do to her. She defied the LORD, the Holy One of Israel.

[30] So her young men will fall in her streets. All her soldiers will be silenced that day — the LORD speaks.

[31] I am against you, Arrogance — this is the Lord GOD of Heaven's Armies. Your day has come. The hour I will deal with you.

[32] Arrogance will stumble and fall, with no one to lift her up. I will set fire to her cities, and the flames will eat everything around her.

[33] This is what the LORD of Heaven's Armies says: The people of Israel are oppressed, and the people of Judah with them. Every one of their captors has them in a tight grip and refuses to let them go.

[34] But their Redeemer is strong. The LORD of Heaven's Armies is his name. He will take up their case with force — to give rest to the land, and trouble to the people of Babylon.

[35] A sword against the Chaldeans — the LORD speaks — against the citizens of Babylon, against her officials, against her wise men.

[36] A sword against her diviners — let them be exposed as fools. A sword against her warriors — let them be shattered.

[37] A sword against her horses and chariots and all the mercenaries inside her — let them turn into women. A sword against her treasures — let them be looted.

[38] A drought on her waters — let them dry up. This is a land of idols, a place gone mad with terrors.

[39] So desert creatures and hyenas will live there. Ostriches will roost in her. She will never be inhabited again, never be settled, generation after generation.

[40] As when God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah and their neighbors — the LORD speaks — no one will live there. No human being will pass through.

[41] Watch — a people is coming from the north. A mighty nation. Many kings are stirring from the ends of the earth.

[42] They wield bow and javelin. They are cruel — no mercy in them. Their roar is like the sea. They ride on horses, drawn up like a man for battle — against you, Daughter Babylon.

[43] The king of Babylon has heard the report. His hands hang limp. Anguish grips him, pain like a woman in labor.

[44] Watch — like a lion coming up from the dense thickets of the Jordan, the predator climbs against the strong pasture. In a flash I will chase them off it, and I will appoint over it whoever I choose. Because who is like me? Who can summon me to court? What shepherd can stand his ground in front of me?

[45] So listen to what the LORD has planned against Babylon, what he has designed against the land of the Chaldeans: Even the smallest of the flock will be dragged off. Even their pasture will recoil in horror at what happens to them.

[46] At the sound of Babylon's capture, the earth will quake. The scream of it will be heard among the nations.

[1] This is what the LORD says: Watch — I am rousing a destroying wind against Babylon, against those who live in Leb-kamai, the heart of my enemies. [2] I will send strangers to Babylon who will winnow her, who will strip her land bare. They will hem her in from every side on the day of her ruin.

[3] Let no archer stretch his bow. Let no warrior strap on his armor. Spare none of her young men. Wipe out her entire army. [4] They will fall, slain, in the land of the Chaldeans, run through in her streets.

[5] Israel and Judah are not abandoned by their God — not by the LORD of Armies — though their land overflowed with guilt against the Holy One of Israel.

[6] Run from Babylon! Save your own skin! Don't go down with her in her ruin. This is the LORD's payback time — he is giving her what she earned. [7] Babylon was a gold cup in the LORD's hand, intoxicating the whole earth. The nations drank her wine and went mad. [8] Then suddenly — Babylon fell. Shattered. Wail over her. Get balm for her wounds. Maybe she can be healed.

[9] We tried to heal Babylon, but she could not be healed. Leave her. Each of us, go home. Her judgment reaches to the heavens, towers up to the clouds. [10] The LORD has cleared us. Come — let's tell it in Zion, the work the LORD our God has done.

[11] Sharpen the arrows! Fill the quivers! The LORD has stirred up the spirit of the kings of the Medes — his plan is Babylon, to wreck her. This is the LORD's vengeance, vengeance for his temple.

[12] Raise the standard against Babylon's walls. Reinforce the watch. Post the sentries. Set the ambush. The LORD has both planned and done what he spoke against the people of Babylon.

[13] You who live by mighty waters, rich in treasure — your end has come. The thread of your life is cut.

[14] The LORD of Armies has sworn by himself: I will fill you with men like a swarm of locusts, and they will raise the victory cry over you.

[15] He made the earth by his power, set the world in place by his wisdom, stretched out the heavens by his understanding. [16] When he thunders, the waters in the heavens roar. He lifts mist from the ends of the earth, sends lightning with the rain, brings the wind out from his storehouses.

[17] Every human is a fool, without knowledge. Every goldsmith is shamed by his idol — those cast images are lies. There's no breath in them. [18] Worthless. A joke. When their judgment comes, they vanish. [19] Not so the Portion of Jacob — he is the Maker of everything, and Israel is the tribe of his inheritance. The LORD of Armies is his name.

[20] You are my war-hammer, my weapon of war. With you I shatter nations. With you I destroy kingdoms. [21] With you I shatter horse and rider. With you I shatter chariot and driver. [22] With you I shatter man and woman. With you I shatter old and young. With you I shatter boy and girl. [23] With you I shatter shepherd and flock. With you I shatter farmer and team. With you I shatter governors and officials.

[24] And right before your eyes I will repay Babylon and every Chaldean for all the evil they did in Zion. This is the LORD's word.

[25] Look — I am against you, mountain of destruction, you who destroyed the whole earth. I will stretch out my hand against you, roll you down from the cliffs, turn you into a charred mountain. [26] No one will take a cornerstone from you, no foundation stone. You'll be desolation, forever. This is the LORD's word.

[27] Raise a banner across the land! Sound the trumpet among the nations! Consecrate the nations against her. Summon the kingdoms — Ararat, Minni, Ashkenaz. Appoint a commander against her. Send up horses like bristling locusts. [28] Consecrate nations against her — the kings of the Medes, their governors and officials and every land they rule. [29] The earth shakes and writhes — the LORD's plans against Babylon stand: to make her land a wasteland with no one living there.

[30] Babylon's warriors have stopped fighting. They sit in their strongholds. Their strength is dried up. They've become women. Her dwellings are torched, her gate-bars broken. [31] Runner races to meet runner, messenger to meet messenger, telling the king of Babylon: the city is taken from end to end. [32] The river crossings are seized. The marshes are burning. The soldiers panic.

[33] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: The daughter of Babylon is a threshing floor at harvest time — soon, very soon, her harvest will come.

[34] Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon devoured me, crushed me, left me an empty vessel. He swallowed me like a sea-monster, gorged on my delicacies, then spat me out. [35] Let the violence done to me — to my own flesh — be on Babylon, says the woman of Zion. Let my blood be on the Chaldeans, says Jerusalem.

[36] So this is what the LORD says: Watch — I am taking up your case. I will execute your vengeance. I will dry up her sea. I will parch her springs. [37] Babylon will become a heap of rubble, a haunt of jackals, an object of horror and hissing — empty, no one living there.

[38] Her people roar together like lions. They growl like lion cubs. [39] When they're heated up, I'll set out their feast and get them drunk, until they're laughing — and then they'll fall into endless sleep and never wake. This is the LORD's word. [40] I'll lead them down to slaughter like lambs, like rams and goats.

[41] How Sheshak is captured! The pride of all the earth, seized! What a horror Babylon has become among the nations! [42] The sea has surged over Babylon — its roaring waves have buried her. [43] Her cities are wasteland, a parched and arid land, a country no one lives in, where no human passes through.

[44] I will punish Bel in Babylon. I will pull from his mouth what he swallowed. Nations will no longer stream to him. The wall of Babylon falls.

[45] Come out of her, my people! Each of you — save your own life from the burning fury of the LORD. [46] Don't lose heart. Don't panic at the rumors running through the land — one rumor this year, another the next, violence in the land, ruler against ruler. [47] The days are coming when I will punish the idols of Babylon. Her whole land will be put to shame. Her slain will lie scattered through her streets. [48] Then heaven and earth and everything in them will shout for joy over Babylon — because the destroyers are coming from the north. This is the LORD's word.

[49] Babylon must fall — because of the slain of Israel, just as the slain of all the earth fell because of Babylon.

[50] You who escaped the sword — go. Don't stand still. Remember the LORD from far away. Let Jerusalem rise up in your hearts.

[51] We are humiliated — we heard the insults. Shame covers our faces. Strangers walked into the holy places of the LORD's house.

[52] So the days are coming, says the LORD, when I will punish her idols, and through her whole land the wounded will groan. [53] Even if Babylon climbs to the sky and fortifies her heights into the clouds, destroyers from me will reach her. This is the LORD's word.

[54] Listen — screaming from Babylon. The crash of catastrophe from the land of the Chaldeans! [55] The LORD is laying Babylon waste, silencing her great noise. Their waves roar like crashing seas — the uproar of their voices rises. [56] A destroyer is coming against her — against Babylon. Her warriors will be captured, their bows snapped. The LORD is a God of payback. He pays in full.

[57] I will make her officials and her wise men drunk — her governors, her officers, her warriors. They will sleep an endless sleep and never wake. So says the King, the LORD of Armies is his name.

[58] This is what the LORD of Armies says: Babylon's massive walls will be leveled to the ground. Her towering gates will burn. The peoples labored for nothing. The nations exhausted themselves only to feed the fire.

[59] These are the orders Jeremiah the prophet gave to Seraiah son of Neriah, son of Mahseiah, when Seraiah went with King Zedekiah of Judah to Babylon in the fourth year of his reign. Seraiah was the quartermaster. [60] Jeremiah had written in a single scroll every disaster that would come on Babylon — all these words written against Babylon. [61] Jeremiah told Seraiah: When you reach Babylon, read aloud every word in this scroll. [62] Then say: LORD, you yourself have spoken against this place — to cut it off, so no one will live in it, neither human nor animal. It will be desolation forever. [63] When you finish reading the scroll, tie a stone to it and throw it into the Euphrates. [64] Then say: This is how Babylon will sink — and never rise — because of the disaster I am bringing on her. They will be exhausted.

The words of Jeremiah end here.

[1] Zedekiah was twenty-one when he took the throne, and he ruled in Jerusalem eleven years. His mother was Hamutal, daughter of Jeremiah, from Libnah.

[2] He did what was evil in the LORD's eyes — every bit as bad as Jehoiakim before him.

[3] It was the LORD's fury that drove what happened in Jerusalem and Judah, until finally he hurled them out of his presence. And Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon.

[4] So in the ninth year of his reign, the tenth month, the tenth day — Nebuchadnezzar came. The whole Babylonian army came with him. They camped against Jerusalem and threw up siege walls around it on every side.

[5] The city stayed under siege until the eleventh year of King Zedekiah.

[6] By the ninth day of the fourth month, the famine inside had become brutal. There was no bread left for anyone.

[7] Then the wall was breached. Every soldier ran. They slipped out of the city by night through the gate between the two walls, by the king's garden — the Babylonians had the city surrounded — and they fled toward the Arabah.

[8] But the Babylonian army chased the king and caught up with Zedekiah on the plains of Jericho. His whole army scattered, leaving him alone.

[9] They seized him and hauled him up to the king of Babylon at Riblah, in the land of Hamath, where sentence was pronounced.

[10] There the king of Babylon slaughtered Zedekiah's sons in front of him. He slaughtered all the officials of Judah at Riblah.
