[11] Then he gouged out Zedekiah's eyes, shackled him in bronze chains, and took him to Babylon. He locked him in prison until the day he died.

[12] In the fifth month, on the tenth day — the nineteenth year of King Nebuchadnezzar — Nebuzaradan, captain of the imperial guard, entered Jerusalem on behalf of the king of Babylon.

[13] He burned the house of the LORD. He burned the royal palace. He burned every house in Jerusalem — every important building went up in flames.

[14] The Babylonian army under his command tore down every wall surrounding Jerusalem.

[15] Nebuzaradan rounded up the poor who remained, the survivors still inside the city, the defectors who had crossed over to the king of Babylon, and what was left of the skilled craftsmen — and he marched them into exile.

[16] But Nebuzaradan left some of the poorest in the land behind to tend the vineyards and work the fields.

[17] The Babylonians shattered the bronze pillars of the LORD's house, the bronze stands, the bronze Sea — and they carried all the bronze back to Babylon.

[18] They took the pots, the shovels, the wick trimmers, the basins, the dishes — every bronze vessel used in the temple service.

[19] The captain of the guard also took the bowls, the firepans, the basins, the pots, the lampstands, the dishes, and the libation cups — anything of pure gold, anything of pure silver, he took.

[20] The two pillars, the one Sea, the twelve bronze bulls beneath it, the stands — what Solomon had made for the house of the LORD — the bronze of all of it was beyond weighing.

[21] Each pillar stood eighteen cubits high, twelve cubits around, hollow, the metal four fingers thick.

[22] On top sat a bronze capital, five cubits tall, ringed with network and pomegranates, all bronze. The second pillar matched, pomegranates and all.

[23] Ninety-six pomegranates faced outward. The total on the network: a hundred, all the way around.

[24] The captain of the guard seized Seraiah the chief priest, Zephaniah the second priest, and the three doorkeepers.

[25] From the city he took an officer who had commanded the soldiers, seven of the king's personal advisors found in the city, the scribe of the army commander who conscripted the people, and sixty men of the land caught inside the city.

[26] Nebuzaradan took them all and brought them to the king of Babylon at Riblah.

[27] There, at Riblah in the land of Hamath, the king of Babylon executed them. So Judah went into exile, far from its own soil.

[28] These are the numbers Nebuchadnezzar deported: in the seventh year, 3,023 Judeans.

[29] In his eighteenth year, 832 souls from Jerusalem.

[30] In his twenty-third year, Nebuzaradan deported 745 more Judeans. Total: 4,600.

[31] In the thirty-seventh year of Jehoiachin's exile — the twelfth month, the twenty-fifth day — Evil-merodach became king of Babylon, and in his first year he lifted Jehoiachin's head and brought him out of prison.

[32] He spoke kindly to him and gave him a seat above the other kings who were with him in Babylon.

[33] Jehoiachin took off his prison clothes. He ate at the king's table for the rest of his life.

[34] And his allowance — a steady, daily allowance from the king of Babylon — kept coming, every day, until the day he died.

[1] In the land of Uz there lived a man named Job — a man of clean hands and honest heart, who feared God and turned his back on evil.

[2] He had seven sons and three daughters,

[3] and his holdings ran deep: seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, five hundred donkeys, and a vast household of servants. Among all the people of the East, no one matched him.

[4] His sons would take turns hosting feasts in their homes, each on his appointed day, and they would send for their three sisters to eat and drink with them.

[5] When the round of feasting had run its course, Job would call them in and consecrate them. Rising at dawn, he would offer a burnt offering for each one, thinking, "Maybe my sons have sinned and cursed God in their hearts." This was Job's habit, again and again.

[6] One day the sons of God came to take their stations before the LORD, and the Accuser came too, walking right in among them.

[7] The LORD said to the Accuser, "Where have you been?" And the Accuser answered, "Roaming the earth. Pacing back and forth across it."

[8] Then the LORD said, "Have you noticed my servant Job? There's no one like him on the earth — a man of clean hands and honest heart, who fears God and turns his back on evil."

[9] The Accuser shot back, "And why wouldn't Job fear God?

[10] You've built a wall around him — around his house, around everything he owns. You bless the work of his hands, and his herds spread across the land.

[11] But stretch out your hand. Touch what he has. He'll curse you to your face."

[12] The LORD said to the Accuser, "Very well. Everything he owns is in your hands. Only — do not lay a finger on the man himself." And the Accuser walked out from the presence of the LORD.

[13] The day came when Job's sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine at the eldest brother's house,

[14] and a messenger burst in on Job: "The oxen were plowing and the donkeys grazing beside them,

[15] when Sabeans swept down, carried them off, and put the servants to the sword. I'm the only one who got out — I alone escaped to tell you."

[16] He was still speaking when another came running: "Fire from God fell from the sky. It burned up the sheep and the servants, devoured them whole. I alone escaped to tell you."

[17] He was still speaking when another came running: "Chaldeans — three raiding parties of them — descended on the camels and carried them off. They put the servants to the sword. I alone escaped to tell you."

[18] He was still speaking when another came running: "Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine at their eldest brother's house,

[19] when a great wind came howling in from across the desert. It struck the four corners of the house. The house collapsed on them. They are all dead. I alone escaped to tell you."

[20] Then Job stood up. He tore his robe. He shaved his head. He fell to the ground — and worshiped.

[21] He said: "Naked I came out of my mother's womb, and naked I will return. The LORD gave. The LORD took. Blessed be the name of the LORD."

[22] In all this, Job did not sin. He did not lay a single charge against God.

[1] Another day came. The sons of God gathered before the LORD, and the Adversary slipped in among them again to present himself.

[2] The LORD said to the Adversary, "Where have you been?"

The Adversary answered, "Roaming the earth. Walking it end to end."

[3] The LORD said, "Did you notice my servant Job? There is no one like him on earth — a man of integrity, upright, reverent toward God, allergic to evil. He is still holding fast to his character, even though you incited me against him to ruin him for nothing."

[4] The Adversary shot back, "Skin for skin. A man will surrender everything he has to save his own hide.

[5] But stretch out your hand and touch his bone, touch his flesh — and watch. He will curse you to your face."

[6] The LORD said, "Fine. He is in your hand. Only — spare his life."

[7] So the Adversary walked out from the presence of the LORD and struck Job with vicious sores from the soles of his feet to the crown of his head.

[8] Job took a shard of broken pottery to scrape himself and sat down in the ash heap.

[9] His wife said to him, "Are you still clinging to your integrity? Curse God. Die."

[10] He answered, "You're talking like a fool. We accept the good from God — do we refuse the bad?"

Through all of this, Job did not sin with his lips.

[11] When Job's three friends heard about every disaster that had crashed down on him, they came — each from his own place. Eliphaz from Teman. Bildad from Shuah. Zophar from Naamah. They agreed together to come and mourn with him and comfort him.

[12] When they spotted him from a distance, they did not recognize him. They broke down weeping. They tore their robes. They threw dust over their heads, into the sky.

[13] Then they sat with him on the ground. Seven days. Seven nights. Nobody said a word. They saw that his pain was past speaking.

[1] Finally Job broke. He opened his mouth and cursed the day he was born.

[2] He said:

[3] Erase the day I came into this world. Blot out the night that whispered, "A boy is conceived."

[4] Let that day go black. Let God above forget it ever existed. Let no light find it.

[5] Let darkness and the shadow of death claim it back. Let storm clouds bury it. Let every eclipse terrify it.

[6] That night — let thick darkness seize it. Strike it from the calendar. Let no month count it among its days.

[7] Yes — let that night be barren. Let no joyful cry break through it.

[8] Let the cursers of days curse it — those skilled at rousing Leviathan.

[9] Let its twilight stars stay dark. Let it wait for dawn and find nothing. Let it never see the first crack of morning light —

[10] because it did not slam shut the womb that carried me, did not hide misery from my eyes.

[11] Why didn't I die at birth? Why didn't I take my first breath and my last in the same moment?

[12] Why were there knees to receive me? Why were there breasts to nurse me?

[13] By now I'd be lying still. I'd be sleeping. I'd be at rest —

[14] alongside kings and counselors of the earth who built their monuments on top of ruins,

[15] alongside princes who hoarded gold and packed their houses with silver.

[16] Or why wasn't I a stillbirth, tossed away, never seeing the light — like infants who never opened their eyes?

[17] Down there the wicked stop their raging. Down there the exhausted finally rest.

[18] Prisoners breathe easy together. They no longer hear the slave driver's voice.

[19] Small and great — same ground. The slave walks free from his master.

[20] Why is light handed to a man in misery? Why life to the bitter soul —

[21] to those who crave death and it won't come, who dig for it like buried treasure,

[22] who would dance with joy, who would celebrate if they could just find the grave?

[23] Why give light to a man whose road is hidden, whom God has fenced in on every side?

[24] My groans arrive before my food. My cries pour out like water.

[25] The thing I feared has hunted me down. What I dreaded has found me.

[26] No peace. No quiet. No rest. Only this — turmoil, unending.

[1] Then Eliphaz the Temanite broke the silence:

[2] "If someone tries a word with you, will you crack? But who can hold their tongue right now?

[3] Look — you've coached so many. You've put strength back into trembling hands.

[4] Your words lifted up the stumbling. You braced knees that were buckling.

[5] Now it lands on you, and you crumble. It touches you, and you fall apart.

[6] Wasn't your reverence supposed to be your confidence? Your blameless life — your hope?

[7] Think back. Name one innocent person who was ever wiped out. Where have the upright been erased?

[8] Here's what I've seen: those who plow trouble and plant misery harvest exactly that.

[9] One breath from God and they're gone. One blast of his nostrils and they vanish.

[10] The lion roars, the great cat snarls — and still, the fangs of the young lions are shattered.

[11] The old lion dies for lack of prey. The cubs of the lioness scatter to nothing.

[12] A word came to me — slipped in like a secret. My ear caught the faintest whisper of it.

[13] In the unease of night visions, when deep sleep falls on humankind,

[14] dread seized me. Terror. My bones rattled.

[15] A breath drifted past my face. The hair on my body stood up.

[16] Something stood there — I couldn't make out its form. A shape before my eyes. Silence. Then a voice:

[17] 'Can a mortal be right before God? Can a human be pure before his Maker?

[18] Look — he doesn't even trust his own servants. He finds fault in his angels.

[19] How much more those who live in houses of clay, whose foundation is dust, who can be crushed like a moth?

[20] Between sunrise and sunset they're broken to pieces. Without anyone noticing, they're gone forever.

[21] Their tent-cord is yanked out from inside them. They die — and never gained wisdom.'"

[1] Go ahead, Job — call out. See if anyone answers. Which of the holy ones will you turn to?

[2] Resentment kills the fool. Envy buries the simpleton.

[3] I have watched a fool sink roots, looking secure — and then suddenly his house was cursed.

[4] His children were stranded, crushed at the city gate with no one to defend them.

[5] The hungry devoured his harvest, snatching it even from the thorns. The thirsty swallowed his wealth whole.

[6] Suffering does not sprout from the dirt. Trouble does not grow up out of the ground.

[7] No — humans are born for trouble as surely as sparks fly upward.

[8] If I were you, I would go straight to God. I would lay my case before him.

[9] He performs wonders past tracing, miracles past counting.

[10] He sends rain on the land and water across the fields.

[11] He lifts the lowly to high places and hoists the grieving to safety.

[12] He unravels the schemes of the cunning so their hands accomplish nothing real.

[13] He traps the clever in their own cleverness; the plans of the twisted collapse fast.

[14] In broad daylight they stumble into darkness — at noon they grope like it is midnight.

[15] But the needy he rescues — from the sword of their mouths, from the grip of the strong.

[16] So the poor have hope, and injustice is forced to shut its mouth.

[17] Listen — the one God corrects is blessed. Do not despise the discipline of the Almighty.

[18] He wounds, then binds the wound. He strikes, then his hands heal.

[19] Six disasters — he will pull you out. Seven — and harm will not touch you.

[20] In famine, he will buy back your life. In war, he will rescue you from the sword.

[21] You will be hidden from the lash of slander, unafraid when destruction comes calling.

[22] You will laugh at ruin and starvation. You will not fear the wild beasts of the earth —

[23] for you will have a covenant with the stones of the field, and the wild animals will keep peace with you.

[24] You will know your tent is safe. You will check on your flocks and find nothing missing.

[25] You will see your children multiply, your descendants spread like grass across the ground.

[26] You will go to the grave in full strength, like wheat gathered in its season.

[27] We have studied this. It is true. So listen — and apply it to yourself.
